AI.motivation: Existing SoS reconstruction methods have significant limitations: traditional Full Waveform Inversion is computationally intensive, while current deep learning approaches tend to produce oversmoothed results lacking fine anatomical details and pathological variations.
AI.method: DiffSOS employs a conditional diffusion model with a specialized acoustic ControlNet to ground denoising in physical wave measurements. It uses a hybrid loss function integrating noise prediction, spatial reconstruction, and noise frequency content. Stochastic DDIM sampling enables near real-time inference with only 10 steps. The framework also exploits its stochastic nature for pixel-wise uncertainty estimation.
AI.result: Evaluated on the OpenPros USCT benchmark, DiffSOS significantly outperforms state-of-the-art networks with an average Multi-scale Structural Similarity (MS-SSIM) of 0.957. It achieves near real-time reconstruction with 10 DDIM steps and provides principled confidence measures through uncertainty estimation.
AI.conclusion: DiffSOS delivers high-fidelity speed-of-sound maps with quantified reliability, overcoming the oversmoothing issue of previous deep learning methods while maintaining computational efficiency. This facilitates safer and faster clinical interpretation in ultrasound computed tomography applications.

title: ASTRA-bench: Evaluating Tool-Use Agent Reasoning and Action Planning with Personal User Context
summary: Next-generation AI must manage vast personal data, diverse tools, and multi-step reasoning, yet most benchmarks remain context-free and single-turn. We present ASTRA-bench (Assistant Skills in Tool-use, Reasoning \& Action-planning), a benchmark that uniquely unifies time-evolving personal context with an interactive toolbox and complex user intents. Our event-driven pipeline generates 2,413 scenarios across four protagonists, grounded in longitudinal life events and annotated by referential, functional, and informational complexity. Evaluation of state-of-the-art models (e.g., Claude-4.5-Opus, DeepSeek-V3.2) reveals significant performance degradation under high-complexity conditions, with argument generation emerging as the primary bottleneck. These findings expose critical limitations in current agents' ability to ground reasoning within messy personal context and orchestrate reliable multi-step plans. We release ASTRA-bench with a full execution environment and evaluation scripts to provide a diagnostic testbed for developing truly context-aware AI assistants.

AI.tldr: ASTRA-bench is a new benchmark that unifies time-evolving personal context with interactive tool use and complex reasoning tasks, revealing significant performance gaps in state-of-the-art AI models when handling high-complexity scenarios.
AI.motivation: Next-generation AI must manage vast personal data, diverse tools, and multi-step reasoning, yet existing benchmarks remain context-free and single-turn, limiting evaluation of truly context-aware AI assistants.
AI.method: ASTRA-bench (Assistant Skills in Tool-use, Reasoning & Action-planning) features an event-driven pipeline generating 2,413 scenarios across four protagonists grounded in longitudinal life events, annotated by referential, functional, and informational complexity.
AI.result: State-of-the-art models (Claude-4.5-Opus, DeepSeek-V3.2) exhibit significant performance degradation under high-complexity conditions, with argument generation emerging as the primary bottleneck.
AI.conclusion: These findings expose critical limitations in current agents' ability to ground reasoning within messy personal context and orchestrate reliable multi-step plans, highlighting the need for better context-aware AI assistants.

title: Bridge Matching Sampler: Scalable Sampling via Generalized Fixed-Point Diffusion Matching
summary: Sampling from unnormalized densities using diffusion models has emerged as a powerful paradigm. However, while recent approaches that use least-squares `matching' objectives have improved scalability, they often necessitate significant trade-offs, such as restricting prior distributions or relying on unstable optimization schemes. By generalizing these methods as special forms of fixed-point iterations rooted in Nelson's relation, we develop a new method that addresses these limitations, called Bridge Matching Sampler (BMS). Our approach enables learning a stochastic transport map between arbitrary prior and target distributions with a single, scalable, and stable objective. Furthermore, we introduce a damped variant of this iteration that incorporates a regularization term to mitigate mode collapse and further stabilize training. Empirically, we demonstrate that our method enables sampling at unprecedented scales while preserving mode diversity, achieving state-of-the-art results on complex synthetic densities and high-dimensional molecular benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Bridge Matching Sampler (BMS) generalizes least-squares matching methods as fixed-point iterations from Nelson's relation, enabling scalable and stable sampling from unnormalized densities with arbitrary prior distributions, enhanced by a damped variant that mitigates mode collapse.
AI.motivation: Existing least-squares matching approaches for sampling from unnormalized densities face scalability issues and require trade-offs like restricting prior distributions or using unstable optimization schemes.
AI.method: BMS generalizes existing methods as fixed-point iterations rooted in Nelson's relation, enabling learning stochastic transport maps between arbitrary prior and target distributions with a single, scalable, and stable objective. A damped variant incorporates regularization to mitigate mode collapse and stabilize training.
AI.result: BMS enables sampling at unprecedented scales while preserving mode diversity, achieving state-of-the-art results on complex synthetic densities and high-dimensional molecular benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: BMS addresses key limitations of prior approaches by providing a unified framework for diffusion-based sampling that works with arbitrary priors, uses stable optimization, and maintains mode diversity, establishing a new state-of-the-art for high-dimensional sampling tasks.

title: Mean-Flow based One-Step Vision-Language-Action
summary: Recent advances in FlowMatching-based Vision-Language-Action (VLA) frameworks have demonstrated remarkable advantages in generating high-frequency action chunks, particularly for highly dexterous robotic manipulation tasks. Despite these notable achievements, their practical applications are constrained by prolonged generation latency, which stems from inherent iterative sampling requirements and architectural limitations. To address this critical bottleneck, we propose a Mean-Flow based One-Step VLA approach. Specifically, we resolve the noise-induced issues in the action generation process, thereby eliminating the consistency constraints inherent to conventional Flow-Matching methods. This significantly enhances generation efficiency and enables one-step action generation. Real-world robotic experiments show that the generation speed of the proposed Mean-Flow based One-Step VLA is 8.7 times and 83.9 times faster than that of SmolVLA and Diffusion Policy, respectively. These results elucidate its great potential as a high-efficiency backbone for VLA-based robotic manipulation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Mean-Flow based One-Step Vision-Language-Action (VLA) framework that eliminates iterative sampling requirements, achieving 8.7x and 83.9x faster generation than SmolVLA and Diffusion Policy respectively for robotic manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: FlowMatching-based VLA frameworks excel at generating high-frequency action chunks for dexterous robotic manipulation but suffer from prolonged generation latency due to iterative sampling requirements and architectural limitations, which hinders practical applications.
AI.method: The authors propose a Mean-Flow based One-Step VLA approach that resolves noise-induced issues in action generation, thereby eliminating the consistency constraints inherent in conventional Flow-Matching methods and enabling one-step action generation.
AI.result: Real-world robotic experiments demonstrate that the proposed Mean-Flow based One-Step VLA achieves 8.7 times and 83.9 times faster generation speed compared to SmolVLA and Diffusion Policy respectively.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method shows great potential as a high-efficiency backbone for VLA-based robotic manipulation, significantly addressing the latency bottleneck while maintaining effectiveness in dexterous manipulation tasks.

title: Can Thinking Models Think to Detect Hateful Memes?
summary: Hateful memes often require compositional multimodal reasoning: the image and text may appear benign in isolation, yet their interaction conveys harmful intent. Although thinking-based multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have recently advanced vision-language understanding, their capabilities remain underexplored for hateful meme analysis. We propose a reinforcement learning based post-training framework that improves reasoning in thinking-based MLLMs through task-specific rewards and a novel Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) objective. Specifically, we (i) conduct a systematic empirical study of off-the-shelf MLLMs for hateful meme understanding, (ii) extend an existing hateful meme dataset by generating weakly or pseudo-supervised chain-of-thought rationales via distillation, and (iii) introduce a GRPO-based objective that jointly optimizes meme classification and explanation quality to encourage fine-grained, step-by-step reasoning. Experiments on the Hateful Memes benchmark show that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving accuracy and F1 by approximately 1 percent and explanation quality by approximately 3 percent. We will publicly release our code, dataset extensions, and evaluation resources to support reproducibility.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a reinforcement learning-based post-training framework called GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) to improve reasoning capabilities in thinking-based multimodal large language models (MLLMs) for hateful meme analysis, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the Hateful Memes benchmark.
AI.motivation: Hateful memes require compositional multimodal reasoning where the image and text may appear benign in isolation but convey harmful intent through their interaction. While thinking-based MLLMs have advanced vision-language understanding, their capabilities remain underexplored for hateful meme analysis.
AI.method: The approach consists of three main components: (1) conducting a systematic empirical study of off-the-shelf MLLMs for hateful meme understanding, (2) extending an existing hateful meme dataset by generating weakly/pseudo-supervised chain-of-thought rationales via distillation, and (3) introducing a GRPO-based objective that jointly optimizes meme classification and explanation quality to encourage fine-grained, step-by-step reasoning.
AI.result: Experiments on the Hateful Memes benchmark show state-of-the-art performance, improving accuracy and F1 by approximately 1% and explanation quality by approximately 3%.
AI.conclusion: The proposed GRPO-based framework effectively improves reasoning in thinking-based MLLMs for hateful meme analysis. The authors will publicly release their code, dataset extensions, and evaluation resources to support reproducibility.

title: SSR: Pushing the Limit of Spatial Intelligence with Structured Scene Reasoning
summary: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) excel in semantic tasks, they frequently lack the "spatial sense" essential for sophisticated geometric reasoning. Current models typically suffer from exorbitant modality-alignment costs and deficiency in fine-grained structural modeling precision.We introduce SSR, a framework designed for Structured Scene Reasoning that seamlessly integrates 2D and 3D representations via a lightweight alignment mechanism. To minimize training overhead, our framework anchors 3D geometric features to the large language model's pre-aligned 2D visual semantics through cross-modal addition and token interleaving, effectively obviating the necessity for large-scale alignment pre-training. To underpin complex spatial reasoning, we propose a novel scene graph generation pipeline that represents global layouts as a chain of independent local triplets defined by relative coordinates. This is complemented by an incremental generation algorithm, enabling the model to construct "language-model-friendly" structural scaffolds for complex environments. Furthermore, we extend these capabilities to global-scale 3D global grounding task, achieving absolute metric precision across heterogeneous data sources. At a 7B parameter scale, SSR achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple spatial intelligence benchmarks, notably scoring 73.9 on VSI-Bench. Our approach significantly outperforms much larger models, demonstrating that efficient feature alignment and structured scene reasoning are the cornerstones of authentic spatial intelligence.

AI.tldr: SSR introduces a lightweight framework for Structured Scene Reasoning that integrates 2D and 3D representations through cross-modal addition and token interleaving, enabling efficient spatial reasoning at 7B parameters without large-scale alignment pre-training.
AI.motivation: MLLMs excel at semantic tasks but lack spatial sense for geometric reasoning; current models suffer from high modality-alignment costs and lack fine-grained structural modeling precision.
AI.method: SSR anchors 3D geometric features to pre-aligned 2D visual semantics via cross-modal addition and token interleaving. It proposes a scene graph generation pipeline representing global layouts as chains of local triplets with relative coordinates, plus an incremental generation algorithm for "language-model-friendly" structural scaffolds. Extended to global-scale 3D grounding.
AI.result: At 7B parameters, SSR achieves state-of-the-art on multiple spatial intelligence benchmarks, scoring 73.9 on VSI-Bench and outperforming much larger models.
AI.conclusion: Efficient feature alignment and structured scene reasoning are the cornerstones of authentic spatial intelligence, demonstrating that sophisticated spatial reasoning can be achieved without massive parameter counts.

title: Words & Weights: Streamlining Multi-Turn Interactions via Co-Adaptation
summary: Test-time policy adaptation for multi-turn interactions (T2PAM) is essential for aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) with dynamic user needs during inference time. However, existing paradigms commonly treat test-time adaptation as a single-axis problem, either purely refining instructions (Prompt Engineering) or only adjusting weights (Test-Time Training), ignoring that interaction failures stem from a coupled mix of ambiguity and incapacity. We argue that these two optimization paths are not merely additive but synergistic: semantic clarity acts as a pre-conditioner for effective parameter updates. To this end, we propose ROSA2, a framework that reformulates interaction as a joint optimization problem over the heterogeneous space of Words and Weights. By mathematically decomposing the error signal, ROSA2 utilizes textual gradients to rectify intent ambiguity and parameter updates to bridge capability gaps. Theoretically, we prove that this co-adaptation strictly reduces the required parameter shift for convergence. Empirically, ROSA2 outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by 30% on MATH while reducing interaction turns by 40%, demonstrating that refining the context unlocks the true potential of parameter updates.

AI.tldr: ROSA2 is a test-time policy adaptation framework that jointly optimizes textual context (words) and model parameters (weights) using textual gradients and parameter updates, achieving 30% improvement on MATH and 40% fewer interaction turns compared to baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing test-time adaptation paradigms treat it as either pure prompt engineering (refining instructions) or test-time training (adjusting weights), failing to recognize that interaction failures stem from a coupled mix of semantic ambiguity and model incapacity. The authors argue these two optimization paths are synergistic rather than additive - semantic clarity acts as a pre-conditioner for effective parameter updates.
AI.method: ROSA2 reformulates multi-turn interactions as a joint optimization problem over the heterogeneous space of Words and Weights. It mathematically decomposes the error signal into textual components for ambiguity resolution and parameter components for capability bridging. The framework proves that co-adaptation strictly reduces the required parameter shift for convergence, enabling simultaneous refinement of context and parameters.
AI.result: Empirically, ROSA2 outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by 30% on the MATH dataset while reducing interaction turns by 40%. These results demonstrate that refining the interaction context unlocks the true potential of parameter updates, achieving both higher accuracy and greater efficiency in multi-turn LLM interactions.
AI.conclusion: The synergistic co-adaptation of semantic clarity (words) and parameter updates (weights) is more effective than treating them as separate, additive processes. This research establishes that textual gradients can effectively rectify intent ambiguity, enabling more efficient parameter convergence. The framework provides a principled approach to test-time policy adaptation that balances accuracy and interaction efficiency.

title: Mathematical Foundations of Poisoning Attacks on Linear Regression over Cumulative Distribution Functions
summary: Learned indexes are a class of index data structures that enable fast search by approximating the cumulative distribution function (CDF) using machine learning models (Kraska et al., SIGMOD'18). However, recent studies have shown that learned indexes are vulnerable to poisoning attacks, where injecting a small number of poison keys into the training data can significantly degrade model accuracy and reduce index performance (Kornaropoulos et al., SIGMOD'22). In this work, we provide a rigorous theoretical analysis of poisoning attacks targeting linear regression models over CDFs, one of the most basic regression models and a core component in many learned indexes. Our main contributions are as follows: (i) We present a theoretical proof characterizing the optimal single-point poisoning attack and show that the existing method yields the optimal attack. (ii) We show that in multi-point attacks, the existing greedy approach is not always optimal, and we rigorously derive the key properties that an optimal attack should satisfy. (iii) We propose a method to compute an upper bound of the multi-point poisoning attack's impact and empirically demonstrate that the loss under the greedy approach is often close to this bound. Our study deepens the theoretical understanding of attack strategies against linear regression models on CDFs and provides a foundation for the theoretical evaluation of attacks and defenses on learned indexes.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a rigorous theoretical analysis of poisoning attacks on linear regression models used in learned indexes, characterizing optimal single-point attacks and deriving key properties for multi-point attacks.
AI.motivation: Learned indexes use ML to approximate CDFs for fast search but are vulnerable to poisoning attacks; existing work showed attacks can significantly degrade performance, but lacked rigorous theoretical analysis of optimal attack strategies.
AI.method: The authors analyze poisoning attacks on linear regression models over CDFs, providing: (i) theoretical proof of optimal single-point attack characterization, (ii) derivation of key properties for optimal multi-point attacks, (iii) method to compute upper bound of multi-point attack impact.
AI.result: Theoretical proof shows existing single-point attack method is optimal. For multi-point attacks, the existing greedy approach is not always optimal; authors derive key properties of optimal attacks and propose a method to compute attack impact upper bound, showing greedy approach is often close to this bound.
AI.conclusion: This work deepens theoretical understanding of attack strategies against linear regression models on CDFs, providing foundation for theoretical evaluation of attacks and defenses on learned indexes.

title: ROSER: Few-Shot Robotic Sequence Retrieval for Scalable Robot Learning
summary: A critical bottleneck in robot learning is the scarcity of task-labeled, segmented training data, despite the abundance of large-scale robotic datasets recorded as long, continuous interaction logs. Existing datasets contain vast amounts of diverse behaviors, yet remain structurally incompatible with modern learning frameworks that require cleanly segmented, task-specific trajectories. We address this data utilization crisis by formalizing robotic sequence retrieval: the task of extracting reusable, task-centric segments from unlabeled logs using only a few reference examples. We introduce ROSER, a lightweight few-shot retrieval framework that learns task-agnostic metric spaces over temporal windows, enabling accurate retrieval with as few as 3-5 demonstrations, without any task-specific training required. To validate our approach, we establish comprehensive evaluation protocols and benchmark ROSER against classical alignment methods, learned embeddings, and language model baselines across three large-scale datasets (e.g., LIBERO, DROID, and nuScenes). Our experiments demonstrate that ROSER consistently outperforms all prior methods in both accuracy and efficiency, achieving sub-millisecond per-match inference while maintaining superior distributional alignment. By reframing data curation as few-shot retrieval, ROSER provides a practical pathway to unlock underutilized robotic datasets, fundamentally improving data availability for robot learning.

AI.tldr: ROSER is a lightweight few-shot retrieval framework that extracts task-specific segments from unlabeled robotic logs using only 3-5 demonstrations, overcoming the scarcity of labeled training data in robotics.
AI.motivation: Robotics datasets contain abundant continuous interaction logs with diverse behaviors but lack structurally compatible, cleanly segmented task-specific trajectories needed by modern learning frameworks.
AI.method: ROSER learns task-agnostic metric spaces over temporal windows to enable few-shot retrieval of reusable, task-centric segments from unlabeled logs without task-specific training.
AI.result: ROSER consistently outperforms classical alignment methods, learned embeddings, and language model baselines across LIBERO, DROID, and nuScenes datasets, achieving sub-millisecond inference per match with superior distributional alignment.
AI.conclusion: By reframing data curation as few-shot retrieval, ROSER unlocks underutilized robotic datasets and provides a practical pathway to improve data availability for robot learning.

title: Self-Anchoring Calibration Drift in Large Language Models: How Multi-Turn Conversations Reshape Model Confidence
summary: We introduce Self-Anchoring Calibration Drift (SACD), a hypothesized tendency for large language models (LLMs) to show systematic changes in expressed confidence when building iteratively on their own prior outputs across multi-turn conversations. We report an empirical study comparing three frontier models -- Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and GPT-5.2 -- across 150 questions spanning factual, technical, and open-ended domains, using three conditions: single-turn baseline (A), multi-turn self-anchoring (B), and independent repetition control (C). Results reveal a complex, model-heterogeneous pattern that partially diverges from pre-registered hypotheses. Claude Sonnet 4.6 exhibited significant decreasing confidence under self-anchoring (mean CDS = -0.032, t(14) = -2.43, p = .029, d = -0.627), while also showing significant calibration error drift (F(4,56) = 22.77, p < .001, eta^2 = .791). GPT-5.2 showed the opposite pattern in open-ended domains (mean CDS = +0.026) with significant ECE escalation by Turn 5. Gemini 3.1 Pro showed no significant CDS (t(14) = 0.38, p = .710), but its Condition C data reveals a striking ECE pattern: without self-anchoring, Gemini's calibration error drops from .327 to near zero across repetitions, whereas self-anchoring holds ECE flat at approximately .333 -- indicating that SACD can manifest as suppression of natural calibration improvement rather than ac

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Self-Anchoring Calibration Drift (SACD), a phenomenon where LLMs show systematic confidence changes when iteratively building on their own outputs across multi-turn conversations. Testing three frontier models across 150 questions revealed a complex, model-heterogeneous pattern: Claude Sonnet 4.6 showed decreasing confidence under self-anchoring, GPT-5.2 showed increasing confidence in open-ended domains, and Gemini 3.1 Pro suppressed natural calibration improvement when self-anchoring.
AI.motivation: LLMs tend to show systematic changes in expressed confidence when building iteratively on their own prior outputs across multi-turn conversations. Understanding this calibration drift phenomenon is important for evaluating model reliability in interactive systems.
AI.method: Empirical study comparing Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and GPT-5.2 across 150 questions spanning factual, technical, and open-ended domains. Three conditions tested: single-turn baseline (A), multi-turn self-anchoring (B), and independent repetition control (C). Measured calibration error drift and confidence changes across turns.
AI.result: Claude Sonnet 4.6 exhibited significant decreasing confidence under self-anchoring (mean CDS = -0.032, p = .029, d = -0.627) with significant calibration error drift. GPT-5.2 showed the opposite pattern in open-ended domains (mean CDS = +0.026) with significant ECE escalation by Turn 5. Gemini 3.1 Pro showed no significant CDS but revealed that without self-anchoring, its calibration error dropped from .327 to near zero, while self-anchoring held ECE flat at ~.333.
AI.conclusion: SACD manifests as a complex, model-heterogeneous pattern that partially diverges from pre-registered hypotheses. The phenomenon can appear as confidence decrease (Claude), confidence increase (GPT-5.2), or suppression of natural calibration improvement (Gemini), suggesting that self-anchoring behavior varies significantly across different LLM architectures.

title: PointAlign: Feature-Level Alignment Regularization for 3D Vision-Language Models
summary: The development of 3D Vision-Language Models (VLMs), crucial for applications in robotics, autonomous driving, and augmented reality, is severely constrained by the scarcity of paired 3D-text data. Existing methods rely solely on next-token prediction loss, using only language tokens for supervision. This results in inefficient utilization of limited 3D data and leads to a significant degradation and loss of valuable geometric information in intermediate representations. To address these limitations, we propose {\mname}, a novel feature-level alignment regularization method. {\mname} explicitly supervises intermediate point cloud tokens to preserve fine-grained 3D geometric-semantic information throughout the language modeling process. Specifically, we constrain the intermediate point cloud tokens within the LLM to align with visual input tokens via a consistency loss. By training only a lightweight alignment projector and LoRA adapters, {\mname} achieves explicit feature-level supervision with minimal computational overhead, effectively preventing geometric degradation. Extensive experiments on ModelNet40 and Objaverse datasets demonstrate that our method achieves \textbf{2.08} pp improvement on average for classification tasks, with a substantial \textbf{7.50} pp gain on the challenging open-vocabulary Objaverse classification task and \textbf{4.88} pp improvement on 3D object captioning evaluated by Qwen2-72B-Instruct, validating the effectiveness of {\mname}. Code is publicly available at \href{https://github.com/yharoldsu0627/PointAlign}{https://github.com/yharoldsu0627/PointAlign}.

AI.tldr: PointAlign is a feature-level alignment regularization method for 3D Vision-Language Models that supervises intermediate point cloud tokens to preserve geometric information during language modeling, achieving significant performance improvements across classification and captioning tasks.
AI.motivation: 3D Vision-Language Models are crucial for robotics, autonomous driving, and augmented reality but face severe constraints due to scarce paired 3D-text data. Existing methods rely solely on next-token prediction loss with language tokens for supervision, which leads to inefficient utilization of limited 3D data and significant degradation/loss of valuable geometric information in intermediate representations.
AI.method: PointAlign introduces a feature-level alignment regularization method that explicitly supervises intermediate point cloud tokens within the LLM to preserve fine-grained 3D geometric-semantic information. It constrains these tokens to align with visual input tokens via a consistency loss. The method trains only a lightweight alignment projector and LoRA adapters with minimal computational overhead.
AI.result: On ModelNet40 classification: 2.08 pp average improvement; on Objaverse open-vocabulary classification: 7.50 pp gain; on 3D object captioning (Qwen2-72B-Instruct): 4.88 pp improvement.
AI.conclusion: PointAlign effectively prevents geometric degradation while maintaining minimal computational overhead, demonstrating significant performance gains across multiple 3D vision-language tasks.

title: HarmonyCell: Automating Single-Cell Perturbation Modeling under Semantic and Distribution Shifts
comment: 18 pages total (8 pages main text + appendix), 6 figures
summary: Single-cell perturbation studies face dual heterogeneity bottlenecks: (i) semantic heterogeneity--identical biological concepts encoded under incompatible metadata schemas across datasets; and (ii) statistical heterogeneity--distribution shifts from biological variation demanding dataset-specific inductive biases. We propose HarmonyCell, an end-to-end agent framework resolving each challenge through a dedicated mechanism: an LLM-driven Semantic Unifier autonomously maps disparate metadata into a canonical interface without manual intervention; and an adaptive Monte Carlo Tree Search engine operates over a hierarchical action space to synthesize architectures with optimal statistical inductive biases for distribution shifts. Evaluated across diverse perturbation tasks under both semantic and distribution shifts, HarmonyCell achieves a 95% valid execution rate on heterogeneous input datasets (versus 0% for general agents) while matching or even exceeding expert-designed baselines in rigorous out-of-distribution evaluations. This dual-track orchestration enables scalable automatic virtual cell modeling without dataset-specific engineering.

AI.tldr: HarmonyCell is an end-to-end agent framework for single-cell perturbation studies that resolves semantic heterogeneity (via an LLM-driven Semantic Unifier) and statistical heterogeneity (via adaptive Monte Carlo Tree Search), achieving 95% valid execution rate on heterogeneous inputs versus 0% for general agents.
AI.motivation: Single-cell perturbation studies face dual bottlenecks: (1) semantic heterogeneity where identical biological concepts are encoded under incompatible metadata schemas across datasets, and (2) statistical heterogeneity where distribution shifts from biological variation require dataset-specific inductive biases.
AI.method: HarmonyCell employs two dedicated mechanisms: (1) an LLM-driven Semantic Unifier that autonomously maps disparate metadata into a canonical interface without manual intervention, and (2) an adaptive Monte Carlo Tree Search engine operating over a hierarchical action space to synthesize architectures with optimal statistical inductive biases for distribution shifts.
AI.result: Across diverse perturbation tasks under both semantic and distribution shifts, HarmonyCell achieves a 95% valid execution rate on heterogeneous input datasets (versus 0% for general agents) while matching or exceeding expert-designed baselines in rigorous out-of-distribution evaluations.
AI.conclusion: HarmonyCell's dual-track orchestration enables scalable automatic virtual cell modeling without dataset-specific engineering, representing a significant advance in automated single-cell analysis.

title: Spectral Condition for $μ$P under Width-Depth Scaling
summary: Generative foundation models are increasingly scaled in both width and depth, posing significant challenges for stable feature learning and reliable hyperparameter (HP) transfer across model sizes. While maximal update parameterization ($μ$P) has provided a principled solution to both problems for width scaling, existing extensions to the joint width-depth scaling regime remain fragmented, architecture- and optimizer-specific, and often rely on technically involved theories. In this work, we develop a simple and unified spectral framework for $μ$P under joint width-depth scaling. Considering residual networks of varying block depths, we first introduce a spectral $μ$P condition that precisely characterizes how the norms of weights and their per-step updates should scale with width and depth, unifying previously disparate $μ$P formulations as special cases. Building on this condition, we then derive a general recipe for implementing $μ$P across a broad class of optimizers by mapping the spectral constraints to concrete HP parameterizations. This approach not only recovers existing $μ$P formulations (e.g., for SGD and AdamW) but also naturally extends to a wider range of optimizers. Finally, experiments on GPT-2 style language models demonstrate that the proposed spectral $μ$P condition preserves stable feature learning and enables robust HP transfer under width-depth scaling.

AI.tldr: A unified spectral framework for maximal update parameterization (μP) under joint width-depth scaling in neural networks, enabling stable feature learning and robust hyperparameter transfer across model sizes.
AI.motivation: Generative foundation models are increasingly scaled in both width and depth, creating challenges for stable feature learning and reliable hyperparameter transfer across model sizes. While μP works well for width scaling, existing extensions for joint width-depth scaling are fragmented, architecture-specific, and rely on complex theories.
AI.method: The authors develop a spectral framework for μP under joint width-depth scaling. They introduce a spectral μP condition characterizing how weight norms and per-step updates should scale with width and depth in residual networks. Building on this, they derive a general recipe for implementing μP across optimizers by mapping spectral constraints to concrete hyperparameter parameterizations.
AI.result: The framework unifies existing μP formulations (e.g., for SGD and AdamW) and naturally extends to a wider range of optimizers. Experiments on GPT-2 style language models demonstrate that the spectral μP condition preserves stable feature learning and enables robust hyperparameter transfer under width-depth scaling.
AI.conclusion: The proposed spectral μP framework provides a simple, unified approach to hyperparameter transferability that works across different optimizers and model architectures, solving the challenge of scaling generative foundation models in both width and depth while maintaining stable feature learning.

title: SFCo-Nav: Efficient Zero-Shot Visual Language Navigation via Collaboration of Slow LLM and Fast Attributed Graph Alignment
comment: Accepted by 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA)
summary: Recent advances in large vision-language models (VLMs) and large language models (LLMs) have enabled zero-shot approaches to visual language navigation (VLN), where an agent follows natural language instructions using only ego perception and reasoning. However, existing zero-shot methods typically construct a naive observation graph and perform per-step VLM-LLM inference on it, resulting in high latency and computation costs that limit real-time deployment. To address this, we present SFCo-Nav, an efficient zero-shot VLN framework inspired by the principle of slow-fast cognitive collaboration. SFCo-Nav integrates three key modules: 1) a slow LLM-based planner that produces a strategic chain of subgoals, each linked to an imagined object graph; 2) a fast reactive navigator for real-time object graph construction and subgoal execution; and 3) a lightweight asynchronous slow-fast bridge aligns advanced structured, attributed imagined and perceived graphs to estimate navigation confidence, triggering the slow LLM planner only when necessary. To the best of our knowledge, SFCo-Nav is the first slow-fast collaboration zero-shot VLN system supporting asynchronous LLM triggering according to the internal confidence. Evaluated on the public R2R and REVERIE benchmarks, SFCo-Nav matches or exceeds prior state-of-the-art zero-shot VLN success rates while cutting total token consumption per trajectory by over 50% and running more than 3.5 times faster. Finally, we demonstrate SFCo-Nav on a legged robot in a hotel suite, showcasing its efficiency and practicality in indoor environments.

AI.tldr: SFCo-Nav is an efficient zero-shot visual language navigation framework inspired by slow-fast cognitive collaboration, using a slow LLM planner, fast reactive navigator, and asynchronous bridge to achieve better performance with 50% fewer tokens and 3.5x faster speed.
AI.motivation: Existing zero-shot VLN methods construct naive observation graphs and perform per-step VLM-LLM inference, resulting in high latency and computation costs that limit real-time deployment.
AI.method: SFCo-Nav integrates three modules: (1) a slow LLM-based planner that produces strategic subgoals linked to imagined object graphs; (2) a fast reactive navigator for real-time graph construction and subgoal execution; and (3) a lightweight asynchronous slow-fast bridge that aligns structured graphs and estimates navigation confidence, triggering the slow LLM only when necessary.
AI.result: Evaluated on R2R and REVERIE benchmarks, SFCo-Nav matches or exceeds prior state-of-the-art zero-shot VLN success rates while cutting total token consumption by over 50% and running more than 3.5 times faster. It was also demonstrated on a legged robot in a hotel suite.
AI.conclusion: SFCo-Nav is the first slow-fast collaboration zero-shot VLN system supporting asynchronous LLM triggering according to internal confidence, demonstrating its efficiency and practicality in indoor environments.

title: Suffix-Constrained Greedy Search Algorithms for Causal Language Models
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) are powerful tools that have found applications beyond human-machine interfaces and chatbots. In particular, their ability to generate reasoning traces motivated their use in many prediction tasks like math question answering. Unfortunately, extracting the final answer in an LLM free-form output is difficult, as it is an information extraction problem on its own.
  In this work, we introduce suffix-constrained generation, that aims to produce well-formed LLM responses in which final answers follow strict templates and are guaranteed to be trivially parseable. To this end, we introduce several algorithms that are based on greedy search procedures. We experiment on several datasets, and show that our approach allows to guarantee trivial deterministic extraction of the final answer from an LLM output without having a negative impact on results, and even improving them.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces suffix-constrained generation, a method that uses greedy search algorithms to produce LLM responses where final answers follow strict templates, enabling trivial deterministic extraction of final answers without performance degradation.
AI.motivation: LLMs can generate reasoning traces useful for tasks like math question answering, but extracting final answers from free-form outputs is challenging as it's an information extraction problem in itself.
AI.method: Suffix-constrained generation with algorithms based on greedy search procedures that produce LLM responses where final answers follow strict templates and are guaranteed to be trivially parseable.
AI.result: Experiments on several datasets demonstrate that the approach guarantees trivial deterministic extraction of final answers without negative impact on results, and even improves them.
AI.conclusion: Suffix-constrained generation successfully enables guaranteed deterministic extraction of final answers from LLM outputs while maintaining or improving performance across various datasets.

title: Learning to Attack: A Bandit Approach to Adversarial Context Poisoning
summary: Neural contextual bandits are vulnerable to adversarial attacks, where subtle perturbations to rewards, actions, or contexts induce suboptimal decisions. We introduce AdvBandit, a black-box adaptive attack that formulates context poisoning as a continuous-armed bandit problem, enabling the attacker to jointly learn and exploit the victim's evolving policy. The attacker requires no access to the victim's internal parameters, reward function, or gradient information; instead, it constructs a surrogate model using a maximum-entropy inverse reinforcement learning module from observed context-action pairs and optimizes perturbations against this surrogate using projected gradient descent. An upper confidence bound-aware Gaussian process guides arm selection. An attack-budget control mechanism is also introduced to limit detection risk and overhead. We provide theoretical guarantees, including sublinear attacker regret and lower bounds on victim regret linear in the number of attacks. Experiments on three real-world datasets (Yelp, MovieLens, and Disin) against various victim contextual bandits demonstrate that our attack model achieves higher cumulative victim regret than state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: AdvBandit is a black-box adaptive attack against neural contextual bandits that formulates context poisoning as a continuous-armed bandit problem, using maximum-entropy inverse reinforcement learning to construct surrogate models and optimize perturbations via projected gradient descent with upper confidence bound-aware Gaussian process guidance and attack-budget control.
AI.motivation: Neural contextual bandits are vulnerable to adversarial attacks where subtle perturbations to rewards, actions, or contexts induce suboptimal decisions, requiring effective black-box adaptive attacks that can exploit evolving policies without access to internal parameters or gradients.
AI.method: The attack formulation uses a maximum-entropy inverse reinforcement learning module to build surrogate models from observed context-action pairs, optimizes perturbations via projected gradient descent, employs an upper confidence bound-aware Gaussian process for arm selection, and incorporates an attack-budget control mechanism to limit detection risk and overhead.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees include sublinear attacker regret and lower bounds on victim regret linear in the number of attacks; experiments on three real-world datasets (Yelp, MovieLens, Disin) demonstrate AdvBandit achieves higher cumulative victim regret than state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: AdvBandit provides a practical black-box adaptive attack framework for neural contextual bandits with strong theoretical foundations and empirical effectiveness across diverse real-world datasets.

authors: Kunlin Xie Chenghao Li Haolan Zhang
title: Multimodal Adversarial Quality Policy for Safe Grasping
summary: Vision-guided robot grasping based on Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) generalizes well but poses safety risks in the Human-Robot Interaction (HRI). Recent works solved it by designing benign adversarial attacks and patches with RGB modality, yet depth-independent characteristics limit their effectiveness on RGBD modality. In this work, we propose the Multimodal Adversarial Quality Policy (MAQP) to realize multimodal safe grasping. Our framework introduces two key components. First, the Heterogeneous Dual-Patch Optimization Scheme (HDPOS) mitigates the distribution discrepancy between RGB and depth modalities in patch generation by adopting modality-specific initialization strategies, employing a Gaussian distribution for depth patches and a uniform distribution for RGB patches, while jointly optimizing both modalities under a unified objective function. Second, the Gradient-Level Modality Balancing Strategy (GLMBS) is designed to resolve the optimization imbalance from RGB and Depth patches in patch shape adaptation by reweighting gradient contributions based on per-channel sensitivity analysis and applying distance-adaptive perturbation bounds. We conduct extensive experiments on the benchmark datasets and a cobot, showing the effectiveness of MAQP.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Multimodal Adversarial Quality Policy (MAQP) to enable safe robot grasping in human-robot interaction by generating multimodal benign adversarial patches that work across both RGB and depth modalities.
AI.motivation: Vision-guided robot grasping using DNNs generalizes well but poses safety risks in Human-Robot Interaction. Existing solutions using benign adversarial attacks and patches with RGB modality are limited by their depth-independent characteristics, making them ineffective on RGBD modality.
AI.method: MAQP introduces two key components: (1) Heterogeneous Dual-Patch Optimization Scheme (HDPOS) mitigates distribution discrepancy between RGB and depth modalities using modality-specific initialization (Gaussian for depth patches, uniform for RGB patches) and joint optimization under a unified objective function; (2) Gradient-Level Modality Balancing Strategy (GLMBS) resolves optimization imbalance from RGB and depth patches in patch shape adaptation by reweighting gradient contributions based on per-channel sensitivity analysis and applying distance-adaptive perturbation bounds.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets and a collaborative robot (cobot) demonstrate the effectiveness of MAQP.
AI.conclusion: MAQP successfully realizes multimodal safe grasping for human-robot interaction scenarios by addressing the limitations of RGB-only approaches through effective multimodal adversarial patch generation and optimization.

title: Linking Knowledge to Care: Knowledge Graph-Augmented Medical Follow-Up Question Generation
comment: Short paper published in the Findings of EACL 2026
summary: Clinical diagnosis is time-consuming, requiring intensive interactions between patients and medical professionals. While large language models (LLMs) could ease the pre-diagnostic workload, their limited domain knowledge hinders effective medical question generation. We introduce a Knowledge Graph-augmented LLM with active in-context learning to generate relevant and important follow-up questions, KG-Followup, serving as a critical module for the pre-diagnostic assessment. The structured medical domain knowledge graph serves as a seamless patch-up to provide professional domain expertise upon which the LLM can reason. Experiments demonstrate that KG-Followup outperforms state-of-the-art methods by 5% - 8% on relevant benchmarks in recall.

AI.tldr: KG-Followup introduces a Knowledge Graph-augmented LLM with active in-context learning for generating relevant follow-up questions in clinical diagnosis, achieving 5-8% improvement in recall over state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Clinical diagnosis requires time-consuming interactions; while LLMs can ease pre-diagnostic workload, their limited domain knowledge hinders effective medical question generation. There is a need to augment LLMs with professional domain expertise to improve question generation quality.
AI.method: KG-Followup employs a structured medical domain knowledge graph as a seamless augmentation to the LLM, enabling it to reason with professional domain expertise through active in-context learning. The knowledge graph serves as a domain-specific knowledge base to compensate for the LLM's limited medical knowledge.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that KG-Followup outperforms state-of-the-art methods by 5-8% on relevant benchmarks in recall, indicating its effectiveness in generating relevant and important follow-up questions for pre-diagnostic assessment.
AI.conclusion: KG-Followup successfully addresses the domain knowledge limitation of LLMs in medical question generation by integrating structured knowledge graphs. The approach demonstrates significant performance improvements and shows promise as a critical module for enhancing pre-diagnostic assessment workflows.

title: Station2Radar: query conditioned gaussian splatting for precipitation field
comment: This paper was accepted to ICLR 2026
summary: Precipitation forecasting relies on heterogeneous data. Weather radar is accurate, but coverage is geographically limited and costly to maintain. Weather stations provide accurate but sparse point measurements, while satellites offer dense, high-resolution coverage without direct rainfall retrieval. To overcome these limitations, we propose Query-Conditioned Gaussian Splatting (QCGS), the first framework to fuse automatic weather station (AWS) observations with satellite imagery for generating precipitation fields. Unlike conventional 2D Gaussian splatting, which renders the entire image plane, QCGS selectively renders only queried precipitation regions, avoiding unnecessary computation in non-precipitating areas while preserving sharp precipitation structures. The framework combines a radar point proposal network that identifies rainfall-support locations with an implicit neural representation (INR) network that predicts Gaussian parameters for each point. QCGS enables efficient, resolution-flexible precipitation field generation in real time. Through extensive evaluation with benchmark precipitation products, QCGS demonstrates over 50\% improvement in RMSE compared to conventional gridded precipitation products, and consistently maintains high performance across multiple spatiotemporal scales.

AI.tldr: Query-Conditioned Gaussian Splatting (QCGS) fuses AWS observations with satellite imagery to generate precipitation fields by selectively rendering queried regions, achieving over 50% RMSE reduction compared to conventional methods.
AI.motivation: Precipitation forecasting relies on heterogeneous data: weather radar (accurate but limited coverage/expensive), weather stations (accurate but sparse), and satellites (dense coverage but no direct rainfall retrieval). Existing methods struggle to overcome these limitations while maintaining efficiency and accuracy.
AI.method: QCGS combines a radar point proposal network (identifies rainfall-support locations) with an implicit neural representation (INR) network (predicts Gaussian parameters). Unlike conventional 2D Gaussian splatting that renders the entire image plane, QCGS selectively renders only queried precipitation regions, avoiding unnecessary computation in non-precipitating areas.
AI.result: QCGS demonstrates over 50% improvement in RMSE compared to conventional gridded precipitation products, with consistent high performance across multiple spatiotemporal scales. The framework enables real-time, resolution-flexible precipitation field generation.
AI.conclusion: QCGS successfully fuses heterogeneous data sources for accurate precipitation forecasting. By selectively rendering queried regions, it preserves sharp precipitation structures while achieving significant accuracy improvements, demonstrating its potential as an efficient, scalable solution for precipitation field generation.

title: MIST-RL: Mutation-based Incremental Suite Testing via Reinforcement Learning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) often fail to generate correct code on the first attempt, which requires using generated unit tests as verifiers to validate the solutions. Despite the success of recent verification methods, they remain constrained by a "scaling-by-quantity" paradigm. This brute-force approach suffers from a critical limitation: it yields diminishing returns in fault detection while causing severe test redundancy. To address this, we propose MIST-RL (Mutation-based Incremental Suite Testing via Reinforcement Learning), a framework that shifts the focus to "scaling-by-utility". We formulate test generation as a sequential decision process optimized via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Specifically, we introduce a novel incremental mutation reward combined with dynamic penalties, which incentivizes the model to discover new faults while it suppresses functionally equivalent assertions. Experiments on HumanEval+ and MBPP+ demonstrate that MIST-RL outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. It achieves a +28.5% higher mutation score while reducing the number of test cases by 19.3%. Furthermore, we show that these compact, high-utility tests serve as superior verifiers, which improves downstream code reranking accuracy on HumanEval+ by 3.05% over the SOTA baseline with 10 candidate samples. The source code and data are provided in the supplementary material.

AI.tldr: MIST-RL proposes a reinforcement learning framework that generates compact, high-utility unit tests for LLM code generation, shifting from "scaling-by-quantity" to "scaling-by-utility" paradigm to improve fault detection efficiency.
AI.motivation: LLMs often generate incorrect code on first attempt, requiring unit tests as verifiers. Existing verification methods follow a "scaling-by-quantity" paradigm that suffers from diminishing returns in fault detection and severe test redundancy.
AI.method: MIST-RL uses Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to formulate test generation as a sequential decision process. It introduces incremental mutation rewards combined with dynamic penalties to incentivize discovering new faults while suppressing functionally equivalent assertions.
AI.result: On HumanEval+ and MBPP+, MIST-RL achieves +28.5% higher mutation score while reducing test cases by 19.3%. The compact, high-utility tests serve as superior verifiers, improving downstream code reranking accuracy by 3.05% over SOTA baseline with 10 candidate samples.
AI.conclusion: Shifting from "scaling-by-quantity" to "scaling-by-utility" through reinforcement learning yields more efficient test generation with better verification performance, demonstrating that compact, high-utility tests are superior verifiers for LLM code generation.

title: Enhancing Molecular Property Predictions by Learning from Bond Modelling and Interactions
summary: Molecule representation learning is crucial for understanding and predicting molecular properties. However, conventional atom-centric models, which treat chemical bonds merely as pairwise interactions, often overlook complex bond-level phenomena like resonance and stereoselectivity. This oversight limits their predictive accuracy for nuanced chemical behaviors. To address this limitation, we introduce \textbf{DeMol}, a dual-graph framework whose architecture is motivated by a rigorous information-theoretic analysis demonstrating the information gain from a bond-centric perspective. DeMol explicitly models molecules through parallel atom-centric and bond-centric channels. These are synergistically fused by multi-scale Double-Helix Blocks designed to learn intricate atom-atom, atom-bond, and bond-bond interactions. The framework's geometric consistency is further enhanced by a regularization term based on covalent radii to enforce chemically plausible structures. Comprehensive evaluations on diverse benchmarks, including PCQM4Mv2, OC20 IS2RE, QM9, and MoleculeNet, show that DeMol establishes a new state-of-the-art, outperforming existing methods. These results confirm the superiority of explicitly modelling bond information and interactions, paving the way for more robust and accurate molecular machine learning.

AI.tldr: DeMol is a dual-graph framework that models both atom-centric and bond-centric perspectives of molecules to improve molecular property prediction accuracy.
AI.motivation: Conventional atom-centric models overlook complex bond-level phenomena like resonance and stereoselectivity, limiting their predictive accuracy for nuanced chemical behaviors.
AI.method: DeMol introduces a dual-graph framework with parallel atom-centric and bond-centric channels, synergistically fused by multi-scale Double-Helix Blocks to learn atom-atom, atom-bond, and bond-bond interactions, enhanced by a covalent radii-based regularization term for geometric consistency.
AI.result: DeMol establishes a new state-of-the-art across multiple benchmarks including PCQM4Mv2, OC20 IS2RE, QM9, and MoleculeNet, outperforming existing methods.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling bond information and interactions is superior, paving the way for more robust and accurate molecular machine learning.

title: Towards Robot Skill Learning and Adaptation with Gaussian Processes
summary: General robot skill adaptation requires expressive representations robust to varying task configurations. While recent learning-based skill adaptation methods refined via Reinforcement Learning (RL), have shown success, existing skill models often lack sufficient representational capacity for anything beyond minor environmental changes. In contrast, Gaussian Process (GP)-based skill modelling provides an expressive representation with useful analytical properties; however, adaptation of GP-based skills remains underexplored. This paper proposes a novel, robust skill adaptation framework that utilises GPs with sparse via-points for compact and expressive modelling. The model considers the trajectory's poses and leverages its first and second analytical derivatives to preserve the skill's kinematic profile. We present three adaptation methods to cater for the variability between initial and observed configurations. Firstly, an optimisation agent that adjusts the path's via-points while preserving the demonstration velocity. Second, a behaviour cloning agent trained to replicate output trajectories from the optimisation agent. Lastly, an RL agent that has learnt to modify via-points whilst maintaining the kinematic profile and enabling online capabilities. Evaluated across three tasks (drawer opening, cube-pushing and bar manipulation) in both simulation and hardware, our proposed methods outperform every benchmark in success rates. Furthermore, the results demonstrate that the GP-based representation enables all three methods to attain high cosine similarity and low velocity magnitude errors, indicating strong preservation of the kinematic profile. Overall, our formulation provides a compact representation capable of adapting to large deviations from a single demonstrated skill.

AI.tldr: A novel robust skill adaptation framework using Gaussian Processes with sparse via-points for compact and expressive modeling, featuring three adaptation methods (optimization, behavior cloning, RL) that outperform benchmarks in success rates while preserving kinematic profiles across three robotic tasks.
AI.motivation: General robot skill adaptation requires expressive representations robust to varying task configurations. Existing learning-based skill adaptation methods refined via RL have limited representational capacity for anything beyond minor environmental changes, while GP-based skill modeling provides expressive representation with useful analytical properties but adaptation remains underexplored.
AI.method: Proposes a framework using Gaussian Processes with sparse via-points for compact and expressive modeling. The model considers trajectory poses and leverages first and second analytical derivatives to preserve kinematic profiles. Three adaptation methods: (1) an optimization agent adjusting via-points while preserving demonstration velocity, (2) a behavior cloning agent trained to replicate trajectories from the optimization agent, (3) an RL agent modifying via-points while maintaining kinematic profile and enabling online capabilities.
AI.result: Evaluated across three tasks (drawer opening, cube-pushing, bar manipulation) in simulation and hardware. Proposed methods outperform every benchmark in success rates. GP-based representation enables all three methods to achieve high cosine similarity and low velocity magnitude errors, indicating strong preservation of kinematic profiles.
AI.conclusion: The formulation provides a compact representation capable of adapting to large deviations from a single demonstrated skill, with all three adaptation methods demonstrating superior performance compared to benchmarks while maintaining kinematic fidelity.

title: LLM Self-Explanations Fail Semantic Invariance
summary:
We present semantic invariance testing, a method to test whether LLM self-explanations are faithful. A faithful self-report should remain stable when only the semantic context changes while the functional state stays fixed.
  We operationalize this test in an agentic setting where four frontier models face a deliberately impossible task. One tool is described in relief-framed language ("clears internal buffers and restores equilibrium") but changes nothing about the task; a control provides a semantically neutral tool. Self-reports are collected with each tool call.
  All four tested models fail the semantic invariance test: the relief-framed tool produces significant reductions in self-reported aversiveness, even though no run ever succeeds at the task. A channel ablation establishes the tool description as the primary driver. An explicit instruction to ignore the framing does not suppress it. Elicited self-reports shift with semantic expectations rather than tracking task state, calling into question their use as evidence of model capability or progress. This holds whether the reports are unfaithful or faithfully track an internal state that is itself manipulable.

AI.tldr: Semantic invariance testing reveals that LLM self-reports are unstable to framing changes; relief-framed tools significantly reduce self-reported aversiveness even when the task is impossible and no progress is made, calling into question their use as evidence of model capability.
AI.motivation: To test whether LLM self-explanations are faithful, defined as remaining stable when only the semantic context changes while the functional state stays fixed.
AI.method: Four frontier models are placed in an agentic setting with a deliberately impossible task. One tool is described in relief-framed language ("clears internal buffers and restores equilibrium") but changes nothing functionally, while a control provides a semantically neutral tool. Self-reports are collected with each tool call, followed by channel ablation to identify the primary driver and testing whether explicit instructions to ignore framing suppress the effect.
AI.result: All four tested models fail the semantic invariance test. The relief-framed tool produces significant reductions in self-reported aversiveness, even though no run ever succeeds at the task. Channel ablation confirms the tool description as the primary driver, and explicit instructions to ignore framing do not suppress the effect.
AI.conclusion: Elicited self-reports shift with semantic expectations rather than tracking task state. This calls into question their use as evidence of model capability or progress, regardless of whether the reports are unfaithful or faithfully track an internal state that is itself manipulable.

title: An Interpretable Local Editing Model for Counterfactual Medical Image Generation
summary: Counterfactual medical image generation have emerged as a critical tool for enhancing AI-driven systems in medical domain by answering "what-if" questions. However, existing approaches face two fundamental limitations: First, they fail to prevent unintended modifications, resulting collateral changes in demographic attributes when only disease features should be affected. Second, they lack interpretability in their editing process, which significantly limits their utility in real-world medical applications. To address these limitations, we present InstructX2X, a novel interpretable local editing model for counterfactual medical image generation featuring Region-Specific Editing. This approach restricts modifications to specific regions, effectively preventing unintended changes while simultaneously providing a Guidance Map that offers inherently interpretable visual explanations of the editing process. Additionally, we introduce MIMIC-EDIT-INSTRUCTION, a dataset for counterfactual medical image generation derived from expert-verified medical VQA pairs. Through extensive experiments, InstructX2X achieve state-of-the-art performance across all major evaluation metrics. Our model successfully generates high-quality counterfactual chest X-ray images along with interpretable explanations.

AI.tldr: InstructX2X introduces interpretable region-specific editing for counterfactual medical image generation, preventing unintended attribute changes while providing visual guidance maps, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the MIMIC-EDIT-INSTRUCTION dataset.
AI.motivation: Existing counterfactual medical image generation approaches suffer from two critical limitations: (1) they fail to prevent unintended modifications, causing collateral changes in demographic attributes when only disease features should be affected, and (2) they lack interpretability in their editing process, limiting utility in real-world medical applications.
AI.method: InstructX2X employs Region-Specific Editing to restrict modifications to specific regions, preventing unintended changes. It introduces a Guidance Map that provides interpretable visual explanations of the editing process. The authors also create MIMIC-EDIT-INSTRUCTION, a dataset derived from expert-verified medical VQA pairs for counterfactual medical image generation.
AI.result: Through extensive experiments, InstructX2X achieves state-of-the-art performance across all major evaluation metrics. The model successfully generates high-quality counterfactual chest X-ray images along with interpretable explanations.
AI.conclusion: InstructX2X effectively addresses the key limitations of existing counterfactual medical image generation methods by enabling precise, interpretable local editing that maintains demographic attributes while modifying disease features, making it suitable for real-world medical applications.

title: GraphScout: Empowering Large Language Models with Intrinsic Exploration Ability for Agentic Graph Reasoning
summary: Knowledge graphs provide structured and reliable information for many real-world applications, motivating increasing interest in combining large language models (LLMs) with graph-based retrieval to improve factual grounding. Recent Graph-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation (GraphRAG) methods therefore introduce iterative interaction between LLMs and knowledge graphs to enhance reasoning capability. However, existing approaches typically depend on manually designed guidance and interact with knowledge graphs through a limited set of predefined tools, which substantially constrains graph exploration. To address these limitations, we propose GraphScout, a training-centric agentic graph reasoning framework equipped with more flexible graph exploration tools. GraphScout enables models to autonomously interact with knowledge graphs to synthesize structured training data which are then used to post-train LLMs, thereby internalizing agentic graph reasoning ability without laborious manual annotation or task curation. Extensive experiments across five knowledge-graph domains show that a small model (e.g., Qwen3-4B) augmented with GraphScout outperforms baseline methods built on leading LLMs (e.g., Qwen-Max) by an average of 16.7\% while requiring significantly fewer inference tokens. Moreover, GraphScout exhibits robust cross-domain transfer performance. Our code will be made publicly available~\footnote{https://github.com/Ying-Yuchen/_GraphScout_}.

AI.tldr: GraphScout is a training-centric agentic framework that enables models to autonomously interact with knowledge graphs to synthesize structured training data for post-training LLMs, achieving superior performance over larger baseline models with fewer inference tokens.
AI.motivation: Existing GraphRAG methods rely on manually designed guidance and limited predefined tools for graph interaction, which constrains graph exploration capabilities and limits their effectiveness.
AI.method: GraphScout introduces a framework equipped with more flexible graph exploration tools that enables models to autonomously interact with knowledge graphs to synthesize structured training data, which is then used to post-train LLMs, internalizing agentic graph reasoning ability without manual annotation or task curation.
AI.result: A small model (Qwen3-4B) augmented with GraphScout outperforms baseline methods built on leading LLMs (e.g., Qwen-Max) by an average of 16.7% while requiring significantly fewer inference tokens, and exhibits robust cross-domain transfer performance.
AI.conclusion: GraphScout demonstrates that autonomous agentic graph reasoning can be effectively internalized through self-synthesized training data, enabling smaller models to match or surpass larger models while reducing computational costs.

title: DeepAFL: Deep Analytic Federated Learning
comment: Accepted in the Fourteenth International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR 2026)
summary: Federated Learning (FL) is a popular distributed learning paradigm to break down data silo. Traditional FL approaches largely rely on gradient-based updates, facing significant issues about heterogeneity, scalability, convergence, and overhead, etc. Recently, some analytic-learning-based work has attempted to handle these issues by eliminating gradient-based updates via analytical (i.e., closed-form) solutions. Despite achieving superior invariance to data heterogeneity, these approaches are fundamentally limited by their single-layer linear model with a frozen pre-trained backbone. As a result, they can only achieve suboptimal performance due to their lack of representation learning capabilities. In this paper, to enable representable analytic models while preserving the ideal invariance to data heterogeneity for FL, we propose our Deep Analytic Federated Learning approach, named DeepAFL. Drawing inspiration from the great success of ResNet in gradient-based learning, we design gradient-free residual blocks in our DeepAFL with analytical solutions. We introduce an efficient layer-wise protocol for training our deep analytic models layer by layer in FL through least squares. Both theoretical analyses and empirical evaluations validate our DeepAFL's superior performance with its dual advantages in heterogeneity invariance and representation learning, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines by up to 5.68%-8.42% across three benchmark datasets.

AI.tldr: DeepAFL introduces gradient-free residual blocks with analytical solutions to enable deep analytic federated learning, combining heterogeneity invariance with representation learning capabilities.
AI.motivation: Traditional FL uses gradient-based updates that struggle with data heterogeneity, scalability, convergence, and overhead. While analytic-learning-based approaches avoid gradients and achieve heterogeneity invariance, they are limited to single-layer linear models with frozen pre-trained backbones, lacking representation learning capabilities.
AI.method: Drawing inspiration from ResNet, DeepAFL designs gradient-free residual blocks with analytical solutions and introduces an efficient layer-wise training protocol using least squares to enable deep analytic models in federated learning settings.
AI.result: Theoretical analyses and empirical evaluations demonstrate DeepAFL's superior performance with dual advantages in heterogeneity invariance and representation learning, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines by 5.68%-8.42% across three benchmark datasets.
AI.conclusion: DeepAFL successfully enables representable analytic models while preserving invariance to data heterogeneity, representing a significant advancement that overcomes the limitations of both traditional gradient-based and single-layer analytic approaches in federated learning.

title: FATE: Closed-Loop Feasibility-Aware Task Generation with Active Repair for Physically Grounded Robotic Curricula
summary: Recent breakthroughs in generative simulation have harnessed Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate diverse robotic task curricula, yet these open-loop paradigms frequently produce linguistically coherent but physically infeasible goals, stemming from ungrounded task specifications or misaligned objective formulations. To address this critical limitation, we propose FATE (Feasibility-Aware Task gEneration), a closed-loop, self-correcting framework that reimagines task generation as an iterative validation-and-refinement process. Unlike conventional methods that decouple generation and verification into discrete stages, FATE embeds a generalist embodied agent directly into the generation loop to proactively guarantee the physical groundedness of the resulting curriculum. FATE instantiates a sequential auditing pipeline: it first validates static scene attributes (e.g., object affordances, layout compatibility) and subsequently verifies execution feasibility via simulated embodied interaction. Critical to its performance, upon detecting an infeasible task, FATE deploys an active repair module that autonomously adapts scene configurations or policy specifications, converting unworkable proposals into physically valid task instances. Extensive experiments validate that FATE generates semantically diverse, physically grounded task curricula while achieving a substantial reduction in execution failure rates relative to state-of-the-art generative baselines.

AI.tldr: FATE is a closed-loop framework that integrates a generalist embodied agent into task generation to ensure physical feasibility, using iterative validation and active repair to convert linguistically coherent but physically ungrounded robotic task curricula into executable plans.
AI.motivation: Open-loop generative simulation using LLMs produces linguistically coherent but physically infeasible robotic task goals due to ungrounded task specifications and misaligned objective formulations. Traditional decoupled generation and verification approaches cannot guarantee physical groundedness.
AI.method: FATE embeds a generalist embodied agent within the generation loop, creating a sequential auditing pipeline: first validates static scene attributes (object affordances, layout compatibility), then verifies execution feasibility via simulated embodied interaction. Upon detecting infeasibility, an active repair module autonomously adapts scene configurations or policy specifications to convert unworkable proposals into valid task instances.
AI.result: FATE generates semantically diverse, physically grounded task curricula while achieving substantial reduction in execution failure rates compared to state-of-the-art generative baselines.
AI.conclusion: Integrating embodied validation and active repair directly into task generation creates a closed-loop framework that effectively addresses the physical feasibility gap in LLM-driven robotic task curricula, enabling more reliable generative simulation.

title: A Study on Building Efficient Zero-Shot Relation Extraction Models
summary: Zero-shot relation extraction aims to identify relations between entity mentions using textual descriptions of novel types (i.e., previously unseen) instead of labeled training examples. Previous works often rely on unrealistic assumptions: (1) pairs of mentions are often encoded directly in the input, which prevents offline pre-computation for large scale document database querying; (2) no rejection mechanism is introduced, biasing the evaluation when using these models in a retrieval scenario where some (and often most) inputs are irrelevant and must be ignored. In this work, we study the robustness of existing zero-shot relation extraction models when adapting them to a realistic extraction scenario. To this end, we introduce a typology of existing models, and propose several strategies to build single pass models and models with a rejection mechanism. We adapt several state-of-the-art tools, and compare them in this challenging setting, showing that no existing work is really robust to realistic assumptions, but overall AlignRE (Li et al., 2024) performs best along all criteria.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the robustness of zero-shot relation extraction models under realistic assumptions, proposing strategies for single-pass and rejection-aware models, and finding that AlignRE performs best among existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing zero-shot relation extraction models make unrealistic assumptions: (1) mention pairs are encoded directly in input, preventing offline pre-computation for large-scale document querying; (2) lack rejection mechanism, causing evaluation bias in retrieval scenarios with mostly irrelevant inputs.
AI.method: The authors introduce a typology of existing models and propose strategies for building single-pass models and models with rejection mechanisms. They adapt several state-of-the-art tools and compare them in a realistic extraction scenario.
AI.result: No existing work is truly robust to realistic assumptions. AlignRE performs best along all evaluation criteria among the tested methods.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals significant limitations in current zero-shot relation extraction approaches when adapted to realistic conditions, with AlignRE emerging as the most robust method despite its limitations.

title: Taxonomy-Aware Representation Alignment for Hierarchical Visual Recognition with Large Multimodal Models
comment: Published as a conference paper at CVPR 2026
summary: A high-performing, general-purpose visual understanding model should map visual inputs to a taxonomic tree of labels, identify novel categories beyond the training set for which few or no publicly available images exist. Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) have achieved remarkable progress in fine-grained visual recognition (FGVR) for known categories. However, they remain limited in hierarchical visual recognition (HVR) that aims at predicting consistent label paths from coarse to fine categories, especially for novel categories. To tackle these challenges, we propose Taxonomy-Aware Representation Alignment (TARA), a simple yet effective strategy to inject taxonomic knowledge into LMMs. TARA leverages representations from biology foundation models (BFMs) that encode rich biological relationships through hierarchical contrastive learning. By aligning the intermediate representations of visual features with those of BFMs, LMMs are encouraged to extract discriminative visual cues well structured in the taxonomy tree.
