Additionally, we align the representations of the first answer token with the ground-truth label, flexibly bridging the gap between contextualized visual features and categories of varying granularity according to user intent. Experiments demonstrate that TARA consistently enhances LMMs' hierarchical consistency and leaf node accuracy, enabling reliable recognition of both known and novel categories within complex biological taxonomies. Code is available at https://github.com/PKU-ICST-MIPL/TARA_CVPR2026.

AI.tldr: TARA aligns visual features with biology foundation models' hierarchical representations and answer tokens with ground-truth labels to improve Large Multimodal Models' ability to recognize both known and novel categories in complex taxonomies.
AI.motivation: Large Multimodal Models excel at fine-grained visual recognition for known categories but struggle with hierarchical visual recognition (predicting consistent label paths from coarse to fine categories, especially for novel categories). A high-performing visual understanding model should map visual inputs to taxonomic trees and identify novel categories with few or no public images.
AI.method: TARA (Taxonomy-Aware Representation Alignment) injects taxonomic knowledge into LMMs through two key mechanisms: (1) Aligning intermediate visual feature representations with biology foundation models (BFMs) that encode rich hierarchical relationships via contrastive learning, encouraging LMMs to extract taxonomically-structured visual cues; (2) Aligning the first answer token representation with ground-truth labels to flexibly bridge the gap between contextualized visual features and categories of varying granularity.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate TARA consistently enhances LMMs' hierarchical consistency and leaf node accuracy, enabling reliable recognition of both known and novel categories within complex biological taxonomies.
AI.conclusion: TARA provides a simple yet effective strategy to improve hierarchical visual recognition capabilities in Large Multimodal Models, particularly for novel categories in complex taxonomies. The approach successfully aligns visual representations with taxonomic knowledge, demonstrating that LMMs can achieve reliable performance on hierarchical classification tasks beyond known categories.

title: Securing the Floor and Raising the Ceiling: A Merging-based Paradigm for Multi-modal Search Agents
summary: Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have motivated the development of multi-modal search agents that can actively invoke external search tools and integrate retrieved evidence through multi-step reasoning. While promising, existing approaches typically rely on large-scale supervised trajectories or expensive reinforcement learning (RL), leading to high training cost, instability, and a severe cold-start problem for standard VLMs. We propose a training-free paradigm to empower VLMs with autonomous search capabilities via cross-modal model merging. By fusing a text-based search agent with a base VLM, we show that multi-modal search capabilities can be effectively composed without any additional multi-modal training data. To mitigate parameter interference during cross-modal integration, we introduce Optimal Brain Merging (OBM), a saliency-aware merging algorithm that identifies task-critical parameters based on their impact on model loss using only a small set of calibration samples. Extensive experiments on search-intensive benchmarks (e.g., InfoSeek, MMSearch) reveal that: (1) Model merging secures a reasonable performance floor as a zero-shot agent, with OBM achieving superior search rates; (2) OBM significantly raises the performance ceiling as a warm-start strategy, achieving faster convergence and higher peak accuracy than standard VLM initialization.

AI.tldr: A training-free paradigm that fuses a text-based search agent with a base VLM via cross-modal model merging, introducing Optimal Brain Merging (OBM) to mitigate parameter interference, achieving strong zero-shot search performance and superior warm-start capabilities compared to traditional supervised or RL approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-modal search agents rely on large-scale supervised trajectories or expensive reinforcement learning, leading to high training costs, instability, and severe cold-start problems for standard VLMs.
AI.method: Cross-modal model merging fuses a text-based search agent with a base VLM without additional training data. To mitigate parameter interference, the authors introduce Optimal Brain Merging (OBM)—a saliency-aware merging algorithm that identifies task-critical parameters based on their impact on model loss using only a small calibration sample set.
AI.result: Experiments on search-intensive benchmarks (InfoSeek, MMSearch) demonstrate that model merging secures a reasonable performance floor as a zero-shot agent, with OBM achieving superior search rates. As a warm-start strategy, OBM significantly raises the performance ceiling, achieving faster convergence and higher peak accuracy than standard VLM initialization.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal model merging with OBM effectively enables VLMs to acquire autonomous search capabilities without additional multi-modal training, securing both performance floor (zero-shot) and performance ceiling (warm-start) advantages over traditional supervised or RL methods.

title: Unlearning Evaluation through Subset Statistical Independence
comment: 21 pages, 6 figures, to appear at ICLR 2026
summary: Evaluating machine unlearning remains challenging, as existing methods typically require retraining reference models or performing membership inference attacks, both of which rely on prior access to training configuration or supervision labels, making them impractical in realistic scenarios. Motivated by the fact that most unlearning algorithms remove a small, random subset of the training data, we propose a subset-level evaluation framework based on statistical independence. Specifically, we design a tailored use of the Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion to assess whether the model outputs on a given subset exhibit statistical dependence, without requiring model retraining or auxiliary classifiers. Our method provides a simple, standalone evaluation procedure that aligns with unlearning workflows. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach reliably distinguishes in-training from out-of-training subsets and clearly differentiates unlearning effectiveness, even when existing evaluations fall short.

AI.tldr: A subset-level evaluation framework for machine unlearning based on statistical independence using the Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion (HSIC), which distinguishes in-training from out-of-training subsets without retraining or auxiliary classifiers.
AI.motivation: Evaluating machine unlearning is challenging because existing methods require retraining reference models or membership inference attacks, which rely on prior access to training configuration or supervision labels—making them impractical in realistic scenarios. Additionally, most unlearning algorithms remove only small, random subsets of training data.
AI.method: A tailored use of the Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion (HSIC) to assess whether model outputs on a given subset exhibit statistical dependence, without requiring model retraining or auxiliary classifiers.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate the approach reliably distinguishes in-training from out-of-training subsets and clearly differentiates unlearning effectiveness, even when existing evaluations fall short.
AI.conclusion: The method provides a simple, standalone evaluation procedure that aligns with unlearning workflows and is more practical than existing approaches.

title: (hu)Man vs. Machine: In the Future of Motorsport, can Autonomous Vehicles Compete?
summary: Motorsport has historically driven technological innovation in the automotive industry. Autonomous racing provides a proving ground to push the limits of performance of autonomous vehicle (AV) systems. In principle, AVs could be at least as fast, if not faster, than humans. However, human driven racing provides broader audience appeal thus far, and is more strategically challenging. Both provide opportunities to push each other even further technologically, yet competitions remain separate. This paper evaluates whether the future of motorsport could encompass joint competition between humans and AVs. Analysis of the current state of the art, as well as recent competition outcomes, shows that while technical performance has reached comparable levels, there are substantial challenges in racecraft, strategy and safety that need to be overcome. Outstanding issues involved in mixed human-AI racing, ranging from an initial assessment of critical factors such as system-level latencies, to effective planning and risk guarantees are explored. The crucial non-technical aspect of audience engagement and appeal regarding the changing character of motorsport is addressed. In the wider context of motorsport and AVs, this work outlines a proposed agenda for future research to 'keep pushing the possible', in the true spirit of motorsport.

AI.tldr: This paper examines the feasibility of mixed human-autonomous vehicle racing competitions, analyzing current technical performance levels alongside racecraft, strategy, and safety challenges required to realize such competitions.
AI.motivation: Motorsport historically drives automotive innovation. While autonomous racing could push AV performance limits, human racing maintains broader audience appeal and strategic complexity. The authors seek to evaluate whether future motorsport could incorporate joint human-AV competitions to further advance both domains.
AI.method: The authors conduct an analysis of current state-of-the-art autonomous vehicle technology and recent competition outcomes, evaluating technical performance parity alongside non-technical considerations such as racecraft, strategy, safety, and audience engagement.
AI.result: Analysis shows that technical performance has reached comparable levels between humans and AVs. However, significant challenges remain in racecraft, strategic decision-making, and safety that must be overcome to enable mixed human-AV racing.
AI.conclusion: Mixed human-AI racing presents substantial technical and non-technical challenges including system-level latency, effective planning, risk guarantees, and audience engagement. The authors propose a research agenda to address these outstanding issues and advance the integration of humans and AVs in motorsport competitions.

title: Spectral Attention Steering for Prompt Highlighting
summary: Attention steering is an important technique for controlling model focus, enabling capabilities such as prompt highlighting, where the model prioritises user-specified text. However, existing attention steering methods require explicit storage of the full attention matrix, making them incompatible with memory-efficient implementations like FlashAttention. We introduce Spectral Editing Key Amplification (SEKA), a training-free steering method that tackles this by directly editing key embeddings before attention computation. SEKA uses spectral decomposition to steer key embeddings towards latent directions that amplify attention scores for certain tokens. We extend this to Adaptive SEKA (AdaSEKA), a query-adaptive variant that uses a training-free routing mechanism to dynamically combine multiple expert subspaces based on the prompt's semantic intent. Our experiments show both methods significantly outperform strong baselines on standard steering benchmarks while adding much lower latency and memory overhead, in compatibility with optimised attention.

AI.tldr: SEKA and AdaSEKA are training-free attention steering methods that edit key embeddings via spectral decomposition to control model focus, offering superior performance and efficiency compared to baseline approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing attention steering methods require explicit storage of full attention matrices, making them incompatible with memory-efficient implementations like FlashAttention, creating a need for methods that can steer attention without full matrix storage.
AI.method: SEKA uses spectral decomposition to edit key embeddings before attention computation, steering them toward latent directions that amplify attention scores for specific tokens. AdaSEKA extends this with a query-adaptive routing mechanism that dynamically combines multiple expert subspaces based on prompt semantic intent.
AI.result: Both SEKA and AdaSEKA significantly outperform strong baselines on standard steering benchmarks while adding much lower latency and memory overhead, maintaining compatibility with optimized attention implementations.
AI.conclusion: Spectral editing of key embeddings provides an effective, memory-efficient approach to attention steering that overcomes the limitations of existing matrix-storage-based methods, enabling better model focus control with minimal computational overhead.

title: TAP-SLF: Parameter-Efficient Adaptation of Vision Foundation Models for Multi-Task Ultrasound Image Analysis
comment: 4 pages, 2 figures, 4 tables; Submitted to ISBI FMC UIA 2026; Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/huiwanHW/Florence-2-adaptation
summary: Executing multiple tasks simultaneously in medical image analysis, including segmentation, classification, detection, and regression, often introduces significant challenges regarding model generalizability and the optimization of shared feature representations. While Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) provide powerful general representations, full fine-tuning on limited medical data is prone to overfitting and incurs high computational costs. Moreover, existing parameter-efficient fine-tuning approaches typically adopt task-agnostic adaptation protocols, overlooking both task-specific mechanisms and the varying sensitivity of model layers during fine-tuning. In this work, we propose Task-Aware Prompting and Selective Layer Fine-Tuning (TAP-SLF), a unified framework for multi-task ultrasound image analysis. TAP-SLF incorporates task-aware soft prompts to encode task-specific priors into the input token sequence and applies LoRA to selected specific top layers of the encoder. This strategy updates only a small fraction of the VFM parameters while keeping the pre-trained backbone frozen. By combining task-aware prompts with selective high-layer fine-tuning, TAP-SLF enables efficient VFM adaptation to diverse medical tasks within a shared backbone. Results on the FMC_UIA 2026 Challenge test set, where TAP-SLF wins fifth place, combined with evaluations on the officially released training dataset using an 8:2 train-test split, demonstrate that task-aware prompting and selective layer tuning are effective strategies for efficient VFM adaptation.

AI.tldr: TAP-SLF proposes task-aware soft prompts and selective layer fine-tuning (using LoRA on top encoder layers) to efficiently adapt Vision Foundation Models to multiple ultrasound medical tasks while keeping the pre-trained backbone frozen and updating only a small fraction of parameters.
AI.motivation: Multi-task medical image analysis faces challenges with model generalizability and shared feature representation optimization. Full fine-tuning on limited medical data causes overfitting and high computational costs. Existing parameter-efficient methods overlook task-specific mechanisms and the varying sensitivity of model layers during fine-tuning.
AI.method: TAP-SLF incorporates task-aware soft prompts to encode task-specific priors into the input token sequence and applies Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) to selected top layers of the encoder, keeping the pre-trained backbone frozen and updating only a small fraction of parameters.
AI.result: TAP-SLF achieves fifth place on the FMC_UIA 2026 Challenge test set. Evaluations on the training dataset with an 8:2 train-test split demonstrate the effectiveness of task-aware prompting and selective layer tuning for efficient VFM adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Task-aware prompting combined with selective high-layer fine-tuning is an effective strategy for efficiently adapting Vision Foundation Models to diverse medical tasks within a shared backbone.

title: SciDER: Scientific Data-centric End-to-end Researcher
summary: Automated scientific discovery with large language models is transforming the research lifecycle from ideation to experimentation, yet existing agents struggle to autonomously process raw data collected from scientific experiments. We introduce SciDER, a data-centric end-to-end system that automates the research lifecycle. Unlike traditional frameworks, our specialized agents collaboratively parse and analyze raw scientific data, generate hypotheses and experimental designs grounded in specific data characteristics, and write and execute corresponding code. Evaluation on three benchmarks shows SciDER excels in specialized data-driven scientific discovery and outperforms general-purpose agents and state-of-the-art models through its self-evolving memory and critic-led feedback loop. Distributed as a modular Python package, we also provide easy-to-use PyPI packages with a lightweight web interface to accelerate autonomous, data-driven research and aim to be accessible to all researchers and developers.

AI.tldr: SciDER is a data-centric end-to-end system that uses specialized agents to automate the research lifecycle, including parsing raw scientific data, generating hypotheses, designing experiments, and writing/executing code, outperforming general-purpose agents through self-evolving memory and critic-led feedback.
AI.motivation: Existing large language model-based scientific discovery agents struggle to autonomously process raw experimental data, and traditional frameworks cannot handle specialized data-driven scientific discovery effectively.
AI.method: SciDER introduces specialized agents that collaboratively parse and analyze raw scientific data, generate hypotheses and experimental designs grounded in specific data characteristics, and write and execute corresponding code. It features self-evolving memory and critic-led feedback loops.
AI.result: Evaluated on three benchmarks, SciDER excels in specialized data-driven scientific discovery and outperforms general-purpose agents and state-of-the-art models.
AI.conclusion: Distributed as a modular Python package with PyPI packages and a lightweight web interface, SciDER aims to make autonomous, data-driven research accessible to all researchers and developers.

title: Energy-Efficient Information Representation in MNIST Classification Using Biologically Inspired Learning
comment: 14 pages, accepted for publication in proceedings of the 10th BWHPC Symposium
summary: Efficient representation learning is essential for optimal information storage and classification. However, it is frequently overlooked in artificial neural networks (ANNs). This neglect results in networks that can become overparameterized by factors of up to 13, increasing redundancy and energy consumption. As the demand for large language models (LLMs) and their scale increase, these issues are further highlighted, raising significant ethical and environmental concerns. We analyze our previously developed biologically inspired learning rule using information-theoretic concepts, evaluating its efficiency on the MNIST classification task. The proposed rule, which emulates the brain's structural plasticity, naturally prevents overparameterization by optimizing synaptic usage and retaining only the essential number of synapses. Furthermore, it outperforms backpropagation (BP) in terms of efficiency and storage capacity. It also eliminates the need for pre-optimization of network architecture, enhances adaptability, and reflects the brain's ability to reserve 'space' for new memories. This approach advances scalable and energy-efficient AI and provides a promising framework for developing brain-inspired models that optimize resource allocation and adaptability.

AI.tldr: A biologically inspired learning rule that emulates brain structural plasticity to prevent overparameterization, outperforming backpropagation in efficiency and storage capacity on MNIST classification.
AI.motivation: Efficient representation learning is often overlooked in ANNs, leading to overparameterized networks (up to 13x redundancy) that consume excessive energy. This becomes particularly problematic for large language models, raising ethical and environmental concerns.
AI.method: Analyzing a previously developed biologically inspired learning rule that emulates the brain's structural plasticity, optimizing synaptic usage to retain only essential synapses using information-theoretic concepts.
AI.result: The rule naturally prevents overparameterization, outperforms backpropagation in efficiency and storage capacity, eliminates the need for pre-optimization of network architecture, enhances adaptability, and reflects the brain's memory reservation capability on MNIST.
AI.conclusion: This approach advances scalable and energy-efficient AI, providing a promising framework for brain-inspired models that optimize resource allocation and adaptability.

title: KERV: Kinematic-Rectified Speculative Decoding for Embodied VLA Models
comment: This paper has been accepted by DAC 2026
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models build a token-domain robot control paradigm, yet suffer from low speed. Speculative Decoding (SD) is an optimization strategy that can boost inference speed. Two key issues emerge when integrating VLA and SD: first, SD relies on re-inference to address token errors, which is computationally expensive; second, to mitigate token errors, the acceptance threshold in SD requires careful adjustment. Existing works fail to address the above two issues effectively. Meanwhile, as the bridge between AI and the physical world, existing embodied intelligence has overlooked the application of robotic kinematics. To address these issues, we innovatively combine token-domain VLA models with kinematic-domain prediction for SD, proposing a kinematic-rectified SD framework named KERV. We employ a kinematics-based Kalman Filter to predict actions and compensate for SD errors, avoiding costly re-inference. Moreover, we design a kinematics-based adjustment strategy to dynamically rectify the acceptance threshold, addressing the difficulty of threshold determination. Experimental results across diverse tasks and environments demonstrate that KERV achieves 27%~37% acceleration with nearly no Success Rate loss.

title: Efficient Extractive Summarization with MAMBA-Transformer Hybrids for Low-Resource Scenarios
summary: Extractive summarization of long documents is bottlenecked by quadratic complexity, often forcing truncation and limiting deployment in resource-constrained settings. We introduce the first Mamba-Transformer hybrid for extractive summarization, combining the semantic strength of pre-trained transformers with the linear-time processing of state space models. Leveraging Mamba's ability to process full documents without truncation, our approach preserves context while maintaining strong summarization quality. The architecture includes: (1) a transformer encoder for sentence-level semantics, (2) a Mamba state space model to capture inter-sentence dependencies efficiently, and (3) a linear classifier for sentence relevance prediction. Across news, argumentative, and scientific domains under low-resource conditions, our method achieves: (1) large gains over BERTSUM and MATCHSUM, including +0.23 ROUGE-1 on ArXiv and statistically significant improvements on all datasets (p < 0.001); (2) consistent advantages across domains, strongest on the longest documents; (3) robust performance with limited training data; and (4) 24-27% faster inference on news summarization (CNN/DailyMail). We introduce the first hybrid Transformer-state space architecture for summarization, showing significant ROUGE improvements in low-resource scenarios.

title: Self-Correction Inside the Model: Leveraging Layer Attention to Mitigate Hallucinations in Large Vision Language Models
summary: Although Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have made substantial progress, hallucination, where generated text is not grounded in the visual input, remains a challenge. As LVLMs become stronger, previously reported hallucination patterns, such as linguistic bias and overthinking phenomenon, become far less consistent, making the corresponding mitigation techniques substantially less effective. In this paper, we introduce an Internal self-Correction mechanism utilizing Layer Attention (ICLA) that operates directly on hidden states during generation. Each layer selectively retrieves information from all preceding layers through a diagonal cross-layer attention mechanism, enabling self-refinement without any external correction signals. With introducing and training only 0.2M and 0.1M additional parameters on LLaVA1.5-7B and Qwen2.5-VL-7B, \ours consistently improves visual grounding across multiple hallucination benchmarks, demonstrating its effectiveness for more advanced LVLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ICLA (Internal self-Correction mechanism utilizing Layer Attention), a parameter-efficient method that improves visual grounding in Large Vision-Language Models by using cross-layer attention to enable self-correction during generation without external correction signals.
AI.motivation: LVLMs still suffer from hallucination where generated text is not grounded in visual input. As LVLMs become stronger, previously reported hallucination patterns become less consistent, making existing mitigation techniques less effective for advanced models.
AI.method: ICLA operates directly on hidden states during generation, where each layer selectively retrieves information from all preceding layers through a diagonal cross-layer attention mechanism. This enables self-refinement without any external correction signals. The method is trained with only 0.2M and 0.1M additional parameters on LLaVA1.5-7B and Qwen2.5-VL-7B, respectively.
AI.result: ICLA consistently improves visual grounding across multiple hallucination benchmarks, demonstrating its effectiveness for more advanced LVLMs.
AI.conclusion: ICLA provides a parameter-efficient, self-contained solution that successfully mitigates hallucination in large vision-language models by enabling them to self-correct during generation.

title: Decoding Answers Before Chain-of-Thought: Evidence from Pre-CoT Probes and Activation Steering
summary: As chain-of-thought (CoT) has become central to scaling reasoning capabilities in large language models (LLMs), it has also emerged as a promising tool for interpretability, suggesting the opportunity to understand model decisions through verbalized reasoning. However, the utility of CoT toward interpretability depends upon its faithfulness -- whether the model's stated reasoning reflects the underlying decision process. We provide mechanistic evidence that instruction-tuned models often determine their answer before generating CoT. Training linear probes on residual stream activations at the last token before CoT, we can predict the model's final answer with 0.9 AUC on most tasks. We find that these directions are not only predictive, but also causal: steering activations along the probe direction flips model answers in over 50% of cases, significantly exceeding orthogonal baselines. When steering induces incorrect answers, we observe two distinct failure modes: non-entailment (stating correct premises but drawing unsupported conclusions) and confabulation (fabricating false premises). While post-hoc reasoning may be instrumentally useful when the model has a correct pre-CoT belief, these failure modes suggest it can result in undesirable behaviors when reasoning from a false belief.

AI.tldr: Instruction-tuned LLMs often determine their answer before generating CoT; linear probes on pre-CoT activations can predict final answers with 0.9 AUC, and steering these activations causally flips model answers in over 50% of cases, revealing non-entailment and confabulation failure modes.
AI.motivation: CoT has become central for scaling reasoning and interpretability in LLMs, but its utility depends on faithfulness—whether stated reasoning reflects the actual decision process.
AI.method: Training linear probes on residual stream activations at the last token before CoT generation, then steering activations along probe directions to test causal effects.
AI.result: Probes predict final answers with 0.9 AUC on most tasks; steering activations flips answers in over 50% of cases (significantly exceeding orthogonal baselines); two failure modes emerge: non-entailment and confabulation.
AI.conclusion: Post-hoc reasoning is instrumentally useful when the model has a correct pre-CoT belief, but can lead to undesirable behaviors when reasoning from a false belief, indicating CoT is not fully faithful to the decision process.

title: Learning to Explore: Policy-Guided Outlier Synthesis for Graph Out-of-Distribution Detection
summary: Detecting out-of-distribution (OOD) graphs is crucial for ensuring the safety and reliability of Graph Neural Networks. In unsupervised graph-level OOD detection, models are typically trained using only in-distribution (ID) data, resulting in incomplete feature space characterization and weak decision boundaries. Although synthesizing outliers offers a promising solution, existing approaches rely on fixed, non-adaptive sampling heuristics (e.g., distance- or density-based), limiting their ability to explore informative OOD regions. We propose a Policy-Guided Outlier Synthesis (PGOS) framework that replaces static heuristics with a learned exploration strategy. Specifically, PGOS trains a reinforcement learning agent to navigate low-density regions in a structured latent space and sample representations that most effectively refine the OOD decision boundary. These representations are then decoded into high-quality pseudo-OOD graphs to improve detector robustness. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PGOS achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple graph OOD and anomaly detection benchmarks.

AI.tldr: PGOS uses reinforcement learning to learn adaptive sampling strategies for generating high-quality pseudo-outlier graphs, achieving state-of-the-art performance in graph-level OOD detection.
AI.motivation: Graph Neural Networks need robust OOD detection for safety. Existing unsupervised methods suffer from incomplete feature space characterization and weak decision boundaries due to reliance on fixed sampling heuristics that cannot effectively explore informative OOD regions.
AI.method: PGOS replaces static heuristics with a learned exploration strategy. It trains a reinforcement learning agent to navigate low-density regions in a structured latent space, sampling representations that most effectively refine the OOD decision boundary. These representations are then decoded into high-quality pseudo-OOD graphs.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that PGOS achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple graph OOD and anomaly detection benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Policy-Guided Outlier Synthesis successfully addresses the limitations of fixed sampling heuristics by using adaptive RL-based exploration, significantly improving detector robustness and OOD detection performance.

title: Learning Thermal-Aware Locomotion Policies for an Electrically-Actuated Quadruped Robot
summary: Electrically-actuated quadrupedal robots possess high mobility on complex terrains, but their motors tend to accumulate heat under high-torque cyclic loads, potentially triggering overheat protection and limiting long-duration tasks. This work proposes a thermal-aware control method that incorporates motor temperatures into reinforcement learning locomotion policies and introduces thermal-constraint rewards to prevent temperature exceedance. Real-world experiments on the Unitree A1 demonstrate that, under a fixed 3 kg payload, the baseline policy triggers overheat protection and stops within approximately 7 minutes, whereas the proposed method can operate continuously for over 27 minutes without thermal interruptions while maintaining comparable command-tracking performance, thereby enhancing sustainable operational capability.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a thermal-aware control method for quadrupedal robots that incorporates motor temperatures into reinforcement learning policies and adds thermal-constraint rewards. Experiments on the Unitree A1 show that this method enables continuous operation for over 27 minutes (vs. ~7 minutes for baseline) under a 3 kg payload while maintaining comparable command-tracking performance, significantly enhancing sustainable operational capability.
AI.motivation: Electrically-actuated quadrupedal robots offer high mobility on complex terrains, but motors accumulate heat under high-torque cyclic loads, triggering overheat protection mechanisms that limit long-duration tasks.
AI.method: A thermal-aware control method that (1) incorporates motor temperatures into reinforcement learning locomotion policies and (2) introduces thermal-constraint rewards to prevent temperature exceedance.
AI.result: Real-world experiments on the Unitree A1 with a fixed 3 kg payload: baseline policy triggers overheat protection and stops within ~7 minutes, while the proposed method operates continuously for over 27 minutes without thermal interruptions while maintaining comparable command-tracking performance.
AI.conclusion: The thermal-aware control method significantly enhances sustainable operational capability by enabling prolonged continuous operation without thermal interruptions while preserving locomotion performance.

title: Individual Turing Test: A Case Study of LLM-based Simulation Using Longitudinal Personal Data
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable human-like capabilities, yet their ability to replicate a specific individual remains under-explored. This paper presents a case study to investigate LLM-based individual simulation with a volunteer-contributed archive of private messaging history spanning over ten years. Based on the messaging data, we propose the "Individual Turing Test" to evaluate whether acquaintances of the volunteer can correctly identify which response in a multi-candidate pool most plausibly comes from the volunteer. We investigate prevalent LLM-based individual simulation approaches including: fine-tuning, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), memory-based approach, and hybrid methods that integrate fine-tuning and RAG or memory. Empirical results show that current LLM-based simulation methods do not pass the Individual Turing Test, but they perform substantially better when the same test is conducted on strangers to the target individual. Additionally, while fine-tuning improves the simulation in daily chats representing the language style of the individual, retrieval-augmented and memory-based approaches demonstrate stronger performance on questions involving personal opinions and preferences. These findings reveal a fundamental trade-off between parametric and non-parametric approaches to individual simulation with LLMs when given a longitudinal context.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates LLM-based individual simulation using a 10+ year private messaging archive. Current methods (fine-tuning, RAG, memory-based, and hybrids) fail the Individual Turing Test when tested on acquaintances, though they perform better with strangers. Fine-tuning excels at replicating language style in daily chats, while RAG and memory-based approaches better capture personal opinions and preferences, revealing a trade-off between parametric and non-parametric simulation approaches.
AI.motivation: While LLMs have demonstrated impressive human-like capabilities, their ability to replicate specific individuals remains under-explored. Understanding LLM-based individual simulation is crucial for advancing personalization, privacy-preserving AI, and ethical considerations around identity replication.
AI.method: The study employs a volunteer-contributed private messaging archive spanning over ten years as the training corpus. It introduces the "Individual Turing Test" where acquaintances of the volunteer must identify which response in a multi-candidate pool most plausibly originates from the volunteer. Four prevalent LLM-based simulation approaches are evaluated: fine-tuning, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), memory-based approaches, and hybrid methods combining fine-tuning with RAG or memory.
AI.result: Current LLM-based simulation methods do not pass the Individual Turing Test when tested on acquaintances, though they perform substantially better with strangers. Fine-tuning improves simulation of language style in daily chats, while retrieval-augmented and memory-based approaches demonstrate stronger performance on questions involving personal opinions and preferences.
AI.conclusion: The findings reveal a fundamental trade-off between parametric (fine-tuning-based) and non-parametric (RAG, memory-based) approaches to individual simulation with LLMs when given longitudinal context. This trade-off suggests that neither approach alone fully captures the complexity of individual identity, indicating limitations in current methods for high-fidelity individual replication.

title: Mamba-CAD: State Space Model For 3D Computer-Aided Design Generative Modeling
summary: Computer-Aided Design (CAD) generative modeling has a strong and long-term application in the industry. Recently, the parametric CAD sequence as the design logic of an object has been widely mined by sequence models. However, the industrial CAD models, especially in component objects, are fine-grained and complex, requiring a longer parametric CAD sequence to define. To address the problem, we introduce Mamba-CAD, a self-supervised generative modeling for complex CAD models in the industry, which can model on a longer parametric CAD sequence. Specifically, we first design an encoder-decoder framework based on a Mamba architecture and pair it with a CAD reconstruction task for pre-training to model the latent representation of CAD models; and then we utilize the learned representation to guide a generative adversarial network to produce the fake representation of CAD models, which would be finally recovered into parametric CAD sequences via the decoder of MambaCAD. To train Mamba-CAD, we further create a new dataset consisting of 77,078 CAD models with longer parametric CAD sequences. Comprehensive experiments are conducted to demonstrate the effectiveness of our model under various evaluation metrics, especially in the generation length of valid parametric CAD sequences. The code and dataset can be achieved from https://github.com/Sunny-Hack/Code-for-Mamba-CAD-AAAI-2025-.

AI.tldr: Mamba-CAD introduces a self-supervised generative modeling framework for complex industrial CAD models that can handle long parametric CAD sequences using a Mamba-architecture encoder-decoder combined with GAN-based generation.
AI.motivation: Industrial CAD models, especially component objects, are fine-grained and complex, requiring longer parametric CAD sequences to define. Existing sequence models struggle to effectively model these long sequences for industrial CAD applications.
AI.method: Mamba-CAD employs a Mamba-architecture encoder-decoder framework pre-trained on a CAD reconstruction task to model latent representations. The learned representation then guides a generative adversarial network (GAN) to produce fake representations, which are recovered into parametric CAD sequences via the decoder. A new dataset of 77,078 CAD models with longer parametric sequences was created for training.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments demonstrate Mamba-CAD's effectiveness under various evaluation metrics, with particular strength in generating valid parametric CAD sequences of longer lengths compared to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: Mamba-CAD successfully addresses the challenge of modeling long parametric CAD sequences for complex industrial CAD models, providing a scalable solution for CAD generative modeling in industry applications.

title: Scaling Tasks, Not Samples: Mastering Humanoid Control through Multi-Task Model-Based Reinforcement Learning
summary: Developing generalist robots capable of mastering diverse skills remains a central challenge in embodied AI. While recent progress emphasizes scaling model parameters and offline datasets, such approaches are limited in robotics, where learning requires active interaction. We argue that effective online learning should scale the \emph{number of tasks}, rather than the number of samples per task. This regime reveals a structural advantage of model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL). Because physical dynamics are invariant across tasks, a shared world model can aggregate multi-task experience to learn robust, task-agnostic representations. In contrast, model-free methods suffer from gradient interference when tasks demand conflicting actions in similar states. Task diversity therefore acts as a regularizer for MBRL, improving dynamics learning and sample efficiency. We instantiate this idea with \textbf{EfficientZero-Multitask (EZ-M)}, a sample-efficient multi-task MBRL algorithm for online learning. Evaluated on \textbf{HumanoidBench}, a challenging whole-body control benchmark, EZ-M achieves state-of-the-art performance with significantly higher sample efficiency than strong baselines, without extreme parameter scaling. These results establish task scaling as a critical axis for scalable robotic learning. The project website is available \href{https://yewr.github.io/ez_m/}{here}.

AI.tldr: EZ-M introduces a sample-efficient multi-task model-based reinforcement learning algorithm that scales task diversity rather than model parameters, achieving state-of-the-art performance on HumanoidBench with superior sample efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches to developing generalist robots focus on scaling model parameters and offline datasets, which are limited in robotics where learning requires active interaction with the environment.
AI.method: The authors propose scaling the number of tasks (task diversity) rather than samples per task. They leverage the structural advantage of model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) where physical dynamics are invariant across tasks, enabling a shared world model to aggregate multi-task experience. This approach is instantiated as EZ-M, a sample-efficient multi-task MBRL algorithm.
AI.result: EZ-M achieves state-of-the-art performance on HumanoidBench with significantly higher sample efficiency than strong baselines, without requiring extreme parameter scaling.
AI.conclusion: Task scaling is established as a critical axis for scalable robotic learning, as task diversity acts as a regularizer for model-based methods, improving dynamics learning and sample efficiency.

title: Multi-Domain Riemannian Graph Gluing for Building Graph Foundation Models
summary: Multi-domain graph pre-training integrates knowledge from diverse domains to enhance performance in the target domains, which is crucial for building graph foundation models. Despite initial success, existing solutions often fall short of answering a fundamental question: how is knowledge integrated or transferred across domains? This theoretical limitation motivates us to rethink the consistency and transferability between model pre-training and domain adaptation. In this paper, we propose a fresh Riemannian geometry perspective, whose core idea is to merge any graph dataset into a unified, smooth Riemannian manifold, enabling a systematic understanding of knowledge integration and transfer. To achieve this, our key contribution is the theoretical establishment of neural manifold gluing, which first characterizes local geometry using an adaptive orthogonal frame and then "glues" the local pieces together into a coherent whole. Building on this theory, we present the GraphGlue framework, which supports batched pre-training with EMA prototyping and provides a transferability measure based on geometric consistence. Extensive experiments demonstrate its superior performance across diverse graph domains. Moreover, we empirically validated GraphGlue's geometric scaling law, showing that larger quantities of datasets improve model transferability by producing a smoother manifold. Codes are available at https://github.com/RiemannGraph/GraphGlue.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces GraphGlue, a multi-domain graph pre-training framework that uses Riemannian geometry to systematically understand and enable knowledge transfer across domains by "gluing" local graph manifolds into a unified, smooth structure.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-domain graph pre-training methods lack theoretical understanding of how knowledge is integrated or transferred between domains. The authors seek to rethink the consistency and transferability between model pre-training and domain adaptation.
AI.method: The authors establish a Riemannian geometry perspective on graph data, introducing neural manifold gluing which characterizes local geometry using adaptive orthogonal frames and "glues" local pieces into a coherent whole. Building on this, they propose the GraphGlue framework with batched pre-training, EMA prototyping, and a transferability measure based on geometric consistency.
AI.result: GraphGlue demonstrates superior performance across diverse graph domains. The authors empirically validate a geometric scaling law showing that larger datasets improve model transferability by producing smoother manifolds.
AI.conclusion: The Riemannian geometry perspective provides a systematic framework for understanding knowledge integration and transfer in multi-domain graph pre-training, with GraphGlue as a practical implementation that achieves state-of-the-art performance.

title: B$^2$F-Map: Crowd-sourced Mapping with Bayesian B-spline Fusion
summary: Crowd-sourced mapping offers a scalable alternative to creating maps using traditional survey vehicles. Yet, existing methods either rely on prior high-definition (HD) maps or neglect uncertainties in the map fusion. In this work, we present a complete pipeline for HD map generation using production vehicles equipped only with a monocular camera, consumer-grade GNSS, and IMU. Our approach includes on-cloud localization using lightweight standard-definition maps, on-vehicle mapping via an extended object trajectory (EOT) Poisson multi-Bernoulli (PMB) filter with Gibbs sampling, and on-cloud multi-drive optimization and Bayesian map fusion. We represent the lane lines using B-splines, where each B-spline is parameterized by a sequence of Gaussian distributed control points, and propose a novel Bayesian fusion framework for B-spline trajectories with differing density representation, enabling principled handling of uncertainties. We evaluate our proposed approach, B$^2$F-Map, on large-scale real-world datasets collected across diverse driving conditions and demonstrate that our method is able to produce geometrically consistent lane-level maps.

AI.tldr: B²F-Map is a complete pipeline for high-definition map generation from production vehicles equipped only with a monocular camera, GNSS, and IMU, using Bayesian fusion of B-spline lane representations with principled uncertainty handling.
AI.motivation: Existing crowd-sourced mapping methods either require expensive prior high-definition maps or fail to properly handle uncertainties during map fusion, limiting their scalability and reliability.
AI.method: The proposed pipeline comprises three main components: (1) on-cloud localization using lightweight standard-definition maps; (2) on-vehicle mapping via an Extended Object Trajectory Poisson Multi-Bernoulli (PMB) filter with Gibbs sampling; (3) on-cloud multi-drive optimization and Bayesian map fusion. Lane lines are represented as B-splines parameterized by Gaussian-distributed control points, with a novel Bayesian fusion framework designed for trajectories with differing density representations.
AI.result: Evaluated on large-scale real-world datasets across diverse driving conditions, B²F-Map successfully produces geometrically consistent lane-level maps, demonstrating that the approach can generate HD maps using only affordable sensors.
AI.conclusion: The proposed B²F-Map pipeline enables scalable HD map generation from production vehicles with minimal sensor requirements, and the Bayesian fusion framework with B-spline representations effectively handles map uncertainties.

title: Catalyst-Agent: Autonomous heterogeneous catalyst screening and optimization with an LLM Agent
summary:
The discovery of novel catalysts tailored for particular applications is a major challenge for the twenty-first century. Traditional methods for this include time-consuming and expensive experimental trial-and-error approaches in labs based on chemical theory or heavily computational first-principles approaches based on density functional theory. Recent studies show that deep learning models like graph neural networks (GNNs) can significantly speed up the screening and discovery of catalyst materials by many orders of magnitude, with very high accuracy and fidelity.
  In this work, we introduce Catalyst-Agent, a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server-based, LLM-powered AI agent. It can explore vast material databases using the OPTIMADE API, make structural modifications, calculate adsorption energies using Meta FAIRchem's UMA (GNN) model via FAIRchem's AdsorbML workflow and slab construction, and make useful material suggestions to the researcher in a closed-loop manner, including surface-level modifications to refine near-miss candidates.
  It is tested on three pivotal reactions: the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), the nitrogen reduction reaction (NRR), and the CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR). Catalyst-Agent achieves a success rate of 23-34 percent among all the materials it chooses and evaluates, and manages to converge in 1-2 trials per successful material on average. This work demonstrates the potential of AI agents to exercise their planning capabilities and tool use to operationalize the catalyst screening workflow, provide useful, testable hypotheses, and accelerate future scientific discoveries for humanity with minimal human intervention.

AI.tldr: Catalyst-Agent is an LLM-powered AI agent integrated with GNN models via MCP server to accelerate catalyst discovery through closed-loop screening, achieving 23-34% success rate across ORR, NRR, and CO2RR reactions.
AI.motivation: Traditional catalyst discovery is time-consuming and expensive. While deep learning models like graph neural networks (GNNs) can accelerate screening, there is a need for systems that can operationalize these models into practical, closed-loop workflows with minimal human intervention.
AI.method: Catalyst-Agent uses an MCP server-based architecture that integrates LLMs with material databases via OPTIMADE API, structural modification capabilities, and Meta FAIRchem's UMA GNN model for adsorption energy calculations. It operates in a closed-loop manner, providing surface-level modifications to refine candidates.
AI.result: The agent achieved a success rate of 23-34% across three pivotal reactions (ORR, NRR, CO2RR) and converged to successful materials in 1-2 trials on average.
AI.conclusion: AI agents can effectively exercise planning capabilities and tool use to operationalize catalyst screening workflows, provide testable hypotheses, and accelerate scientific discoveries with minimal human intervention.

title: SesaHand: Enhancing 3D Hand Reconstruction via Controllable Generation with Semantic and Structural Alignment
summary: Recent studies on 3D hand reconstruction have demonstrated the effectiveness of synthetic training data to improve estimation performance. However, most methods rely on game engines to synthesize hand images, which often lack diversity in textures and environments, and fail to include crucial components like arms or interacting objects. Generative models are promising alternatives to generate diverse hand images, but still suffer from misalignment issues. In this paper, we present SesaHand, which enhances controllable hand image generation from both semantic and structural alignment perspectives for 3D hand reconstruction. Specifically, for semantic alignment, we propose a pipeline with Chain-of-Thought inference to extract human behavior semantics from image captions generated by the Vision-Language Model. This semantics suppresses human-irrelevant environmental details and ensures sufficient human-centric contexts for hand image generation. For structural alignment, we introduce hierarchical structural fusion to integrate structural information with different granularity for feature refinement to better align the hand and the overall human body in generated images. We further propose a hand structure attention enhancement method to efficiently enhance the model's attention on hand regions. Experiments demonstrate that our method not only outperforms prior work in generation performance but also improves 3D hand reconstruction with the generated hand images.

AI.tldr: SesaHand is a method for controllable hand image generation that improves 3D hand reconstruction by enhancing semantic and structural alignment in generated images through Chain-of-Thought inference and hierarchical structural fusion.
AI.motivation: Recent 3D hand reconstruction studies show synthetic training data effectiveness, but existing game-engine-based methods lack texture/environment diversity and fail to include arms or interacting objects. Generative models offer diversity but suffer from misalignment issues.
AI.method: SesaHand employs two main approaches: (1) Semantic alignment using Chain-of-Thought inference to extract human behavior semantics from Vision-Language Model captions, suppressing irrelevant environmental details; (2) Structural alignment using hierarchical structural fusion to integrate structural information at different granularities and a hand structure attention enhancement method to focus on hand regions.
AI.result: The method outperforms prior work in generation performance and improves 3D hand reconstruction quality when using the generated hand images as training data.
AI.conclusion: SesaHand successfully enhances controllable hand image generation for 3D hand reconstruction by addressing misalignment issues through semantic and structural alignment techniques.

title: ProtRLSearch: A Multi-Round Multimodal Protein Search Agent with Large Language Models Trained via Reinforcement Learning
summary: Protein analysis tasks arising in healthcare settings often require accurate reasoning under protein sequence constraints, involving tasks such as functional interpretation of disease-related variants, protein-level analysis for clinical research, and similar scenarios. To address such tasks, search agents are introduced to search protein-related information, providing support for disease-related variant analysis and protein function reasoning in protein-centric inference. However, such search agents are mostly limited to single-round, text-only modality search, which prevents the protein sequence modality from being incorporated as a multimodal input into the search decision-making process. Meanwhile, their reliance on reinforcement learning (RL) supervision that focuses solely on the final answer results in a lack of search process constraints, making deviations in keyword selection and reasoning directions difficult to identify and correct in a timely manner. To address these limitations, we propose ProtRLSearch, a multi-round protein search agent trained with multi-dimensional reward based RL, which jointly leverages protein sequence and text as multimodal inputs during real-time search to produce high quality reports. To evaluate the ability of models to integrate protein sequence information and text-based multimodal inputs in realistic protein query settings, we construct ProtMCQs, a benchmark of 3,000 multiple choice questions (MCQs) organized into three difficulty levels. The benchmark evaluates protein query tasks that range from sequence constrained reasoning about protein function and phenotype changes to comprehensive protein reasoning that integrates multi-dimensional sequence features with signal pathways and regulatory networks.

AI.tldr: ProtRLSearch is a multi-round protein search agent trained with multi-dimensional reward reinforcement learning that jointly leverages protein sequence and text as multimodal inputs to produce high-quality reports for protein-related queries, evaluated on a new benchmark of 3,000 MCQs across three difficulty levels.
AI.motivation: Healthcare protein analysis tasks require accurate reasoning under protein sequence constraints for functional interpretation of disease-related variants and protein-level analysis. Existing search agents are limited to single-round, text-only search, fail to incorporate protein sequence as multimodal input, and rely on RL supervision focused only on final answers without search process constraints, making it difficult to identify and correct deviations in keyword selection and reasoning directions.
AI.method: ProtRLSearch is proposed as a multi-round protein search agent trained with multi-dimensional reward based reinforcement learning. The method jointly leverages protein sequence and text as multimodal inputs during real-time search to produce high-quality reports.
AI.result: The authors construct ProtMCQs, a benchmark of 3,000 multiple choice questions organized into three difficulty levels. The benchmark evaluates protein query tasks ranging from sequence constrained reasoning about protein function and phenotype changes to comprehensive protein reasoning that integrates multi-dimensional sequence features with signal pathways and regulatory networks.
AI.conclusion: ProtRLSearch enables the integration of protein sequence information and text-based multimodal inputs for realistic protein query settings, addressing limitations of existing single-round, text-only search agents by incorporating protein sequence as multimodal input and providing multi-dimensional reward-based RL supervision for search process constraints.

title: IDER: IDempotent Experience Replay for Reliable Continual Learning
summary: Catastrophic forgetting, the tendency of neural networks to forget previously learned knowledge when learning new tasks, has been a major challenge in continual learning (CL). To tackle this challenge, CL methods have been proposed and shown to reduce forgetting. Furthermore, CL models deployed in mission-critical settings can benefit from uncertainty awareness by calibrating their predictions to reliably assess their confidences. However, existing uncertainty-aware continual learning methods suffer from high computational overhead and incompatibility with mainstream replay methods. To address this, we propose idempotent experience replay (IDER), a novel approach based on the idempotent property where repeated function applications yield the same output. Specifically, we first adapt the training loss to make model idempotent on current data streams. In addition, we introduce an idempotence distillation loss. We feed the output of the current model back into the old checkpoint and then minimize the distance between this reprocessed output and the original output of the current model. This yields a simple and effective new baseline for building reliable continual learners, which can be seamlessly integrated with other CL approaches. Extensive experiments on different CL benchmarks demonstrate that IDER consistently improves prediction reliability while simultaneously boosting accuracy and reducing forgetting. Our results suggest the potential of idempotence as a promising principle for deploying efficient and trustworthy continual learning systems in real-world applications.Our code is available at https://github.com/YutingLi0606/Idempotent-Continual-Learning.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes idempotent experience replay (IDER) to address catastrophic forgetting in continual learning while improving prediction reliability and reducing computational overhead.
AI.motivation: Existing uncertainty-aware continual learning methods suffer from high computational overhead and incompatibility with mainstream replay methods. The authors aim to develop a simpler approach that maintains model reliability without sacrificing efficiency.
AI.method: IDER is based on the idempotent property (repeated function applications yield the same output). The method adapts the training loss to make the model idempotent on current data streams and introduces an idempotence distillation loss. This involves feeding model outputs back to old checkpoints and minimizing the distance between reprocessed outputs and original outputs.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on different continual learning benchmarks demonstrate that IDER consistently improves prediction reliability while simultaneously boosting accuracy and reducing forgetting.
AI.conclusion: IDER provides a simple and effective baseline for building reliable continual learners that can be seamlessly integrated with other CL approaches. The results suggest that idempotence is a promising principle for deploying efficient and trustworthy continual learning systems in real-world applications.

title: TacMamba: A Tactile History Compression Adapter Bridging Fast Reflexes and Slow VLA Reasoning
summary: In visually ambiguous manipulation such as detecting button click tactile feedback is often the sole source of ground truth. However, fusing tactile data poses a significant challenge due to a spatiotemporal mismatch: tactile perception requires high-frequency processing with long-horizon memory (System 1), whereas visual policies operate at low control frequencies (System 2). Existing architectures struggle to bridge this gap: Transformers are computationally prohibitive for high-frequency loops (>100Hz), while LSTMs suffer from forgetting over extended interaction histories. In this paper, we introduce TacMamba, a hierarchical architecture that aligns high-bandwidth tactile reflexes with low-frequency visual planning. Our approach comprises three core contributions: (1) a custom high-frequency tactile interface designed for flexible integration; (2) a Mamba-based Tactile History Compressor that encodes continuous force history into a compact state with O(1) inference latency (0.45 ms), enabling plug-and-play fusion with VLA models without joint pre-training and (3) a Tactile-Guided Dual-Stage Training strategy that leverages temporal discrimination for self-supervised representation learning and phase-uniform sampling to mitigate data sparsity. Experiments on discrete counting and implicit state switching demonstrate that TacMamba achieves 100% success rates, significantly outperforming the visual-only pi_0.5 baseline, while strictly satisfying hard real-time constraints.

AI.tldr: TacMamba is a hierarchical architecture that fuses high-frequency tactile feedback with low-frequency visual planning in robotic manipulation, achieving 100% success on counting and state-switching tasks while meeting hard real-time constraints (0.45ms latency).
AI.motivation: In visually ambiguous manipulation, tactile feedback is essential but fusing it with vision is challenging due to spatiotemporal mismatch: tactile perception requires high-frequency processing with long-horizon memory, while visual policies operate at low control frequencies. Existing approaches fail: Transformers are computationally prohibitive for high-frequency loops, and LSTMs suffer from forgetting over extended interaction histories.
AI.method: TacMamba comprises three core innovations: (1) a custom high-frequency tactile interface for flexible integration; (2) a Mamba-based Tactile History Compressor that encodes continuous force history into a compact state with O(1) inference latency (0.45ms), enabling plug-and-play fusion with VLA models; (3) a Tactile-Guided Dual-Stage Training strategy using temporal discrimination for self-supervised representation learning and phase-uniform sampling to mitigate data sparsity.
AI.result: TacMamba achieves 100% success rates on discrete counting and implicit state switching tasks, significantly outperforming the visual-only pi_0.5 baseline while strictly satisfying hard real-time constraints with 0.45ms latency.
AI.conclusion: TacMamba successfully bridges the gap between high-bandwidth tactile reflexes and low-frequency visual planning, demonstrating that Mamba-based architectures can effectively fuse tactile and visual modalities without joint pre-training, making it suitable for real-time robotic manipulation applications.

title: Truth as a Trajectory: What Internal Representations Reveal About Large Language Model Reasoning
summary: Existing explainability methods for Large Language Models (LLMs) typically treat hidden states as static points in activation space, assuming that correct and incorrect inferences can be separated using representations from an individual layer. However, these activations are saturated with polysemantic features, leading to linear probes learning surface-level lexical patterns rather than underlying reasoning structures. We introduce Truth as a Trajectory (TaT), which models the transformer inference as an unfolded trajectory of iterative refinements, shifting analysis from static activations to layer-wise geometric displacement. By analyzing displacement of representations across layers, TaT uncovers geometric invariants that distinguish valid reasoning from spurious behavior. We evaluate TaT across dense and Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures on benchmarks spanning commonsense reasoning, question answering, and toxicity detection. Without access to the activations themselves and using only changes in activations across layers, we show that TaT effectively mitigates reliance on static lexical confounds, outperforming conventional probing, and establishes trajectory analysis as a complementary perspective on LLM explainability.

AI.tldr: TaT (Truth as a Trajectory) is a novel explainability method for LLMs that analyzes the geometric displacement of representations across transformer layers rather than static activations, enabling the distinction of valid reasoning from spurious behavior.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM explainability methods treat hidden states as static points in activation space, assuming correct/incorrect inferences can be separated by individual layer activations. However, activations are saturated with polysemantic features, causing linear probes to learn surface-level lexical patterns rather than underlying reasoning structures.
AI.method: TaT models transformer inference as an unfolded trajectory of iterative refinements, shifting analysis from static activations to layer-wise geometric displacement. It analyzes representation displacement across layers to uncover geometric invariants distinguishing valid reasoning from spurious behavior.
AI.result: TaT was evaluated across dense and Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures on benchmarks spanning commonsense reasoning, question answering, and toxicity detection. Without access to activations themselves and using only changes across layers, TaT effectively mitigates reliance on static lexical confounds and outperforms conventional probing methods.
AI.conclusion: TaT establishes trajectory analysis as a complementary perspective on LLM explainability, demonstrating that analyzing geometric displacement across layers provides valuable insights beyond static activation analysis.

title: Improved Adversarial Diffusion Compression for Real-World Video Super-Resolution
summary: While many diffusion models have achieved impressive results in real-world video super-resolution (Real-VSR) by generating rich and realistic details, their reliance on multi-step sampling leads to slow inference. One-step networks like SeedVR2, DOVE, and DLoRAL alleviate this through condensing generation into one single step, yet they remain heavy, with billions of parameters and multi-second latency. Recent adversarial diffusion compression (ADC) offers a promising path via pruning and distilling these models into a compact AdcSR network, but directly applying it to Real-VSR fails to balance spatial details and temporal consistency due to its lack of temporal awareness and the limitations of standard adversarial learning. To address these challenges, we propose an improved ADC method for Real-VSR. Our approach distills a large diffusion Transformer (DiT) teacher DOVE equipped with 3D spatio-temporal attentions, into a pruned 2D Stable Diffusion (SD)-based AdcSR backbone, augmented with lightweight 1D temporal convolutions, achieving significantly higher efficiency. In addition, we introduce a dual-head adversarial distillation scheme, in which discriminators in both pixel and feature domains explicitly disentangle the discrimination of details and consistency into two heads, enabling both objectives to be effectively optimized without sacrificing one for the other. Experiments demonstrate that the resulting compressed AdcVSR model reduces complexity by 95% in parameters and achieves an 8$\times$ acceleration over its DiT teacher DOVE, while maintaining competitive video quality and efficiency.

AI.tldr: This paper improves adversarial diffusion compression (ADC) for real-world video super-resolution by distilling a 3D DiT teacher into a compact 2D SD-based backbone with dual-head adversarial distillation, achieving 95% parameter reduction and 8x speedup over the teacher while maintaining video quality.
AI.motivation: Multi-step diffusion models for Real-VSR are slow, while existing one-step networks remain heavy with billions of parameters. Standard ADC compression fails for Real-VSR due to lack of temporal awareness and limitations of standard adversarial learning that cannot balance spatial details and temporal consistency.
AI.method: The authors distill a 3D spatio-temporal DiT teacher (DOVE) into a pruned 2D Stable Diffusion-based AdcSR backbone augmented with lightweight 1D temporal convolutions, and introduce a dual-head adversarial distillation scheme with discriminators in both pixel and feature domains that disentangle detail and consistency discrimination into two separate heads.
AI.result: The compressed AdcVSR model reduces complexity by 95% in parameters and achieves 8× acceleration over its DiT teacher DOVE, while maintaining competitive video quality and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed improved ADC method effectively balances spatial details and temporal consistency in Real-VSR through efficient model compression and dual-head adversarial distillation, demonstrating that compact, fast models can achieve quality comparable to large teacher networks.

title: Harmonizing Dense and Sparse Signals in Multi-turn RL: Dual-Horizon Credit Assignment for Industrial Sales Agents
summary: Optimizing large language models for industrial sales requires balancing long-term commercial objectives (e.g., conversion rate) with immediate linguistic constraints such as fluency and compliance. Conventional reinforcement learning often merges these heterogeneous goals into a single reward, causing high-magnitude session-level rewards to overwhelm subtler turn-level signals, which leads to unstable training or reward hacking. To address this issue, we propose Dual-Horizon Credit Assignment (DuCA), a framework that disentangles optimization across time scales. Its core, Horizon-Independent Advantage Normalization (HIAN), separately normalizes advantages from turn-level and session-level rewards before fusion, ensuring balanced gradient contributions from both immediate and long-term objectives to the policy update. Extensive experiments with a high-fidelity user simulator show DuCA outperforms the state-of-the-art GRPO baseline, achieving a 6.82% relative improvement in conversion rate, reducing inter-sentence repetition by 82.28%, and lowering identity detection rate by 27.35%, indicating a substantial improvement for an industrial sales scenario that effectively balances the dual demands of strategic performance and naturalistic language generation.

AI.tldr: DuCA is a reinforcement learning framework that uses Horizon-Independent Advantage Normalization to disentangle turn-level and session-level objectives, enabling large language models to better balance immediate linguistic constraints with long-term commercial goals in industrial sales scenarios.
AI.motivation: Industrial sales applications require simultaneously optimizing long-term commercial metrics (e.g., conversion rate) and immediate linguistic quality (fluency, compliance). Conventional reinforcement learning merges these heterogeneous objectives into a single reward, causing high-magnitude session-level rewards to overwhelm subtle turn-level signals, leading to unstable training and reward hacking.
AI.method: Dual-Horizon Credit Assignment (DuCA) with Horizon-Independent Advantage Normalization (HIAN). The framework disentangles optimization across time scales by separately normalizing advantages derived from turn-level and session-level rewards before fusion, ensuring balanced gradient contributions from both immediate and long-term objectives during policy updates.
AI.result: Extensive experiments with high-fidelity user simulators show DuCA outperforms state-of-the-art GRPO baseline: 6.82% relative improvement in conversion rate, 82.28% reduction in inter-sentence repetition, and 27.35% lower identity detection rate.
AI.conclusion: DuCA substantially improves industrial sales scenarios by effectively balancing the dual demands of strategic performance and naturalistic language generation, demonstrating that disentangled credit assignment across time scales is crucial for optimizing heterogeneous objectives in RL-based LLM applications.

title: Adapt Data to Model: Adaptive Transformation Optimization for Domain-shared Time Series Foundation Models
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026
summary: Large time series models (LTMs) have emerged as powerful tools for universal forecasting, yet they often struggle with the inherent diversity and nonstationarity of real-world time series data, leading to an unsatisfactory trade-off between forecasting accuracy and generalization. Rather than continually finetuning new LTM instances for each domain, we propose a data-centric framework, time-series adaptive transformation optimization (TATO), that enables a single frozen pre-trained LTM to adapt to diverse downstream domains through an optimally configured transformation pipeline. Specifically, TATO constructs three representative types of transformations, including context slicing, scale normalization, and outlier correction, to help LTMs better align with target domain characteristics. To ensure robustness, we incorporate carefully selected time series augmentations and a two-stage ranking mechanism that filters out pipelines underperforming on specific metrics. Extensive experiments on state-of-the-art LTMs and widely used datasets demonstrate that TATO consistently and significantly improves domain-adaptive forecasting performance, achieving a maximum reduction in MSE of 65.4\% and an average reduction of 13.6\%. Moreover, TATO is highly efficient, typically completing optimization in under 2 minutes, making it practical for real-world deployment. The source code is available at https://github.com/thulab/TATO.

AI.tldr: TATO is a data-centric framework that enables a single frozen pre-trained large time series model to adapt to diverse downstream domains through an optimally configured transformation pipeline comprising context slicing, scale normalization, and outlier correction, achieving significant MSE reduction (up to 65.4%) while maintaining high efficiency (under 2 minutes optimization time).
AI.motivation: Large time series models (LTMs) struggle with the inherent diversity and nonstationarity of real-world time series data, creating an unsatisfactory trade-off between forecasting accuracy and generalization. Rather than continually fine-tuning new LTM instances for each domain, the authors propose a more efficient data-centric approach.
AI.method: TATO constructs three transformation types (context slicing, scale normalization, outlier correction) to align LTMs with target domain characteristics. It incorporates carefully selected time series augmentations and a two-stage ranking mechanism that filters out pipelines underperforming on specific metrics.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate TATO consistently and significantly improves domain-adaptive forecasting performance, achieving a maximum MSE reduction of 65.4% and an average reduction of 13.6%. The framework optimizes in under 2 minutes, making it practical for real-world deployment.
AI.conclusion: TATO proves that a single frozen pre-trained LTM can effectively adapt to diverse domains through optimized transformation pipelines, offering superior performance and practical efficiency compared to the traditional approach of fine-tuning new instances for each domain.

title: A Safety-Aware Shared Autonomy Framework with BarrierIK Using Control Barrier Functions
summary: Shared autonomy blends operator intent with autonomous assistance. In cluttered environments, linear blending can produce unsafe commands even when each source is individually collision-free. Many existing approaches model obstacle avoidance through potentials or cost terms, which only enforce safety as a soft constraint. In contrast, safety-critical control requires hard guarantees. We investigate the use of control barrier functions (CBFs) at the inverse kinematics (IK) layer of shared autonomy, targeting post-blend safety while preserving task performance. Our approach is evaluated in simulation on representative cluttered environments and in a VR teleoperation study comparing pure teleoperation with shared autonomy. Across conditions, employing CBFs at the IK layer reduces violation time and increases minimum clearance while maintaining task performance. In the user study, participants reported higher perceived safety and trust, lower interference, and an overall preference for shared autonomy with our safety filter. Additional materials available at https://berkguler.github.io/barrierik.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates using control barrier functions (CBFs) at the inverse kinematics layer of shared autonomy systems to provide hard safety guarantees in cluttered environments, reducing command violations and improving user safety perception compared to traditional soft-constraint approaches.
AI.motivation: Shared autonomy blends operator intent with autonomous assistance, but linear blending can produce unsafe commands in cluttered environments even when each source is individually collision-free. Existing obstacle avoidance methods use potentials or cost terms that only enforce safety as soft constraints, whereas safety-critical control requires hard guarantees.
