AI.method: The authors investigate using control barrier functions (CBFs) at the inverse kinematics (IK) layer of shared autonomy systems to target post-blend safety while preserving task performance, evaluated through simulation on cluttered environments and a VR teleoperation study comparing pure teleoperation with shared autonomy.
AI.result: Employing CBFs at the IK layer reduces violation time and increases minimum clearance while maintaining task performance. In the VR study, participants reported higher perceived safety and trust, lower interference, and an overall preference for shared autonomy with the safety filter.
AI.conclusion: CBFs at the IK layer can provide hard safety guarantees for shared autonomy systems while preserving task performance and significantly improving user experience through enhanced perceived safety and trust.

title: MetaState: Persistent Working Memory for Discrete Diffusion Language Models
summary: Discrete diffusion language models (dLLMs) generate text by iteratively denoising a masked sequence. Compared with autoregressive models, this paradigm naturally supports parallel decoding, bidirectional context, and flexible generation patterns. However, standard dLLMs condition each denoising step only on the current hard-masked sequence, while intermediate continuous representations are discarded after sampling and remasking. We refer to this bottleneck as the \textbf{Information Island} problem. It leads to redundant recomputation across steps and can degrade cross-step consistency. We address this limitation with \textbf{MetaState}, a lightweight recurrent augmentation that equips a frozen dLLM backbone with a persistent, fixed-size working memory that remains independent of sequence length. \textbf{MetaState} consists of three trainable modules: a cross-attention Mixer that reads backbone activations into memory slots, a GRU-style Updater that integrates information across denoising steps, and a cross-attention Injector that feeds the updated memory back into backbone activations. We train these modules with $K$-step unrolling to expose them to multi-step denoising dynamics during fine-tuning. On LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B, \textbf{MetaState} introduces negligible trainable parameters while keeping the backbone frozen, and it consistently improves accuracy over frozen baselines. These results demonstrate that persistent cross-step memory is an effective mechanism for bridging denoising steps and improving generation quality in discrete diffusion language models.

AI.tldr: MetaState introduces a lightweight recurrent memory augmentation that bridges denoising steps in discrete diffusion language models, addressing the "Information Island" problem and improving generation quality with negligible trainable parameters.
AI.motivation: Standard dLLMs discard intermediate continuous representations after sampling and remasking, leading to the "Information Island" problem which causes redundant recomputation across steps and degrades cross-step consistency.
AI.method: MetaState adds three trainable modules to a frozen dLLM backbone: (1) cross-attention Mixer to read backbone activations into memory slots, (2) GRU-style Updater to integrate information across steps, and (3) cross-attention Injector to feed updated memory back. These modules are trained with K-step unrolling during fine-tuning.
AI.result: On LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B, MetaState introduces negligible trainable parameters while keeping the backbone frozen, consistently improving accuracy over frozen baselines.
AI.conclusion: Persistent cross-step memory is an effective mechanism for bridging denoising steps and improving generation quality in discrete diffusion language models.

title: Explainable Continuous-Time Mask Refinement with Local Self-Similarity Priors for Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Accurate semantic segmentation of foot ulcers is essential for automated wound monitoring, yet boundary delineation remains challenging due to tissue heterogeneity and poor contrast with surrounding skin. To overcome the limitations of standard intensity-based networks, we present LSS-LTCNet:an ante-hoc explainable framework synergizing deterministic structural priors with continuous-time neural dynamics. Our architecture departs from traditional black-box models by employing a Local Self-Similarity (LSS) mechanism that extracts dense, illumination-invariant texture descriptors to explicitly disentangle necrotic tissue from background artifacts. To enforce topological precision, we introduce a Liquid Time-Constant (LTC) refinement module that treats boundary evolution as an ODEgoverned dynamic system, iteratively refining masks over continuous time-steps. Comprehensive evaluation on the MICCAI FUSeg dataset demonstrates that LSS-LTCNet achieves state-of-the-art boundary alignment, securing a peak Dice score of 86.96% and an exceptional 95th percentile Hausdorff Distance (HD95) of 8.91 pixels. Requiring merely 25.70M parameters, the model significantly outperforms heavier U-Net and transformer baselines in efficiency. By providing inherent visual audit trails alongside high-fidelity predictions, LSS-LTCNet offers a robust and transparent solution for computer-aided diagnosis in mobile healthcare (mHealth) settings.

AI.tldr: LSS-LTCNet is an explainable framework for foot ulcer segmentation that combines Local Self-Similarity (LSS) texture descriptors with Liquid Time-Constant (LTC) ODE-based dynamics to achieve state-of-the-art boundary alignment with only 25.70M parameters.
AI.motivation: Accurate foot ulcer segmentation is essential for automated wound monitoring, but boundary delineation is challenging due to tissue heterogeneity and poor contrast with surrounding skin. Standard intensity-based networks have limitations.
AI.method: The architecture employs a Local Self-Similarity (LSS) mechanism to extract dense, illumination-invariant texture descriptors for disentangling necrotic tissue from background artifacts. A Liquid Time-Constant (LTC) refinement module treats boundary evolution as an ODE-governed dynamic system, iteratively refining masks over continuous time-steps.
AI.result: On the MICCAI FUSeg dataset, LSS-LTCNet achieves a peak Dice score of 86.96% and a Hausdorff Distance (HD95) of 8.91 pixels, outperforming heavier U-Net and transformer baselines while using only 25.70M parameters.
AI.conclusion: LSS-LTCNet provides a robust and transparent solution for computer-aided diagnosis in mobile healthcare (mHealth) settings, offering inherent visual audit trails alongside high-fidelity predictions.

title: Agentic Multi-Source Grounding for Enhanced Query Intent Understanding: A DoorDash Case Study
summary: Accurately mapping user queries to business categories is a fundamental Information Retrieval challenge for multi-category marketplaces, where context-sparse queries such as "Wildflower" exhibit intent ambiguity, simultaneously denoting a restaurant chain, a retail product, and a floral item. Traditional classifiers force a winner-takes-all assignment, while general-purpose LLMs hallucinate unavailable inventory. We introduce an Agentic Multi-Source Grounded system that addresses both failure modes by grounding LLM inference in (i) a staged catalog entity retrieval pipeline and (ii) an agentic web-search tool invoked autonomously for cold-start queries. Rather than predicting a single label, the model emits an ordered multi-intent set, resolved by a configurable disambiguation layer that applies deterministic business policies and is designed for extensibility to personalization signals. This decoupled design generalizes across domains, allowing any marketplace to supply its own grounding sources and resolution rules without modifying the core architecture. Evaluated on DoorDash's multi-vertical search platform, the system achieves +10.9pp over the ungrounded LLM baseline and +4.6pp over the legacy production system. On long-tail queries, incremental ablations attribute +8.3pp to catalog grounding, +3.2pp to agentic web search grounding, and +1.5pp to dual intent disambiguation, yielding 90.7% accuracy (+13.0pp over baseline). The system is deployed in production, serving over 95% of daily search impressions, and establishes a generalizable paradigm for applications requiring foundation models grounded in proprietary context and real-time web knowledge to resolve ambiguous, context-sparse decision problems at scale.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an Agentic Multi-Source Grounded system for resolving ambiguous, context-sparse queries in multi-category marketplaces. The approach combines catalog entity retrieval, agentic web search, and dual-intent disambiguation to achieve 90.7% accuracy on long-tail queries, outperforming both ungrounded LLM baselines and legacy production systems by +10.9pp and +4.6pp respectively.
AI.motivation: Context-sparse queries like "Wildflower" exhibit intent ambiguity across multiple business categories (restaurants, retail products, floral items). Traditional classifiers force winner-takes-all assignments, while general-purpose LLMs hallucinate unavailable inventory. The system aims to address both failure modes by grounding LLM inference in structured catalog data and real-time web knowledge.
AI.method: The Agentic Multi-Source Grounded system features: (1) a staged catalog entity retrieval pipeline for grounding LLM inference, (2) an autonomous agentic web-search tool for cold-start queries, (3) an ordered multi-intent output mechanism instead of single-label prediction, and (4) a configurable disambiguation layer applying deterministic business policies. The architecture is decoupled, allowing any marketplace to supply custom grounding sources and resolution rules.
AI.result: Evaluated on DoorDash's multi-vertical search platform, the system achieves +10.9pp over ungrounded LLM baseline and +4.6pp over legacy production system. On long-tail queries, ablations attribute +8.3pp to catalog grounding, +3.2pp to agentic web search grounding, and +1.5pp to dual intent disambiguation. The system reaches 90.7% accuracy (+13.0pp over baseline) and is deployed in production serving over 95% of daily search impressions.
AI.conclusion: The decoupled, generalizable architecture demonstrates that foundation models can be effectively grounded in proprietary context and real-time web knowledge to resolve ambiguous, context-sparse decision problems at scale. The system establishes a reusable paradigm for applications requiring multi-source grounding and multi-intent resolution, with extensibility to personalization signals and domain-specific business policies.

title: Retrodictive Forecasting: A Proof-of-Concept for Exploiting Temporal Asymmetry in Time Series Prediction
comment: 27 pages, 13 figures, 5 tables, Code available at https://github.com/cdamour/retrodictive-forecasting (Zenodo: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18803446)
summary: We propose a retrodictive forecasting paradigm for time series: instead of predicting the future from the past, we identify the future that best explains the observed present via inverse MAP optimization over a Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE). This conditioning is a statistical modeling choice for Bayesian inversion; it does not assert that future events cause past observations. The approach is theoretically grounded in an information-theoretic arrow-of-time measure: the symmetrized Kullback-Leibler divergence between forward and time-reversed trajectory ensembles provides both the conceptual rationale and an operational GO/NO-GO diagnostic for applicability. We implement the paradigm as MAP inference over an inverse CVAE with a learned RealNVP normalizing-flow prior and evaluate it on six time series cases: four synthetic processes with controlled temporal asymmetry and two ERA5 reanalysis datasets (wind speed and solar irradiance). The work makes four contributions: (i) a formal retrodictive inference formulation; (ii) an inverse CVAE architecture; (iii) a model-free irreversibility diagnostic; and (iv) a falsifiable validation protocol with four pre-specified predictions. All pre-specified predictions are empirically supported: the diagnostic correctly classifies all six cases; the learned flow prior improves over an isotropic Gaussian baseline on GO cases; the inverse MAP yields no spurious advantage on time-reversible dynamics; and on irreversible GO cases, it achieves competitive or superior RMSE relative to forward baselines, with a statistically significant 17.7% reduction over a forward MLP on ERA5 solar irradiance. These results provide a structured proof-of-concept that retrodictive forecasting can constitute a viable alternative to conventional forward prediction when statistical time-irreversibility is present and exploitable.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a retrodictive forecasting paradigm for time series where instead of predicting the future from the past, the approach identifies the future that best explains the observed present via inverse MAP optimization over a Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE).
AI.motivation: To explore an alternative forecasting approach grounded in statistical time-irreversibility, where instead of forward prediction from past to future, one performs retrodiction by identifying the future that explains the present.
AI.method: The method includes: (i) formal retrodictive inference formulation using inverse MAP optimization over a CVAE, (ii) inverse CVAE architecture with a learned RealNVP normalizing-flow prior, (iii) a model-free irreversibility diagnostic based on symmetrized Kullback-Leibler divergence between forward and time-reversed trajectory ensembles, and (iv) a falsifiable validation protocol with pre-specified predictions.
AI.result: All pre-specified predictions were empirically supported: the diagnostic correctly classified all six cases (synthetic processes and ERA5 datasets); the learned flow prior improved over isotropic Gaussian baseline on GO cases; inverse MAP showed no advantage on time-reversible dynamics; and on irreversible GO cases, it achieved competitive or superior RMSE, with a statistically significant 17.7% reduction over a forward MLP on ERA5 solar irradiance.
AI.conclusion: Retrodictive forecasting can constitute a viable alternative to conventional forward prediction when statistical time-irreversibility is present and exploitable, providing a structured proof-of-concept for this approach.

title: PanCanBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Evaluating Large Language Models in Pancreatic Oncology
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved expert-level performance on standardized examinations, yet multiple-choice accuracy poorly reflects real-world clinical utility and safety. As patients and clinicians increasingly use LLMs for guidance on complex conditions such as pancreatic cancer, evaluation must extend beyond general medical knowledge. Existing frameworks, such as HealthBench, rely on simulated queries and lack disease-specific depth. Moreover, high rubric-based scores do not ensure factual correctness, underscoring the need to assess hallucinations. We developed a human-in-the-loop pipeline to create expert rubrics for de-identified patient questions from the Pancreatic Cancer Action Network (PanCAN). The resulting benchmark, PanCanBench, includes 3,130 question-specific criteria across 282 authentic patient questions. We evaluated 22 proprietary and open-source LLMs using an LLM-as-a-judge framework, measuring clinical completeness, factual accuracy, and web-search integration. Models showed substantial variation in rubric-based completeness, with scores ranging from 46.5% to 82.3%. Factual errors were common, with hallucination rates (the percentages of responses containing at least one factual error) ranging from 6.0% for Gemini-2.5 Pro and GPT-4o to 53.8% for Llama-3.1-8B. Importantly, newer reasoning-optimized models did not consistently improve factuality: although o3 achieved the highest rubric score, it produced inaccuracies more frequently than other GPT-family models. Web-search integration did not inherently guarantee better responses. The average score changed from 66.8% to 63.9% for Gemini-2.5 Pro and from 73.8% to 72.8% for GPT-5 when web search was enabled. Synthetic AI-generated rubrics inflated absolute scores by 17.9 points on average while generally maintaining similar relative ranking.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PanCanBench, a benchmark for evaluating LLMs on authentic pancreatic cancer patient questions, revealing substantial variability in both completeness (46.5%-82.3%) and factual accuracy (6.0%-53.8% hallucination rates), with web search and synthetic rubrics providing limited improvement.
AI.motivation: LLMs achieve expert-level performance on standardized exams but this poorly reflects real-world clinical utility. Existing frameworks like HealthBench lack disease-specific depth, and high rubric scores don't ensure factual correctness, necessitating assessment of hallucinations in medical contexts.
AI.method: Developed a human-in-the-loop pipeline to create expert rubrics from de-identified PanCAN patient questions, resulting in PanCanBench with 3,130 criteria across 282 questions. Evaluated 22 proprietary and open-source LLMs using LLM-as-a-judge framework measuring clinical completeness, factual accuracy, and web-search integration.
AI.result: Models showed wide variation in completeness (46.5%-82.3%) with hallucination rates ranging from 6.0% (Gemini-2.5 Pro, GPT-4o) to 53.8% (Llama-3.1-8B). Newer reasoning-optimized models didn't consistently improve factuality. Web-search integration didn't guarantee better responses (scores decreased from 66.8% to 63.9% for Gemini-2.5 Pro). Synthetic AI rubrics inflated scores by 17.9 points on average.
AI.conclusion: LLMs exhibit substantial variability in performance on complex medical questions, and high rubric scores don't ensure factual correctness. Human-in-the-loop rubric creation is essential for accurate evaluation, as synthetic rubrics artificially inflate scores. Web search integration does not inherently improve medical response quality.

title: ReMoT: Reinforcement Learning with Motion Contrast Triplets
summary: We present ReMoT, a unified training paradigm to systematically address the fundamental shortcomings of VLMs in spatio-temporal consistency -- a critical failure point in navigation, robotics, and autonomous driving. ReMoT integrates two core components: (1) A rule-based automatic framework that generates ReMoT-16K, a large-scale (16.5K triplets) motion-contrast dataset derived from video meta-annotations, surpassing costly manual or model-based generation. (2) Group Relative Policy Optimization, which we empirically validate yields optimal performance and data efficiency for learning this contrastive reasoning, far exceeding standard Supervised Fine-Tuning. We also construct the first benchmark for fine-grained motion contrast triplets to measure a VLM's discrimination of subtle motion attributes (e.g., opposing directions). The resulting model achieves state-of-the-art performance on our new benchmark and multiple standard VLM benchmarks, culminating in a remarkable 25.1% performance leap on spatio-temporal reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: ReMoT is a unified training paradigm that improves vision-language models' spatio-temporal consistency through a large-scale motion-contrast dataset (ReMoT-16K) and Group Relative Policy Optimization, achieving a 25.1% performance boost on spatio-temporal reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: VLMs suffer from critical failures in spatio-temporal consistency, which is essential for navigation, robotics, and autonomous driving applications.
AI.method: Two core components: (1) Rule-based automatic framework generating ReMoT-16K dataset (16.5K motion-contrast triplets from video meta-annotations), (2) Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for learning contrastive reasoning, outperforming standard Supervised Fine-Tuning.
AI.result: Constructs the first benchmark for fine-grained motion contrast triplets. Achieves state-of-the-art performance on new benchmark and multiple standard VLM benchmarks, with a remarkable 25.1% performance increase on spatio-temporal reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: ReMoT effectively addresses VLM spatio-temporal consistency through systematic training with large-scale motion-contrast data and GRPO, demonstrating superior performance and data efficiency compared to traditional methods.

title: LLM-assisted Semantic Option Discovery for Facilitating Adaptive Deep Reinforcement Learning
summary: Despite achieving remarkable success in complex tasks, Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) is still suffering from critical issues in practical applications, such as low data efficiency, lack of interpretability, and limited cross-environment transferability. However, the learned policy generating actions based on states are sensitive to the environmental changes, struggling to guarantee behavioral safety and compliance. Recent research shows that integrating Large Language Models (LLMs) with symbolic planning is promising in addressing these challenges. Inspired by this, we introduce a novel LLM-driven closed-loop framework, which enables semantic-driven skill reuse and real-time constraint monitoring by mapping natural language instructions into executable rules and semantically annotating automatically created options. The proposed approach utilizes the general knowledge of LLMs to facilitate exploration efficiency and adapt to transferable options for similar environments, and provides inherent interpretability through semantic annotations. To validate the effectiveness of this framework, we conduct experiments on two domains, Office World and Montezuma's Revenge, respectively. The results demonstrate superior performance in data efficiency, constraint compliance, and cross-task transferability.

AI.tldr: An LLM-driven closed-loop framework that enhances deep reinforcement learning by mapping natural language instructions to executable rules, enabling semantic-driven skill reuse, real-time constraint monitoring, and improved data efficiency, interpretability, and cross-environment transferability.
AI.motivation: Deep Reinforcement Learning suffers from critical practical issues including low data efficiency, lack of interpretability, and limited cross-environment transferability. Additionally, learned policies are sensitive to environmental changes, making it difficult to guarantee behavioral safety and compliance. Recent research indicates that integrating Large Language Models with symbolic planning shows promise in addressing these challenges.
AI.method: The proposed framework introduces an LLM-driven closed-loop system that: (1) maps natural language instructions into executable rules, (2) semantically annotates automatically created options, (3) leverages LLM general knowledge to facilitate exploration efficiency, and (4) enables adaptation to transferable options for similar environments. The approach combines LLM capabilities with symbolic planning to provide inherent interpretability through semantic annotations.
AI.result: Experiments conducted on two domains (Office World and Montezuma's Revenge) demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves superior performance compared to baseline methods in three key areas: data efficiency, constraint compliance, and cross-task transferability.
AI.conclusion: The LLM-driven closed-loop framework effectively addresses the critical challenges in deep reinforcement learning by combining large language models with symbolic planning. It successfully enables semantic-driven skill reuse, real-time constraint monitoring, and provides significant improvements in data efficiency, interpretability, and cross-environment transferability across different task domains.

title: Reward-Modulated Local Learning in Spiking Encoders: Controlled Benchmarks with STDP and Hybrid Rate Readouts
comment: 10 pages, 5 figures. Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
summary: This paper presents a controlled empirical study of biologically motivated local learning for handwritten digit recognition. We evaluate an STDP-inspired competitive proxy and a practical hybrid benchmark built on the same spiking population encoder. The proxy is motivated by leaky integrate-and-fire E/I circuit models with three-factor delayed reward modulation. The hybrid update is local in pre x post rates but uses supervised labels and no timing-based credit assignment. On sklearn digits, fixed-seed evaluation shows classical pixel baselines from 98.06 to 98.22% accuracy, while local spike-based models reach 86.39 +/- 4.75% (hybrid default) and 87.17 +/- 3.74% (STDP-style competitive proxy). Ablations identify normalization and reward-shaping settings as the strongest observed levers, with a best hybrid ablation of 95.52 +/- 1.11%. A network-free synthetic temporal benchmark supports the same timing-versus-rate interpretation under matched local-update training. A descriptive 2x2 analysis further shows reward-shaping effects can reverse sign across stabilization regimes, so reward-shaping conclusions should be reported jointly with normalization settings.

AI.tldr: This paper empirically evaluates biologically motivated local learning (STDP-inspired competitive proxy and hybrid benchmark) for handwritten digit recognition. While classical pixel baselines achieve 98% accuracy, local spike-based models reach 86-87% accuracy, but with proper normalization and reward-shaping they can approach 95.5% accuracy, supporting the timing-versus-rate interpretation under matched local-update training.
AI.motivation: The authors investigate whether biologically plausible local learning mechanisms (particularly STDP-inspired approaches) can achieve competitive performance for handwritten digit recognition, aiming to bridge the gap between neuroscientific models of synaptic plasticity and practical machine learning applications.
AI.method: The study evaluates two approaches on the sklearn digits dataset: (1) an STDP-inspired competitive proxy based on leaky integrate-and-fire E/I circuit models with three-factor delayed reward modulation, and (2) a hybrid benchmark that is local in pre/post rates but uses supervised labels without timing-based credit assignment. Both use the same spiking population encoder. Ablation studies and a network-free synthetic temporal benchmark are also conducted to isolate the effects of normalization and reward-shaping.
AI.result: Classical pixel baselines achieve 98.06-98.22% accuracy, while local spike-based models reach 86.39 +/- 4.75% (hybrid default) and 87.17 +/- 3.74% (STDP-style competitive proxy). Ablations identify normalization and reward-shaping as the strongest performance levers, with the best hybrid ablation achieving 95.52 +/- 1.11% accuracy. A 2x2 analysis reveals reward-shaping effects can reverse sign across stabilization regimes.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that local, biologically motivated learning can approach classical supervised learning performance (95.5% vs 98%) when proper normalization and reward-shaping are applied. However, conclusions about reward-shaping should be reported jointly with normalization settings, as effects can reverse sign across different stabilization regimes, supporting the timing-versus-rate interpretation under matched local-update training.

title: Neural Implicit Action Fields: From Discrete Waypoints to Continuous Functions for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Despite the rapid progress of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, the prevailing paradigm of predicting discrete waypoints remains fundamentally misaligned with the intrinsic continuity of physical motion. This discretization imposes rigid sampling rates, lacks high-order differentiability, and introduces quantization artifacts that hinder precise, compliant interaction. We propose Neural Implicit Action Fields (NIAF), a paradigm shift that reformulates action prediction from discrete waypoints to continuous action function regression. By utilizing an MLLM as a hierarchical spectral modulator over a learnable motion prior, NIAF synthesizes infinite-resolution trajectories as continuous-time manifolds. This formulation enables analytical differentiability, allowing for explicit supervision of velocity, acceleration, and jerk to ensure mathematical consistency and physical plausibility. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art results on CALVIN and LIBERO benchmarks across diverse backbones. Furthermore, real-world experiments demonstrate that NIAF enables stable impedance control, bridging the gap between high-level semantic understanding and low-level dynamic execution.

AI.tldr: Neural Implicit Action Fields (NIAF) reformulates action prediction in Vision-Language-Action models from discrete waypoints to continuous action function regression using an MLLM as a hierarchical spectral modulator over a learnable motion prior, achieving state-of-the-art results on CALVIN and LIBERO benchmarks while enabling stable impedance control in real-world experiments.
AI.motivation: The prevailing VLA paradigm of predicting discrete waypoints is misaligned with the intrinsic continuity of physical motion, causing issues such as rigid sampling rates, lack of high-order differentiability, and quantization artifacts that hinder precise, compliant interaction.
AI.method: NIAF reformulates action prediction as continuous action function regression using an MLLM as a hierarchical spectral modulator over a learnable motion prior, synthesizing infinite-resolution trajectories as continuous-time manifolds. This formulation enables analytical differentiability and explicit supervision of velocity, acceleration, and jerk.
AI.result: NIAF achieves state-of-the-art results on CALVIN and LIBERO benchmarks across diverse backbones, and real-world experiments demonstrate that it enables stable impedance control, bridging the gap between high-level semantic understanding and low-level dynamic execution.
AI.conclusion: By shifting from discrete waypoint prediction to continuous action function regression, NIAF addresses fundamental misalignments between VLA models and physical motion, providing a mathematically consistent and physically plausible approach that enables more precise and compliant robot interactions.

title: Toward Graph-Tokenizing Large Language Models with Reconstructive Graph Instruction Tuning
summary: The remarkable success of large language models (LLMs) has motivated researchers to adapt them as universal predictors for various graph-related tasks, with the ultimate goal of developing a graph foundation model that generalizes diverse scenarios. The key challenge is to align graph data with language spaces so that LLMs can better comprehend graphs. As a popular paradigm, Graph-Tokenizing LLMs (GTokenLLMs) encode complex structures and lengthy texts into a graph token sequence, and then align them with text tokens via language instructions tuning. Despite their initial success, our information-theoretic analysis reveals that existing GTokenLLMs rely solely on text supervision from language instructions, which achieve only implicit graph-text alignment, resulting in a text-dominant bias that underutilizes graph context. To overcome this limitation, we first prove that the alignment objective is upper-bounded by the mutual information between the input graphs and their hidden representations in the LLM, which motivates us to improve this upper bound to achieve better alignment. To this end, we further propose a reconstructive graph instruction tuning pipeline, RGLM. Our key idea is to reconstruct the graph information from the LLM's graph token outputs, explicitly incorporating graph supervision to constrain the alignment process. Technically, we embody RGLM by exploring three distinct variants from two complementary perspectives: RGLM-Decoder from the input space; RGLM-Similarizer and RGLM-Denoiser from the latent space. Additionally, we theoretically analyze the alignment effectiveness of each variant. Extensive experiments on various benchmarks and task scenarios validate the effectiveness of the proposed RGLM, paving the way for new directions in GTokenLLMs' alignment research.

AI.tldr: RGLM overcomes text-dominant bias in Graph-Tokenizing LLMs by explicitly incorporating graph supervision to reconstruct graph information from LLM outputs, improving graph-text alignment through three variants: RGLM-Decoder (input space), RGLM-Similarizer, and RGLM-Denoiser (latent space).
AI.motivation: Existing Graph-Tokenizing LLMs (GTokenLLMs) rely solely on text supervision from language instructions, achieving only implicit graph-text alignment. This results in a text-dominant bias that underutilizes graph context. The key challenge is aligning graph data with language spaces so LLMs can better comprehend graphs.
AI.method: The authors propose RGLM (Reconstructive Graph Instruction Tuning), which reconstructs graph information from LLM's graph token outputs to explicitly incorporate graph supervision. Three variants are developed: RGLM-Decoder from the input space, and RGLM-Similarizer and RGLM-Denoiser from the latent space. A theoretical analysis shows the alignment objective is upper-bounded by the mutual information between input graphs and LLM hidden representations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on various benchmarks and task scenarios validate the effectiveness of RGLM, demonstrating that explicitly incorporating graph supervision significantly improves alignment quality compared to existing text-only approaches.
AI.conclusion: RGLM effectively overcomes the text-dominant bias limitation of existing GTokenLLMs by explicitly incorporating graph supervision, providing a new direction for research on graph-text alignment in foundation models.

title: OPGAgent: An Agent for Auditable Dental Panoramic X-ray Interpretation
summary: Orthopantomograms (OPGs) are the standard panoramic radiograph in dentistry, used for full-arch screening across multiple diagnostic tasks. While Vision Language Models (VLMs) now allow multi-task OPG analysis through natural language, they underperform task-specific models on most individual tasks. Agentic systems that orchestrate specialized tools offer a path to both versatility and accuracy, this approach remains unexplored in the field of dental imaging. To address this gap, we propose OPGAgent, a multi-tool agentic system for auditable OPG interpretation. OPGAgent coordinates specialized perception modules with a consensus mechanism through three components: (1) a Hierarchical Evidence Gathering module that decomposes OPG analysis into global, quadrant, and tooth-level phases with dynamically invoking tools, (2) a Specialized Toolbox encapsulating spatial, detection, utility, and expert zoos, and (3) a Consensus Subagent that resolves conflicts through anatomical constraints. We further propose OPG-Bench, a structured-report protocol based on (Location, Field, Value) triples derived from real clinical reports, which enables a comprehensive review of findings and hallucinations, extending beyond the limitations of VQA indicators. On our OPG-Bench and the public MMOral-OPG benchmark, OPGAgent outperforms current dental VLMs and medical agent frameworks across both structured-report and VQA evaluation. Code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: OPGAgent is a multi-tool agentic system for auditable OPG interpretation that coordinates specialized perception modules with a consensus mechanism, outperforming current dental VLMs and medical agent frameworks.
AI.motivation: OPGs are standard panoramic radiographs used for full-arch screening across multiple diagnostic tasks. While Vision Language Models (VLMs) allow multi-task OPG analysis through natural language, they underperform task-specific models on most individual tasks. Agentic systems that orchestrate specialized tools offer a path to both versatility and accuracy, but this approach remains unexplored in dental imaging.
AI.method: OPGAgent coordinates specialized perception modules with a consensus mechanism through three components: (1) Hierarchical Evidence Gathering module decomposing OPG analysis into global, quadrant, and tooth-level phases with dynamically invoking tools, (2) Specialized Toolbox encapsulating spatial, detection, utility, and expert zoos, and (3) Consensus Subagent resolving conflicts through anatomical constraints. OPG-Bench is a structured-report protocol based on (Location, Field, Value) triples derived from real clinical reports.
AI.result: On OPG-Bench and the public MMOral-OPG benchmark, OPGAgent outperforms current dental VLMs and medical agent frameworks across both structured-report and VQA evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Code will be released upon acceptance.

title: Multimodal Mixture-of-Experts with Retrieval Augmentation for Protein Active Site Identification
summary: Accurate identification of protein active sites at the residue level is crucial for understanding protein function and advancing drug discovery. However, current methods face two critical challenges: vulnerability in single-instance prediction due to sparse training data, and inadequate modality reliability estimation that leads to performance degradation when unreliable modalities dominate fusion processes. To address these challenges, we introduce Multimodal Mixture-of-Experts with Retrieval Augmentation (MERA), the first retrieval-augmented framework for protein active site identification. MERA employs hierarchical multi-expert retrieval that dynamically aggregates contextual information from chain, sequence, and active-site perspectives through residue-level mixture-of-experts gating. To prevent modality degradation, we propose a reliability-aware fusion strategy based on Dempster-Shafer evidence theory that quantifies modality trustworthiness through belief mass functions and learnable discounting coefficients, enabling principled multimodal integration. Extensive experiments on ProTAD-Gen and TS125 datasets demonstrate that MERA achieves state-of-the-art performance, with 90% AUPRC on active site prediction and significant gains on peptide-binding site identification, validating the effectiveness of retrieval-augmented multi-expert modeling and reliability-guided fusion.

AI.tldr: MERA introduces the first retrieval-augmented framework for protein active site identification, using hierarchical multi-expert retrieval and a reliability-aware fusion strategy based on Dempster-Shafer evidence theory to address sparse training data and modality reliability challenges.
AI.motivation: Accurate residue-level protein active site identification is crucial for understanding protein function and drug discovery. Current methods face two critical challenges: vulnerability in single-instance prediction due to sparse training data, and inadequate modality reliability estimation that leads to performance degradation when unreliable modalities dominate fusion processes.
AI.method: MERA employs hierarchical multi-expert retrieval that dynamically aggregates contextual information from chain, sequence, and active-site perspectives through residue-level mixture-of-experts gating. To prevent modality degradation, it proposes a reliability-aware fusion strategy based on Dempster-Shafer evidence theory that quantifies modality trustworthiness through belief mass functions and learnable discounting coefficients.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on ProTAD-Gen and TS125 datasets demonstrate that MERA achieves state-of-the-art performance, with 90% AUPRC on active site prediction and significant gains on peptide-binding site identification, validating the effectiveness of retrieval-augmented multi-expert modeling and reliability-guided fusion.
AI.conclusion: The combination of retrieval-augmented multi-expert modeling and reliability-guided fusion provides an effective approach for protein active site identification, successfully addressing the challenges of sparse data and modality reliability in current methods.

title: Frozen Policy Iteration: Computationally Efficient RL under Linear $Q^π$ Realizability for Deterministic Dynamics
summary:
We study computationally and statistically efficient reinforcement learning under the linear $Q^π$ realizability assumption, where any policy's $Q$-function is linear in a given state-action feature representation. Prior methods in this setting are either computationally intractable, or require (local) access to a simulator. In this paper, we propose a computationally efficient online RL algorithm, named Frozen Policy Iteration, under the linear $Q^π$ realizability setting that works for Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) with stochastic initial states, stochastic rewards and deterministic transitions. Our algorithm achieves a regret bound of $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{d^2H^6T})$, where $d$ is the dimensionality of the feature space, $H$ is the horizon length, and $T$ is the total number of episodes. Our regret bound is optimal for linear (contextual) bandits which is a special case of our setting with $H = 1$.
  Existing policy iteration algorithms under the same setting heavily rely on repeatedly sampling the same state by access to the simulator, which is not implementable in the online setting with stochastic initial states studied in this paper. In contrast, our new algorithm circumvents this limitation by strategically using only high-confidence part of the trajectory data and freezing the policy for well-explored states, which ensures that all data used by our algorithm remains effectively on-policy during the whole course of learning. We further demonstrate the versatility of our approach by extending it to the Uniform-PAC setting and to function classes with bounded eluder dimension.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Frozen Policy Iteration, a computationally efficient online RL algorithm under linear $Q^π$ realizability that achieves optimal regret bounds $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{d^2H^6T})$ for MDPs with stochastic initial states and rewards, without requiring simulator access.
AI.motivation: Prior methods are either computationally intractable or require simulator access. Existing policy iteration algorithms rely on repeatedly sampling the same state, which is not implementable in online settings with stochastic initial states and rewards.
AI.method: The algorithm strategically uses only high-confidence trajectory data and freezes the policy for well-explored states, ensuring all data remains effectively on-policy. Extensions include Uniform-PAC setting and function classes with bounded eluder dimension.
AI.result: Achieves regret bound $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{d^2H^6T})$, which is optimal for linear (contextual) bandits (H=1 case), and works for MDPs with stochastic initial states, stochastic rewards, and deterministic transitions.
AI.conclusion: Frozen Policy Iteration circumvents simulator limitations and provides a versatile approach that can be extended to Uniform-PAC and function classes with bounded eluder dimension, offering an efficient online learning solution under the linear $Q^π$ realizability assumption.

title: Quantifying Conversational Reliability of Large Language Models under Multi-Turn Interaction
comment: Accepted at the Workshop on Assessing and Improving Reliability of Foundation Models in the Real World (AAAI 2026)
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in real-world applications where users engage in extended, mixed-topic conversations that depend on prior context. Yet, their reliability under realistic multi-turn interactions remains poorly understood. We conduct a systematic evaluation of conversational reliability through three representative tasks that reflect practical interaction challenges: (1) maintaining global constraints across topic shifts, (2) selecting the correct tool or agent amid interleaved intents, and (3) tracking structured entities under revisions and distractions. Each task pairs single-turn and multi-turn settings, allowing us to quantify reliability degradation under extended dialogue. Across both commercial and open-source models, we observe substantial declines in reliability, particularly for smaller models. Error analyses reveal recurring failure modes such as instruction drift, intent confusion, and contextual overwriting, which compromise dependable behavior in operational systems. Our findings highlight the need for stress-testing LLMs for conversational reliability and developing more robust evaluation methods for trustworthy deployment.

AI.tldr: LLMs show significant reliability degradation in multi-turn conversations, particularly for smaller models, due to failure modes like instruction drift, intent confusion, and contextual overwriting.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly deployed in real-world applications with extended, mixed-topic conversations, but their reliability under realistic multi-turn interactions remains poorly understood, creating deployment risks.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of conversational reliability across three representative tasks (maintaining global constraints across topic shifts, selecting correct tools/agents amid interleaved intents, tracking structured entities under revisions and distractions), with each task evaluated in both single-turn and multi-turn settings using both commercial and open-source models.
AI.result: Substantial reliability declines observed across both commercial and open-source models, particularly for smaller models. Error analyses identified recurring failure modes including instruction drift, intent confusion, and contextual overwriting.
AI.conclusion: Findings highlight the critical need for stress-testing LLMs specifically for conversational reliability and developing more robust evaluation methods to ensure trustworthy deployment in operational systems.

title: DreamWorld: Unified World Modeling in Video Generation
summary: Despite impressive progress in video generation, existing models remain limited to surface-level plausibility, lacking a coherent and unified understanding of the world. Prior approaches typically incorporate only a single form of world-related knowledge or rely on rigid alignment strategies to introduce additional knowledge. However, aligning the single world knowledge is insufficient to constitute a world model that requires jointly modeling multiple heterogeneous dimensions (e.g., physical commonsense, 3D and temporal consistency). To address this limitation, we introduce \textbf{DreamWorld}, a unified framework that integrates complementary world knowledge into video generators via a \textbf{Joint World Modeling Paradigm}, jointly predicting video pixels and features from foundation models to capture temporal dynamics, spatial geometry, and semantic consistency. However, naively optimizing these heterogeneous objectives can lead to visual instability and temporal flickering. To mitigate this issue, we propose \textit{Consistent Constraint Annealing (CCA)} to progressively regulate world-level constraints during training, and \textit{Multi-Source Inner-Guidance} to enforce learned world priors at inference. Extensive evaluations show that DreamWorld improves world consistency, outperforming Wan2.1 by 2.26 points on VBench. Code will be made publicly available at \href{https://github.com/ABU121111/DreamWorld}{\textcolor{mypink}{\textbf{Github}}}.

AI.tldr: DreamWorld is a unified framework that integrates complementary world knowledge into video generators via a Joint World Modeling Paradigm, using Consistent Constraint Annealing (CCA) and Multi-Source Inner-Guidance to improve world consistency and reduce temporal flickering.
AI.motivation: Existing video generation models lack coherent understanding of the world, typically incorporating only a single form of world knowledge or relying on rigid alignment strategies. A true world model requires jointly modeling multiple heterogeneous dimensions (physical commonsense, 3D and temporal consistency), which single-knowledge approaches cannot achieve.
AI.method: DreamWorld introduces a Joint World Modeling Paradigm that jointly predicts video pixels and features from foundation models to capture temporal dynamics, spatial geometry, and semantic consistency. To mitigate visual instability and temporal flickering, it proposes Consistent Constraint Annealing (CCA) for progressive regulation of world-level constraints during training, and Multi-Source Inner-Guidance for enforcing learned world priors at inference.
AI.result: DreamWorld improves world consistency, outperforming Wan2.1 by 2.26 points on VBench evaluation.
AI.conclusion: By integrating complementary world knowledge through a unified framework with advanced training and inference techniques, DreamWorld successfully addresses the limitations of existing approaches and establishes a more coherent and unified understanding of the world in video generation.

title: MARS: Harmonizing Multimodal Convergence via Adaptive Rank Search
comment: 17 pages; Project Page: this https URL: https://minkyoungcho.github.io/mars/
summary: Fine-tuning Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) with parameter-efficient methods like Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is crucial for task adaptation. However, imbalanced training dynamics across modalities often lead to suboptimal accuracy due to negative interference, a challenge typically addressed with inefficient heuristic methods such as manually tuning separate learning rates. To overcome this, we introduce MARS (Multimodal Adaptive Rank Search), an approach to discover optimal rank pairs that balance training dynamics while maximizing performance. Our key innovation, a proposed framework of dual scaling laws, enables this search: one law models module-specific convergence time to prune the search space to candidates with aligned dynamics, while the other predicts final task performance to select the optimal pair from the pruned set. By re-purposing the LoRA rank as a controller for modality-specific convergence speed, MARS outperforms baseline methods and provides a robust, automated strategy for optimizing MLLM fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: MARS (Multimodal Adaptive Rank Search) is a method that discovers optimal rank pairs for parameter-efficient fine-tuning of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) using dual scaling laws to balance training dynamics and maximize performance.
AI.motivation: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning of MLLMs with LoRA is crucial for task adaptation, but imbalanced training dynamics across modalities lead to suboptimal accuracy due to negative interference. Existing solutions use inefficient heuristic methods like manually tuning separate learning rates.
AI.method: MARS introduces a framework of dual scaling laws: (1) one law models module-specific convergence time to prune the search space to candidates with aligned dynamics, and (2) the other predicts final task performance to select the optimal pair. The LoRA rank is repurposed as a controller for modality-specific convergence speed.
AI.result: MARS outperforms baseline methods and provides a robust, automated strategy for optimizing MLLM fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: MARS enables automated discovery of optimal rank pairs that balance training dynamics, overcoming the limitations of manual tuning approaches and improving fine-tuning performance for MLLMs.

title: LaSER: Internalizing Explicit Reasoning into Latent Space for Dense Retrieval
summary: LLMs have fundamentally transformed dense retrieval, upgrading backbones from discriminative encoders to generative architectures. However, a critical disconnect remains: while LLMs possess strong reasoning capabilities, current retrievers predominantly utilize them as static encoders, leaving their potential for complex reasoning unexplored. To address this, existing approaches typically adopt rewrite-then-retrieve pipelines to generate explicit CoT rationales before retrieval. However, this incurs prohibitive latency. In this paper, we propose LaSER, a novel self-distillation framework that internalizes explicit reasoning into the latent space of dense retrievers. Operating on a shared LLM backbone, LaSER introduces a dual-view training mechanism: an Explicit view that explicitly encodes ground-truth reasoning paths, and a Latent view that performs implicit latent thinking. To bridge the gap between these views, we design a multi-grained alignment strategy. Beyond standard output alignment, we introduce a trajectory alignment mechanism that synchronizes the intermediate latent states of the latent path with the semantic progression of the explicit reasoning segments. This allows the retriever to think silently and effectively without autoregressive text generation. Extensive experiments on both in-domain and out-of-domain reasoning-intensive benchmarks demonstrate that LaSER significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. Furthermore, analyses across diverse backbones and model scales validate the robustness of our approach, confirming that our unified learning framework is essential for eliciting effective latent thinking. Our method successfully combines the reasoning depth of explicit CoT pipelines with the inference efficiency of standard dense retrievers.

AI.tldr: LaSER is a self-distillation framework that enables LLM-based dense retrievers to perform complex reasoning in latent space without explicit autoregressive text generation, achieving superior performance over state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.motivation: While LLMs possess strong reasoning capabilities, current retrievers predominantly utilize them as static encoders, leaving their potential for complex reasoning unexplored. Existing approaches that adopt rewrite-then-retrieve pipelines to generate explicit Chain-of-Thought rationales before retrieval incur prohibitive latency.
AI.method: LaSER introduces a dual-view training mechanism operating on a shared LLM backbone: an Explicit view that encodes ground-truth reasoning paths, and a Latent view that performs implicit latent thinking. To bridge these views, it employs a multi-grained alignment strategy with a novel trajectory alignment mechanism that synchronizes intermediate latent states of the latent path with the semantic progression of explicit reasoning segments.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on both in-domain and out-of-domain reasoning-intensive benchmarks demonstrate that LaSER significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. Analyses across diverse backbones and model scales validate the robustness of the approach.
AI.conclusion: LaSER successfully combines the reasoning depth of explicit CoT pipelines with the inference efficiency of standard dense retrievers. The unified learning framework is essential for eliciting effective latent thinking, validated across diverse backbones and model scales.

title: High Dynamic Range Imaging Based on an Asymmetric Event-SVE Camera System
comment: This paper has been accepted by Optics Express
summary: High dynamic range (HDR) imaging under extreme illumination remains challenging for conventional cameras due to overexposure. Event cameras provide microsecond temporal resolution and high dynamic range, while spatially varying exposure (SVE) sensors offer single-shot radiometric diversity.We present a hardware--algorithm co-designed HDR imaging system that tightly integrates an SVE micro-attenuation camera with an event sensor in an asymmetric dual-modality configuration. To handle non-coaxial geometry and heterogeneous optics, we develop a two-stage cross-modal alignment framework that combines feature-guided coarse homography estimation with a multi-scale refinement module based on spatial pooling and frequency-domain filtering. On top of aligned representations, we develop a cross-modal HDR reconstruction network with convolutional fusion, mutual-information regularization, and a learnable fusion loss that adaptively balances intensity cues and event-derived structural constraints. Comprehensive experiments on both synthetic benchmarks and real captures demonstrate that the proposed system consistently improves highlight recovery, edge fidelity, and robustness compared with frame-only or event-only HDR pipelines. The results indicate that jointly optimizing optical design, cross-modal alignment, and computational fusion provides an effective foundation for reliable HDR perception in highly dynamic and radiometrically challenging environments.

AI.tldr: A hardware-algorithm co-designed HDR imaging system that integrates a spatially varying exposure (SVE) micro-attenuation camera with an event sensor, employing two-stage cross-modal alignment and a convolutional fusion network for superior HDR reconstruction under extreme illumination.
AI.motivation: HDR imaging under extreme illumination is challenging for conventional cameras due to overexposure. Event cameras offer high dynamic range and microsecond temporal resolution but lack spatial resolution, while SVE sensors provide single-shot radiometric diversity but require temporal integration. The motivation is to combine these complementary modalities to overcome individual limitations.
AI.method: 1. Hardware co-design: asymmetric dual-modality configuration integrating SVE micro-attenuation camera with event sensor. 2. Two-stage cross-modal alignment: feature-guided coarse homography estimation + multi-scale refinement using spatial pooling and frequency-domain filtering. 3. Cross-modal HDR reconstruction network with convolutional fusion, mutual-information regularization, and learnable fusion loss for adaptive balancing of intensity cues and event-derived structural constraints.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments on synthetic benchmarks and real captures demonstrate consistent improvements in highlight recovery, edge fidelity, and robustness compared to frame-only or event-only HDR pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Jointly optimizing optical design, cross-modal alignment, and computational fusion provides an effective foundation for reliable HDR perception in highly dynamic and radiometrically challenging environments.

title: Graph-Based Self-Healing Tool Routing for Cost-Efficient LLM Agents
comment: Working paper. 27 references, 13 figures, 8 tables, pseudocode appendix
summary: Tool-using LLM agents face a reliability-cost tradeoff: routing every decision through the LLM improves correctness but incurs high latency and inference cost, while pre-coded workflow graphs reduce cost but become brittle under unanticipated compound tool failures. We present Self-Healing Router, a fault-tolerant orchestration architecture that treats most agent control-flow decisions as routing rather than reasoning. The system combines (i) parallel health monitors that assign priority scores to runtime conditions such as tool outages and risk signals, and (ii) a cost-weighted tool graph where Dijkstra's algorithm performs deterministic shortest-path routing. When a tool fails mid-execution, its edges are reweighted to infinity and the path is recomputed -- yielding automatic recovery without invoking the LLM. The LLM is reserved exclusively for cases where no feasible path exists, enabling goal demotion or escalation. Prior graph-based tool-use systems (ControlLLM, ToolNet, NaviAgent) focus on tool selection and planning; our contribution is runtime fault tolerance with deterministic recovery and binary observability -- every failure is either a logged reroute or an explicit escalation, never a silent skip. Across 19 scenarios spanning three graph topologies (linear pipeline, dependency DAG, parallel fan-out), Self-Healing Router matches ReAct's correctness while reducing control-plane LLM calls by 93% (9 vs 123 aggregate) and eliminating the silent-failure cases observed in a well-engineered static workflow baseline under compound failures.

AI.tldr: Self-Healing Router is a fault-tolerant orchestration architecture for tool-using LLM agents that routes most control decisions deterministically via Dijkstra's algorithm on a cost-weighted tool graph, reserving the LLM only for cases requiring goal demotion or escalation. It achieves 93% reduction in LLM calls while matching ReAct's correctness and eliminating silent failures.
AI.motivation: Tool-using LLM agents face a reliability-cost tradeoff: LLM-based routing improves correctness but incurs high latency and inference cost, while pre-coded workflows reduce cost but become brittle under unanticipated compound tool failures. Existing graph-based systems focus on tool selection/planning but lack runtime fault tolerance with deterministic recovery.
AI.method: The system combines (i) parallel health monitors that assign priority scores to runtime conditions (tool outages, risk signals), and (ii) a cost-weighted tool graph where Dijkstra's algorithm performs deterministic shortest-path routing. When a tool fails mid-execution, its edges are reweighted to infinity and the path is recomputed without invoking the LLM. The LLM is reserved exclusively for cases where no feasible path exists, enabling goal demotion or escalation.
AI.result: Across 19 scenarios spanning three graph topologies (linear pipeline, dependency DAG, parallel fan-out), Self-Healing Router matches ReAct's correctness while reducing control-plane LLM calls by 93% (9 vs 123 aggregate) and eliminating silent-failure cases observed in a well-engineered static workflow baseline under compound failures.
AI.conclusion: Self-Healing Router demonstrates that treating most agent control-flow decisions as routing rather than reasoning enables fault-tolerant orchestration with deterministic recovery and binary observability. The approach significantly reduces LLM inference costs while maintaining performance and eliminating silent failures, offering a practical solution to the reliability-cost tradeoff in tool-using LLM agents.

title: To Use or not to Use Muon: How Simplicity Bias in Optimizers Matters
summary: For a long period of time, Adam has served as the ubiquitous default choice for training deep neural networks. Recently, many new optimizers have been introduced, out of which Muon has perhaps gained the highest popularity due to its superior training speed. While many papers set out to validate the benefits of Muon, our paper investigates the potential downsides stemming from the mechanism driving this speedup. We explore the biases induced when optimizing with Muon, providing theoretical analysis and its consequences to the learning trajectories and solutions learned. While the theory does provide justification for the benefits Muon brings, it also guides our intuition when coming up with a couple of examples where Muon-optimized models have disadvantages. The core problem we emphasize is that Muon optimization removes a simplicity bias that is naturally preserved by older, more thoroughly studied methods like Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD). We take first steps toward understanding consequences this may have: Muon might struggle to uncover common underlying structure across tasks, and be more prone to fitting spurious features. More broadly, this paper should serve as a reminder: when developing new optimizers, it is essential to consider the biases they introduce, as these biases can fundamentally change a model's behavior -- for better or for worse.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the biases introduced by Muon optimizer, a fast alternative to Adam, revealing that it removes a "simplicity bias" inherent in SGD, potentially causing models to struggle with common underlying structures and be more prone to fitting spurious features.
AI.motivation: Adam has been the default optimizer for deep neural networks, but Muon has gained popularity due to its superior training speed. While many papers validate Muon's benefits, this paper aims to investigate potential downsides stemming from the mechanism driving its speedup.
AI.method: The paper provides theoretical analysis of biases induced by Muon optimization, examining consequences on learning trajectories and the solutions learned by the model.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis reveals that Muon optimization removes a "simplicity bias" naturally preserved by older methods like Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD). This can cause Muon to struggle with uncovering common underlying structure across tasks and be more prone to fitting spurious features.
AI.conclusion: When developing new optimizers, it is essential to consider the biases they introduce, as these biases can fundamentally change a model's behavior—whether improving or degrading performance. Muon's speed benefits come with trade-offs that must be carefully considered.

title: SaferPath: Hierarchical Visual Navigation with Learned Guidance and Safety-Constrained Control
summary: Visual navigation is a core capability for mobile robots, yet end-to-end learning-based methods often struggle with generalization and safety in unseen, cluttered, or narrow environments. These limitations are especially pronounced in dense indoor settings, where collisions are likely and end-to-end models frequently fail. To address this, we propose SaferPath, a hierarchical visual navigation framework that leverages learned guidance from existing end-to-end models and refines it through a safety-constrained optimization-control module. SaferPath transforms visual observations into a traversable-area map and refines guidance trajectories using Model Predictive Stein Variational Evolution Strategy (MP-SVES), efficiently generating safe trajectories in only a few iterations. The refined trajectories are tracked by an MPC controller, ensuring robust navigation in complex environments. Extensive experiments in scenarios with unseen obstacles, dense unstructured spaces, and narrow corridors demonstrate that SaferPath consistently improves success rates and reduces collisions, outperforming representative baselines such as ViNT and NoMaD, and enabling safe navigation in challenging real-world settings.

AI.tldr: SaferPath is a hierarchical visual navigation framework that enhances end-to-end models with safety-constrained optimization using Model Predictive Stein Variational Evolution Strategy (MP-SVES) and Model Predictive Control (MPC), achieving improved success rates and reduced collisions in challenging indoor environments.
AI.motivation: End-to-end learning-based visual navigation methods struggle with generalization and safety in unseen, cluttered, or narrow environments, particularly in dense indoor settings where collisions are likely and models frequently fail.
AI.method: SaferPath integrates learned guidance from existing end-to-end models with a safety-constrained optimization-control module. It transforms visual observations into traversable-area maps and refines guidance trajectories using Model Predictive Stein Variational Evolution Strategy (MP-SVES), with refined trajectories tracked by an MPC controller.
AI.result: Extensive experiments in scenarios with unseen obstacles, dense unstructured spaces, and narrow corridors demonstrate that SaferPath consistently improves success rates and reduces collisions, outperforming baselines like ViNT and NoMaD.
AI.conclusion: SaferPath enables safe navigation in challenging real-world settings by effectively combining learned visual guidance with robust safety constraints through hierarchical optimization and control.

title: Understanding the Physics of Key-Value Cache Compression for LLMs through Attention Dynamics
summary: As context windows in LLMs scale to 100K+ tokens, the key-value (KV) cache becomes the dominant memory bottleneck, with recent methods claiming 80-90% savings and minimal benchmark degradation. We argue these evaluations miss a structural issue: attention is not just storage but routing, and retaining KV pairs does not guarantee semantic accessibility. We propose a physics-inspired view of KV compression as a controlled perturbation of token-level routing, distinguishing retention, accessibility, and utilization. Using synthetic tasks probing multi-entity tracking, disambiguation, coreference, and multi-hop reasoning, we find that moderate compression degrades internal representations with little accuracy loss, revealing redundancy; all models exhibit a sharp hallucination safety cliff near 90% compression, correlated with spikes in Global Eviction Ratio (GER), suggesting a phase transition in semantic reachability; and architectures differ in routing dynamics, with LLaMA showing early consensus and late diversification, and Qwen showing funnel-like late convergence, leading to distinct resilience profiles. Beyond erasure, we identify representational rigidity, where excessive head-level consensus collapses routing flexibility despite token survival. These results suggest sparse token-route structures govern compression tolerance, reframing KV compression as a structural probe of attention geometry and linking long-context scalability to sparsity and the lottery ticket hypothesis in self-attention.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that KV cache compression evaluations miss the structural role of attention as routing, proposing a physics-inspired framework that distinguishes retention, accessibility, and utilization. Through synthetic tasks probing multi-entity tracking and coreference, the authors find that moderate compression reveals redundancy, while near 90% compression triggers a sharp hallucination safety cliff correlated with a phase transition in semantic reachability, and architectures differ in routing dynamics leading to distinct resilience profiles.
AI.motivation: As context windows scale to 100K+ tokens, KV cache compression methods claiming 80-90% memory savings with minimal benchmark degradation are being adopted, but the authors argue these evaluations miss the fundamental structural role of attention as routing rather than just storage, and that retaining KV pairs doesn't guarantee semantic accessibility.
AI.method: The authors propose a physics-inspired view of KV compression as controlled perturbation of token-level routing, distinguishing retention, accessibility, and utilization. They use synthetic tasks probing multi-entity tracking, disambiguation, coreference, and multi-hop reasoning to evaluate compression effects. They measure the Global Eviction Ratio (GER) and examine representational rigidity across architectures.
AI.result: Moderate compression reveals redundancy in internal representations with little accuracy loss, but models exhibit a sharp hallucination safety cliff near 90% compression correlated with spikes in GER, indicating a phase transition in semantic reachability. Different architectures show distinct routing dynamics - LLaMA shows early consensus and late diversification, while Qwen shows funnel-like late convergence.
AI.conclusion: Sparse token-route structures govern compression tolerance, reframing KV compression as a structural probe of attention geometry rather than just memory optimization, linking long-context scalability to sparsity and the lottery ticket hypothesis in self-attention.

title: U-VLM: Hierarchical Vision Language Modeling for Report Generation
summary: Automated radiology report generation is key for reducing radiologist workload and improving diagnostic consistency, yet generating accurate reports for 3D medical imaging remains challenging. Existing vision-language models face two limitations: they do not leverage segmentation-pretrained encoders, and they inject visual features only at the input layer of language models, losing multi-scale information. We propose U-VLM, which enables hierarchical vision-language modeling in both training and architecture: (1) progressive training from segmentation to classification to report generation, and (2) multi-layer visual injection that routes U-Net encoder features to corresponding language model layers. Each training stage can leverage different datasets without unified annotations. U-VLM achieves state-of-the-art performance on CT-RATE (F1: 0.414 vs 0.258, BLEU-mean: 0.349 vs 0.305) and AbdomenAtlas 3.0 (F1: 0.624 vs 0.518 for segmentation-based detection) using only a 0.1B decoder trained from scratch, demonstrating that well-designed vision encoder pretraining outweighs the benefits of 7B+ pre-trained language models. Ablation studies show that progressive pretraining significantly improves F1, while multi-layer injection improves BLEU-mean. Code is available at https://github.com/yinghemedical/U-VLM.

AI.tldr: U-VLM is a hierarchical vision-language model for automated radiology report generation from 3D medical imaging that employs progressive training and multi-layer visual injection, achieving state-of-the-art performance with a small decoder while outperforming large pre-trained language models.
AI.motivation: Automated radiology report generation reduces radiologist workload and improves diagnostic consistency, but existing vision-language models face two limitations: (1) they do not leverage segmentation-pretrained encoders, and (2) they inject visual features only at the input layer of language models, losing multi-scale information.
AI.method: U-VLM proposes two key innovations: (1) progressive training from segmentation to classification to report generation, enabling each stage to leverage different datasets without unified annotations; (2) multi-layer visual injection that routes U-Net encoder features to corresponding language model layers, enabling hierarchical vision-language modeling in both training and architecture.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance achieved on CT-RATE (F1: 0.414 vs 0.258, BLEU-mean: 0.349 vs 0.305) and AbdomenAtlas 3.0 (F1: 0.624 vs 0.518 for segmentation-based detection). The model uses only a 0.1B decoder trained from scratch and outperforms 7B+ pre-trained language models. Ablation studies confirm that progressive pretraining significantly improves F1, while multi-layer injection improves BLEU-mean.
AI.conclusion: Well-designed vision encoder pretraining outweighs the benefits of large pre-trained language models. The hierarchical approach with progressive training and multi-layer visual injection is more effective for 3D medical imaging report generation than simply using larger pre-trained language models.

title: State-Action Inpainting Diffuser for Continuous Control with Delay
summary: Signal delay poses a fundamental challenge in continuous control and reinforcement learning (RL) by introducing a temporal gap between interaction and perception. Current solutions have largely evolved along two distinct paradigms: model-free approaches which utilize state augmentation to preserve Markovian properties, and model-based methods which focus on inferring latent beliefs via dynamics modeling. In this paper, we bridge these perspectives by introducing State-Action Inpainting Diffuser (SAID), a framework that integrates the inductive bias of dynamics learning with the direct decision-making capability of policy optimization. By formulating the problem as a joint sequence inpainting task, SAID implicitly captures environmental dynamics while directly generating consistent plans, effectively operating at the intersection of model-based and model-free paradigms. Crucially, this generative formulation allows SAID to be seamlessly applied to both online and offline RL. Extensive experiments on delayed continuous control benchmarks demonstrate that SAID achieves state-of-the-art and robust performance. Our study suggests a new methodology to advance the field of RL with delay.

AI.tldr: State-Action Inpainting Diffuser (SAID) is a novel framework that combines model-free and model-based approaches to handle signal delay in reinforcement learning by formulating the problem as a joint sequence inpainting task.
AI.motivation: Signal delay introduces a temporal gap between interaction and perception, creating a fundamental challenge in continuous control and reinforcement learning. Current solutions are divided into two paradigms: model-free approaches that use state augmentation to preserve Markovian properties, and model-based methods that focus on inferring latent beliefs through dynamics modeling.
AI.method: SAID integrates the inductive bias of dynamics learning with the direct decision-making capability of policy optimization. It formulates the signal delay problem as a joint sequence inpainting task, allowing the model to implicitly capture environmental dynamics while directly generating consistent plans. This generative formulation enables seamless application to both online and offline RL.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on delayed continuous control benchmarks demonstrate that SAID achieves state-of-the-art and robust performance.
AI.conclusion: This study presents a new methodology for advancing the field of reinforcement learning with signal delay, effectively bridging the gap between model-based and model-free paradigms.

title: ResGene-T: A Tensor-Based Residual Network Approach for Genomic Prediction
comment: Double column 11 Pages, 6 Figure, and 8 Tables
summary:
In this work, we propose a new deep learning model for Genomic Prediction (GP), which involves correlating genotypic data with phenotypic. The genotypes are typically fed as a sequence of characters to the 1D-Convolution Neural Network layer of the underlying deep learning model. Inspired by earlier work that represented genotype as a 2D-image for genotype-phenotype classification, we extend this idea to GP, which is a regression task. We use a ResNet-18 as the underlying architecture, and term this model as ResGene-2D.
  Although the 2D-image representation captures biological interactions well, it requires all the layers of the model to do so. This limits training efficiency. Thus, as seen in the earlier work that proposed a 2D-image representation, our ResGene-2D performs almost the same as other models (3% improvement). To overcome this, we propose a novel idea of converting the 2D-image into a 3D/ tensor and feed this to the ResNet-18 architecture, and term this model as ResGene-T.
  We evaluate our proposed models on three crop species having ten phenotypic traits and compare it with seven most popular models (two statistical, two machine learning, and three deep learning). ResGene-T performs the best among all these seven methods (gains from 14.51% to 41.51%).

AI.tldr: This paper proposes two deep learning models for genomic prediction: ResGene-2D (using ResNet-18 with 2D-image representation of genotypes) and ResGene-T (extending the approach by converting genotypes to a 3D tensor representation). ResGene-2D achieved only marginal improvement (3%), while ResGene-T outperformed seven baseline models across three crop species with gains of 14.51% to 41.51%.
AI.motivation: The authors sought to extend the successful 2D-image representation of genotypes from genotype-phenotype classification tasks to genomic prediction (regression). However, they discovered that the 2D-image representation requires all model layers to capture biological interactions, which limits training efficiency. Their ResGene-2D model performed only marginally better (3% improvement) compared to other models, motivating the development of a more efficient approach.
AI.method: ResGene-2D uses ResNet-18 architecture with genotypes fed as a sequence of characters to 1D-Convolution Neural Network layer, extended to 2D-image representation. ResGene-T converts the 2D-image into a 3D tensor representation and feeds it to ResNet-18.
