Both models were evaluated on three crop species with ten phenotypic traits and compared against seven baseline models (2 statistical, 2 machine learning, 3 deep learning).
AI.result: ResGene-2D achieved only a 3% improvement over other models. In contrast, ResGene-T significantly outperformed all seven baseline methods, with performance gains ranging from 14.51% to 41.51%. The 3D tensor representation improved both accuracy and training efficiency by better capturing biological interactions without requiring all model layers to process the representation.
AI.conclusion: The 3D tensor representation approach in ResGene-T provides a more efficient and effective method for genomic prediction than traditional 2D-image representations. By converting genotypes to 3D tensors before feeding them to ResNet-18, the model achieves substantial performance improvements (14.51%-41.51% gains) while maintaining training efficiency, demonstrating the value of innovative data representation techniques for computational biology applications.

title: Closed-Loop Action Chunks with Dynamic Corrections for Training-Free Diffusion Policy
summary: Diffusion-based policies have achieved remarkable results in robotic manipulation but often struggle to adapt rapidly in dynamic scenarios, leading to delayed responses or task failures. We present DCDP, a Dynamic Closed-Loop Diffusion Policy framework that integrates chunk-based action generation with real-time correction. DCDP integrates a self-supervised dynamic feature encoder, cross-attention fusion, and an asymmetric action encoder-decoder to inject environmental dynamics before action execution, achieving real-time closed-loop action correction and enhancing the system's adaptability in dynamic scenarios. In dynamic PushT simulations, DCDP improves adaptability by 19\% without retraining while requiring only 5\% additional computation. Its modular design enables plug-and-play integration, achieving both temporal coherence and real-time responsiveness in dynamic robotic scenarios, including real-world manipulation tasks. The project page is at: https://github.com/wupengyuan/dcdp

AI.tldr: DCDP (Dynamic Closed-Loop Diffusion Policy) is a framework that enhances robotic manipulation in dynamic scenarios by integrating chunk-based action generation with real-time correction through a self-supervised dynamic feature encoder, cross-attention fusion, and an asymmetric action encoder-decoder.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based policies have achieved remarkable results in robotic manipulation but struggle to adapt rapidly in dynamic scenarios, leading to delayed responses or task failures.
AI.method: DCDP integrates chunk-based action generation with real-time correction using three key components: (1) a self-supervised dynamic feature encoder, (2) cross-attention fusion, and (3) an asymmetric action encoder-decoder. This architecture injects environmental dynamics before action execution to enable closed-loop correction.
AI.result: In dynamic PushT simulations, DCDP improves adaptability by 19% without retraining while requiring only 5% additional computation. The modular design enables plug-and-play integration for both temporal coherence and real-time responsiveness.
AI.conclusion: The framework achieves real-time closed-loop action correction and enhances system adaptability in dynamic scenarios, with demonstrated effectiveness in both simulation and real-world manipulation tasks.

title: Enhancing Persona Following at Decoding Time via Dynamic Importance Estimation for Role-Playing Agents
summary: The utility of Role-Playing Language Agents in sociological research is growing alongside the adoption of Large Language Models. For realism in social simulation, these agents must adhere to their personas defined by character profiles, yet existing strategies-static prompt engineering or costly fine-tuning-fail to adapt personas to dynamic scenarios. Psychological theories, such as the Cognitive-Affective Personality Systems, provide a crucial explanation for this failure: a persona's influence on behavior is not static but varies with the scenarios. This context-dependence highlights the critical need for adaptive persona management. To address this gap, we propose a novel, theory-driven method that dynamically estimates context-dependent persona importance and integrates it into weighted reward-guided decoding, enabling inference-time persona following. Specifically, we introduce the Persona Dynamic Decoding (PDD) framework, which consists of two key components: (1) Persona Importance Estimation (PIE) module, which dynamically quantifies the contextual importance of persona attributes without requiring ground-truth supervision; and (2) Persona-Guided Inference-Time Alignment (PIA) paradigm, which leverages these importance scores to construct weighted multi-objective rewards and modulate generation probabilities during inference. Extensive experiments show the effectiveness of our method in utterance consistency and behavioral fidelity.

AI.tldr: Persona Dynamic Decoding (PDD) is a theory-driven framework that dynamically estimates context-dependent persona importance via a Persona Importance Estimation (PIE) module and uses it for weighted reward-guided decoding to improve persona adherence in role-playing language agents.
AI.motivation: Role-playing language agents must adhere to dynamic personas, but static prompt engineering or costly fine-tuning fails to adapt personas to varying scenarios. Psychological theories (Cognitive-Affective Personality Systems) demonstrate that persona influence is context-dependent, not static. This necessitates adaptive persona management to maintain realism in social simulation.
AI.method: PDD framework comprising (1) Persona Importance Estimation (PIE) module that dynamically quantifies contextual importance of persona attributes without ground-truth supervision, and (2) Persona-Guided Inference-Time Alignment (PIA) paradigm that constructs weighted multi-objective rewards and modulates generation probabilities during inference using the importance scores.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate the method's effectiveness in improving utterance consistency and behavioral fidelity in role-playing scenarios.
AI.conclusion: PDD successfully addresses the challenge of context-dependent persona management, enabling language agents to follow personas more effectively in dynamic scenarios without requiring ground-truth supervision or expensive fine-tuning.

title: TokenCom: Vision-Language Model for Multimodal and Multitask Token Communications
summary: Visual-Language Models (VLMs), with their strong capabilities in image and text understanding, offer a solid foundation for intelligent communications. However, their effectiveness is constrained by limited token granularity, overlong visual token sequences, and inadequate cross-modal alignment. To overcome these challenges, we propose TaiChi, a novel VLM framework designed for token communications. TaiChi adopts a dual-visual tokenizer architecture that processes both high- and low-resolution images to collaboratively capture pixel-level details and global conceptual features. A Bilateral Attention Network (BAN) is introduced to intelligently fuse multi-scale visual tokens, thereby enhancing visual understanding and producing compact visual tokens. In addition, a Kolmogorov Arnold Network (KAN)-based modality projector with learnable activation functions is employed to achieve precise nonlinear alignment from visual features to the text semantic space, thus minimizing information loss. Finally, TaiChi is integrated into a multimodal and multitask token communication system equipped with a joint VLM-channel coding scheme. Experimental results validate the superior performance of TaiChi, as well as the feasibility and effectiveness of the TaiChi-driven token communication system.

AI.tldr: TaiChi is a novel VLM framework for token communications that employs a dual-visual tokenizer, bilateral attention network, and KAN-based modality projector to address token granularity limitations, overlong sequences, and cross-modal alignment challenges in intelligent communications.
AI.motivation: VLMs possess strong image and text understanding capabilities but are constrained by limited token granularity, overlong visual token sequences, and inadequate cross-modal alignment, which restrict their effectiveness in intelligent communication applications.
AI.method: TaiChi proposes a dual-visual tokenizer architecture processing both high- and low-resolution images, a Bilateral Attention Network (BAN) for multi-scale visual token fusion, and a Kolmogorov Arnold Network (KAN)-based modality projector with learnable activation functions. The framework is integrated into a multimodal and multitask token communication system with a joint VLM-channel coding scheme.
AI.result: Experimental results validate the superior performance of TaiChi and demonstrate the feasibility and effectiveness of the TaiChi-driven token communication system.
AI.conclusion: TaiChi effectively addresses the key limitations of traditional VLMs in token communications through its innovative architecture, demonstrating both theoretical improvements and practical utility in multimodal communication scenarios.

title: S5-HES Agent: Society 5.0-driven Agentic Framework to Democratize Smart Home Environment Simulation
summary: The smart home is a key domain within the Society 5.0 vision for a human-centered society. Smart home technologies rapidly evolve, and research should diversify while remaining aligned with Society 5.0 objectives. Democratizing smart home research would engage a broader community of innovators beyond traditional limited experts. This shift necessitates inclusive simulation frameworks that support research across diverse fields in industry and academia. However, existing smart home simulators require significant technical expertise, offer limited adaptability, and lack automated evolution, thereby failing to meet the holistic needs of Society 5.0. These constraints impede researchers from efficiently conducting simulations and experiments for security, energy, health, climate, and socio-economic research. To address these challenges, this paper presents the Society 5.0-driven Smart Home Environment Simulator Agent (S5-HES Agent), an agentic simulation framework that transforms traditional smart home simulation through autonomous AI orchestration. The framework coordinates specialized agents through interchangeable large language models (LLMs), enabling natural-language-driven end-to-end smart home simulation configuration without programming expertise. A retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline with semantic, keyword, and hybrid search retrieves smart home knowledge. Comprehensive evaluation on S5-HES Agent demonstrates that the RAG pipeline achieves near-optimal retrieval fidelity, simulated device behaviour and threat scenarios align with real-world IoT datasets, and simulation engine scales predictably across home configurations, establishing a stable foundation for Society 5.0 smart home research. Source code is available under the MIT License at https://github.com/AsiriweLab/S5-HES-Agent.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces S5-HES Agent, an agentic simulation framework that enables natural-language-driven smart home simulation using LLMs and RAG, overcoming technical barriers for Society 5.0 research.
AI.motivation: Smart home research needs democratization beyond technical experts to support Society 5.0 objectives, but existing simulators require significant technical expertise, have limited adaptability, and lack automated evolution, hindering research across security, energy, health, climate, and socio-economic domains.
AI.method: S5-HES Agent employs interchangeable large language models (LLMs) to coordinate specialized agents, with a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline featuring semantic, keyword, and hybrid search for smart home knowledge retrieval, enabling end-to-end configuration via natural language without programming.
AI.result: The RAG pipeline achieves near-optimal retrieval fidelity, simulated device behavior and threat scenarios align with real-world IoT datasets, and the simulation engine scales predictably across home configurations.
AI.conclusion: S5-HES Agent establishes a stable foundation for Society 5.0 smart home research by democratizing simulation capabilities, allowing researchers to efficiently conduct experiments across diverse fields without programming expertise.

title: Bi-cLSTM: Residual-Corrected Bidirectional LSTM for Aero-Engine RUL Estimation
summary: Accurate Remaining Useful Life (RUL) prediction is a key requirement for effective Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) in safety-critical systems such as aero-engines. Existing deep learning approaches, particularly LSTM-based models, often struggle to generalize across varying operating conditions and are sensitive to noise in multivariate sensor data. To address these challenges, we propose a novel Bidirectional Residual Corrected LSTM (Bi-cLSTM) model for robust RUL estimation. The proposed architecture combines bidirectional temporal modeling with an adaptive residual correction mechanism to iteratively refine sequence representations. In addition, we introduce a condition-aware preprocessing pipeline incorporating regime-based normalization, feature selection, and exponential smoothing to improve robustness under complex operating environments. Extensive experiments on all four subsets of the NASA C-MAPSS dataset demonstrate that the proposed Bi-cLSTM consistently outperforms LSTM-based baselines and achieves competitive state-of-the-art performance, particularly in challenging multi-condition scenarios. These results highlight the effectiveness of combining bidirectional temporal learning with residual correction for reliable RUL prediction.

AI.tldr: A novel Bidirectional Residual Corrected LSTM (Bi-cLSTM) model is proposed for robust Remaining Useful Life (RUL) prediction in aero-engines, combining bidirectional temporal modeling with adaptive residual correction and a condition-aware preprocessing pipeline (regime-based normalization, feature selection, exponential smoothing).
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning approaches, particularly LSTM-based models, struggle to generalize across varying operating conditions and are sensitive to noise in multivariate sensor data in safety-critical systems like aero-engines.
AI.method: The proposed Bi-cLSTM architecture combines bidirectional temporal modeling with an adaptive residual correction mechanism to iteratively refine sequence representations. A condition-aware preprocessing pipeline is introduced featuring regime-based normalization, feature selection, and exponential smoothing to improve robustness under complex operating environments.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on all four NASA C-MAPSS dataset subsets demonstrate that Bi-cLSTM consistently outperforms LSTM-based baselines and achieves competitive state-of-the-art performance, particularly in challenging multi-condition scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Combining bidirectional temporal learning with residual correction is effective for reliable RUL prediction. The proposed approach addresses key challenges in RUL estimation by improving generalization across varying operating conditions and robustness to noise.

title: From Transportation to Manipulation: Transforming Magnetic Levitation to Magnetic Robotics
summary: Magnetic Levitation (MagLev) systems fundamentally increase the flexibility of in-machine material flow in industrial automation. Therefore, these systems enable dynamic throughput optimization, which is especially beneficial for high-mix low-volume manufacturing. Until now, MagLev installations have been used primarily for in-machine transport, while their potential for manipulation is largely unexplored. This paper introduces the 6D-Platform MagBot, a low-cost six degrees of freedom parallel kinematic that couples two movers into a composite robotic platform. Experiments show that the 6D-Platform MagBot achieves sub-millimeter positioning accuracy and supports fully autonomous pick up and drop off via a docking station, allowing rapid and repeatable reconfiguration of the machine. Relative to a single mover, the proposed platform substantially expands the reachable workspace, payload, and functional dexterity. By unifying transportation and manipulation, this work advances Magnetic Levitation towards Magnetic Robotics, enabling manufacturing solutions that are more agile, efficient, and adaptable.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the 6D-Platform MagBot, a low-cost parallel kinematic platform that couples two magnetic levitation movers to achieve sub-millimeter positioning accuracy, expanding MagLev from transportation-only systems to full robotic manipulation capabilities.
AI.motivation: MagLev systems increase flexibility and enable dynamic throughput optimization for high-mix low-volume manufacturing. However, current MagLev installations are used primarily for in-machine transport, while their potential for manipulation remains largely unexplored. The authors aim to expand MagLev capabilities beyond transportation to enable more agile, efficient, and adaptable manufacturing solutions.
AI.method: The researchers developed the 6D-Platform MagBot, a six degrees of freedom parallel kinematic system that couples two magnetic levitation movers into a composite robotic platform. The system includes a docking station enabling fully autonomous pick-up and drop-off operations. The platform was designed to unify transportation and manipulation functions in a single system.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate sub-millimeter positioning accuracy, support for fully autonomous pick-up and drop-off via the docking station, and significant improvements over a single mover in reachable workspace, payload capacity, and functional dexterity. The platform enables rapid and repeatable reconfiguration of manufacturing machines.
AI.conclusion: By unifying transportation and manipulation capabilities, this work advances Magnetic Levitation technology toward Magnetic Robotics, enabling manufacturing solutions that are more agile, efficient, and adaptable. The 6D-Platform MagBot demonstrates that MagLev systems can support complex manipulation tasks while maintaining the flexibility benefits of levitation-based transport.

title: Anatomy of the Modality Gap: Dissecting the Internal States of End-to-End Speech LLMs
summary: Recent advancements in Large Speech-Language Models have significantly bridged the gap between acoustic signals and linguistic understanding. However, a persistent performance disparity remains in speech-based input tasks compared to direct text inference. In this paper, we investigate the dynamic roots of this modality gap beyond static geometric alignment, analyzing how speech and text representations evolve layer-by-layer. We evaluate four open-weight end-to-end models on SpeechMMLU and VoiceBench BBH. Using cross-layer CKA analysis with speech-text token alignment, we find that speech representations exhibit a broad cross-layer alignment band, attributable to the redundant nature of speech where semantic content spans multiple frames. We show that these alignment patterns are structurally stable across different analysis configurations. Crucially, simple statistical calibration is insufficient and can be detrimental when applied at the input layer, indicating that the modality gap is not a mere distribution shift. Overall, our results suggest that the bottleneck lies in condensing redundant speech into stable late-layer decisions, motivating future solutions that operate at the token or temporal granularity instead of feature-level matching.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the performance gap between speech-based and text-based Large Speech-Language Models by analyzing layer-by-layer representation alignment using CKA. The authors find that speech has broad cross-layer alignment due to redundancy, simple calibration is insufficient, and the bottleneck lies in condensing redundant speech into stable late-layer decisions.
AI.motivation: Large Speech-Language Models have narrowed the gap between acoustic signals and linguistic understanding, but a persistent performance disparity remains between speech and text inference. The authors aim to understand the "dynamic roots" of this modality gap beyond static geometric alignment by analyzing how speech and text representations evolve layer-by-layer.
AI.method: The authors evaluate four open-weight end-to-end models on SpeechMMLU and VoiceBench BBH. Using cross-layer Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) analysis with speech-text token alignment, they examine how representations evolve across layers to identify alignment patterns and assess whether simple statistical calibration can bridge the modality gap.
AI.result: Speech representations exhibit a broad cross-layer alignment band attributable to the redundant nature of speech where semantic content spans multiple frames. These alignment patterns are structurally stable across different analysis configurations. Simple statistical calibration is insufficient and can be detrimental when applied at the input layer, indicating the modality gap is not merely a distribution shift.
AI.conclusion: The bottleneck lies in condensing redundant speech into stable late-layer decisions. Future solutions should operate at the token or temporal granularity instead of feature-level matching to better address the structural nature of the modality gap.

title: RAISE: Requirement-Adaptive Evolutionary Refinement for Training-Free Text-to-Image Alignment
summary: Recent text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models achieve remarkable realism, yet faithful prompt-image alignment remains challenging, particularly for complex prompts with multiple objects, relations, and fine-grained attributes. Existing training-free inference-time scaling methods rely on fixed iteration budgets that cannot adapt to prompt difficulty, while reflection-tuned models require carefully curated reflection datasets and extensive joint fine-tuning of diffusion and vision-language models, often overfitting to reflection paths data and lacking transferability across models. We introduce RAISE (Requirement-Adaptive Self-Improving Evolution), a training-free, requirement-driven evolutionary framework for adaptive T2I generation. RAISE formulates image generation as a requirement-driven adaptive scaling process, evolving a population of candidates at inference time through a diverse set of refinement actions-including prompt rewriting, noise resampling, and instructional editing. Each generation is verified against a structured checklist of requirements, enabling the system to dynamically identify unsatisfied items and allocate further computation only where needed. This achieves adaptive test-time scaling that aligns computational effort with semantic query complexity. On GenEval and DrawBench, RAISE attains state-of-the-art alignment (0.94 overall GenEval) while incurring fewer generated samples (reduced by 30-40%) and VLM calls (reduced by 80%) than prior scaling and reflection-tuned baselines, demonstrating efficient, generalizable, and model-agnostic multi-round self-improvement. Code is available at https://github.com/LiyaoJiang1998/RAISE.

AI.tldr: RAISE is a training-free, requirement-driven evolutionary framework for adaptive T2I generation that dynamically allocates computation based on prompt complexity through prompt rewriting, noise resampling, and instructional editing, achieving state-of-the-art alignment with fewer samples and VLM calls than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Text-to-image diffusion models achieve remarkable realism but struggle with faithful prompt-image alignment for complex prompts (multiple objects, relations, fine-grained attributes). Existing approaches have limitations: training-free scaling uses fixed iteration budgets that cannot adapt to prompt difficulty; reflection-tuned models require curated datasets and extensive joint fine-tuning, often overfitting and lacking transferability.
AI.method: RAISE (Requirement-Adaptive Self-Improving Evolution) formulates image generation as a requirement-driven adaptive scaling process. At inference time, it evolves a population of candidates through diverse refinement actions (prompt rewriting, noise resampling, instructional editing). Each generation is verified against a structured checklist of requirements, enabling dynamic identification of unsatisfied items and adaptive computation allocation.
AI.result: On GenEval and DrawBench, RAISE achieves state-of-the-art alignment (0.94 overall GenEval) while reducing generated samples by 30-40% and VLM calls by 80% compared to prior scaling and reflection-tuned baselines.
AI.conclusion: RAISE demonstrates efficient, generalizable, and model-agnostic multi-round self-improvement, achieving adaptive test-time scaling that aligns computational effort with semantic query complexity through a training-free, requirement-driven evolutionary approach.

title: Benchmarking LLM Summaries of Multimodal Clinical Time Series for Remote Monitoring
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) can generate fluent clinical summaries of remote therapeutic monitoring time series. However, it remains unclear whether these narratives faithfully capture clinically significant events, such as sustained abnormalities. Existing evaluation metrics primarily focus on semantic similarity and linguistic quality, leaving event-level correctness largely unmeasured.
  To address this gap, we introduce an event-based evaluation framework for multimodal time-series summarization using the Technology-Integrated Health Management (TIHM)-1.5 dementia monitoring dataset. Clinically grounded daily events are derived through rule-based abnormal thresholds and temporal persistence criteria. Model-generated summaries are then aligned with these structured facts.
  Our evaluation protocol measures abnormality recall, duration recall, measurement coverage, and hallucinated event mentions. We benchmark three approaches: zero-shot prompting, statistical prompting, and a vision-based pipeline that uses rendered time-series visualizations. The results reveal a striking decoupling between conventional metrics and clinical event fidelity. Models that achieve high semantic similarity scores often exhibit near-zero abnormality recall. In contrast, the vision-based approach demonstrates the strongest event alignment, achieving 45.7% abnormality recall and 100% duration recall.
  These findings underscore the importance of event-aware evaluation to ensure reliable clinical time-series summarization.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an event-based evaluation framework for clinical time-series summarization by large language models, revealing that conventional metrics fail to capture clinically significant events and that vision-based approaches outperform semantic similarity metrics.
AI.motivation: LLMs generate fluent clinical summaries of remote therapeutic monitoring time series, but it remains unclear whether these narratives faithfully capture clinically significant events such as sustained abnormalities. Existing evaluation metrics primarily focus on semantic similarity and linguistic quality, leaving event-level correctness largely unmeasured.
AI.method: The authors introduce an event-based evaluation framework using the TIHM-1.5 dementia monitoring dataset. Clinically grounded daily events are derived through rule-based abnormal thresholds and temporal persistence criteria. Model-generated summaries are aligned with these structured facts. The evaluation protocol measures abnormality recall, duration recall, measurement coverage, and hallucinated event mentions. Three approaches are benchmarked: zero-shot prompting, statistical prompting, and a vision-based pipeline using rendered time-series visualizations.
AI.result: The results reveal a striking decoupling between conventional metrics and clinical event fidelity. Models that achieve high semantic similarity scores often exhibit near-zero abnormality recall. The vision-based approach demonstrates the strongest event alignment, achieving 45.7% abnormality recall and 100% duration recall.
AI.conclusion: These findings underscore the importance of event-aware evaluation to ensure reliable clinical time-series summarization, suggesting that conventional metrics are insufficient for assessing the clinical relevance of LLM-generated summaries.

title: General Proximal Flow Networks
summary: This paper introduces General Proximal Flow Networks (GPFNs), a generalization of Bayesian Flow Networks that broadens the class of admissible belief-update operators. In Bayesian Flow Networks, each update step is a Bayesian posterior update, which is equivalent to a proximal step with respect to the Kullback-Leibler divergence. GPFNs replace this fixed choice with an arbitrary divergence or distance function, such as the Wasserstein distance, yielding a unified proximal-operator framework for iterative generative modeling. The corresponding training and sampling procedures are derived, establishing a formal link to proximal optimization and recovering the standard BFN update as a special case. Empirical evaluations confirm that adapting the divergence to the underlying data geometry yields measurable improvements in generation quality, highlighting the practical benefits of this broader framework.

AI.tldr: General Proximal Flow Networks (GPFNs) generalize Bayesian Flow Networks by replacing fixed KL divergence with arbitrary divergence functions, creating a unified proximal-operator framework for iterative generative modeling that adapts to data geometry.
AI.motivation: Bayesian Flow Networks (BFNs) constrain belief-update steps to KL-divergence-based proximal updates, which limits flexibility and adaptability to different data geometries. A more general framework is needed to accommodate diverse divergence measures.
AI.method: GPFNs introduce arbitrary divergence or distance functions (e.g., Wasserstein distance) as proximal steps, derive corresponding training and sampling procedures, and formally link to proximal optimization theory, recovering standard BFN updates as a special case.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show measurable improvements in generation quality when adapting the divergence to underlying data geometry, validating the practical benefits of the broader framework.
AI.conclusion: GPFNs provide a unified proximal-operator framework for generative modeling that outperforms fixed-KL approaches by allowing divergence choice to match data geometry, establishing a valuable extension to Bayesian Flow Networks.

title: Learning Vision-Based Omnidirectional Navigation: A Teacher-Student Approach Using Monocular Depth Estimation
summary: Reliable obstacle avoidance in industrial settings demands 3D scene understanding, but widely used 2D LiDAR sensors perceive only a single horizontal slice of the environment, missing critical obstacles above or below the scan plane. We present a teacher-student framework for vision-based mobile robot navigation that eliminates the need for LiDAR sensors. A teacher policy trained via Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) in NVIDIA Isaac Lab leverages privileged 2D LiDAR observations that account for the full robot footprint to learn robust navigation. The learned behavior is distilled into a student policy that relies solely on monocular depth maps predicted by a fine-tuned Depth Anything V2 model from four RGB cameras. The complete inference pipeline, comprising monocular depth estimation (MDE), policy execution, and motor control, runs entirely onboard an NVIDIA Jetson Orin AGX mounted on a DJI RoboMaster platform, requiring no external computation for inference. In simulation, the student achieves success rates of 82-96.5%, consistently outperforming the standard 2D LiDAR teacher (50-89%). In real-world experiments, the MDE-based student outperforms the 2D LiDAR teacher when navigating around obstacles with complex 3D geometries, such as overhanging structures and low-profile objects, that fall outside the single scan plane of a 2D LiDAR.

AI.tldr: A teacher-student framework transfers LiDAR-based navigation skills to a vision-only system using depth maps from 4 RGB cameras, achieving 82-96.5% success in simulation and outperforming 2D LiDAR on complex 3D obstacles in real-world tests.
AI.motivation: Industrial obstacle avoidance requires 3D scene understanding, but 2D LiDAR sensors only perceive a single horizontal slice, missing critical obstacles above or below the scan plane.
AI.method: A teacher-student framework where a teacher policy trained via PPO in NVIDIA Isaac Lab uses privileged 2D LiDAR observations, which is distilled into a student policy using only monocular depth maps from 4 RGB cameras (predicted by fine-tuned Depth Anything V2). The complete inference pipeline runs onboard an NVIDIA Jetson Orin AGX.
AI.result: In simulation, the student achieves 82-96.5% success rates, consistently outperforming the standard 2D LiDAR teacher (50-89%). In real-world experiments, the MDE-based student outperforms the 2D LiDAR teacher when navigating around obstacles with complex 3D geometries like overhanging structures and low-profile objects.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully transfers LiDAR-based navigation skills to a purely vision-based system, demonstrating superior performance to 2D LiDAR alone on complex 3D obstacles while eliminating the need for LiDAR hardware.

title: Extracting Training Dialogue Data from Large Language Model based Task Bots
comment: Accepted for publication in IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security (TIFS). \c{opyright} 2026 IEEE
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have been widely adopted to enhance Task-Oriented Dialogue Systems (TODS) by modeling complex language patterns and delivering contextually appropriate responses. However, this integration introduces significant privacy risks, as LLMs, functioning as soft knowledge bases that compress extensive training data into rich knowledge representations, can inadvertently memorize training dialogue data containing not only identifiable information such as phone numbers but also entire dialogue-level events like complete travel schedules. Despite the critical nature of this privacy concern, how LLM memorization is inherited in developing task bots remains unexplored. In this work, we address this gap through a systematic quantitative study that involves evaluating existing training data extraction attacks, analyzing key characteristics of task-oriented dialogue modeling that render existing methods ineffective, and proposing novel attack techniques tailored for LLM-based TODS that enhance both response sampling and membership inference. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed data extraction attack. Our method can extract thousands of training labels of dialogue states with best-case precision exceeding 70%. Furthermore, we provide an in-depth analysis of training data memorization in LLM-based TODS by identifying and quantifying key influencing factors and discussing targeted mitigation strategies.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically studies the privacy risks of LLMs in task-oriented dialogue systems, proposing novel data extraction attacks that can extract thousands of training dialogue labels with precision exceeding 70%.
AI.motivation: LLMs integrated into Task-Oriented Dialogue Systems (TODS) can inadvertently memorize training dialogue data containing sensitive identifiable information (phone numbers) and entire dialogue-level events (travel schedules). Despite the critical nature of this privacy concern, how LLM memorization is inherited in developing task bots remains unexplored.
AI.method: The study involves (1) evaluating existing training data extraction attacks, (2) analyzing key characteristics of task-oriented dialogue modeling that render existing methods ineffective, and (3) proposing novel attack techniques tailored for LLM-based TODS that enhance both response sampling and membership inference.
AI.result: The proposed data extraction attack can extract thousands of training labels of dialogue states with best-case precision exceeding 70%, demonstrating the effectiveness of the attack method.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides an in-depth analysis of training data memorization in LLM-based TODS by identifying and quantifying key influencing factors and discusses targeted mitigation strategies to address these privacy concerns.

title: Random Wins All: Rethinking Grouping Strategies for Vision Tokens
summary: Since Transformers are introduced into vision architectures, their quadratic complexity has always been a significant issue that many research efforts aim to address. A representative approach involves grouping tokens, performing self-attention calculations within each group, or pooling the tokens within each group into a single token. To this end, various carefully designed grouping strategies have been proposed to enhance the performance of Vision Transformers. Here, we pose the following questions: \textbf{Are these carefully designed grouping methods truly necessary? Is there a simpler and more unified token grouping method that can replace these diverse methods?} Therefore, we propose the random grouping strategy, which involves a simple and fast random grouping strategy for vision tokens. We validate this approach on multiple baselines, and experiments show that random grouping almost outperforms all other grouping methods. When transferred to downstream tasks, such as object detection, random grouping demonstrates even more pronounced advantages. In response to this phenomenon, we conduct a detailed analysis of the advantages of random grouping from multiple perspectives and identify several crucial elements for the design of grouping strategies: positional information, head feature diversity, global receptive field, and fixed grouping pattern. We demonstrate that as long as these four conditions are met, vision tokens require only an extremely simple grouping strategy to efficiently and effectively handle various visual tasks. We also validate the effectiveness of our proposed random method across multiple modalities, including visual tasks, point cloud processing, and vision-language models. Code will be available at https://github.com/qhfan/random.

AI.tldr: A simple random grouping strategy for Vision Transformers that outperforms complex grouping methods and achieves state-of-the-art results across multiple modalities including visual tasks, point clouds, and vision-language models.
AI.motivation: Vision Transformers suffer from quadratic complexity issues. Many existing approaches use carefully designed token grouping strategies, but the authors question whether these complex designs are truly necessary and if a simpler unified method could replace them.
AI.method: Propose a random grouping strategy that involves simple and fast random grouping of vision tokens, with analysis showing that four key conditions (positional information, head feature diversity, global receptive field, and fixed grouping pattern) enable efficient and effective grouping with minimal complexity.
AI.result: Random grouping almost outperforms all other grouping methods across multiple baselines. When transferred to downstream tasks like object detection, it demonstrates even more pronounced advantages. The method also validates effectiveness across multiple modalities: visual tasks, point cloud processing, and vision-language models.
AI.conclusion: Vision tokens require only an extremely simple grouping strategy to efficiently and effectively handle various visual tasks, provided four crucial conditions are met: positional information, head feature diversity, global receptive field, and fixed grouping pattern.

title: RubricBench: Aligning Model-Generated Rubrics with Human Standards
summary: As Large Language Model (LLM) alignment evolves from simple completions to complex, highly sophisticated generation, Reward Models are increasingly shifting toward rubric-guided evaluation to mitigate surface-level biases. However, the community lacks a unified benchmark to assess this evaluation paradigm, as existing benchmarks lack both the discriminative complexity and the ground-truth rubric annotations required for rigorous analysis. To bridge this gap, we introduce RubricBench, a curated benchmark with 1,147 pairwise comparisons specifically designed to assess the reliability of rubric-based evaluation. Our construction employs a multi-dimensional filtration pipeline to target hard samples featuring nuanced input complexity and misleading surface bias, augmenting each with expert-annotated, atomic rubrics derived strictly from instructions. Comprehensive experiments reveal a substantial capability gap between human-annotated and model-generated rubrics, indicating that even state-of-the-art models struggle to autonomously specify valid evaluation criteria, lagging considerably behind human-guided performance.

AI.tldr: RubricBench introduces a 1,147-pair comparison benchmark to evaluate rubric-guided evaluation in LLMs, revealing significant capability gaps between human and model-generated rubrics.
AI.motivation: As LLM evaluation evolves from simple completions to complex rubric-guided assessment, the community lacks a unified benchmark with discriminative complexity and ground-truth rubric annotations needed for rigorous analysis of this evaluation paradigm.
AI.method: Constructed RubricBench with 1,147 pairwise comparisons using a multi-dimensional filtration pipeline targeting hard samples with nuanced complexity and surface bias, augmented with expert-annotated atomic rubrics derived strictly from instructions.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments revealed a substantial capability gap between human-annotated and model-generated rubrics, indicating even state-of-the-art models struggle to autonomously specify valid evaluation criteria, lagging behind human-guided performance.
AI.conclusion: RubricBench provides the first unified benchmark for assessing rubric-based LLM evaluation, demonstrating that current models lack the ability to generate reliable evaluation criteria comparable to human annotators, highlighting the need for continued human oversight in sophisticated LLM evaluation.

title: Identifying and Characterising Response in Clinical Trials: Development and Validation of a Machine Learning Approach in Colorectal Cancer
summary: Precision medicine promises to transform health care by offering individualised treatments that dramatically improve clinical outcomes. A necessary prerequisite is to identify subgroups of patients who respond differently to different therapies. Current approaches are limited to static measures of treatment success, neglecting the repeated measures found in most clinical trials. Our approach combines the concept of partly conditional modelling with treatment effect estimation based on the Virtual Twins method. The resulting time-specific responses to treatment are characterised using survLIME, an extension of Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations (LIME) to survival data. Performance was evaluated using synthetic data and applied to clinical trials examining the effectiveness of panitumumab to treat metastatic colorectal cancer. An area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of 0.77 for identifying fixed responders was achieved in a 1000 patient simulation. When considering dynamic responders, partly conditional modelling increased the AUC from 0.597 to 0.685. Applying the approach to colorectal cancer trials found genetic mutations, sites of metastasis, and ethnicity as important factors for response to treatment. Our approach can accommodate a dynamic response to treatment while potentially providing better performance than existing methods in instances of a fixed response to treatment. When applied to clinical data we attain results consistent with the literature.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a method that combines partly conditional modeling with the Virtual Twins treatment effect estimation framework and extends LIME to survival data (survLIME) to identify patient subgroups with differential treatment responses over time, demonstrating improved performance for both fixed and dynamic responders in synthetic and clinical data.
AI.motivation: Current precision medicine approaches are limited to static measures of treatment success, neglecting the repeated measures found in most clinical trials. The authors aim to identify subgroups of patients who respond differently to different therapies to enable truly individualized treatments.
AI.method: The approach combines partly conditional modelling with treatment effect estimation based on the Virtual Twins method. Time-specific treatment responses are characterized using survLIME, an extension of Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations (LIME) to survival data.
AI.result: In synthetic data with 1000 patients, the method achieved an AUC of 0.77 for identifying fixed responders and increased AUC from 0.597 to 0.685 for dynamic responders when using partly conditional modeling. Applied to colorectal cancer trials, the method identified genetic mutations, metastasis sites, and ethnicity as important factors for treatment response.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach can accommodate dynamic responses to treatment while potentially providing better performance than existing methods for fixed responses. When applied to clinical data, results are consistent with the literature, suggesting the method is both theoretically sound and practically useful.

title: CHOP: Counterfactual Human Preference Labels Improve Obstacle Avoidance in Visuomotor Navigation Policies
summary: Visuomotor navigation policies have shown strong perception-action coupling for embodied agents, yet they often struggle with safe navigation and dynamic obstacle avoidance in complex real-world environments. We introduce CHOP, a novel approach that leverages Counterfactual Human Preference Labels to align visuomotor navigation policies towards human intuition of safety and obstacle avoidance in navigation. In CHOP, for each visual observation, the robot's executed trajectory is included among a set of counterfactual navigation trajectories: alternative trajectories the robot could have followed under identical conditions. Human annotators provide pairwise preference labels over these trajectories based on anticipated outcomes such as collision risk and path efficiency. These aggregated preferences are then used to fine-tune visuomotor navigation policies, aligning their behavior with human preferences in navigation. Experiments on the SCAND dataset show that visuomotor navigation policies fine-tuned with CHOP reduce near-collision events by 49.7%, decrease deviation from human-preferred trajectories by 45.0%, and increase average obstacle clearance by 19.8% on average across multiple state-of-the-art models, compared to their pretrained baselines. These improvements transfer to real-world deployments on a Ghost Robotics Vision60 quadruped, where CHOP-aligned policies improve average goal success rates by 24.4%, increase minimum obstacle clearance by 6.8%, reduce collision and intervention events by 45.7%, and improve normalized path completion by 38.6% on average across navigation scenarios, compared to their pretrained baselines. Our results highlight the value of counterfactual preference supervision in bridging the gap between large-scale visuomotor policies and human-aligned, safety-aware embodied navigation.

AI.tldr: CHOP aligns visuomotor navigation policies with human intuition of safety and obstacle avoidance using counterfactual human preference labels derived from alternative trajectories under identical conditions.
AI.motivation: Visuomotor navigation policies exhibit strong perception-action coupling but often struggle with safe navigation and dynamic obstacle avoidance in complex real-world environments.
AI.method: CHOP constructs counterfactual navigation trajectories for each visual observation (including the executed trajectory and alternatives under identical conditions). Human annotators provide pairwise preference labels based on anticipated outcomes such as collision risk and path efficiency. These aggregated preferences are used to fine-tune visuomotor navigation policies.
AI.result: On the SCAND dataset, CHOP reduces near-collision events by 49.7%, decreases deviation from human-preferred trajectories by 45.0%, and increases average obstacle clearance by 19.8% across multiple state-of-the-art models. In real-world deployment on a Ghost Robotics Vision60 quadruped, CHOP-aligned policies improve goal success rates by 24.4%, increase minimum obstacle clearance by 6.8%, reduce collision and intervention events by 45.7%, and improve normalized path completion by 38.6%.
AI.conclusion: Counterfactual preference supervision effectively bridges the gap between large-scale visuomotor policies and human-aligned, safety-aware embodied navigation.

title: Markovian ODE-guided scoring can assess the quality of offline reasoning traces in language models
summary: Reasoning traces produced by generative language models are increasingly used for tasks ranging from mathematical problem solving to automated fact checking. However, existing evaluation methods remain largely mechanical and fail to capture human-centric notions of reasoning quality in a way that generalizes across varied and progressively degraded reasoning. We introduce MarODE, an offline evaluation framework that assigns quality scores to reasoning traces. Its effectiveness is assessed using human-centric perturbations and human judgments, which jointly evaluate the fundamental dimensions of an evaluation metric - goodness and soundness. The approach is grounded in a Markovian formulation of reasoning progression and an ordinary differential equation based characterization of trace dynamics, enabling efficient evaluation of reasoning quality. In a large-scale evaluation, MarODE outperforms existing baselines by over 250% under Somers' D correlation. Our results emphasize the value of theory-driven evaluation frameworks as reasoning traces become central to language model-based systems.

AI.tldr: MarODE is a theory-driven offline evaluation framework that scores reasoning traces using Markovian and ODE-based formulations, outperforming baselines by 250% in correlation with human judgments.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation methods for generative language model reasoning traces are mechanical and fail to capture human-centric notions of reasoning quality that generalize across varied and progressively degraded reasoning tasks.
AI.method: MarODE uses a Markovian formulation of reasoning progression combined with ordinary differential equation (ODE) characterization of trace dynamics to efficiently evaluate reasoning quality through offline scoring.
AI.result: In large-scale evaluation, MarODE outperforms existing baselines by over 250% under Somers' D correlation metric, demonstrating superior alignment with human judgments of reasoning quality.
AI.conclusion: The results emphasize the value of theory-driven evaluation frameworks as reasoning traces become central to language model-based systems, suggesting that mathematical and formal approaches can effectively capture human-centric reasoning quality.

title: Beyond Length Scaling: Synergizing Breadth and Depth for Generative Reward Models
summary: Recent advancements in Generative Reward Models (GRMs) have demonstrated that scaling the length of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning considerably enhances the reliability of evaluation. However, current works predominantly rely on unstructured length scaling, ignoring the divergent efficacy of different reasoning mechanisms: Breadth-CoT (B-CoT, i.e., multi-dimensional principle coverage) and Depth-CoT (D-CoT, i.e., substantive judgment soundness). To address this, we introduce Mix-GRM, a framework that reconfigures raw rationales into structured B-CoT and D-CoT through a modular synthesis pipeline, subsequently employing Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) to internalize and optimize these mechanisms. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that Mix-GRM establishes a new state-of-the-art across five benchmarks, surpassing leading open-source RMs by an average of 8.2\%. Our results reveal a clear divergence in reasoning: B-CoT benefits subjective preference tasks, whereas D-CoT excels in objective correctness tasks. Consequently, misaligning the reasoning mechanism with the task directly degrades performance. Furthermore, we demonstrate that RLVR acts as a switching amplifier, inducing an emergent polarization where the model spontaneously allocates its reasoning style to match task demands. The synthesized data and models are released at \href{https://huggingface.co/collections/DonJoey/mix-grm}{Hugging Face}, and the code is released at \href{https://github.com/Don-Joey/Mix-GRM}{Github}.

AI.tldr: Mix-GRM is a framework that reconfigures Chain-of-Thought reasoning into Breadth-CoT (multi-dimensional principle coverage) and Depth-CoT (substantive judgment soundness), using SFT and RLVR to optimize these mechanisms, achieving state-of-the-art performance on five benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current GRMs rely on unstructured length scaling, ignoring the divergent efficacy of Breadth-CoT and Depth-CoT reasoning mechanisms. Misaligning reasoning mechanism with task directly degrades performance.
AI.method: Mix-GRM introduces a modular synthesis pipeline to reconfigure raw rationales into structured B-CoT and D-CoT, followed by Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) to internalize and optimize these mechanisms.
AI.result: Mix-GRM establishes a new state-of-the-art across five benchmarks, surpassing leading open-source RMs by 8.2%. B-CoT benefits subjective preference tasks while D-CoT excels in objective correctness tasks. RLVR acts as a switching amplifier, inducing emergent polarization where models spontaneously allocate reasoning style to match task demands.
AI.conclusion: Mix-GRM demonstrates that task-reasoning mechanism alignment is critical for performance, and RLVR can effectively drive this alignment through emergent polarization, with both synthesized data and models released for community use.

title: Interpretable Cross-Network Attention for Resting-State fMRI Representation Learning
summary: Understanding how large-scale functional brain networks reorganize during cognitive decline remains a central challenge in neuroimaging. While recent self-supervised models have shown promise for learning representations from resting-state fMRI, their internal mechanisms are difficult to interpret, limiting mechanistic insight. We propose BrainInterNet, a network-aware self-supervised framework based on masked reconstruction with cross-attention that explicitly models inter-network dependencies in rs-fMRI. By selectively masking predefined functional networks and reconstructing them from remaining context, our approach enables direct quantification of network predictability and interpretable analysis of cross-network interactions. We train BrainInterNet on multi-cohort fMRI data (from the ABCD, HCP Development, HCP Young Adults, and HCP Aging datasets) and evaluate on the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) dataset, in total comprising 5,582 recordings. Our method reveals systematic alterations in the brain's network interactions under AD, including in the default mode, limbic, and attention networks. In parallel, the learned representations support accurate Alzheimer's-spectrum classification and yield a compact summary marker that tracks disease severity longitudinally. Together, these results demonstrate that network-guided masked modeling with cross-attention provides an interpretable and effective framework for characterizing functional reorganization in neurodegeneration.

AI.tldr: BrainInterNet is a network-aware self-supervised framework that uses masked reconstruction with cross-attention to model inter-network dependencies in resting-state fMRI, enabling interpretable quantification of network predictability and revealing systematic alterations in brain network interactions during Alzheimer's disease progression.
AI.motivation: Understanding how large-scale functional brain networks reorganize during cognitive decline is a central challenge in neuroimaging. While recent self-supervised models show promise for learning representations from resting-state fMRI, their internal mechanisms remain difficult to interpret, limiting mechanistic insight into functional reorganization in neurodegeneration.
AI.method: BrainInterNet employs masked reconstruction with cross-attention to explicitly model inter-network dependencies in resting-state fMRI. The framework selectively masks predefined functional networks and reconstructs them from remaining context, enabling direct quantification of network predictability and interpretable analysis of cross-network interactions. It was trained on multi-cohort fMRI data (ABCD, HCP Development, HCP Young Adults, and HCP Aging) comprising 5,582 recordings and evaluated on the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) dataset.
AI.result: The method reveals systematic alterations in brain network interactions under AD, including in the default mode, limbic, and attention networks. The learned representations support accurate Alzheimer's-spectrum classification and yield a compact summary marker that tracks disease severity longitudinally.
AI.conclusion: Network-guided masked modeling with cross-attention provides an interpretable and effective framework for characterizing functional reorganization in neurodegeneration, demonstrating that explicit modeling of inter-network dependencies enables both mechanistic insight and practical diagnostic utility in Alzheimer's disease research.

title: LAD-Drive: Bridging Language and Trajectory with Action-Aware Diffusion Transformers
summary: While multimodal large language models (MLLMs) provide advanced reasoning for autonomous driving, translating their discrete semantic knowledge into continuous trajectories remains a fundamental challenge. Existing methods often rely on unimodal planning heads that inherently limit their ability to represent multimodal driving behavior. Furthermore, most generative approaches frequently condition on one-hot encoded actions, discarding the nuanced navigational uncertainty critical for complex scenarios. To resolve these limitations, we introduce LAD-Drive, a generative framework that structurally disentangles high-level intention from low-level spatial planning. LAD-Drive employs an action decoder to infer a probabilistic meta-action distribution, establishing an explicit belief state that preserves the nuanced intent typically lost by one-hot encodings. This distribution, fused with the vehicle's kinematic state, conditions an action-aware diffusion decoder that utilizes a truncated denoising process to refine learned motion anchors into safe, kinematically feasible trajectories. Extensive evaluations on the LangAuto benchmark demonstrate that LAD-Drive achieves state-of-the-art results, outperforming competitive baselines by up to 59% in Driving Score while significantly reducing route deviations and collisions. We will publicly release the code and models on https://github.com/iis-esslingen/lad-drive.

AI.tldr: LAD-Drive is a generative framework for autonomous driving that disentangles high-level intention from low-level spatial planning using a probabilistic action decoder and action-aware diffusion model to produce safe, kinematically feasible trajectories.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLM-based planning methods suffer from unimodal planning heads that limit multimodal behavior representation and one-hot encoded action conditioning that discards navigational uncertainty, making it difficult to handle complex driving scenarios.
AI.method: LAD-Drive introduces a structural disentanglement of high-level intention from low-level spatial planning. It uses an action decoder to infer a probabilistic meta-action distribution (explicit belief state), which is fused with vehicle kinematics and fed into an action-aware diffusion decoder with truncated denoising to refine motion anchors into safe trajectories.
AI.result: Evaluated on LangAuto benchmark, LAD-Drive achieves state-of-the-art results with up to 59% improvement in Driving Score over baselines, significantly reducing route deviations and collisions.
AI.conclusion: LAD-Drive effectively addresses the limitations of existing approaches by preserving nuanced intent through probabilistic modeling, enabling safer and more accurate autonomous driving trajectory generation. The code and models are publicly released.

title: More Data, Fewer Diacritics: Scaling Arabic TTS
summary: Arabic Text-to-Speech (TTS) research has been hindered by the availability of both publicly available training data and accurate Arabic diacritization models. In this paper, we address the limitation by exploring Arabic TTS training on large automatically annotated data. Namely, we built a robust pipeline for collecting Arabic recordings and processing them automatically using voice activity detection, speech recognition, automatic diacritization, and noise filtering, resulting in around 4,000 hours of Arabic TTS training data. We then trained several robust TTS models with voice cloning using varying amounts of data, namely 100, 1,000, and 4,000 hours with and without diacritization. We show that though models trained on diacritized data are generally better, larger amounts of training data compensate for the lack of diacritics to a significant degree. We plan to release a public Arabic TTS model that works without the need for diacritization.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the limitation in Arabic TTS research by building a pipeline for collecting and processing large amounts of automatically annotated Arabic data (4,000 hours), and training TTS models with voice cloning using varying amounts of data (100, 1,000, and 4,000 hours) with and without diacritization.
AI.motivation: Arabic Text-to-Speech (TTS) research has been hindered by the limited availability of publicly available training data and accurate Arabic diacritization models.
AI.method: A robust pipeline was built for collecting Arabic recordings and processing them automatically using voice activity detection, speech recognition, automatic diacritization, and noise filtering, resulting in approximately 4,000 hours of Arabic TTS training data. Several robust TTS models with voice cloning were then trained using varying amounts of data (100, 1,000, and 4,000 hours) with and without diacritization.
AI.result: Models trained on diacritized data are generally better, but larger amounts of training data compensate for the lack of diacritics to a significant degree.
AI.conclusion: The authors plan to release a public Arabic TTS model that works without the need for diacritization.

title: COG: Confidence-aware Optimal Geometric Correspondence for Unsupervised Single-reference Novel Object Pose Estimation
summary: Estimating the 6DoF pose of a novel object with a single reference view is challenging due to occlusions, view-point changes, and outliers. A core difficulty lies in finding robust cross-view correspondences, as existing methods often rely on discrete one-to-one matching that is non-differentiable and tends to collapse onto sparse key-points. We propose Confidence-aware Optimal Geometric Correspondence (COG), an unsupervised framework that formulates correspondence estimation as a confidence-aware optimal transport problem. COG produces balanced soft correspondences by predicting point-wise confidences and injecting them as optimal transport marginals, suppressing non-overlapping regions. Semantic priors from vision foundation models further regularize the correspondences, leading to stable pose estimation. This design integrates confidence into the correspondence finding and pose estimation pipeline, enabling unsupervised learning. Experiments show unsupervised COG achieves comparable performance to supervised methods, and supervised COG outperforms them.

AI.tldr: COG (Confidence-aware Optimal Geometric Correspondence) formulates correspondence estimation as a confidence-aware optimal transport problem, enabling unsupervised 6DoF pose estimation of novel objects from a single reference view.
AI.motivation: Estimating 6DoF pose of novel objects with a single reference view is challenging due to occlusions, viewpoint changes, and outliers. Existing methods rely on discrete one-to-one matching which is non-differentiable and tends to collapse onto sparse keypoints.
AI.method: COG is an unsupervised framework that formulates correspondence estimation as a confidence-aware optimal transport problem. It produces balanced soft correspondences by predicting point-wise confidences and injecting them as optimal transport marginals to suppress non-overlapping regions. Semantic priors from vision foundation models further regularize the correspondences.
AI.result: Unsupervised COG achieves comparable performance to supervised methods, and supervised COG outperforms them.
AI.conclusion: The design integrates confidence into the correspondence finding and pose estimation pipeline, enabling unsupervised learning while maintaining strong performance.

title: CARE: Towards Clinical Accountability in Multi-Modal Medical Reasoning with an Evidence-Grounded Agentic Framework
summary: Large visual language models (VLMs) have shown strong multi-modal medical reasoning ability, but most operate as end-to-end black boxes, diverging from clinicians' evidence-based, staged workflows and hindering clinical accountability. Complementarily, expert visual grounding models can accurately localize regions of interest (ROIs), providing explicit, reliable evidence that improves both reasoning accuracy and trust. In this paper, we introduce CARE, advancing Clinical Accountability in multi-modal medical Reasoning with an Evidence-grounded agentic framework. Unlike existing approaches that couple grounding and reasoning within a single generalist model, CARE decomposes the task into coordinated sub-modules to reduce shortcut learning and hallucination: a compact VLM proposes relevant medical entities; an expert entity-referring segmentation model produces pixel-level ROI evidence; and a grounded VLM reasons over the full image augmented by ROI hints. The VLMs are optimized with reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards to align answers with supporting evidence. Furthermore, a VLM coordinator plans tool invocation and reviews evidence-answer consistency, providing agentic control and final verification. Evaluated on standard medical VQA benchmarks, our CARE-Flow (coordinator-free) improves average accuracy by 10.9% over the same size (10B) state-of-the-art (SOTA). With dynamic planning and answer review, our CARE-Coord yields a further gain, outperforming the heavily pre-trained SOTA by 5.2%. Our experiments demonstrate that an agentic framework that emulates clinical workflows, incorporating decoupled specialized models and explicit evidence, yields more accurate and accountable medical AI.

AI.tldr: CARE introduces an evidence-grounded agentic framework that decomposes multi-modal medical reasoning into coordinated sub-modules (entity proposal, ROI segmentation, grounded reasoning) with RL-based optimization and a coordinator for tool invocation and answer verification, achieving 10.9% accuracy improvement over same-size SOTA and 5.2% over heavily pre-trained SOTA.
AI.motivation: Most VLMs operate as end-to-end black boxes that diverge from clinicians' evidence-based, staged workflows and hinder clinical accountability; existing approaches couple grounding and reasoning within single generalist models, leading to shortcut learning and hallucination.
AI.method: CARE decomposes the task into coordinated sub-modules: a compact VLM proposes relevant medical entities; an expert entity-referring segmentation model produces pixel-level ROI evidence; a grounded VLM reasons over the full image augmented by ROI hints. VLMs are optimized with RL using verifiable rewards. A VLM coordinator plans tool invocation and reviews evidence-answer consistency for agentic control.
AI.result: CARE-Flow improves average accuracy by 10.9% over same-size (10B) SOTA; with dynamic planning and answer review, CARE-Coord outperforms heavily pre-trained SOTA by 5.2% on standard medical VQA benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: An agentic framework that emulates clinical workflows, incorporating decoupled specialized models and explicit evidence, yields more accurate and accountable medical AI.

title: Identifying the Geographic Foci of US Local News
comment: This is a research paper accepted to the 18th ACM Web Science Conference 2026
summary: Local journalism is vital in democratic societies where it informs people about local issues like, school board elections, small businesses, local health services, etc. But mounting economic pressures have made it increasingly difficult for local news stations to report these issues, underscoring the need to identify the salient geographical locations covered in local news (geo-foci). In response, we propose a novel geo-foci model for labeling US local news articles with the geographic locations (i.e., the names of counties, cities, states, countries) central to their subject matter. First, we manually labeled US local news articles from all 50 states with four administrative division labels (local, state, national, and international) corresponding to their geo-foci, and none for articles without a geographic focus. Second, we extracted and disambiguated geographic locations from them using Large Language Models (LLMs), since local news often contains ambiguous geographic entities (e.g., Paris, Texas vs. Paris, France). LLMs outperformed all eight geographic entity disambiguation methods we evaluated. Third, we engineered a rich set of spatial-semantic features capturing the prominence, frequency, and contextual positions of geographic entities. Using these features, we trained a classifier to accurately (F1: 0.86) detect the geographic foci of US local news articles. Our model could be applied to assess shifts from local to national narratives, and more broadly, enable researchers to better study local media.

AI.tldr: A novel geo-foci model that identifies and labels US local news articles with administrative division categories (local, state, national, international) using LLMs and spatial-semantic features, achieving 0.86 F1 score.
AI.motivation: Local journalism is essential for democratic societies, but economic pressures have made it difficult for local news stations to report effectively. There is a need to identify salient geographical locations (geo-foci) in local news to assess shifts from local to national narratives and enable better study of local media.
AI.method: The authors (1) manually labeled US local news articles from all 50 states with four administrative division labels (local, state, national, international) and none for articles without geographic focus; (2) extracted and disambiguated geographic locations using Large Language Models (LLMs), which outperformed eight evaluated geographic entity disambiguation methods; (3) engineered a rich set of spatial-semantic features capturing the prominence, frequency, and contextual positions of geographic entities; and (4) trained a classifier using these features.
AI.result: The model accurately detects geographic foci of US local news articles with F1 score of 0.86. LLMs demonstrated superior performance compared to all eight geographic entity disambiguation methods evaluated.
AI.conclusion: The proposed geo-foci model can be applied to assess shifts from local to national narratives and more broadly enable researchers to better study local media. This work addresses the critical need to understand how local news coverage evolves and where geographic attention is focused.

title: $π$-StepNFT: Wider Space Needs Finer Steps in Online RL for Flow-based VLAs
summary: Flow-based vision-language-action (VLA) models excel in embodied control but suffer from intractable likelihoods during multi-step sampling, hindering online reinforcement learning. We propose \textbf{\textit{$\boldsymbolπ$-StepNFT}} (Step-wise Negative-aware Fine-Tuning), a critic-and-likelihood-free framework that requires only a single forward pass per optimization step and eliminates auxiliary value networks. We identify that wider exploration spaces necessitate finer-grained, step-wise guidance for alignment. Empirically, $π$-StepNFT unlocks latent potential on LIBERO with competitive few-shot robustness. Moreover, it achieves superior generalization on ManiSkill, outperforming value-based baselines in OOD scenarios by preventing overfitting to multimodal features. This property offers a scalable solution promising for complex real-world applications.

AI.tldr: π-StepNFT (Step-wise Negative-aware Fine-Tuning) is a critic-and-likelihood-free framework for flow-based VLA models that enables efficient online RL with a single forward pass per optimization step and prevents overfitting to multimodal features, achieving superior generalization on ManiSkill and competitive few-shot robustness on LIBERO.
AI.motivation: Flow-based VLA models suffer from intractable likelihoods during multi-step sampling, which hinders online reinforcement learning. Wider exploration spaces necessitate finer-grained, step-wise guidance for effective alignment.
AI.method: A critic-and-likelihood-free framework requiring only a single forward pass per optimization step, eliminating auxiliary value networks, and employing step-wise negative-aware fine-tuning to provide fine-grained guidance across exploration steps.
AI.result: Unlocks latent potential on LIBERO with competitive few-shot robustness; achieves superior generalization on ManiSkill, outperforming value-based baselines in OOD scenarios by preventing overfitting to multimodal features.
AI.conclusion: Offers a scalable solution promising for complex real-world applications, addressing key challenges in online RL for embodied control with flow-based VLA models.

title: Measuring What VLMs Don't Say: Validation Metrics Hide Clinical Terminology Erasure in Radiology Report Generation
comment: This is an extended version of a manuscript currently under review
summary: Reliable deployment of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) in radiology requires validation metrics that go beyond surface-level text similarity to ensure clinical fidelity and demographic fairness. This paper investigates a critical blind spot in current model evaluation: the use of decoding strategies that lead to high aggregate token-overlap scores despite succumbing to template collapse, in which models generate only repetitive, safe generic text and omit clinical terminology. Unaddressed, this blind spot can lead to metric gaming, where models that perform well on benchmarks prove clinically uninformative. Instead, we advocate for lexical diversity measures to check model generations for clinical specificity. We introduce Clinical Association Displacement (CAD), a vocabulary-level framework that quantifies shifts in demographic-based word associations in generated reports. Weighted Association Erasure (WAE) aggregates these shifts to measure the clinical signal loss across demographic groups. We show that deterministic decoding produces high levels of semantic erasure, while stochastic sampling generates diverse outputs but risks introducing new bias, motivating a fundamental rethink of how "optimal" reporting is defined.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses a blind spot in evaluating Vision-Language Models for radiology where high token-overlap scores can mask template collapse - models generating repetitive generic text instead of clinically specific content. It introduces Clinical Association Displacement (CAD) and Weighted Association Erasure (WAE) to detect semantic erasure and bias across demographic groups.
AI.motivation: Reliable deployment of VLMs in radiology requires validation metrics beyond surface-level text similarity. Current evaluation has a blind spot where models can achieve high aggregate token-overlap scores while succumbing to template collapse (generating only repetitive, safe generic text that omits clinical terminology), leading to metric gaming where models perform well on benchmarks but are clinically uninformative.
AI.method: Two metrics are introduced: Clinical Association Displacement (CAD) - a vocabulary-level framework quantifying shifts in demographic-based word associations in generated reports; and Weighted Association Erasure (WAE) - aggregating these shifts to measure clinical signal loss across demographic groups. The study examines decoding strategies including deterministic decoding and stochastic sampling.
AI.result: Deterministic decoding produces high levels of semantic erasure (clinical signal loss), while stochastic sampling generates diverse outputs but risks introducing new bias. Both strategies have significant drawbacks.
AI.conclusion: The findings motivate a fundamental rethink of how "optimal" reporting is defined. The paper advocates for lexical diversity measures to check model generations for clinical specificity rather than relying solely on surface-level metrics like token-overlap.

title: M$^2$: Dual-Memory Augmentation for Long-Horizon Web Agents via Trajectory Summarization and Insight Retrieval
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) based agents have demonstrated remarkable potential in autonomous web navigation. However, handling long-horizon tasks remains a critical bottleneck. Prevailing strategies often rely heavily on extensive data collection and model training, yet still struggle with high computational costs and insufficient reasoning capabilities when facing complex, long-horizon scenarios. To address this, we propose M$^2$, a training-free, memory-augmented framework designed to optimize context efficiency and decision-making robustness. Our approach incorporates a dual-tier memory mechanism that synergizes Dynamic Trajectory Summarization (Internal Memory) to compress verbose interaction history into concise state updates, and Insight Retrieval Augmentation (External Memory) to guide the agent with actionable guidelines retrieved from an offline insight bank. Extensive evaluations across WebVoyager and OnlineMind2Web demonstrate that M$^2$ consistently surpasses baselines, yielding up to a 19.6% success rate increase and 58.7% token reduction for Qwen3-VL-32B, while proprietary models like Claude achieve accuracy gains up to 12.5% alongside significantly lower computational overhead.

AI.tldr: M² is a training-free, memory-augmented framework that enhances multimodal LLM agents' performance on long-horizon web navigation tasks through dual-tier memory (Dynamic Trajectory Summarization + Insight Retrieval Augmentation).
