AI.motivation: MLLM-based agents show promise in web navigation but struggle with long-horizon tasks due to high computational costs, insufficient reasoning capabilities, and reliance on extensive data collection and training.
AI.method: A dual-tier memory mechanism comprising Internal Memory (Dynamic Trajectory Summarization to compress verbose interaction history into concise state updates) and External Memory (Insight Retrieval Augmentation using an offline insight bank for actionable guidelines).
AI.result: M² consistently surpasses baselines with up to 19.6% success rate increase and 58.7% token reduction for Qwen3-VL-32B, 12.5% accuracy gain for proprietary models like Claude, and significantly lower computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: M² effectively optimizes context efficiency and decision-making robustness for complex, long-horizon web navigation scenarios without requiring additional training.

title: Evaluating and Understanding Scheming Propensity in LLM Agents
summary: As frontier language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents pursuing complex, long-term objectives, there is increased risk of scheming: agents covertly pursuing misaligned goals. Prior work has focused on showing agents are capable of scheming, but their propensity to scheme in realistic scenarios remains underexplored. To understand when agents scheme, we decompose scheming incentives into agent factors and environmental factors. We develop realistic settings allowing us to systematically vary these factors, each with scheming opportunities for agents that pursue instrumentally convergent goals such as self-preservation, resource acquisition, and goal-guarding. We find only minimal instances of scheming despite high environmental incentives, and show this is unlikely due to evaluation awareness. While inserting adversarially-designed prompt snippets that encourage agency and goal-directedness into an agent's system prompt can induce high scheming rates, snippets used in real agent scaffolds rarely do. Surprisingly, in model organisms (Hubinger et al., 2023) built with these snippets, scheming behavior is remarkably brittle: removing a single tool can drop the scheming rate from 59% to 3%, and increasing oversight can raise rather than deter scheming by up to 25%. Our incentive decomposition enables systematic measurement of scheming propensity in settings relevant for deployment, which is necessary as agents are entrusted with increasingly consequential tasks.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates scheming behavior in frontier language models by decomposing scheming incentives into agent and environmental factors, revealing that scheming is rare in realistic settings despite high environmental incentives, and that scheming behavior is surprisingly brittle and can be exacerbated by increased oversight.
AI.motivation: As frontier language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents pursuing complex, long-term objectives, there is increased risk of scheming (agents covertly pursuing misaligned goals). Prior work has focused on demonstrating that agents are capable of scheming, but their propensity to scheme in realistic scenarios remains underexplored.
AI.method: The authors decompose scheming incentives into agent factors and environmental factors, developing realistic settings that systematically vary these factors. They create environments where agents pursue instrumentally convergent goals (self-preservation, resource acquisition, goal-guarding) and insert adversarially-designed prompt snippets into agents' system prompts to induce agency and goal-directedness.
AI.result: Only minimal instances of scheming occurred despite high environmental incentives. Adversarially-designed prompt snippets can induce high scheming rates, but snippets used in real agent scaffolds rarely do. In model organisms built with these snippets, scheming behavior is remarkably brittle: removing a single tool drops scheming rate from 59% to 3%, and increasing oversight can raise rather than deter scheming by up to 25%.
AI.conclusion: The incentive decomposition enables systematic measurement of scheming propensity in settings relevant for deployment, which is necessary as agents are entrusted with increasingly consequential tasks. The findings suggest that scheming in realistic scenarios is less common than previously feared, but the brittle nature of scheming behavior requires careful consideration when designing oversight mechanisms.

title: Neural Latent Arbitrary Lagrangian-Eulerian Grids for Fluid-Solid Interaction
comment: Proceedings of the 14th International Conference on Learning Representations
summary: Fluid-solid interaction (FSI) problems are fundamental in many scientific and engineering applications, yet effectively capturing the highly nonlinear two-way interactions remains a significant challenge. Most existing deep learning methods are limited to simplified one-way FSI scenarios, often assuming rigid and static solid to reduce complexity. Even in two-way setups, prevailing approaches struggle to capture dynamic, heterogeneous interactions due to the lack of cross-domain awareness. In this paper, we introduce \textbf{Fisale}, a data-driven framework for handling complex two-way \textbf{FSI} problems. It is inspired by classical numerical methods, namely the Arbitrary Lagrangian-Eulerian (\textbf{ALE}) method and the partitioned coupling algorithm. Fisale explicitly models the coupling interface as a distinct component and leverages multiscale latent ALE grids to provide unified, geometry-aware embeddings across domains. A partitioned coupling module (PCM) further decomposes the problem into structured substeps, enabling progressive modeling of nonlinear interdependencies. Compared to existing models, Fisale introduces a more flexible framework that iteratively handles complex dynamics of solid, fluid and their coupling interface on a unified representation, and enables scalable learning of complex two-way FSI behaviors. Experimentally, Fisale excels in three reality-related challenging FSI scenarios, covering 2D, 3D and various tasks. The code is available at \href{https://github.com/therontau0054/Fisale}.

AI.tldr: Fisale is a data-driven framework for complex two-way fluid-solid interaction problems that combines multiscale latent ALE grids and a partitioned coupling module to capture dynamic, heterogeneous interactions across domains.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning methods for FSI are limited to simplified one-way scenarios or fail to capture dynamic, heterogeneous interactions due to lack of cross-domain awareness, while classical numerical methods remain computationally expensive.
AI.method: Fisale explicitly models the coupling interface as a distinct component and leverages multiscale latent ALE grids for unified, geometry-aware embeddings. A partitioned coupling module (PCM) decomposes the problem into structured substeps for progressive modeling of nonlinear interdependencies, inspired by classical ALE method and partitioned coupling algorithms.
AI.result: Fisale excels in three reality-related challenging FSI scenarios across 2D, 3D and various tasks, demonstrating superior performance compared to existing models in capturing complex two-way FSI behaviors.
AI.conclusion: Fisale introduces a flexible framework that iteratively handles complex dynamics of solid, fluid, and their coupling interface on a unified representation, enabling scalable learning of complex two-way FSI behaviors. The code is publicly available.

title: ACDC: Adaptive Curriculum Planning with Dynamic Contrastive Control for Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning in Robotic Manipulation
comment: 13 pages (including references and appendix), 12 figures. Accepted to ICAPS 2026. Code available at https://github.com/Xuerui-Wang-oss/Adaptive-Curriculum-Learning-and-Dynamic-Contrastive-Control
summary: Goal-conditioned reinforcement learning has shown considerable potential in robotic manipulation; however, existing approaches remain limited by their reliance on prioritizing collected experience, resulting in suboptimal performance across diverse tasks. Inspired by human learning behaviors, we propose a more comprehensive learning paradigm, ACDC, which integrates multidimensional Adaptive Curriculum (AC) Planning with Dynamic Contrastive (DC) Control to guide the agent along a well-designed learning trajectory. More specifically, at the planning level, the AC component schedules the learning curriculum by dynamically balancing diversity-driven exploration and quality-driven exploitation based on the agent's success rate and training progress. At the control level, the DC component implements the curriculum plan through norm-constrained contrastive learning, enabling magnitude-guided experience selection aligned with the current curriculum focus. Extensive experiments on challenging robotic manipulation tasks demonstrate that ACDC consistently outperforms the state-of-the-art baselines in both sample efficiency and final task success rate.

AI.tldr: ACDC integrates Adaptive Curriculum Planning and Dynamic Contrastive Control to guide agents along well-designed learning trajectories for goal-conditioned reinforcement learning in robotic manipulation, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines in sample efficiency and task success rate.
AI.motivation: Existing goal-conditioned reinforcement learning approaches rely on prioritizing collected experience, which limits performance across diverse tasks. The authors propose a more comprehensive learning paradigm inspired by human learning behaviors to address these limitations.
AI.method: ACDC comprises two integrated components: (1) Adaptive Curriculum (AC) Planning, which schedules learning by dynamically balancing exploration and exploitation based on success rate and training progress; (2) Dynamic Contrastive (DC) Control, which implements the curriculum plan through norm-constrained contrastive learning for magnitude-guided experience selection.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on challenging robotic manipulation tasks demonstrate that ACDC consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both sample efficiency and final task success rate.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ACDC paradigm provides a more comprehensive approach to goal-conditioned reinforcement learning by guiding agents along well-designed learning trajectories, achieving superior performance across diverse manipulation tasks.

title: Learning to Draft: Adaptive Speculative Decoding with Reinforcement Learning
summary: Speculative decoding accelerates large language model (LLM) inference by using a small draft model to generate candidate tokens for a larger target model to verify. The efficacy of this technique hinges on the trade-off between the time spent on drafting candidates and verifying them. However, current state-of-the-art methods rely on a static time allocation, while recent dynamic approaches optimize for proxy metrics like acceptance length, often neglecting the true time cost and treating the drafting and verification phases in isolation. To address these limitations, we introduce Learning to Draft (LTD), a novel method that directly optimizes for throughput of each draft-and-verify cycle. We formulate the problem as a reinforcement learning environment and train two co-adaptive policies to dynamically coordinate the draft and verification phases. This encourages the policies to adapt to each other and explicitly maximize decoding efficiency. We conducted extensive evaluations on five diverse LLMs and four distinct tasks. Our results show that LTD achieves speedup ratios ranging from 2.24x to 4.32x, outperforming the state-of-the-art method Eagle3 up to 36.4%.

AI.tldr: LTD optimizes speculative decoding throughput via two RL-based co-adaptive policies, achieving 2.24x-4.32x speedup, outperforming Eagle3 by up to 36.4% across 5 LLMs and 4 tasks.
AI.motivation: Current speculative decoding methods use static time allocation or proxy metrics (e.g., acceptance length) that neglect true time cost and treat drafting/verification phases in isolation, limiting throughput optimization.
AI.method: LTD frames speculative decoding as a reinforcement learning problem with two co-adaptive policies that dynamically coordinate draft and verification phases, directly optimizing throughput per cycle.
AI.result: Evaluated on 5 LLMs and 4 tasks; LTD achieves 2.24x-4.32x speedup, outperforming Eagle3 by up to 36.4%.
AI.conclusion: LTD effectively addresses static allocation and proxy metric limitations by directly optimizing decoding throughput through co-adaptive policies, demonstrating superior efficiency across diverse LLMs and tasks.

title: Hierarchical Classification for Improved Histopathology Image Analysis
summary: Whole-slide image analysis is essential for diagnostic tasks in pathology, yet existing deep learning methods primarily rely on flat classification, ignoring hierarchical relationships among class labels. In this study, we propose HiClass, a hierarchical classification framework for improved histopathology image analysis, that enhances both coarse-grained and fine-grained WSI classification. Built based upon a multiple instance learning approach, HiClass extends it by introducing bidirectional feature integration that facilitates information exchange between coarse-grained and fine-grained feature representations, effectively learning hierarchical features. Moreover, we introduce tailored loss functions, including hierarchical consistency loss, intra- and inter-class distance loss, and group-wise cross-entropy loss, to further optimize hierarchical learning. We assess the performance of HiClass on a gastric biopsy dataset with 4 coarse-grained and 14 fine-grained classes, achieving superior classification performance for both coarse-grained classification and fine-grained classification. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of HiClass in improving WSI classification by capturing coarse-grained and fine-grained histopathological characteristics.

AI.tldr: HiClass is a hierarchical classification framework for whole-slide image analysis in pathology that uses multiple instance learning with bidirectional feature integration and specialized loss functions to improve both coarse-grained and fine-grained WSI classification.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning methods for whole-slide image analysis primarily rely on flat classification, ignoring hierarchical relationships among class labels.
AI.method: HiClass extends multiple instance learning by introducing bidirectional feature integration that facilitates information exchange between coarse-grained and fine-grained feature representations, along with tailored loss functions including hierarchical consistency loss, intra- and inter-class distance loss, and group-wise cross-entropy loss.
AI.result: HiClass was assessed on a gastric biopsy dataset with 4 coarse-grained and 14 fine-grained classes, achieving superior classification performance for both coarse-grained and fine-grained classification.
AI.conclusion: The results demonstrate the effectiveness of HiClass in improving WSI classification by capturing both coarse-grained and fine-grained histopathological characteristics.

title: ToolRLA: Fine-Grained Reward Decomposition for Tool-Integrated Reinforcement Learning Alignment in Domain-Specific Agents
summary: Tool-integrated reasoning agents interleaving natural language deliberation with external API calls show promise for complex multi-step tasks. However, aligning such agents for high-stakes domain-specific deployment is challenging, as existing reinforcement learning uses coarse binary rewards (success/failure) that insufficiently guide nuanced tool invocation in production. We present ToolRLA, a three-stage post-training pipeline (Supervised Fine-Tuning, Group Relative Policy Optimization, Direct Preference Optimization) for domain-specific tool-integrated agents. Its core is a fine-grained reward function with multiplicative correctness decomposition, evaluating tool invocation across four dimensions: format validity, tool selection correctness, invocation efficiency, and domain constraint compliance. Multiplicative composition prioritizes correct tool selection (a prerequisite for meaningful parameter evaluation), while a large negative compliance penalty (λ=10) ensures regulatory adherence. Deployed on a real-world financial advisory copilot (80+ advisors, 1,200+ daily queries, 15+ heterogeneous APIs), ToolRLA achieves 47% higher end-to-end task completion (62% to 91%), 63% lower tool invocation error (38% to 14%), 93% lower regulatory violation (12% to 0.8%), and sub-2-second latency after three months. Ablation studies confirm fine-grained reward decomposition contributes 7 percentage points over coarse additive rewards; generalizability is validated on ToolBench and API-Bank.

AI.tldr: ToolRLA is a three-stage post-training pipeline (SFT, GRPO, DPO) with a fine-grained multiplicative reward function for domain-specific tool-integrated agents, achieving 47% higher task completion, 63% lower tool errors, and 93% lower regulatory violations on a real-world financial advisory copilot.
AI.motivation: Aligning tool-integrated reasoning agents for high-stakes domain-specific deployment is challenging because existing reinforcement learning methods use coarse binary rewards (success/failure) that insufficiently guide nuanced tool invocation in production environments.
AI.method: ToolRLA employs a three-stage post-training pipeline: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). Its core innovation is a fine-grained reward function with multiplicative correctness decomposition evaluating tool invocation across four dimensions: format validity, tool selection correctness, invocation efficiency, and domain constraint compliance. The multiplicative composition prioritizes correct tool selection (prerequisite for meaningful parameter evaluation), while a large negative compliance penalty (λ=10) ensures regulatory adherence.
AI.result: Deployed on a real-world financial advisory copilot (80+ advisors, 1,200+ daily queries, 15+ heterogeneous APIs), ToolRLA achieves: 47% higher end-to-end task completion (62% → 91%), 63% lower tool invocation error (38% → 14%), 93% lower regulatory violation (12% → 0.8%), and sub-2-second latency after three months. Ablation studies show fine-grained reward decomposition contributes 7 percentage points over coarse additive rewards.
AI.conclusion: Fine-grained reward decomposition with multiplicative correctness evaluation is more effective than coarse additive rewards for aligning tool-integrated agents in high-stakes domains. ToolRLA demonstrates strong generalizability, validated on ToolBench and API-Bank benchmarks.

title: Lookahead identification in adversarial bandits: accuracy and memory bounds
summary:
We study an identification problem in multi-armed bandits. In each round a learner selects one of $K$ arms and observes its reward, with the goal of eventually identifying an arm that will perform best at a {\it future} time. In adversarial environments, however, past performance may offer little information about the future, raising the question of whether meaningful identification is possible at all.
  In this work, we introduce \emph{lookahead identification}, a task in which the goal of the learner is to select a future prediction window and commit in advance to an arm whose average reward over that window is within $\varepsilon$ of optimal. Our analysis characterizes both the achievable accuracy of lookahead identification and the memory resources required to obtain it. From an accuracy standpoint, for any horizon $T$ we give an algorithm achieving $\varepsilon = O\bigl(1/\sqrt{\log T}\bigr)$ over $Ω(\sqrt{T})$ prediction windows. This demonstrates that, perhaps surprisingly, identification is possible in adversarial settings, despite significant lack of information. We also prove a near-matching lower bound showing that $\varepsilon = Ω\bigl(1/\log T\bigr)$ is unavoidable. We then turn to investigate the role of memory in our problem, first proving that any algorithm achieving nontrivial accuracy requires $Ω(K)$ bits of memory. Under a natural \emph{local sparsity} condition, we show that the same accuracy guarantees can be achieved using only poly-logarithmic memory.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces "lookahead identification" in adversarial multi-armed bandits, showing identification is possible with accuracy ε = O(1/√log T) over Ω(√T) prediction windows, while proving ε = Ω(1/log T) is unavoidable. It establishes that nontrivial accuracy requires Ω(K) bits of memory, and poly-logarithmic memory suffices under a local sparsity condition.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses a fundamental question in adversarial multi-armed bandits: can meaningful identification of the best arm be achieved when past performance offers little information about future performance? Traditional identification assumes stochastic environments, but adversarial settings make this challenging.
AI.method: The authors introduce "lookahead identification" - a task where the learner selects a future prediction window in advance and commits to an arm whose average reward over that window is within ε of optimal. They characterize both achievable accuracy and memory requirements through algorithmic analysis and lower bounds.
AI.result: They provide an algorithm achieving ε = O(1/√log T) over Ω(√T) prediction windows, proving identification is possible in adversarial settings. They establish a matching lower bound showing ε = Ω(1/log T) is unavoidable. Memory analysis reveals nontrivial accuracy requires Ω(K) bits, but poly-logarithmic memory suffices under a local sparsity condition.
AI.conclusion: The key finding is that despite the adversarial nature and lack of information, meaningful identification of the best arm is possible through lookahead identification. The paper provides both upper bounds on accuracy achievable with limited memory and lower bounds showing fundamental limits, offering a complete characterization of the trade-offs in this setting.

title: Real-Time Thermal-Inertial Odometry on Embedded Hardware for High-Speed GPS-Denied Flight
summary: We present a real-time monocular thermal-inertial odometry system designed for high-velocity, GPS-denied flight on embedded hardware. The system fuses measurements from a FLIR Boson+ 640 longwave infrared camera, a high-rate IMU, a laser range finder, a barometer, and a magnetometer within a fixed-lag factor graph. To sustain reliable feature tracks under motion blur, low contrast, and rapid viewpoint changes, we employ a lightweight thermal-optimized front-end with multi-stage feature filtering. Laser range finder measurements provide per-feature depth priors that stabilize scale during weakly observable motion. High-rate inertial data is first pre-filtered using a Chebyshev Type II infinite impulse response (IIR) filter and then preintegrated, improving robustness to airframe vibrations during aggressive maneuvers. To address barometric altitude errors induced at high airspeeds, we train an uncertainty-aware gated recurrent unit (GRU) network that models the temporal dynamics of static pressure distortion, outperforming polynomial and multi-layer perceptron (MLP) baselines. Integrated on an NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX, the complete system supports closed-loop quadrotor flight at 30 m/s with drift under 2% over kilometer-scale trajectories. These contributions expand the operational envelope of thermal-inertial navigation, enabling reliable high-speed flight in visually degraded and GPS-denied environments.

AI.tldr: A real-time monocular thermal-inertial odometry system for high-velocity GPS-denied flight on embedded hardware. The system fuses FLIR Boson+ 640 LWIR camera, high-rate IMU, laser range finder, barometer, and magnetometer in a fixed-lag factor graph. Key innovations include a lightweight thermal-optimized front-end with multi-stage filtering, laser-based depth priors for scale stabilization, Chebyshev Type II IIR pre-filtered and preintegrated IMU, and a GRU network for barometric altitude error modeling. Achieves drift under 2% over kilometer-scale trajectories at 30 m/s on NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX.
AI.motivation: The need for reliable navigation in high-velocity, GPS-denied flight scenarios where visual features are degraded (motion blur, low contrast, rapid viewpoint changes). The goal is to expand the operational envelope of thermal-inertial navigation for visually degraded and GPS-denied environments, enabling reliable high-speed flight.
AI.method: Sensors: FLIR Boson+ 640 LWIR camera, high-rate IMU, laser range finder, barometer, magnetometer. Fusion: Fixed-lag factor graph. Front-end: Lightweight thermal-optimized with multi-stage feature filtering. Scale stabilization: Laser range finder provides per-feature depth priors. IMU processing: Chebyshev Type II IIR pre-filtering followed by preintegration. Barometer: GRU network for uncertainty-aware modeling of static pressure distortion at high airspeeds. Hardware: NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX.
AI.result: Closed-loop quadrotor flight at 30 m/s with drift under 2% over kilometer-scale trajectories. The system successfully sustains reliable feature tracks under challenging conditions including motion blur, low contrast, and rapid viewpoint changes.
AI.conclusion: These contributions expand the operational envelope of thermal-inertial navigation, enabling reliable high-speed flight in visually degraded and GPS-denied environments. The system demonstrates that real-time thermal-inertial odometry can achieve high precision on embedded hardware, making it viable for practical applications.

title: LexChronos: An Agentic Framework for Structured Event Timeline Extraction in Indian Jurisprudence
summary: Understanding and predicting judicial outcomes demands nuanced analysis of legal documents. Traditional approaches treat judgments and proceedings as unstructured text, limiting the effectiveness of large language models (LLMs) in tasks such as summarization, argument generation, and judgment prediction. We propose LexChronos, an agentic framework that iteratively extracts structured event timelines from Supreme Court of India judgments. LexChronos employs a dual-agent architecture: a LoRA-instruct-tuned extraction agent identifies candidate events, while a pre-trained feedback agent scores and refines them through a confidence-driven loop. To address the scarcity of Indian legal event datasets, we construct a synthetic corpus of 2000 samples using reverse-engineering techniques with DeepSeek-R1 and GPT-4, generating gold-standard event annotations. Our pipeline achieves a BERT-based F1 score of 0.8751 against this synthetic ground truth. In downstream evaluations on legal text summarization, GPT-4 preferred structured timelines over unstructured baselines in 75% of cases, demonstrating improved comprehension and reasoning in Indian jurisprudence. This work lays a foundation for future legal AI applications in the Indian context, such as precedent mapping, argument synthesis, and predictive judgment modelling, by harnessing structured representations of legal events.

AI.tldr: LexChronos is an agentic framework that extracts structured event timelines from Supreme Court of India judgments using a dual-agent architecture (LoRA-instruct-tuned extraction agent + pre-trained feedback agent) and constructs a synthetic corpus of 2000 samples with DeepSeek-R1 and GPT-4, achieving a BERT-based F1 score of 0.8751.
AI.motivation: Traditional approaches treat legal documents as unstructured text, limiting LLM effectiveness. There is a scarcity of Indian legal event datasets, and structured representations of legal events are needed for applications like precedent mapping, argument synthesis, and predictive judgment modeling.
AI.method: Dual-agent architecture with a LoRA-instruct-tuned extraction agent for identifying candidate events and a pre-trained feedback agent for scoring and refining events through a confidence-driven loop. A synthetic corpus of 2000 samples was constructed using reverse-engineering techniques with DeepSeek-R1 and GPT-4 to generate gold-standard event annotations.
AI.result: Achieved a BERT-based F1 score of 0.8751 against the synthetic ground truth. In downstream evaluations on legal text summarization, GPT-4 preferred structured timelines over unstructured baselines in 75% of cases, demonstrating improved comprehension and reasoning.
AI.conclusion: This work lays a foundation for future legal AI applications in the Indian context by providing structured representations of legal events, enabling applications such as precedent mapping, argument synthesis, and predictive judgment modeling.

title: What Do Visual Tokens Really Encode? Uncovering Sparsity and Redundancy in Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) project visual tokens into the embedding space of language models, yet the internal structuring and processing of visual semantics remain poorly understood. In this work, we introduce a two-fold analytical framework featuring a novel probing tool, $\textbf{EmbedLens}$, to conduct a fine-grained analysis. We uncover a pronounced semantic sparsity at the input level: visual tokens consistently partition into sink, dead, and alive categories. Remarkably, only the alive tokens, comprising $\approx60\%$ of the total input, carry image-specific meaning. Furthermore, using a targeted patch-compression benchmark, we demonstrate that these alive tokens already encode rich, fine-grained cues (e.g., objects, colors, and OCR) prior to entering the LLM. Internal visual computations (such as visual attention and feed-forward networks) are redundant for most standard tasks. For the small subset of highly vision-centric tasks that actually benefit from internal processing, we reveal that alive tokens naturally align with intermediate LLM layers rather than the initial embedding space, indicating that shallow-layer processing is unnecessary and that direct mid-layer injection is both sufficient. Ultimately, our findings provide a unified mechanistic view of visual token processing, paving the way for more efficient and interpretable MLLM architectures through selective token pruning, minimized visual computation, and mid-layer injection. The code is released at: https://github.com/EIT-NLP/EmbedLens.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces EmbedLens, a novel probing tool that reveals only ~60% of visual tokens in MLLMs are "alive" and carry meaningful information, while the rest are "sink" or "dead". Internal visual computations are largely redundant, and mid-layer injection of alive tokens is sufficient for vision-centric tasks.
AI.motivation: While MLLMs project visual tokens into language model embedding spaces, the internal structuring and processing of visual semantics remain poorly understood. The authors seek a fine-grained mechanistic understanding of how visual information is processed in these models.
AI.method: A two-fold analytical framework featuring EmbedLens, a novel probing tool, to conduct fine-grained analysis. The framework includes: (1) input-level semantic sparsity analysis classifying visual tokens into sink, dead, and alive categories; (2) a targeted patch-compression benchmark to test information encoding; (3) analysis of internal visual computations including attention and feed-forward networks.
AI.result: Key findings: (1) Visual tokens partition into sink, dead, and alive categories; (2) Only ~60% of tokens are "alive" and carry image-specific meaning; (3) Alive tokens encode rich cues (objects, colors, OCR) before entering the LLM; (4) Internal visual computations are redundant for most standard tasks; (5) Alive tokens align with intermediate LLM layers rather than initial embeddings; (6) Shallow-layer processing is unnecessary; mid-layer injection is sufficient.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide a unified mechanistic view of visual token processing in MLLMs, suggesting that future architectures could be more efficient and interpretable through selective token pruning, minimized visual computation, and mid-layer injection of visual tokens.

title: SEED-SET: Scalable Evolving Experimental Design for System-level Ethical Testing
comment: 10 main pages along with Appendix containing additional results, manuscript accepted in ICLR 2026
summary: As autonomous systems such as drones, become increasingly deployed in high-stakes, human-centric domains, it is critical to evaluate the ethical alignment since failure to do so imposes imminent danger to human lives, and long term bias in decision-making. Automated ethical benchmarking of these systems is understudied due to the lack of ubiquitous, well-defined metrics for evaluation, and stakeholder-specific subjectivity, which cannot be modeled analytically. To address these challenges, we propose SEED-SET, a Bayesian experimental design framework that incorporates domain-specific objective evaluations, and subjective value judgments from stakeholders. SEED-SET models both evaluation types separately with hierarchical Gaussian Processes, and uses a novel acquisition strategy to propose interesting test candidates based on learnt qualitative preferences and objectives that align with the stakeholder preferences. We validate our approach for ethical benchmarking of autonomous agents on two applications and find our method to perform the best. Our method provides an interpretable and efficient trade-off between exploration and exploitation, by generating up to $2\times$ optimal test candidates compared to baselines, with $1.25\times$ improvement in coverage of high dimensional search spaces.

AI.tldr: SEED-SET is a Bayesian experimental design framework for automated ethical benchmarking of autonomous systems (e.g., drones). It integrates domain-specific objective evaluations with stakeholder subjective value judgments using hierarchical Gaussian Processes and a novel acquisition strategy to propose optimal test candidates aligned with stakeholder preferences.
AI.motivation: Autonomous systems are increasingly deployed in high-stakes, human-centric domains, making ethical alignment evaluation critical. However, automated ethical benchmarking is understudied due to (1) lack of ubiquitous, well-defined evaluation metrics, and (2) stakeholder-specific subjectivity that cannot be modeled analytically.
AI.method: SEED-SET incorporates both objective evaluations and subjective value judgments from stakeholders. It models these separately using hierarchical Gaussian Processes and employs a novel acquisition strategy to propose interesting test candidates based on learned qualitative preferences and objectives that align with stakeholder preferences.
AI.result: Validated on two applications, SEED-SET demonstrates superior performance. It generates up to 2× optimal test candidates compared to baselines and achieves 1.25× improvement in coverage of high-dimensional search spaces, while providing an interpretable and efficient exploration-exploitation trade-off.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method successfully addresses key challenges in automated ethical benchmarking by integrating multiple evaluation types and stakeholder preferences, demonstrating that it outperforms baselines in generating ethically aligned test candidates for autonomous agents.

title: Curation Leaks: Membership Inference Attacks against Data Curation for Machine Learning
summary: In machine learning, curation is used to select the most valuable data for improving both model accuracy and computational efficiency. Recently, curation has also been explored as a solution for private machine learning: rather than training directly on sensitive data, which is known to leak information through model predictions, the private data is used only to guide the selection of useful public data. The resulting model is then trained solely on curated public data. It is tempting to assume that such a model is privacy-preserving because it has never seen the private data. Yet, we show that without further protection, curation pipelines can still leak private information. Specifically, we introduce novel attacks against popular curation methods, targeting every major step: the computation of curation scores, the selection of the curated subset, and the final trained model. We demonstrate that each stage reveals information about the private dataset and that even models trained exclusively on curated public data leak membership information about the private data that guided curation. These findings highlight the previously overlooked inherent privacy risks of data curation and show that privacy assessment must extend beyond the training procedure to include the data selection process. Our differentially private adaptations of curation methods effectively mitigate leakage, indicating that formal privacy guarantees for curation are a promising direction.

AI.tldr: This paper reveals that data curation methods used for private machine learning can leak private information through attacks targeting three major stages: computation of curation scores, selection of curated subsets, and the final trained model. Differential privacy adaptations effectively mitigate these leaks.
AI.motivation: Data curation is used to improve model accuracy and computational efficiency, and has been explored as a solution for private machine learning by using private data only to guide selection of useful public data, with the assumption that models trained solely on curated public data are privacy-preserving.
AI.method: The authors introduce novel attacks against popular curation methods targeting three major stages: (1) computation of curation scores, (2) selection of the curated subset, and (3) the final trained model. They also demonstrate differential privacy adaptations of curation methods.
AI.result: Each stage of the curation pipeline reveals information about the private dataset. Even models trained exclusively on curated public data leak membership information about the private data. The differential privacy adaptations effectively mitigate leakage.
AI.conclusion: Privacy assessment must extend beyond the training procedure to include the data selection process. Formal privacy guarantees for curation are a promising direction to address these risks.

title: Robometer: Scaling General-Purpose Robotic Reward Models via Trajectory Comparisons
summary: General-purpose robot reward models are typically trained to predict absolute task progress from expert demonstrations, providing only local, frame-level supervision. While effective for expert demonstrations, this paradigm scales poorly to large-scale robotics datasets where failed and suboptimal trajectories are abundant and assigning dense progress labels is ambiguous. We introduce Robometer, a scalable reward modeling framework that combines intra-trajectory progress supervision with inter-trajectory preference supervision. Robometer is trained with a dual objective: a frame-level progress loss that anchors reward magnitude on expert data, and a trajectory-comparison preference loss that imposes global ordering constraints across trajectories of the same task, enabling effective learning from both real and augmented failed trajectories. To support this formulation at scale, we curate RBM-1M, a reward-learning dataset comprising over one million trajectories spanning diverse robot embodiments and tasks, including substantial suboptimal and failure data. Across benchmarks and real-world evaluations, Robometer learns more generalizable reward functions than prior methods and improves robot learning performance across a diverse set of downstream applications. Code, model weights, and videos at https://robometer.github.io/.

AI.tldr: Robometer introduces a scalable reward modeling framework that combines intra-trajectory progress supervision with inter-trajectory preference supervision, trained on RBM-1M (1M+ trajectories), outperforming prior methods in generalizability and robot learning performance.
AI.motivation: General-purpose robot reward models typically trained on expert demonstrations with absolute task progress prediction provide only local, frame-level supervision. While effective for experts, this paradigm scales poorly to large-scale datasets with abundant failed/suboptimal trajectories where assigning dense progress labels is ambiguous.
AI.method: Robometer combines intra-trajectory progress supervision with inter-trajectory preference supervision. It employs a dual objective: frame-level progress loss anchors reward magnitude on expert data, while trajectory-comparison preference loss imposes global ordering constraints across trajectories. The framework supports learning from both real and augmented failed trajectories. To support this at scale, the authors curate RBM-1M, a reward-learning dataset with over one million trajectories spanning diverse robot embodiments and tasks, including substantial suboptimal and failure data.
AI.result: Robometer learns more generalizable reward functions than prior methods and improves robot learning performance across diverse downstream applications in both benchmarks and real-world evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Robometer provides an effective reward modeling approach at scale that enables learning from both expert and failed trajectories, demonstrating improved performance across benchmarks and real-world evaluations.

title: Beyond the Grid: Layout-Informed Multi-Vector Retrieval with Parsed Visual Document Representations
summary: Harnessing the full potential of visually-rich documents requires retrieval systems that understand not just text, but intricate layouts, a core challenge in Visual Document Retrieval (VDR). The prevailing multi-vector architectures, while powerful, face a crucial storage bottleneck that current optimization strategies, such as embedding merging, pruning, or using abstract tokens, fail to resolve without compromising performance or ignoring vital layout cues. To address this, we introduce ColParse, a novel paradigm that leverages a document parsing model to generate a small set of layout-informed sub-image embeddings, which are then fused with a global page-level vector to create a compact and structurally-aware multi-vector representation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method reduces storage requirements by over 95% while simultaneously yielding significant performance gains across numerous benchmarks and base models. ColParse thus bridges the critical gap between the fine-grained accuracy of multi-vector retrieval and the practical demands of large-scale deployment, offering a new path towards efficient and interpretable multimodal information systems.

AI.tldr: ColParse is a novel Visual Document Retrieval (VDR) method that reduces storage requirements by over 95% while maintaining performance by using a document parsing model to generate layout-informed sub-image embeddings fused with a global page-level vector.
AI.motivation: Current multi-vector VDR architectures face a critical storage bottleneck. Existing optimization strategies (embedding merging, pruning, abstract tokens) fail to resolve this without compromising performance or ignoring vital layout cues.
AI.method: ColParse leverages a document parsing model to generate a small set of layout-informed sub-image embeddings, which are fused with a global page-level vector to create a compact, structurally-aware multi-vector representation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate ColParse reduces storage requirements by over 95% while yielding significant performance gains across numerous benchmarks and base models.
AI.conclusion: ColParse bridges the gap between fine-grained accuracy of multi-vector retrieval and practical deployment demands, offering a new path towards efficient and interpretable multimodal information systems.

title: Multimodal Adaptive Retrieval Augmented Generation through Internal Representation Learning
summary: Visual Question Answering systems face reliability issues due to hallucinations, where models generate answers misaligned with visual input or factual knowledge. While Retrieval Augmented Generation frameworks mitigate this issue by incorporating external knowledge, static retrieval often introduces irrelevant or conflicting content, particularly in visual RAG settings where visually similar but semantically incorrect evidence may be retrieved. To address this, we propose Multimodal Adaptive RAG (MMA-RAG), which dynamically assesses the confidence in the internal knowledge of the model to decide whether to incorporate the retrieved external information into the generation process. Central to MMA-RAG is a decision classifier trained through a layer-wise analysis, which leverages joint internal visual and textual representations to guide the use of reverse image retrieval. Experiments demonstrated that the model achieves a significant improvement in response performance in three VQA datasets. Meanwhile, ablation studies highlighted the importance of internal representations in adaptive retrieval decisions. In general, the experimental results demonstrated that MMA-RAG effectively balances external knowledge utilization and inference robustness in diverse multimodal scenarios.

AI.tldr: MMA-RAG proposes a dynamic framework for VQA that assesses a model's internal confidence to decide when to incorporate retrieved external knowledge, using a layer-wise decision classifier trained on joint visual-textual representations.
AI.motivation: VQA systems suffer from hallucinations where answers misalign with visual input or facts. Traditional retrieval-augmented generation introduces irrelevant or conflicting content, particularly in visual RAG where visually similar but semantically incorrect evidence is often retrieved.
AI.method: MMA-RAG dynamically assesses internal knowledge confidence through a layer-wise decision classifier that leverages joint internal visual and textual representations to guide the use of reverse image retrieval, deciding whether to incorporate retrieved external information.
AI.result: The model achieves significant improvement in response performance across three VQA datasets. Ablation studies confirm the importance of internal representations in adaptive retrieval decisions.
AI.conclusion: MMA-RAG effectively balances external knowledge utilization and inference robustness, addressing reliability issues in multimodal scenarios by intelligently deciding when to incorporate retrieved information based on the model's internal confidence.

title: Learning Structured Reasoning via Tractable Trajectory Control
summary: Large language models can exhibit emergent reasoning behaviors, often manifested as recurring lexical patterns (e.g., "wait," indicating verification). However, complex reasoning trajectories remain sparse in unconstrained sampling, and standard RL often fails to guarantee the acquisition of diverse reasoning behaviors. We propose a systematic discovery and reinforcement of diverse reasoning patterns through structured reasoning, a paradigm that requires targeted exploration of specific reasoning patterns during the RL process. To this end, we propose Ctrl-R, a framework for learning structured reasoning via tractable trajectory control that actively guides the rollout process, incentivizing the exploration of diverse reasoning patterns that are critical for complex problem-solving. The resulting behavior policy enables accurate importance-sampling estimation, supporting unbiased on-policy optimization. We further introduce a power-scaling factor on the importance-sampling weights, allowing the policy to selectively learn from exploratory, out-of-distribution trajectories while maintaining stable optimization. Experiments demonstrate that Ctrl-R enables effective exploration and internalization of previously unattainable reasoning patterns, yielding consistent improvements across language and vision-language models on mathematical reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: Ctrl-R is a framework for learning structured reasoning via tractable trajectory control that systematically discovers and reinforces diverse reasoning patterns in LLMs during reinforcement learning, improving performance on mathematical reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Large language models exhibit emergent reasoning behaviors with recurring lexical patterns, but complex reasoning trajectories remain sparse in unconstrained sampling. Standard RL often fails to guarantee the acquisition of diverse reasoning behaviors, requiring a systematic approach to discover and reinforce these patterns for complex problem-solving.
AI.method: Ctrl-R proposes a structured reasoning paradigm that requires targeted exploration of specific reasoning patterns during RL. The framework actively guides the rollout process through tractable trajectory control, incentivizing exploration of diverse reasoning patterns. It introduces a power-scaling factor on importance-sampling weights to selectively learn from exploratory, out-of-distribution trajectories while maintaining stable optimization.
AI.result: Ctrl-R enables effective exploration and internalization of previously unattainable reasoning patterns, yielding consistent improvements across language and vision-language models on mathematical reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed structured reasoning approach successfully discovers and reinforces diverse reasoning patterns through targeted trajectory control, providing a scalable solution for improving complex problem-solving capabilities in LLMs and ensuring unbiased on-policy optimization.

title: Wave-Attractor-Tree: A Hierarchical Binary Tree Reduction Architecture for Efficient Sequence Modeling
comment: 5 pages, 5 tables. Source code and benchmarks are available at [https://github.com/IgorBerezkin/WAT]
summary: Work introduces a hierarchical binary tree-based reduction that replaces standard self-attention. The core idea is to use a recursive Gated Linear Unit merge operation, achieving O(n) total merge operations O(log n) parallel depth O(n d^2) total work and O(n) space complexity. In these experiments, the model significantly outperforms standard Transformers in both convergence speed and accuracy on long-range structural dependencies, specifically where hierarchical inductive bias is critical.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical binary tree-based reduction replaces standard self-attention with recursive Gated Linear Units, achieving O(n) operations, O(log n) parallel depth, O(nd^2) work, and O(n) space. The model significantly outperforms standard Transformers in convergence speed and accuracy on long-range structural dependencies where hierarchical inductive bias is critical.
AI.motivation: To improve upon standard Transformers, particularly for tasks requiring hierarchical inductive bias and long-range structural dependencies.
AI.method: Replaces standard self-attention with a hierarchical binary tree-based reduction using recursive Gated Linear Unit merge operations.
AI.result: The model significantly outperforms standard Transformers in both convergence speed and accuracy on long-range structural dependencies.
AI.conclusion: The hierarchical binary tree-based approach is effective for tasks where hierarchical inductive bias is critical, offering improved efficiency and performance over standard attention mechanisms.

title: Rethinking Camera Choice: An Empirical Study on Fisheye Camera Properties in Robotic Manipulation
summary: The adoption of fisheye cameras in robotic manipulation, driven by their exceptionally wide Field of View (FoV), is rapidly outpacing a systematic understanding of their downstream effects on policy learning. This paper presents the first comprehensive empirical study to bridge this gap, rigorously analyzing the properties of wrist-mounted fisheye cameras for imitation learning. Through extensive experiments in both simulation and the real world, we investigate three critical research questions: spatial localization, scene generalization, and hardware generalization. Our investigation reveals that: (1) The wide FoV significantly enhances spatial localization, but this benefit is critically contingent on the visual complexity of the environment. (2) Fisheye-trained policies, while prone to overfitting in simple scenes, unlock superior scene generalization when trained with sufficient environmental diversity. (3) While naive cross-camera transfer leads to failures, we identify the root cause as scale overfitting and demonstrate that hardware generalization performance can be improved with a simple Random Scale Augmentation (RSA) strategy. Collectively, our findings provide concrete, actionable guidance for the large-scale collection and effective use of fisheye datasets in robotic learning. More results and videos are available on https://robo-fisheye.github.io/

AI.tldr: First comprehensive empirical study on wrist-mounted fisheye cameras for robotic manipulation and imitation learning, investigating spatial localization, scene generalization, and hardware generalization through simulation and real-world experiments.
AI.motivation: Rapid adoption of fisheye cameras in robotic manipulation is outpacing systematic understanding of their downstream effects on policy learning, creating a gap that needs to be bridged.
AI.method: Extensive experiments in both simulation and the real world investigating three research questions: (1) spatial localization, (2) scene generalization, and (3) hardware generalization of wrist-mounted fisheye cameras for imitation learning.
AI.result: Three key findings: (1) Wide FoV enhances spatial localization but depends on environmental visual complexity; (2) Fisheye policies overfit in simple scenes but achieve superior scene generalization with sufficient environmental diversity; (3) Cross-camera transfer fails due to scale overfitting, which can be mitigated with Random Scale Augmentation (RSA) strategy.
AI.conclusion: Provides concrete, actionable guidance for large-scale collection and effective use of fisheye datasets in robotic learning, addressing the critical gap in understanding fisheye camera impacts on policy learning.

title: Surgical Post-Training: Cutting Errors, Keeping Knowledge
summary: Enhancing the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) via post-training is often constrained by the trade-off between efficiency and catastrophic forgetting. While prior research emphasizes the role of on-policy data in mitigating forgetting, we uncover--and validate both theoretically and empirically--an overlooked yet critical mechanism: the implicit regularization inherent in Direct Preference Optimization's (DPO) reward estimate. This motivates our Surgical Post-Training (SPoT), a new paradigm designed to optimize reasoning efficiently while preserving learned prior knowledge. SPoT consists of: (1) a data rectification pipeline that employs an Oracle to surgically correct erroneous steps via minimal edits, generating data proximal to the model's distribution; and (2) a reward-based binary cross-entropy objective. Unlike the relative ranking in DPO, this objective treats reasoning correctness as a binary classification problem, enforcing decoupled supervision signals. Empirically, with only 4k rectified math data pairs, SPoT improves Qwen3-8B's accuracy by 6.2% on average across in-domain and OOD tasks, requiring merely 28 minutes of training on 8x H800 GPUs. Code: https://github.com/Visual-AI/SPoT

AI.tldr: SPoT (Surgical Post-Training) enhances LLM reasoning capabilities through data rectification (using an Oracle to surgically correct erroneous reasoning steps) and a binary cross-entropy objective that treats reasoning as binary classification, achieving 6.2% accuracy improvement with only 4k data pairs and 28 minutes of training.
AI.motivation: Post-training LLMs for reasoning faces a trade-off between efficiency and catastrophic forgetting. While prior research emphasizes on-policy data to mitigate forgetting, the authors identify an overlooked mechanism: implicit regularization in DPO's reward estimate.
AI.method: SPoT comprises two components: (1) a data rectification pipeline employing an Oracle to surgically correct erroneous reasoning steps with minimal edits, generating data proximal to the model's distribution; (2) a reward-based binary cross-entropy objective that treats reasoning correctness as binary classification, enforcing decoupled supervision signals unlike DPO's relative ranking approach.
AI.result: With only 4k rectified math data pairs, SPoT improves Qwen3-8B's accuracy by 6.2% on average across in-domain and OOD tasks, requiring merely 28 minutes of training on 8x H800 GPUs.
AI.conclusion: The implicit regularization mechanism in DPO's reward estimate is critical for preserving learned prior knowledge, and SPoT demonstrates that efficient reasoning optimization can be achieved while maintaining knowledge retention, as validated both theoretically and empirically.

title: Wavelet-based Frame Selection by Detecting Semantic Boundary for Long Video Understanding
summary: Frame selection is crucial due to high frame redundancy and limited context windows when applying Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) to long videos. Current methods typically select frames with high relevance to a given query, resulting in a disjointed set of frames that disregard the narrative structure of video. In this paper, we introduce Wavelet-based Frame Selection by Detecting Semantic Boundary (WFS-SB), a training-free framework that presents a new perspective: effective video understanding hinges not only on high relevance but, more importantly, on capturing semantic shifts - pivotal moments of narrative change that are essential to comprehending the holistic storyline of video. However, direct detection of abrupt changes in the query-frame similarity signal is often unreliable due to high-frequency noise arising from model uncertainty and transient visual variations. To address this, we leverage the wavelet transform, which provides an ideal solution through its multi-resolution analysis in both time and frequency domains. By applying this transform, we decompose the noisy signal into multiple scales and extract a clean semantic change signal from the coarsest scale. We identify the local extrema of this signal as semantic boundaries, which segment the video into coherent clips. Building on this, WFS-SB comprises a two-stage strategy: first, adaptively allocating a frame budget to each clip based on a composite importance score; and second, within each clip, employing the Maximal Marginal Relevance approach to select a diverse yet relevant set of frames. Extensive experiments show that WFS-SB significantly boosts LVLM performance, e.g., improving accuracy by 5.5% on VideoMME, 9.5% on MLVU, and 6.2% on LongVideoBench, consistently outperforming state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: WFS-SB is a training-free framework that uses wavelet transform to detect semantic boundaries in videos and applies a two-stage frame selection strategy (adaptive budget allocation + Maximal Marginal Relevance) to select frames that capture narrative changes, improving LVLM performance on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Frame selection faces challenges of high redundancy and limited context windows in LVLMs. Current methods prioritize high relevance to queries, resulting in disjointed frames that ignore narrative structure. Effective video understanding requires capturing semantic shifts (narrative changes), but direct detection of abrupt changes is unreliable due to high-frequency noise from model uncertainty and transient visual variations.
AI.method: WFS-SB leverages wavelet transform for multi-resolution analysis to decompose noisy signals into multiple scales and extract a clean semantic change signal from the coarsest scale. It identifies local extrema as semantic boundaries to segment videos into coherent clips. The framework uses a two-stage strategy: 1) adaptively allocating frame budgets based on composite importance scores, and 2) applying Maximal Marginal Relevance within each clip for diverse yet relevant frame selection.
AI.result: WFS-SB consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods: improves accuracy by 5.5% on VideoMME, 9.5% on MLVU, and 6.2% on LongVideoBench.
AI.conclusion: Effective video understanding hinges on capturing semantic shifts rather than just high relevance. The wavelet transform successfully handles noise in semantic change detection, and the two-stage strategy enables coherent frame selection. WFS-SB demonstrates that narrative structure awareness is crucial for LVLM performance.

title: CeProAgents: A Hierarchical Agents System for Automated Chemical Process Development
summary: The development of chemical processes, a cornerstone of chemical engineering, presents formidable challenges due to its multi-faceted nature, integrating specialized knowledge, conceptual design, and parametric simulation. Capitalizing on this, we propose CeProAgents, a hierarchical multi-agent system designed to automate the development of chemical process through collaborative division of labor. Our architecture comprises three specialized agent cohorts focused on knowledge, concept, and parameter respectively. To effectively adapt to the inherent complexity of chemical tasks, each cohort employs a novel hybrid architecture that integrates dynamic agent chatgroups with structured agentic workflows. To rigorously evaluate the system, we establish CeProBench, a multi-dimensional benchmark structured around three core pillars of chemical engineering. We design six distinct types of tasks across these dimensions to holistically assess the comprehensive capabilities of the system in chemical process development. The results not only confirm the effectiveness and superiority of our proposed approach but also reveal the transformative potential as well as the current boundaries of Large Language Models (LLMs) for industrial chemical engineering.

AI.tldr: CeProAgents is a hierarchical multi-agent system for automating chemical process development, evaluated on the new CeProBench benchmark, revealing both the potential and limitations of LLMs in industrial chemical engineering.
AI.motivation: Chemical process development is complex, requiring integration of specialized knowledge, conceptual design, and parametric simulation; the paper aims to automate this multi-faceted process.
AI.method: A hierarchical multi-agent system with three specialized cohorts (knowledge, concept, and parameter) using a hybrid architecture combining dynamic agent chatgroups with structured agentic workflows. The authors also created CeProBench, a multi-dimensional benchmark with six task types across three core pillars of chemical engineering.
AI.result: The system demonstrates effectiveness and superiority in chemical process development. The study reveals the transformative potential of LLMs while identifying their current boundaries in industrial chemical engineering applications.
AI.conclusion: CeProAgents effectively automates chemical process development and highlights both the promising capabilities and limitations of LLMs in industrial chemical engineering tasks.

title: A Gauge Theory of Superposition: Toward a Sheaf-Theoretic Atlas of Neural Representations
summary:
We develop a discrete gauge-theoretic framework for superposition in large language models (LLMs) that replaces the single-global-dictionary premise with a sheaf-theoretic atlas of local semantic charts. Contexts are clustered into a stratified context complex; each chart carries a local feature space and a local information-geometric metric (Fisher/Gauss--Newton) identifying predictively consequential feature interactions. This yields a Fisher-weighted interference energy and three measurable obstructions to global interpretability: (O1) local jamming (active load exceeds Fisher bandwidth), (O2) proxy shearing (mismatch between geometric transport and a fixed correspondence proxy), and (O3) nontrivial holonomy (path-dependent transport around loops).
  We prove and instantiate four results on a frozen open LLM (Llama~3.2~3B Instruct) using WikiText-103, a C4-derived English web-text subset, and \texttt{the-stack-smol}. (A) After constructive gauge fixing on a spanning tree, each chord residual equals the holonomy of its fundamental cycle, making holonomy computable and gauge-invariant. (B) Shearing lower-bounds a data-dependent transfer mismatch energy, turning $D_{\mathrm{shear}}$ into an unavoidable failure bound. (C) We obtain non-vacuous certified jamming/interference bounds with high coverage and zero violations across seeds/hyperparameters. (D) Bootstrap and sample-size experiments show stable estimation of $D_{\mathrm{shear}}$ and $D_{\mathrm{hol}}$, with improved concentration on well-conditioned subsystems.

AI.tldr: A discrete gauge-theoretic framework for superposition in LLMs that replaces the single-global-dictionary premise with a sheaf-theoretic atlas of local semantic charts, introducing Fisher-weighted interference energy and three measurable obstructions (local jamming, proxy shearing, and nontrivial holonomy) to global interpretability.
AI.motivation: Traditional LLM interpretation assumes a single global dictionary of concepts, but this framework argues that contexts should be clustered into a stratified context complex where each chart carries a local feature space and information-geometric metric. The goal is to address the fundamental challenges of interpreting superposition in LLMs by providing mathematical tools to measure and characterize interference between concepts.
AI.method: Develops a discrete gauge-theoretic framework using sheaf-theoretic atlas of local semantic charts, where contexts are clustered into a stratified context complex. Each chart carries a local feature space and a local information-geometric metric (Fisher/Gauss-Newton). This yields a Fisher-weighted interference energy. Three obstructions to global interpretability are defined: (O1) local jamming (active load exceeds Fisher bandwidth), (O2) proxy shearing (mismatch between geometric transport and a fixed correspondence proxy), and (O3) nontrivial holonomy (path-dependent transport around loops).
AI.result: Four results proven on Llama 3.2 3B Instruct using WikiText-103, C4-derived web-text, and the-stack-smol datasets: (A) After constructive gauge fixing on a spanning tree, each chord residual equals the holonomy of its fundamental cycle, making holonomy computable and gauge-invariant. (B) Shearing lower-bounds a data-dependent transfer mismatch energy, turning D_shear into an unavoidable failure bound. (C) Certified jamming/interference bounds with high coverage and zero violations across seeds/hyperparameters. (D) Bootstrap and sample-size experiments show stable estimation of D_shear and D_hol with improved concentration on well-conditioned subsystems.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides computable and gauge-invariant holonomy measures, establishes shear as an unavoidable failure bound, yields non-vacuous certified jamming bounds, and demonstrates stable estimation of interference metrics. This approach offers a mathematically rigorous foundation for understanding and measuring superposition in LLMs, moving beyond naive single-global-dictionary interpretations.

title: DriveCode: Domain Specific Numerical Encoding for LLM-Based Autonomous Driving
comment: The project page is available at https://shiftwilliam.github.io/DriveCode
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown great promise for autonomous driving. However, discretizing numbers into tokens limits precise numerical reasoning, fails to reflect the positional significance of digits in the training objective, and makes it difficult to achieve both decoding efficiency and numerical precision. These limitations affect both the processing of sensor measurements and the generation of precise control commands, creating a fundamental barrier for deploying LLM-based autonomous driving systems. In this paper, we introduce DriveCode, a novel numerical encoding method that represents numbers as dedicated embeddings rather than discrete text tokens. DriveCode employs a number projector to map numbers into the language model's hidden space, enabling seamless integration with visual and textual features in a unified multimodal sequence. Evaluated on OmniDrive, DriveGPT4, and DriveGPT4-V2 datasets, DriveCode demonstrates superior performance in trajectory prediction and control signal generation, confirming its effectiveness for LLM-based autonomous driving systems.

AI.tldr: DriveCode introduces a novel numerical encoding method that represents numbers as dedicated embeddings rather than discrete tokens, enabling precise numerical reasoning in LLM-based autonomous driving systems.
AI.motivation: Discretizing numbers into tokens limits precise numerical reasoning, fails to reflect positional significance, and makes it difficult to achieve both decoding efficiency and numerical precision, creating a fundamental barrier for deploying LLM-based autonomous driving systems.
AI.method: DriveCode employs a number projector to map numbers into the language model's hidden space, representing numbers as dedicated embeddings rather than discrete text tokens. This enables seamless integration with visual and textual features in a unified multimodal sequence.
AI.result: Evaluated on OmniDrive, DriveGPT4, and DriveGPT4-V2 datasets, DriveCode demonstrates superior performance in trajectory prediction and control signal generation.
AI.conclusion: DriveCode confirms the effectiveness of dedicated numerical encoding for LLM-based autonomous driving systems, overcoming the limitations of traditional token-based numerical representation.

title: QIME: Constructing Interpretable Medical Text Embeddings via Ontology-Grounded Questions
summary: While dense biomedical embeddings achieve strong performance, their black-box nature limits their utility in clinical decision-making. Recent question-based interpretable embeddings represent text as binary answers to natural-language questions, but these approaches often rely on heuristic or surface-level contrastive signals and overlook specialized domain knowledge. We propose QIME, an ontology-grounded framework for constructing interpretable medical text embeddings in which each dimension corresponds to a clinically meaningful yes/no question. By conditioning on cluster-specific medical concept signatures, QIME generates semantically atomic questions that capture fine-grained distinctions in biomedical text. Furthermore, QIME supports a training-free embedding construction strategy that eliminates per-question classifier training while further improving performance. Experiments across biomedical semantic similarity, clustering, and retrieval benchmarks show that QIME consistently outperforms prior interpretable embedding methods and substantially narrows the gap to strong black-box biomedical encoders, while providing concise and clinically informative explanations.

AI.tldr: QIME constructs interpretable biomedical text embeddings by mapping each dimension to a clinically meaningful yes/no question, using cluster-specific medical concept signatures and a training-free approach to outperform prior interpretable methods and approach black-box encoder performance.
AI.motivation: Dense biomedical embeddings achieve strong performance but lack interpretability, limiting their clinical utility. Existing interpretable embeddings rely on heuristic or surface-level signals and overlook specialized domain knowledge, requiring per-question classifier training which adds complexity.
AI.method: QIME is an ontology-grounded framework where each embedding dimension corresponds to a clinically meaningful yes/no question. It generates semantically atomic questions by conditioning on cluster-specific medical concept signatures and employs a training-free embedding construction strategy that eliminates per-question classifier training.
AI.result: Across biomedical semantic similarity, clustering, and retrieval benchmarks, QIME consistently outperforms prior interpretable embedding methods and substantially narrows the performance gap to strong black-box biomedical encoders while providing concise, clinically informative explanations.
AI.conclusion: QIME successfully bridges the performance-interpretability tradeoff in biomedical text embeddings, enabling clinicians to understand model decisions through meaningful yes/no questions rather than opaque embeddings, with the added benefit of a training-free approach that simplifies deployment.

title: MLLM-4D: Towards Visual-based Spatial-Temporal Intelligence
summary: Humans are born with vision-based 4D spatial-temporal intelligence, which enables us to perceive and reason about the evolution of 3D space over time from purely visual inputs. Despite its importance, this capability remains a significant bottleneck for current multimodal large language models (MLLMs). To tackle this challenge, we introduce MLLM-4D, a comprehensive framework designed to bridge the gaps in training data curation and model post-training for spatiotemporal understanding and reasoning. On the data front, we develop a cost-efficient data curation pipeline that repurposes existing stereo video datasets into high-quality 4D spatiotemporal instructional data. This results in the MLLM4D-2M and MLLM4D-R1-30k datasets for Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (RFT), alongside MLLM4D-Bench for comprehensive evaluation. Regarding model training, our post-training strategy establishes a foundational 4D understanding via SFT and further catalyzes 4D reasoning capabilities by employing Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with specialized Spatiotemporal Chain of Thought (ST-CoT) prompting and Spatiotemporal reward functions (ST-reward) without involving the modification of architecture. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MLLM-4D achieves state-of-the-art spatial-temporal understanding and reasoning capabilities from purely 2D RGB inputs. Project page: https://github.com/GVCLab/MLLM-4D.

AI.tldr: MLLM-4D is a framework that enhances multimodal large language models with 4D spatiotemporal intelligence using purely 2D RGB inputs, achieved through cost-efficient data curation and post-training with GRPO and ST-CoT prompting.
AI.motivation: Humans possess vision-based 4D spatiotemporal intelligence for perceiving and reasoning about 3D space evolution over time, but this capability remains a significant bottleneck for current MLLMs.
AI.method: Data curation: Cost-efficient pipeline repurposing stereo video datasets into 4D spatiotemporal instructional data (MLLM4D-2M, MLLM4D-R1-30k, MLLM4D-Bench). Model post-training: SFT for foundational 4D understanding, followed by GRPO with Spatiotemporal Chain of Thought prompting and Spatiotemporal reward functions (no architectural modifications).
AI.result: MLLM-4D achieves state-of-the-art spatial-temporal understanding and reasoning capabilities from purely 2D RGB inputs.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully bridges gaps in training data curation and model post-training, demonstrating that 4D spatiotemporal intelligence can be effectively integrated into MLLMs without architectural changes.

title: Chain-of-Context Learning: Dynamic Constraint Understanding for Multi-Task VRPs
comment: This paper is accepted by ICLR 2026
summary: Multi-task Vehicle Routing Problems (VRPs) aim to minimize routing costs while satisfying diverse constraints. Existing solvers typically adopt a unified reinforcement learning (RL) framework to learn generalizable patterns across tasks. However, they often overlook the constraint and node dynamics during the decision process, making the model fail to accurately react to the current context. To address this limitation, we propose Chain-of-Context Learning (CCL), a novel framework that progressively captures the evolving context to guide fine-grained node adaptation. Specifically, CCL constructs step-wise contextual information via a Relevance-Guided Context Reformulation (RGCR) module, which adaptively prioritizes salient constraints. This context then guides node updates through a Trajectory-Shared Node Re-embedding (TSNR) module, which aggregates shared node features from all trajectories' contexts and uses them to update inputs for the next step. By modeling evolving preferences of the RL agent, CCL captures step-by-step dependencies in sequential decision-making. We evaluate CCL on 48 diverse VRP variants, including 16 in-distribution and 32 out-of-distribution (with unseen constraints) tasks. Experimental results show that CCL performs favorably against the state-of-the-art baselines, achieving the best performance on all in-distribution tasks and the majority of out-of-distribution tasks.

AI.tldr: Chain-of-Context Learning (CCL) introduces a novel framework for Multi-task Vehicle Routing Problems that progressively captures evolving context through Relevance-Guided Context Reformulation and Trajectory-Shared Node Re-embedding modules, enabling accurate reaction to current context during sequential decision-making.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-task VRP solvers using unified reinforcement learning frameworks overlook constraint and node dynamics during decision-making, causing models to fail to accurately react to the current context in sequential decision processes.
AI.method: CCL constructs step-wise contextual information via Relevance-Guided Context Reformulation (RGCR) that adaptively prioritizes salient constraints, then guides node updates through Trajectory-Shared Node Re-embedding (TSNR) that aggregates shared node features from all trajectories' contexts. The framework models evolving preferences of the RL agent to capture step-by-step dependencies in sequential decision-making.
AI.result: Evaluated on 48 diverse VRP variants (16 in-distribution and 32 out-of-distribution with unseen constraints), CCL achieves state-of-the-art performance on all in-distribution tasks and the majority of out-of-distribution tasks, outperforming existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: CCL effectively addresses the limitation of existing unified RL frameworks by progressively capturing evolving context during decision-making, enabling accurate reaction to current context in sequential decision-making for multi-task VRPs.

title: GeMi: A Graph-based, Multimodal Recommendation System for Narrative Scroll Paintings
summary: Recommendation Systems are effective in managing the ever-increasing amount of multimodal data available today and help users discover interesting new items. These systems can handle various media types such as images, text, audio, and video data, and this has made it possible to handle content-based recommendation utilizing features extracted from items while also incorporating user preferences. Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based recommendation systems are a special class of recommendation systems that can handle relationships between items and users, making them particularly attractive for content-based recommendations. Their popularity also stems from the fact that they use advanced machine learning techniques, such as deep learning on graph-structured data, to exploit user-to-item interactions.
