The nodes in the graph can access higher-order neighbor information along with state-of-the-art vision-language models for processing multimodal content, and there are well-designed algorithms for embedding, message passing, and propagation. In this work, we present the design of a GNN-based recommendation system on a novel data set collected from field research. Designed for an endangered performing art form, the recommendation system uses multimodal content (text and image data) to suggest similar paintings for viewing and purchase. To the best of our knowledge, there is no recommendation system designed for narrative scroll paintings -- our work therefore serves several purposes, including art conservation, a data storage system for endangered art objects, and a state-of-the-art recommendation system that leverages both the novel characteristics of the data and preferences of the user population interested in narrative scroll paintings.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a novel Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based recommendation system for narrative scroll paintings using multimodal content (text and image data).
AI.motivation: There is currently no recommendation system designed for narrative scroll paintings. This work aims to address this gap while serving multiple purposes: art conservation, data storage for endangered art objects, and providing a state-of-the-art recommendation system that leverages both the unique characteristics of narrative scroll paintings and user preferences.
AI.method: The authors designed a GNN-based recommendation system on a novel dataset collected from field research. The system processes multimodal content (text and image data) using vision-language models, and employs advanced ML techniques including deep learning on graph-structured data. It incorporates algorithms for embedding, message passing, and propagation, allowing nodes to access higher-order neighbor information.
AI.result: A fully designed GNN-based recommendation system for narrative scroll paintings has been presented. The system can suggest similar paintings for viewing and purchase based on multimodal content analysis.
AI.conclusion: This work represents the first recommendation system designed for narrative scroll paintings. It serves as a valuable tool for art conservation, provides a data storage system for endangered art objects, and establishes a state-of-the-art recommendation system that effectively leverages both the novel characteristics of the data and user preferences.

title: Building a Strong Instruction Language Model for a Less-Resourced Language
comment: Currently under review at Natural Language Processing Special Issue on Language Models for Low-Resource Languages
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have become an essential tool for natural language processing and artificial intelligence in general. Current open-source models are primarily trained on English texts, resulting in poorer performance on less-resourced languages and cultures. We present a set of methodological approaches necessary for the successful adaptation of an LLM to a less-resourced language, and demonstrate them using the Slovene language. We present GaMS3-12B, a generative model for Slovene with 12 billion parameters, and demonstrate that it is the best-performing open-source model for Slovene within its parameter range. We adapted the model to the Slovene language using three-stage continual pre-training of the Gemma 3 model, followed by two-stage supervised fine-tuning (SFT). We trained the model on a combination of 140B Slovene, English, Bosnian, Serbian, and Croatian pretraining tokens, and over 200 thousand English and Slovene SFT examples. We evaluate GaMS3-12B on the Slovenian-LLM-Eval datasets, English-to-Slovene translation, and the Slovene LLM arena. We show that the described model outperforms 12B Gemma 3 across all three scenarios and performs comparably to much larger commercial GPT-4o in the Slovene LLM arena, achieving a win rate of over 60 %.

AI.tldr: The paper presents GaMS3-12B, a 12-billion parameter generative model for Slovene, developed through adaptation of Gemma 3 using continual pre-training and supervised fine-tuning, outperforming other open-source models and rivaling GPT-4o in Slovene tasks.
AI.motivation: LLMs are essential tools for NLP but current open-source models are primarily trained on English, leading to poor performance on less-resourced languages and cultures. The authors aim to address this gap by developing effective methodological approaches for adapting LLMs to less-resourced languages like Slovene.
AI.method: The authors adapted the Gemma 3 model to Slovene using a three-stage continual pre-training approach on 140B tokens (Slovene, English, Bosnian, Serbian, Croatian), followed by two-stage supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on over 200k English and Slovene examples.
AI.result: GaMS3-12B outperforms the 12B Gemma 3 across all three evaluation scenarios (Slovenian-LLM-Eval datasets, English-to-Slovene translation, and Slovene LLM arena) and achieves a win rate of over 60% against GPT-4o in the Slovene LLM arena, demonstrating comparable performance to much larger commercial models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methodological approaches for adapting LLMs to less-resourced languages are effective, as demonstrated by the superior performance of GaMS3-12B. This establishes GaMS3-12B as the best-performing open-source model for Slovene within its parameter range and validates the approach for adapting LLMs to other less-resourced languages.

title: Vision-TTT: Efficient and Expressive Visual Representation Learning with Test-Time Training
summary: Learning efficient and expressive visual representation has long been the pursuit of computer vision research. While Vision Transformers (ViTs) gradually replace traditional Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) as more scalable vision learners, their applications are plagued by the quadratic complexity of the self-attention mechanism. To address the challenge, we introduce a new linear-time sequence modeling method Test-Time Training (TTT) into vision and propose Vision-TTT, which compresses the visual token sequence in a novel self-supervised learning manner. By incorporating bidirectional scan strategy and the Conv2d module, Vision-TTT effectively extends vanilla TTT to model 2D visual correlations with global receptive fields. Extensive experiments show that \texttt{Vittt-T/S/B} achieve 77.3%,81.2%,82.5% Top-1 accuracy on ImageNet classification and also greatly outperform their counterparts on downstream tasks. At 1280x1280 resolution, \texttt{Vittt-T} reduces FLOPs by 79.4% and runs 4.38x faster with 88.9% less memory than DeiT-T. These results demonstrate the expressiveness and efficiency of Vision-TTT as a strong candidate for the next-generation generic visual backbone.

AI.tldr: Vision-TTT introduces Test-Time Training to Vision Transformers, using a self-supervised sequence compression method with bidirectional scanning and Conv2d modules to achieve linear complexity while maintaining high accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Vision Transformers (ViTs) are replacing Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) due to their scalability, but the self-attention mechanism has quadratic complexity, limiting their efficiency and applicability.
AI.method: The paper proposes Vision-TTT, which applies Test-Time Training (TTT) to vision by compressing visual token sequences in a self-supervised manner. It incorporates a bidirectional scan strategy and Conv2d module to extend vanilla TTT to model 2D visual correlations with global receptive fields.
AI.result: Vision-TTT models (T/S/B) achieve 77.3%, 81.2%, and 82.5% Top-1 accuracy on ImageNet classification, outperforming their counterparts on downstream tasks. At 1280x1280 resolution, Vision-TTT-T reduces FLOPs by 79.4%, runs 4.38x faster, and uses 88.9% less memory than DeiT-T.
AI.conclusion: Vision-TTT demonstrates both expressiveness and efficiency, making it a strong candidate for next-generation generic visual backbones, effectively addressing the quadratic complexity challenge of Vision Transformers while maintaining competitive performance.

title: FT-Dojo: Towards Autonomous LLM Fine-Tuning with Language Agents
summary: Fine-tuning large language models for vertical domains remains a labor-intensive and expensive process, requiring domain experts to curate data, configure training, and iteratively diagnose model behavior. Despite growing interest in autonomous machine learning, no prior work has tackled end-to-end LLM fine-tuning with agents. Can LLM-based agents automate this complete process? We frame this as a substantially open problem: agents must navigate an open-ended search space spanning data curation from diverse data sources, processing with complex tools, building a training pipeline, and iteratively refining their approach based on evaluation outcomes in rapidly growing logs--an overall scenario far more intricate than existing benchmarks. To study this question, we introduce FT-Dojo, an interactive environment comprising 13 tasks across 5 domains. We further develop FT-Agent, an autonomous system that mirrors human experts by leveraging evaluation-driven feedback to iteratively diagnose failures and refine fine-tuning strategies. Experiments on FT-Dojo demonstrate that purpose-built fine-tuning agents significantly outperform general-purpose alternatives, with FT-Agent achieving the best performance on 10 out of 13 tasks across all five domains. Ablations show that the approach generalizes effectively to 3B models, with additional insights on data scaling trade-offs and backbone sensitivity. Case analyses reveal that agents can recover from failures through cumulative learning from historical experience, while also exposing fundamental limitations in causal reasoning--highlighting both the promise and current boundaries of autonomous LLM fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces FT-Dojo, an interactive environment for autonomous LLM fine-tuning, and FT-Agent, an evaluation-driven system that outperforms general-purpose alternatives across 13 tasks across 5 domains, demonstrating both the promise and limitations of agent-based fine-tuning workflows.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning LLMs for vertical domains is labor-intensive and expensive, requiring domain experts to curate data, configure training, and iteratively diagnose model behavior. The authors question whether LLM-based agents can automate this complete end-to-end process, which involves navigating an open-ended search space spanning data curation, processing with complex tools, building training pipelines, and iteratively refining approaches based on evaluation outcomes in rapidly growing logs.
AI.method: The authors introduce FT-Dojo, an interactive environment comprising 13 tasks across 5 domains designed to study autonomous LLM fine-tuning. They develop FT-Agent, an autonomous system that mirrors human experts by leveraging evaluation-driven feedback to iteratively diagnose failures and refine fine-tuning strategies. The approach includes capabilities for data curation from diverse sources, processing with complex tools, building training pipelines, and iterative refinement based on evaluation outcomes.
AI.result: FT-Agent achieved the best performance on 10 out of 13 tasks across all five domains, significantly outperforming general-purpose alternatives. Ablation studies demonstrate that the approach generalizes effectively to 3B models, with insights on data scaling trade-offs and backbone sensitivity. Case analyses reveal that agents can recover from failures through cumulative learning from historical experience, while also exposing fundamental limitations in causal reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The results highlight both the promise and current boundaries of autonomous LLM fine-tuning. Purpose-built fine-tuning agents are significantly more effective than general-purpose alternatives, can learn from historical experience to recover from failures, but still face fundamental limitations in causal reasoning capabilities.

title: Navigating Time's Possibilities: Plausible Counterfactual Explanations for Multivariate Time-Series Forecast through Genetic Algorithms
summary: Counterfactual learning has become promising for understanding and modeling causality in complex and dynamic systems. This paper presents a novel method for counterfactual learning in the context of multivariate time series analysis and forecast. The primary objective is to uncover hidden causal relationships and identify potential interventions to achieve desired outcomes. The proposed methodology integrates genetic algorithms and rigorous causality tests to infer and validate counterfactual dependencies within temporal sequences. More specifically, we employ Granger causality to enhance the reliability of identified causal relationships, rigorously assessing their statistical significance. Then, genetic algorithms, in conjunction with quantile regression, are used to exploit these intricate causal relationships to project future scenarios. The synergy between genetic algorithms and causality tests ensures a thorough exploration of the temporal dynamics present in the data, revealing hidden dependencies and enabling the projection of outcomes under hypothetical interventions. We evaluate the performance of our algorithm on real-world data, showcasing its ability to handle complex causal relationships, revealing meaningful counterfactual insights, and allowing for the prediction of outcomes under hypothetical interventions.

AI.tldr: A novel method for counterfactual learning in multivariate time series that integrates genetic algorithms with Granger causality tests and quantile regression to uncover hidden causal relationships and project future scenarios under hypothetical interventions.
AI.motivation: Counterfactual learning is essential for understanding causality in complex, dynamic systems; the goal is to uncover hidden causal relationships and identify interventions that can achieve desired outcomes.
AI.method: The methodology combines genetic algorithms with rigorous causality testing (Granger causality with statistical significance assessment) and quantile regression to infer and validate counterfactual dependencies within temporal sequences.
AI.result: Evaluated on real-world data, the algorithm demonstrates the ability to handle complex causal relationships, reveal meaningful counterfactual insights, and predict outcomes under hypothetical interventions.
AI.conclusion: The synergy between genetic algorithms and causality tests enables thorough exploration of temporal dynamics, revealing hidden dependencies and supporting counterfactual projections.

title: Monocular 3D Object Position Estimation with VLMs for Human-Robot Interaction
comment: Accepted at Workshop on Integrating Image Processing with Large-Scale Language/Vision Models for Advanced Visual Understanding (LVLM) at IEEE International Conference on Image Processing (ICIP) 2025
summary: Pre-trained general-purpose Vision-Language Models (VLM) hold the potential to enhance intuitive human-machine interactions due to their rich world knowledge and 2D object detection capabilities. However, VLMs for 3D coordinates detection tasks are rare. In this work, we investigate interactive abilities of VLMs by returning 3D object positions given a monocular RGB image from a wrist-mounted camera, natural language input, and robot states. We collected and curated a heterogeneous dataset of more than 100,000 images and finetuned a VLM using QLoRA with a custom regression head. By implementing conditional routing, our model maintains its ability to process general visual queries while adding specialized 3D position estimation capabilities. Our results demonstrate robust predictive performance with a median MAE of 13 mm on the test set and a five-fold improvement over a simpler baseline without finetuning. In about 25% of the cases, predictions are within a range considered acceptable for the robot to interact with objects.

AI.tldr: Pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are fine-tuned using QLoRA and conditional routing to detect 3D object positions from monocular RGB images, achieving median MAE of 13 mm and five-fold improvement over baseline.
AI.motivation: Pre-trained VLMs have rich world knowledge and 2D detection capabilities for intuitive human-machine interactions, but VLMs specifically designed for 3D coordinate detection are rare. This work investigates VLM interactive abilities for returning 3D object positions given monocular RGB images, natural language input, and robot states.
AI.method: Collected and curated a heterogeneous dataset of over 100,000 images. Fine-tuned a VLM using QLoRA with a custom regression head. Implemented conditional routing to maintain general visual query processing while adding specialized 3D position estimation capabilities.
AI.result: Robust predictive performance with median MAE of 13 mm on test set. Achieved five-fold improvement over a simpler baseline without finetuning. Approximately 25% of predictions are within acceptable range for robot interaction.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach demonstrates that pre-trained VLMs can be effectively fine-tuned for 3D coordinate detection tasks, maintaining general visual query capabilities while achieving accurate 3D position estimation suitable for robot interaction.

title: Legal RAG Bench: an end-to-end benchmark for legal RAG
summary: We introduce Legal RAG Bench, a benchmark and evaluation methodology for assessing the end-to-end performance of legal RAG systems. As a benchmark, Legal RAG Bench consists of 4,876 passages from the Victorian Criminal Charge Book alongside 100 complex, hand-crafted questions demanding expert knowledge of criminal law and procedure. Both long-form answers and supporting passages are provided. As an evaluation methodology, Legal RAG Bench leverages a full factorial design and novel hierarchical error decomposition framework, enabling apples-to-apples comparisons of the contributions of retrieval and reasoning models in RAG. We evaluate three state-of-the-art embedding models (Isaacus' Kanon 2 Embedder, Google's Gemini Embedding 001, and OpenAI's Text Embedding 3 Large) and two frontier LLMs (Gemini 3.1 Pro and GPT-5.2), finding that information retrieval is the primary driver of legal RAG performance, with LLMs exerting a more moderate effect on correctness and groundedness. Kanon 2 Embedder, in particular, had the largest positive impact on performance, improving average correctness by 17.5 points, groundedness by 4.5 points, and retrieval accuracy by 34 points. We observe that many errors attributed to hallucinations in legal RAG systems are in fact triggered by retrieval failures, concluding that retrieval sets the ceiling for the performance of many modern legal RAG systems. We document why and how we built Legal RAG Bench alongside the results of our evaluations. We also openly release our code and data to assist with reproduction of our findings.

AI.tldr: Legal RAG Bench is a benchmark and evaluation framework for legal RAG systems containing 4,876 Victorian criminal charge passages and 100 complex questions. Using full factorial design and hierarchical error decomposition, the study evaluated three embedding models and two LLMs, finding retrieval is the primary performance driver with Kanon 2 Embedder providing the largest gains (34-point retrieval accuracy improvement).
AI.motivation: The authors recognize the need for rigorous end-to-end evaluation of legal RAG systems, noting that existing methodologies lack apples-to-apples comparisons between retrieval and reasoning components, making it difficult to isolate the true performance drivers in complex legal applications.
AI.method: Legal RAG Bench was constructed with 4,876 passages from the Victorian Criminal Charge Book and 100 hand-crafted complex questions requiring expert legal knowledge. The evaluation employs a full factorial design and hierarchical error decomposition framework to systematically assess retrieval and reasoning contributions. Three embedding models (Kanon 2 Embedder, Gemini Embedding 001, OpenAI Text Embedding 3 Large) and two frontier LLMs (Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.2) were evaluated.
AI.result: Information retrieval was identified as the primary driver of legal RAG performance, with LLMs exerting a more moderate effect on correctness and groundedness. Kanon 2 Embedder showed the largest positive impact, improving average correctness by 17.5 points, groundedness by 4.5 points, and retrieval accuracy by 34 points. Many errors attributed to LLM hallucinations were actually triggered by retrieval failures.
AI.conclusion: Retrieval systems set the performance ceiling for modern legal RAG applications. The findings suggest that improving retrieval components yields greater benefits than optimizing LLMs alone. The authors release all code and data to enable reproduction and further research.

title: Jano: Adaptive Diffusion Generation with Early-stage Convergence Awareness
summary: Diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in generative AI, yet their computational efficiency remains a significant challenge, particularly for Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) requiring intensive full-attention computation. While existing acceleration approaches focus on content-agnostic uniform optimization strategies, we observe that different regions in generated content exhibit heterogeneous convergence patterns during the denoising process. We present Jano, a training-free framework that leverages this insight for efficient region-aware generation. Jano introduces an early-stage complexity recognition algorithm that accurately identifies regional convergence requirements within initial denoising steps, coupled with an adaptive token scheduling runtime that optimizes computational resource allocation. Through comprehensive evaluation on state-of-the-art models, Jano achieves substantial acceleration (average 2.0 times speedup, up to 2.4 times) while preserving generation quality. Our work challenges conventional uniform processing assumptions and provides a practical solution for accelerating large-scale content generation. The source code of our implementation is available at https://github.com/chen-yy20/Jano.

AI.tldr: Jano is a training-free acceleration framework for Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) that uses early-stage complexity recognition and adaptive token scheduling to achieve 2.0× average speedup (up to 2.4×) while preserving generation quality.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models have achieved remarkable success, but computational efficiency remains a challenge, especially for Diffusion Transformers requiring intensive full-attention computation. Existing acceleration approaches focus on content-agnostic uniform optimization strategies, yet the authors observe that different regions in generated content exhibit heterogeneous convergence patterns during the denoising process.
AI.method: Jano introduces an early-stage complexity recognition algorithm that accurately identifies regional convergence requirements within initial denoising steps, coupled with an adaptive token scheduling runtime that optimizes computational resource allocation. The framework operates in a training-free manner.
AI.result: Through comprehensive evaluation on state-of-the-art models, Jano achieves substantial acceleration (average 2.0 times speedup, up to 2.4 times) while preserving generation quality.
AI.conclusion: This work challenges conventional uniform processing assumptions and provides a practical solution for accelerating large-scale content generation.

title: GMP: A Benchmark for Content Moderation under Co-occurring Violations and Dynamic Rules
summary: Online content moderation is essential for maintaining a healthy digital environment, and reliance on AI for this task continues to grow. Consider a user comment using national stereotypes to insult a politician. This example illustrates two critical challenges in real-world scenarios: (1) Co-occurring Violations, where a single post violates multiple policies (e.g., prejudice and personal attacks); (2) Dynamic rules of moderation, where determination of a violation depends on platform-specific guidelines that evolve across contexts . The intersection of co-occurring harms and dynamically changing rules highlights a core limitation of current AI systems: although large language models (LLMs) are adept at following fixed guidelines, their judgment capabilities degrade when policies are unstable or context-dependent . In practice, such shortcomings lead to inconsistent moderation: either erroneously restricting legitimate expression or allowing harmful content to remain online . This raises a critical question for evaluation: Does high performance on existing static benchmarks truly guarantee robust generalization of AI judgment to real-world scenarios involving co-occurring violations and dynamically changing rules?

AI.tldr: This abstract examines limitations of current AI systems in handling online content moderation challenges involving co-occurring policy violations and dynamically changing rules, questioning whether existing static benchmarks truly guarantee robust generalization to real-world scenarios.
AI.motivation: Online content moderation is essential for maintaining digital ecosystem health. However, current AI systems struggle with two critical real-world challenges: (1) Co-occurring violations where a single post violates multiple policies (e.g., prejudice and personal attacks), and (2) Dynamic moderation rules that depend on platform-specific guidelines evolving across contexts. These challenges reveal a core limitation: while large language models (LLMs) excel at following fixed guidelines, their judgment capabilities degrade when policies are unstable or context-dependent, leading to inconsistent moderation outcomes.
AI.method: The authors illustrate these challenges through a concrete example of a user comment employing national stereotypes to insult a politician. This case demonstrates how moderation decisions must handle both co-occurring policy violations and context-dependent rules simultaneously. The abstract positions this as a fundamental limitation in current AI moderation systems that current static evaluation benchmarks fail to capture.
AI.result: Large language models (LLMs) demonstrate strong performance on existing static moderation benchmarks, but this performance does not translate to robust generalization when facing real-world scenarios involving co-occurring violations and dynamically changing rules. When policies become unstable or context-dependent, LLM judgment capabilities degrade, resulting in inconsistent moderation outcomes.
AI.conclusion: The abstract raises a critical evaluation question: Does high performance on existing static benchmarks truly guarantee robust generalization of AI judgment to real-world scenarios involving co-occurring violations and dynamically changing rules? This suggests that current evaluation methodologies may not adequately assess AI systems' capabilities for dynamic, context-sensitive content moderation tasks.

title: Bootstrapping Embeddings for Low Resource Languages
summary: Embedding models are crucial to modern NLP. However, the creation of the most effective models relies on carefully constructed supervised finetuning data. For high resource languages, such as English, such datasets are readily available. However, for hundreds of other languages, they are simply non-existent. We investigate whether the advent of large language models can help to bridge this gap. We test three different strategies for generating synthetic triplet data used to optimise embedding models. These include in-context learning as well as two novel approaches, leveraging adapter composition and cross lingual finetuning of the LLM generator (XL-LoRA) respectively. We find that while in-context learning still falls short of strong non-synthetic baselines, adapter composition and XL-LoRA yield strong performance gains across a wide array of tasks and languages, offering a clear, scalable pathway to producing performant embedding models for a wide variety of languages.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates using large language models to generate synthetic triplet data for training embedding models in low-resource languages, finding that adapter composition and XL-LoRA significantly outperform in-context learning approaches.
AI.motivation: Embedding models are essential for NLP but require supervised fine-tuning data. While high-resource languages like English have abundant datasets, hundreds of other languages lack such resources, creating a significant gap that needs to be bridged.
AI.method: The authors tested three strategies for generating synthetic triplet data to optimize embedding models: (1) in-context learning, (2) adapter composition (a novel approach), and (3) cross-lingual fine-tuning of the LLM generator using XL-LoRA.
AI.result: In-context learning fell short of strong non-synthetic baselines, while adapter composition and XL-LoRA yielded strong performance gains across a wide array of tasks and languages, demonstrating their effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Adapter composition and XL-LoRA provide a clear, scalable pathway for producing performant embedding models for a wide variety of languages, particularly low-resource ones where data is scarce.

title: Mesh-Pro: Asynchronous Advantage-guided Ranking Preference Optimization for Artist-style Quadrilateral Mesh Generation
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has demonstrated remarkable success in text and image generation, yet its potential in 3D generation remains largely unexplored. Existing attempts typically rely on offline direct preference optimization (DPO) method, which suffers from low training efficiency and limited generalization. In this work, we aim to enhance both the training efficiency and generation quality of RL in 3D mesh generation. Specifically, (1) we design the first asynchronous online RL framework tailored for 3D mesh generation post-training efficiency improvement, which is 3.75$\times$ faster than synchronous RL. (2) We propose Advantage-guided Ranking Preference Optimization (ARPO), a novel RL algorithm that achieves a better trade-off between training efficiency and generalization than current RL algorithms designed for 3D mesh generation, such as DPO and group relative policy optimization (GRPO). (3) Based on asynchronous ARPO, we propose Mesh-Pro, which additionally introduces a novel diagonal-aware mixed triangular-quadrilateral tokenization for mesh representation and a ray-based reward for geometric integrity. Mesh-Pro achieves state-of-the-art performance on artistic and dense meshes.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Mesh-Pro, a reinforcement learning framework for 3D mesh generation that combines asynchronous online training with a novel preference optimization algorithm (ARPO) and diagonal-aware mixed triangular-quadrilateral tokenization, achieving 3.75× faster training and state-of-the-art performance on artistic and dense meshes.
AI.motivation: RL has achieved remarkable success in text and image generation, but its potential in 3D mesh generation remains underexplored. Existing approaches like direct preference optimization (DPO) suffer from low training efficiency and limited generalization capabilities. This work aims to address these limitations by improving both training efficiency and generation quality in 3D mesh generation.
AI.method: The authors propose three key contributions: (1) an asynchronous online RL framework for 3D mesh generation that is 3.75× faster than synchronous RL; (2) Advantage-guided Ranking Preference Optimization (ARPO), a new RL algorithm that improves the trade-off between training efficiency and generalization; and (3) Mesh-Pro, which incorporates diagonal-aware mixed triangular-quadrilateral tokenization for mesh representation and a ray-based reward mechanism for geometric integrity.
AI.result: The asynchronous framework achieves 3.75× faster training compared to synchronous RL. ARPO demonstrates a better efficiency-generalization trade-off than existing algorithms like DPO and GRPO. Mesh-Pro achieves state-of-the-art performance on both artistic and dense mesh benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: By combining asynchronous training with novel optimization techniques and mesh representation methods, the proposed framework significantly advances 3D mesh generation quality and training efficiency, establishing a strong foundation for future research in 3D generation using reinforcement learning.

title: GAM-RAG: Gain-Adaptive Memory for Evolving Retrieval in Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) grounds large language models with external evidence, but many implementations rely on pre-built indices that remain static after construction. Related queries therefore repeat similar multi-hop traversal, increasing latency and compute. Motivated by schema-based learning in cognitive neuroscience, we propose GAM-RAG, a training-free framework that accumulates retrieval experience from recurring or related queries and updates retrieval memory over time. GAM-RAG builds a lightweight, relation-free hierarchical index whose links capture potential co-occurrence rather than fixed semantic relations. During inference, successful retrieval episodes provide sentence-level feedback, updating sentence memories so evidence useful for similar reasoning types becomes easier to activate later. To balance stability and adaptability under noisy feedback, we introduce an uncertainty-aware, Kalman-inspired gain rule that jointly updates memory states and perplexity-based uncertainty estimates. It applies fast updates for reliable novel signals and conservative refinement for stable or noisy memories. We provide a theoretical analysis of the update dynamics, and empirically show that GAM-RAG improves average performance by 3.95% over the strongest baseline and by 8.19% with 5-turn memory, while reducing inference cost by 61%. Our code and datasets are available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/GAM_RAG-2EF6.

AI.tldr: GAM-RAG is a training-free framework that accumulates retrieval experience from recurring queries and updates retrieval memory over time using a relation-free hierarchical index and uncertainty-aware Kalman-inspired updates, achieving 3.95% average performance improvement and 61% inference cost reduction.
AI.motivation: RAG implementations rely on static pre-built indices, causing related queries to repeat similar multi-hop traversal and increasing latency/compute. Motivated by schema-based learning in cognitive neuroscience, the authors seek to enable dynamic memory accumulation from recurring queries.
AI.method: GAM-RAG builds a lightweight, relation-free hierarchical index where links capture potential co-occurrence rather than fixed semantic relations. During inference, successful retrieval provides sentence-level feedback that updates sentence memories. A Kalman-inspired uncertainty-aware gain rule jointly updates memory states and perplexity-based uncertainty estimates, applying fast updates for reliable novel signals and conservative refinement for stable/noisy memories.
AI.result: GAM-RAG improves average performance by 3.95% over the strongest baseline and by 8.19% with 5-turn memory, while reducing inference cost by 61%. A theoretical analysis of update dynamics is provided.
AI.conclusion: GAM-RAG effectively balances stability and adaptability under noisy feedback through its uncertainty-aware update mechanism, with code and datasets available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/GAM_RAG-2EF6.

title: Active Flow Matching
summary: Discrete diffusion and flow matching models capture complex, non-additive and non-autoregressive structure in high-dimensional objective landscapes through parallel, iterative refinement. However, their implicit generative nature precludes direct integration with principled variational frameworks for online black-box optimisation, such as variational search distributions (VSD) and conditioning by adaptive sampling (CbAS). We introduce Active Flow Matching (AFM), which reformulates variational objectives to operate on conditional endpoint distributions along the flow, enabling gradient-based steering of flow models toward high-fitness regions while preserving the rigour of VSD and CbAS. We derive forward and reverse Kullback-Leibler (KL) variants using self-normalised importance sampling. Across a suite of online protein and small molecule design tasks, forward-KL AFM consistently performs competitively compared to state-of-the-art baselines, demonstrating effective exploration-exploitation under tight experimental budgets.

AI.tldr: Active Flow Matching (AFM) reformulates variational objectives to operate on conditional endpoint distributions along the flow, enabling gradient-based steering of flow models toward high-fitness regions while preserving the rigor of variational search distributions (VSD) and conditioning by adaptive sampling (CbAS).
AI.motivation: Discrete diffusion and flow matching models capture complex, non-additive and non-autoregressive structure in high-dimensional objective landscapes through parallel, iterative refinement. However, their implicit generative nature precludes direct integration with principled variational frameworks for online black-box optimisation, such as variational search distributions (VSD) and conditioning by adaptive sampling (CbAS).
AI.method: AFM reformulates variational objectives to operate on conditional endpoint distributions along the flow, enabling gradient-based steering of flow models toward high-fitness regions while preserving the rigor of VSD and CbAS. The method derives forward and reverse Kullback-Leibler (KL) variants using self-normalised importance sampling.
AI.result: Forward-KL AFM consistently performs competitively compared to state-of-the-art baselines across a suite of online protein and small molecule design tasks, demonstrating effective exploration-exploitation under tight experimental budgets.
AI.conclusion: AFM successfully bridges the gap between flow models and variational optimization frameworks, enabling principled online black-box optimisation with complex generative models.

title: Integrating LTL Constraints into PPO for Safe Reinforcement Learning
summary: This paper proposes Proximal Policy Optimization with Linear Temporal Logic Constraints (PPO-LTL), a framework that integrates safety constraints written in LTL into PPO for safe reinforcement learning. LTL constraints offer rigorous representations of complex safety requirements, such as regulations that broadly exist in robotics, enabling systematic monitoring of safety requirements. Violations against LTL constraints are monitored by limit-deterministic Büchi automata, and then translated by a logic-to-cost mechanism into penalty signals. The signals are further employed for guiding the policy optimization via the Lagrangian scheme. Extensive experiments on the Zones and CARLA environments show that our PPO-LTL can consistently reduce safety violations, while maintaining competitive performance, against the state-of-the-art methods. The code is at https://github.com/EVIEHub/PPO-LTL.

AI.tldr: PPO-LTL integrates Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) safety constraints into Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) for safe reinforcement learning, using limit-deterministic Büchi automata for monitoring and a Lagrangian scheme to incorporate penalty signals.
AI.motivation: Robotic systems require rigorous representations of complex safety requirements (e.g., regulations). Existing methods lack systematic monitoring capabilities for such constraints, making it challenging to ensure safety while maintaining competitive performance in reinforcement learning.
AI.method: The framework incorporates LTL constraints into PPO by: (1) monitoring violations using limit-deterministic Büchi automata, (2) translating violations into penalty signals via a logic-to-cost mechanism, and (3) guiding policy optimization through a Lagrangian scheme that incorporates these penalties.
AI.result: Experiments on Zones and CARLA environments demonstrate that PPO-LTL consistently reduces safety violations while maintaining competitive performance against state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: PPO-LTL effectively integrates LTL safety constraints into PPO, providing a systematic approach to ensuring safety in reinforcement learning while preserving learning performance. The framework enables rigorous monitoring and handling of complex safety requirements in robotics applications.

title: AnnoABSA: A Web-Based Annotation Tool for Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis with Retrieval-Augmented Suggestions
comment: Accepted for publication at LREC 2026. Final version will appear in the ACL Anthology
summary: We introduce AnnoABSA, the first web-based annotation tool to support the full spectrum of Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA) tasks. The tool is highly customizable, enabling flexible configuration of sentiment elements and task-specific requirements. Alongside manual annotation, AnnoABSA provides optional Large Language Model (LLM)-based retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) suggestions that offer context-aware assistance in a human-in-the-loop approach, keeping the human annotator in control. To improve prediction quality over time, the system retrieves the ten most similar examples that are already annotated and adds them as few-shot examples in the prompt, ensuring that suggestions become increasingly accurate as the annotation process progresses. Released as open-source software under the MIT License, AnnoABSA is freely accessible and easily extendable for research and practical applications.

AI.tldr: AnnoABSA is the first web-based annotation tool for full-spectrum Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA), featuring high customizability and optional LLM-based RAG suggestions with few-shot learning for progressively accurate assistance.
AI.motivation: Existing annotation tools for ABSA lacked support for the full spectrum of tasks and flexibility. The authors aimed to create a customizable tool that enables human-in-the-loop annotation while integrating modern AI assistance through retrieval-augmented generation.
AI.method: AnnoABSA is a web-based tool built with customizable sentiment elements and task-specific configurations. It incorporates LLM-based RAG suggestions that retrieve the ten most similar previously annotated examples as few-shot prompts, progressively improving suggestion accuracy as annotation progresses. The system maintains human control throughout the annotation process.
AI.result: AnnoABSA was released as open-source software under the MIT License, making it freely accessible and easily extendable for both research and practical applications.
AI.conclusion: AnnoABSA addresses the need for flexible, comprehensive ABSA annotation by combining human control with AI-assisted suggestions that improve through few-shot learning, and provides a freely available, extensible solution for the ABSA community.

title: TP-Spikformer: Token Pruned Spiking Transformer
summary: Spiking neural networks (SNNs) offer an energy-efficient alternative to traditional neural networks due to their event-driven computing paradigm. However, recent advancements in spiking transformers have focused on improving accuracy with large-scale architectures, which require significant computational resources and limit deployment on resource-constrained devices. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective token pruning method for spiking transformers, termed TP-Spikformer, that reduces storage and computational overhead while maintaining competitive performance. Specifically, we first introduce a heuristic spatiotemporal information-retaining criterion that comprehensively evaluates tokens' importance, assigning higher scores to informative tokens for retention and lower scores to uninformative ones for pruning. Based on this criterion, we propose an information-retaining token pruning framework that employs a block-level early stopping strategy for uninformative tokens, instead of removing them outright. This also helps preserve more information during token pruning. We demonstrate the effectiveness, efficiency and scalability of TP-Spikformer through extensive experiments across diverse architectures, including Spikformer, QKFormer and Spike-driven Transformer V1 and V3, and a range of tasks such as image classification, object detection, semantic segmentation and event-based object tracking. Particularly, TP-Spikformer performs well in a training-free manner. These results reveal its potential as an efficient and practical solution for deploying SNNs in real-world applications with limited computational resources.

AI.tldr: TP-Spikformer is a training-free token pruning method for spiking transformers that uses a heuristic spatiotemporal information-retaining criterion with block-level early stopping to reduce computational overhead while maintaining competitive performance across multiple architectures and tasks.
AI.motivation: Large-scale spiking transformers focus on accuracy but require significant computational resources, limiting deployment on resource-constrained devices. An energy-efficient pruning method is needed to make SNNs practical for real-world applications.
AI.method: TP-Spikformer introduces a heuristic spatiotemporal information-retaining criterion that evaluates token importance and assigns retention scores. It employs a block-level early stopping strategy for uninformative tokens instead of outright removal to preserve information during pruning.
AI.result: TP-Spikformer demonstrates effectiveness, efficiency, and scalability across diverse architectures (Spikformer, QKFormer, Spike-driven Transformer V1/V3) and tasks (image classification, object detection, semantic segmentation, event-based object tracking), performing competitively in a training-free manner.
AI.conclusion: TP-Spikformer shows strong potential as an efficient and practical solution for deploying SNNs in resource-constrained real-world applications, reducing storage and computational overhead while maintaining competitive accuracy.

title: Incremental, inconsistency-resilient reasoning over Description Logic Abox streams
summary: More and more, data is being produced in a streaming fashion. This has led to increased interest into how actionable insights can be extracted in real time from data streams through Stream Reasoning. Reasoning over data streams raises multiple challenges, notably the high velocity of data, the real time requirement of the reasoning, and the noisy and volatile nature of streams. This paper proposes novel semantics for incremental reasoning over streams of Description Logic ABoxes, in order to tackle these challenges. To address the first two challenges, our semantics for reasoning over sliding windows on streams allow for incrementally computing the materialization of the window based on the materialization of the previous window. Furthermore, to deal with the volatile nature of streams, we present novel semantics for inconsistency repair on such windows, based on preferred repair semantics. We then detail our proposed semi-naive algorithms for incremental materialization maintenance in the case of OWL2 RL, both in the presence of inconsistencies and without.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes novel semantics for incremental reasoning over ABoxes in data streams, addressing challenges like high data velocity, real-time requirements, and stream volatility through sliding window semantics and preferred repair semantics, along with semi-naive algorithms for incremental materialization maintenance in OWL2 RL.
AI.motivation: The increasing production of data in streaming fashion has increased interest in Stream Reasoning, which faces multiple challenges: the high velocity of data, real-time reasoning requirements, and the noisy and volatile nature of streams.
AI.method: The paper proposes novel semantics for incremental reasoning over streams of Description Logic ABoxes, including semantics for reasoning over sliding windows that allow incrementally computing window materialization, and inconsistency repair semantics based on preferred repair semantics. It also details semi-naive algorithms for incremental materialization maintenance in OWL2 RL, both with and without inconsistencies.
AI.result: The paper presents the proposed semantics and semi-naive algorithms for incremental materialization maintenance in OWL2 RL, addressing the challenges of reasoning over data streams.
AI.conclusion: By providing novel semantics for incremental reasoning over ABoxes and efficient algorithms, the paper tackles the key challenges of stream reasoning including high data velocity, real-time requirements, and stream volatility.

title: Knowledge without Wisdom: Measuring Misalignment between LLMs and Intended Impact
summary: LLMs increasingly excel on AI benchmarks, but doing so does not guarantee validity for downstream tasks. This study evaluates the performance of leading foundation models (FMs, i.e., generative pre-trained base LLMs) with out-of-distribution (OOD) tasks of the teaching and learning of schoolchildren. Across all FMs, inter-model behaviors on disparate tasks correlate higher than they do with expert human behaviors on target tasks. These biases shared across LLMs are poorly aligned with downstream measures of teaching quality and often \textit{negatively aligned with learning outcomes}. Further, we find multi-model ensembles, both unanimous model voting and expert-weighting by benchmark performance, further exacerbate misalignment with learning. We measure that 50\% of the variation in misalignment error is shared across foundation models, suggesting that common pretraining accounts for much of the misalignment in these tasks. We demonstrate methods for robustly measuring alignment of complex tasks and provide unique insights into both educational applications of foundation models and to understanding limitations of models.

AI.tldr: LLMs excel on benchmarks but show poor alignment with real-world educational tasks, especially out-of-distribution teaching scenarios. Multi-model ensembles further worsen this misalignment.
AI.motivation: LLMs perform well on AI benchmarks, but this success doesn't guarantee validity for downstream tasks, particularly in education.
AI.method: Evaluates leading foundation models on out-of-distribution (OOD) teaching and learning tasks for schoolchildren. Measures inter-model correlations, misalignment with learning outcomes, and tests multi-model ensembles (unanimous voting and expert-weighted).
AI.result: Across all FMs, inter-model behaviors correlate more than with human expert behaviors. Shared biases across LLMs are negatively aligned with learning outcomes. Multi-model ensembles exacerbate misalignment. 50% of misalignment variation is shared across models, suggesting common pretraining accounts for much of the problem.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates methods for robustly measuring alignment of complex tasks and provides insights into educational applications of foundation models and their limitations.

title: Beyond the Resumé: A Rubric-Aware Automatic Interview System for Information Elicitation
summary: Effective hiring is integral to the success of an organisation, but it is very challenging to find the most suitable candidates because expert evaluation (e.g.\ interviews conducted by a technical manager) are expensive to deploy at scale. Therefore, automated resume scoring and other applicant-screening methods are increasingly used to coarsely filter candidates, making decisions on limited information. We propose that large language models (LLMs) can play the role of subject matter experts to cost-effectively elicit information from each candidate that is nuanced and role-specific, thereby improving the quality of early-stage hiring decisions. We present a system that leverages an LLM interviewer to update belief over an applicant's rubric-oriented latent traits in a calibrated way. We evaluate our system on simulated interviews and show that belief converges towards the simulated applicants' artificially-constructed latent ability levels. We release code, a modest dataset of public-domain/anonymised resumes, belief calibration tests, and simulated interviews, at \href{https://github.com/mbzuai-nlp/beyond-the-resume}{https://github.com/mbzuai-nlp/beyond-the-resume}. Our demo is available at \href{https://btr.hstu.net}{https://btr.hstu.net}.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an LLM-based interview system that elicits nuanced, role-specific information from candidates to improve early-stage hiring decisions by updating beliefs over applicants' latent traits in a calibrated manner.
AI.motivation: Expert evaluation (e.g., technical interviews) is expensive to deploy at scale, while automated resume screening makes decisions based on limited information. The authors propose that LLMs can act as cost-effective subject matter experts to gather more detailed candidate information.
AI.method: A system that leverages an LLM interviewer to update beliefs over an applicant's rubric-oriented latent traits through simulated interviews. The system elicits role-specific information from candidates to better assess their suitability.
AI.result: Evaluated on simulated interviews, the system demonstrates that belief updates converge towards the simulated applicants' artificially-constructed latent ability levels, indicating effective calibration.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can effectively play the role of subject matter experts to elicit nuanced, role-specific information from candidates, thereby improving the quality of early-stage hiring decisions in a cost-effective manner.

title: CaptionFool: Universal Image Captioning Model Attacks
summary: Image captioning models are encoder-decoder architectures trained on large-scale image-text datasets, making them susceptible to adversarial attacks. We present CaptionFool, a novel universal (input-agnostic) adversarial attack against state-of-the-art transformer-based captioning models. By modifying only 7 out of 577 image patches (approximately 1.2% of the image), our attack achieves 94-96% success rate in generating arbitrary target captions, including offensive content. We further demonstrate that CaptionFool can generate "slang" terms specifically designed to evade existing content moderation filters. Our findings expose critical vulnerabilities in deployed vision-language models and underscore the urgent need for robust defenses against such attacks. Warning: This paper contains model outputs which are offensive in nature.

AI.tldr: CaptionFool is a universal adversarial attack that modifies only 1.2% of image patches to generate arbitrary target captions (including offensive content) with 94-96% success rate against transformer-based image captioning models.
AI.motivation: Transformer-based image captioning models are vulnerable to adversarial attacks, and understanding these vulnerabilities is critical for developing robust defenses in deployed vision-language systems.
AI.method: The researchers developed CaptionFool, a universal (input-agnostic) adversarial attack that modifies only 7 out of 577 image patches (~1.2% of the image) to manipulate caption generation.
AI.result: CaptionFool achieves 94-96% success rate in generating arbitrary target captions, including offensive content. It can also generate "slang" terms specifically designed to evade existing content moderation filters.
AI.conclusion: These findings expose critical vulnerabilities in deployed vision-language models, underscoring the urgent need for robust defenses against such adversarial attacks.

title: What Papers Don't Tell You: Recovering Tacit Knowledge for Automated Paper Reproduction
comment: 32 pages (+ appendix), 8 figures. Lehui Li and Ruining Wang contributed equally. Yongshun Gong is the corresponding author
summary: Automated paper reproduction -- generating executable code from academic papers -- is bottlenecked not by information retrieval but by the tacit knowledge that papers inevitably leave implicit. We formalize this challenge as the progressive recovery of three types of tacit knowledge -- relational, somatic, and collective -- and propose \method, a graph-based agent framework with a dedicated mechanism for each: node-level relation-aware aggregation recovers relational knowledge by analyzing implementation-unit-level reuse and adaptation relationships between the target paper and its citation neighbors; execution-feedback refinement recovers somatic knowledge through iterative debugging driven by runtime signals; and graph-level knowledge induction distills collective knowledge from clusters of papers sharing similar implementations. On an extended ReproduceBench spanning 3 domains, 10 tasks, and 40 recent papers, \method{} achieves an average performance gap of 10.04\% against official implementations, improving over the strongest baseline by 24.68\%. The code will be publicly released upon acceptance; the repository link will be provided in the final version.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes \method, a graph-based agent framework that recovers three types of tacit knowledge (relational, somatic, and collective) to enable automated code generation from academic papers, achieving a 10.04% performance gap against official implementations on an extended ReproduceBench spanning 40 recent papers across 3 domains.
AI.motivation: Automated paper reproduction is bottlenecked not by information retrieval but by tacit knowledge that papers inevitably leave implicit, requiring a systematic approach to recover this hidden knowledge for successful code generation.
AI.method: A graph-based agent framework with three dedicated mechanisms: (1) node-level relation-aware aggregation for relational knowledge recovery via implementation-unit-level reuse and adaptation analysis; (2) execution-feedback refinement for somatic knowledge recovery through iterative debugging driven by runtime signals; (3) graph-level knowledge induction for collective knowledge distillation from clusters of similar papers.
AI.result: On an extended ReproduceBench (3 domains, 10 tasks, 40 recent papers), \method{} achieves an average performance gap of 10.04\% against official implementations, improving over the strongest baseline by 24.68\%.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully addresses the challenge of recovering tacit knowledge in automated paper reproduction, demonstrating significant improvements over baselines while maintaining a reasonable performance gap to official implementations.

title: Unifying Language-Action Understanding and Generation for Autonomous Driving
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are emerging as a promising paradigm for end-to-end autonomous driving, valued for their potential to leverage world knowledge and reason about complex driving scenes. However, existing methods suffer from two critical limitations: a persistent misalignment between language instructions and action outputs, and the inherent inefficiency of typical auto-regressive action generation. In this paper, we introduce LinkVLA, a novel architecture that directly addresses these challenges to enhance both alignment and efficiency. First, we establish a structural link by unifying language and action tokens into a shared discrete codebook, processed within a single multi-modal model. This structurally enforces cross-modal consistency from the ground up. Second, to create a deep semantic link, we introduce an auxiliary action understanding objective that trains the model to generate descriptive captions from trajectories, fostering a bidirectional language-action mapping. Finally, we replace the slow, step-by-step generation with a two-step coarse-to-fine generation method C2F that efficiently decodes the action sequence, saving 86% inference time. Experiments on closed-loop driving benchmarks show consistent gains in instruction following accuracy and driving performance, alongside reduced inference latency.

AI.tldr: LinkVLA introduces a novel VLA architecture for autonomous driving that unifies language and action tokens into a shared discrete codebook for structural alignment, employs an auxiliary action understanding objective for bidirectional semantic mapping, and replaces inefficient auto-regressive generation with a two-step coarse-to-fine (C2F) method, achieving 86% faster inference while improving instruction following accuracy and driving performance.
AI.motivation: Existing VLA methods suffer from two critical limitations: (1) persistent misalignment between language instructions and action outputs, and (2) the inherent inefficiency of typical auto-regressive action generation, which hampers their practical applicability in autonomous driving.
AI.method: LinkVLA addresses these challenges through three key innovations: (1) structural alignment by unifying language and action tokens into a shared discrete codebook processed within a single multi-modal model; (2) semantic alignment via an auxiliary action understanding objective that trains the model to generate descriptive captions from trajectories, creating a bidirectional language-action mapping; (3) efficiency enhancement through a two-step coarse-to-fine (C2F) generation method that replaces slow step-by-step auto-regressive generation.
AI.result: Experiments on closed-loop driving benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in instruction following accuracy and driving performance, alongside a 86% reduction in inference time compared to baseline auto-regressive methods.
AI.conclusion: LinkVLA effectively addresses both alignment and efficiency challenges in VLA models for autonomous driving, providing a practical solution that balances high-quality instruction following with fast inference, making it a promising approach for real-world deployment.

title: FreeAct: Freeing Activations for LLM Quantization
summary: Quantization is pivotal for mitigating the significant memory and computational overhead of Large Language Models (LLMs). While emerging transformation-based methods have successfully enhanced quantization by projecting feature spaces onto smoother manifolds using orthogonal matrices, they typically enforce a rigid one-to-one transformation constraint. This static approach fails to account for the dynamic patterns inherent in input activations, particularly within diffusion LLMs (dLLMs) and Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs), where varying token types exhibit distinct distributions. To advance this, we propose FreeAct, a novel quantization framework that relaxes the static one-to-one constraint to accommodate dynamic activation disparities. Theoretically, we leverage the rank-deficient nature of activations to derive a solution space that extends beyond simple inverse matrices, enabling the decoupling of activation transformations from weights. Methodologically, FreeAct identifies token-specific dynamics (i.e., vision v.s. text, or masked tokens) and allocates distinct transformation matrices to the activation side, while maintaining a unified, static transformation for the weights. Extensive experiments across dLLMs and MLLMs demonstrate that FreeAct significantly outperforms baselines, up to 5.3% performance improvement, with in-depth analyses. Our code will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: FreeAct introduces a dynamic quantization framework for LLMs that relaxes the rigid one-to-one transformation constraint to accommodate token-specific activation disparities, achieving up to 5.3% performance improvement over baselines in diffusion and multimodal LLMs.
AI.motivation: Quantization reduces memory/computation overhead in LLMs, but existing transformation-based methods enforce a static one-to-one constraint that fails to account for dynamic patterns in input activations, particularly in diffusion LLMs (dLLMs) and Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) where varying token types exhibit distinct distributions.
AI.method: FreeAct relaxes the static transformation constraint by leveraging the rank-deficient nature of activations to derive a solution space beyond simple inverse matrices. It identifies token-specific dynamics (e.g., vision vs. text, masked tokens) and allocates distinct transformation matrices to the activation side while maintaining a unified, static transformation for the weights.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across dLLMs and MLLMs demonstrate that FreeAct significantly outperforms baselines, achieving up to 5.3% performance improvement with in-depth analyses.
AI.conclusion: FreeAct successfully decouples activation transformations from weights through dynamic token-specific matrix allocation, demonstrating that relaxing rigid one-to-one constraints can substantially improve quantization performance for complex LLM architectures.

title: RAFM: Retrieval-Augmented Flow Matching for Unpaired CBCT-to-CT Translation
summary: Cone-beam CT (CBCT) is routinely acquired in radiotherapy but suffers from severe artifacts and unreliable Hounsfield Unit (HU) values, limiting its direct use for dose calculation. Synthetic CT (sCT) generation from CBCT is therefore an important task, yet paired CBCT--CT data are often unavailable or unreliable due to temporal gaps, anatomical variation, and registration errors. In this work, we introduce rectified flow (RF) into unpaired CBCT-to-CT translation in medical imaging. Although RF is theoretically compatible with unpaired learning through distribution-level coupling and deterministic transport, its practical effectiveness under small medical datasets and limited batch sizes remains underexplored. Direct application with random or batch-local pseudo pairing can produce unstable supervision due to semantically mismatched endpoint samples. To address this challenge, we propose Retrieval-Augmented Flow Matching (RAFM), which adapts RF to the medical setting by constructing retrieval-guided pseudo pairs using a frozen DINOv3 encoder and a global CT memory bank. This strategy improves empirical coupling quality and stabilizes unpaired flow-based training. Experiments on SynthRAD2023 under a strict subject-level true-unpaired protocol show that RAFM outperforms existing methods across FID, MAE, SSIM, PSNR, and SegScore. The code is available at https://github.com/HiLab-git/RAFM.git.

AI.tldr: RAFM (Retrieval-Augmented Flow Matching) uses rectified flow with retrieval-guided pseudo pairs (via DINOv3 encoder and CT memory bank) to enable stable unpaired CBCT-to-CT translation, outperforming existing methods across multiple metrics on SynthRAD2023.
AI.motivation: CBCT suffers from artifacts and unreliable HU values, limiting direct dose calculation. Paired CBCT-CT data are often unavailable/unreliable due to temporal gaps, anatomical variation, and registration errors, making sCT generation challenging.
AI.method: Introduces rectified flow (RF) for unpaired CBCT-to-CT translation. Proposes RAFM which constructs retrieval-guided pseudo pairs using a frozen DINOv3 encoder and global CT memory bank to improve empirical coupling quality and stabilize training.
AI.result: On SynthRAD2023 under strict subject-level true-unpaired protocol, RAFM outperforms existing methods across all evaluated metrics: FID, MAE, SSIM, PSNR, and SegScore.
AI.conclusion: Retrieval-augmented pseudo pairing effectively adapts RF to medical imaging, stabilizing unpaired flow-based training and improving sCT generation quality even with small medical datasets.

title: Emerging Human-like Strategies for Semantic Memory Foraging in Large Language Models
summary: Both humans and Large Language Models (LLMs) store a vast repository of semantic memories. In humans, efficient and strategic access to this memory store is a critical foundation for a variety of cognitive functions. Such access has long been a focus of psychology and the computational mechanisms behind it are now well characterized. Much of this understanding has been gleaned from a widely-used neuropsychological and cognitive science assessment called the Semantic Fluency Task (SFT), which requires the generation of as many semantically constrained concepts as possible. Our goal is to apply mechanistic interpretability techniques to bring greater rigor to the study of semantic memory foraging in LLMs. To this end, we present preliminary results examining SFT as a case study. A central focus is on convergent and divergent patterns of generative memory search, which in humans play complementary strategic roles in efficient memory foraging. We show that these same behavioral signatures, critical to human performance on the SFT, also emerge as identifiable patterns in LLMs across distinct layers. Potentially, this analysis provides new insights into how LLMs may be adapted into closer cognitive alignment with humans, or alternatively, guided toward productive cognitive \emph{disalignment} to enhance complementary strengths in human-AI interaction.

AI.tldr: This study applies mechanistic interpretability to analyze convergent and divergent memory search patterns in Large Language Models using the Semantic Fluency Task, finding that human-like behavioral signatures emerge across LLM layers.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to bring greater rigor to the study of semantic memory in LLMs by applying mechanistic interpretability techniques. Drawing from extensive human cognitive science research on memory foraging, particularly the Semantic Fluency Task, they seek to understand how LLMs process and retrieve semantic information.
AI.method: The researchers use the Semantic Fluency Task (SFT) as a case study to examine generative memory search patterns in LLMs. They employ mechanistic interpretability techniques to identify convergent and divergent patterns of memory search across different layers of LLMs, comparing these patterns to human cognitive mechanisms.
AI.result: The study finds that the behavioral signatures critical to human performance on the SFT—specifically convergent and divergent patterns of generative memory search—also emerge as identifiable patterns in LLMs across distinct layers.
AI.conclusion: This analysis provides insights for adapting LLMs toward cognitive alignment with humans, or alternatively, guiding them toward productive cognitive disalignment to enhance complementary strengths in human-AI interaction.

title: Evaluating AI Grading on Real-World Handwritten College Mathematics: A Large-Scale Study Toward a Benchmark
summary:
Grading in large undergraduate STEM courses often yields minimal feedback due to heavy instructional workloads. We present a large-scale empirical study of AI grading on real, handwritten single-variable calculus work from UC Irvine. Using OCR-conditioned large language models with structured, rubric-guided prompting, our system produces scores and formative feedback for thousands of free-response quiz submissions from nearly 800 students. In a setting with no single ground-truth label, we evaluate performance against official teaching-assistant grades, student surveys, and independent human review, finding strong alignment with TA scoring and a large majority of AI-generated feedback rated as correct or acceptable across quizzes.
  Beyond calculus, this setting highlights core challenges in OCR-conditioned mathematical reasoning and partial-credit assessment. We analyze key failure modes, propose practical rubric- and prompt-design principles, and introduce a multi-perspective evaluation protocol for reliable, real-course deployment. Building on the dataset and evaluation framework developed here, we outline a standardized benchmark for AI grading of handwritten mathematics to support reproducible comparison and future research.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a large-scale empirical study of AI grading for handwritten single-variable calculus work, using OCR-conditioned LLMs with structured, rubric-guided prompting to produce scores and formative feedback for thousands of student submissions from nearly 800 students.
AI.motivation: Grading in large undergraduate STEM courses often yields minimal feedback due to heavy instructional workloads; the authors sought to explore whether AI could effectively handle this scale.
AI.method: The researchers used OCR-conditioned large language models with structured, rubric-guided prompting to grade handwritten single-variable calculus work. Performance was evaluated against official teaching-assistant grades, student surveys, and independent human review in a setting without ground-truth labels.
AI.result: The system demonstrated strong alignment with TA scoring, and a large majority of AI-generated feedback was rated as correct or acceptable. The study identified key failure modes related to OCR-conditioned mathematical reasoning and partial-credit assessment.
AI.conclusion: The authors propose practical rubric- and prompt-design principles, introduce a multi-perspective evaluation protocol for real-course deployment, and outline a standardized benchmark for AI grading of handwritten mathematics to support reproducible comparison and future research.

title: LLM-as-an-Annotator: Training Lightweight Models with LLM-Annotated Examples for Aspect Sentiment Tuple Prediction
comment: Accepted for publication at LREC 2026. Final version will appear in the ACL Anthology
summary: Training models for Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA) tasks requires manually annotated data, which is expensive and time-consuming to obtain. This paper introduces LA-ABSA, a novel approach that leverages Large Language Model (LLM)-generated annotations to fine-tune lightweight models for complex ABSA tasks. We evaluate our approach on five datasets for Target Aspect Sentiment Detection (TASD) and Aspect Sentiment Quad Prediction (ASQP). Our approach outperformed previously reported augmentation strategies and achieved competitive performance with LLM-prompting in low-resource scenarios, while providing substantial energy efficiency benefits. For example, using 50 annotated examples for in-context learning (ICL) to guide the annotation of unlabeled data, LA-ABSA achieved an F1 score of 49.85 for ASQP on the SemEval Rest16 dataset, closely matching the performance of ICL prompting with Gemma-3-27B (51.10), while requiring significantly lower computational resources.

AI.tldr: LA-ABSA uses LLM-generated annotations to fine-tune lightweight models for ABSA tasks, outperforming previous augmentation strategies and matching LLM prompting performance while being far more energy-efficient.
AI.motivation: Training ABSA models requires expensive and time-consuming manual annotations, creating a bottleneck for developing effective models.
AI.method: LA-ABSA leverages Large Language Model (LLM)-generated annotations to fine-tune lightweight models for complex ABSA tasks, evaluated on five datasets for TASD and ASQP.
AI.result: LA-ABSA outperformed previous augmentation strategies and achieved competitive performance with LLM-prompting in low-resource scenarios (F1=49.85 vs Gemma-3-27B's 51.10 for ASQP on SemEval Rest16), while requiring significantly lower computational resources.
AI.conclusion: LA-ABSA provides a more efficient alternative to LLM prompting for ABSA tasks in low-resource settings, offering comparable accuracy with substantially reduced energy consumption and computational costs.

title: Adaptive Dynamic Dehazing via Instruction-Driven and Task-Feedback Closed-Loop Optimization for Diverse Downstream Task Adaptation
summary: In real-world vision systems,haze removal is required not only to enhance image visibility but also to meet the specific needs of diverse downstream tasks.To address this challenge,we propose a novel adaptive dynamic dehazing framework that incorporates a closed-loop optimization mechanism.It enables feedback-driven refinement based on downstream task performance and user instruction-guided adjustment during inference,allowing the model to satisfy the specific requirements of multiple downstream tasks without retraining.Technically,our framework integrates two complementary and innovative mechanisms: (1)a task feedback loop that dynamically modulates dehazing outputs based on performance across multiple downstream tasks,and (2) a text instruction interface that allows users to specify high-level task preferences.This dual-guidance strategy enables the model to adapt its dehazing behavior after training,tailoring outputs in real time to the evolving needs of multiple tasks.Extensive experiments across various vision tasks demonstrate the strong effectiveness,robustness,and generalizability of our approach.These results establish a new paradigm for interactive,task-adaptive dehazing that actively collaborates with downstream applications.

AI.tldr: A novel adaptive dynamic dehazing framework with closed-loop optimization that enables feedback-driven refinement and user instruction-guided adjustment for task-specific dehazing across multiple downstream vision applications.
AI.motivation: Haze removal in real-world vision systems must satisfy not only enhanced visibility but also the specific requirements of diverse downstream tasks, requiring models to adapt to varying task needs without retraining.
AI.method: The framework integrates two complementary mechanisms: (1) a task feedback loop that dynamically modulates dehazing outputs based on performance across multiple downstream tasks, and (2) a text instruction interface that allows users to specify high-level task preferences, enabling post-training adaptation and real-time output tailoring.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across various vision tasks demonstrate the strong effectiveness, robustness, and generalizability of the proposed approach.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a new paradigm for interactive, task-adaptive dehazing that actively collaborates with downstream applications, moving beyond static dehazing models to adaptive systems that can respond to evolving task requirements.

title: CoVe: Training Interactive Tool-Use Agents via Constraint-Guided Verification
summary: Developing multi-turn interactive tool-use agents is challenging because real-world user needs are often complex and ambiguous, yet agents must execute deterministic actions to satisfy them. To address this gap, we introduce \textbf{CoVe} (\textbf{Co}nstraint-\textbf{Ve}rification), a post-training data synthesis framework designed for training interactive tool-use agents while ensuring both data complexity and correctness. CoVe begins by defining explicit task constraints, which serve a dual role: they guide the generation of complex trajectories and act as deterministic verifiers for assessing trajectory quality. This enables the creation of high-quality training trajectories for supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and the derivation of accurate reward signals for reinforcement learning (RL). Our evaluation on the challenging $τ^2$-bench benchmark demonstrates the effectiveness of the framework. Notably, our compact \textbf{CoVe-4B} model achieves success rates of 43.0\% and 59.4\% in the Airline and Retail domains, respectively; its overall performance significantly outperforms strong baselines of similar scale and remains competitive with models up to $17\times$ its size. These results indicate that CoVe provides an effective and efficient pathway for synthesizing training data for state-of-the-art interactive tool-use agents.
