To support future research, we open-source our code, trained model, and the full set of 12K high-quality trajectories used for training.

AI.tldr: CoVe is a post-training data synthesis framework for interactive tool-use agents that uses explicit task constraints to generate complex training trajectories and verify their correctness, enabling efficient training of small models (e.g., 4B parameters) that outperform larger baselines on the τ²-bench benchmark.
AI.motivation: Multi-turn interactive tool-use agents face a fundamental challenge: real-world user needs are complex and ambiguous, yet agents must execute deterministic actions to satisfy them. Existing approaches struggle to balance data complexity with correctness in training data.
AI.method: CoVe defines explicit task constraints that serve dual purposes: (1) guiding the generation of complex, multi-turn trajectories for supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and (2) acting as deterministic verifiers to assess trajectory quality and derive accurate reward signals for reinforcement learning (RL). The framework enables high-quality training data synthesis through constraint-based generation and verification.
AI.result: On the challenging τ²-bench benchmark, CoVe-4B achieves 43.0% success rate in the Airline domain and 59.4% in the Retail domain. Its performance significantly outperforms strong baselines of similar scale and remains competitive with models up to 17× its size (e.g., 68B parameters), demonstrating that small models can achieve state-of-the-art performance through effective data synthesis.
AI.conclusion: CoVe provides an effective and efficient pathway for synthesizing training data for state-of-the-art interactive tool-use agents. By combining constraint-guided trajectory generation with deterministic verification, the framework enables both data complexity and correctness, making it feasible to train high-performing agents even with compact model sizes. The authors open-source their code, trained models, and the full set of 12K high-quality trajectories to support future research.

title: Principled Fast and Meta Knowledge Learners for Continual Reinforcement Learning
summary: Inspired by the human learning and memory system, particularly the interplay between the hippocampus and cerebral cortex, this study proposes a dual-learner framework comprising a fast learner and a meta learner to address continual Reinforcement Learning~(RL) problems. These two learners are coupled to perform distinct yet complementary roles: the fast learner focuses on knowledge transfer, while the meta learner ensures knowledge integration. In contrast to traditional multi-task RL approaches that share knowledge through average return maximization, our meta learner incrementally integrates new experiences by explicitly minimizing catastrophic forgetting, thereby supporting efficient cumulative knowledge transfer for the fast learner. To facilitate rapid adaptation in new environments, we introduce an adaptive meta warm-up mechanism that selectively harnesses past knowledge. We conduct experiments in various pixel-based and continuous control benchmarks, revealing the superior performance of continual learning for our proposed dual-learner approach relative to baseline methods. The code is released in https://github.com/datake/FAME.

AI.tldr: A dual-learner framework (fast learner + meta learner) inspired by human hippocampus-cortex interplay for continual RL, featuring adaptive meta warm-up and explicit catastrophic forgetting minimization to enable efficient knowledge transfer and integration across tasks.
AI.motivation: Human learning/memory system involves hippocampus-cortex interplay; traditional multi-task RL shares knowledge through average return maximization but struggles with catastrophic forgetting and efficient cumulative knowledge transfer in continual learning scenarios.
AI.method: Proposes a dual-learner framework with a fast learner (knowledge transfer focus) and meta learner (knowledge integration focus) that minimizes catastrophic forgetting to support cumulative knowledge transfer. Introduces adaptive meta warm-up mechanism to selectively harness past knowledge for rapid adaptation in new environments.
AI.result: Superior performance across various pixel-based and continuous control benchmarks compared to baseline continual learning methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dual-learner approach effectively addresses continual RL problems by enabling efficient knowledge transfer between fast and meta learners through explicit catastrophic forgetting minimization and adaptive meta warm-up.

title: Pri4R: Learning World Dynamics for Vision-Language-Action Models with Privileged 4D Representation
summary: Humans learn not only how their bodies move, but also how the surrounding world responds to their actions. In contrast, while recent Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models exhibit impressive semantic understanding, they often fail to capture the spatiotemporal dynamics governing physical interaction. In this paper, we introduce Pri4R, a simple yet effective approach that endows VLA models with an implicit understanding of world dynamics by leveraging privileged 4D information during training. Specifically, Pri4R augments VLAs with a lightweight point track head that predicts 3D point tracks. By injecting VLA features into this head to jointly predict future 3D trajectories, the model learns to incorporate evolving scene geometry within its shared representation space, enabling more physically aware context for precise control. Due to its architectural simplicity, Pri4R is compatible with dominant VLA design patterns with minimal changes. During inference, we run the model using the original VLA architecture unchanged; Pri4R adds no extra inputs, outputs, or computational overhead. Across simulation and real-world evaluations, Pri4R significantly improves performance on challenging manipulation tasks, including a +10% gain on LIBERO-Long and a +40% gain on RoboCasa. We further show that 3D point track prediction is an effective supervision target for learning action-world dynamics, and validate our design choices through extensive ablations.

AI.tldr: Pri4R enhances Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models with 3D point track prediction during training to improve their understanding of physical interaction dynamics, achieving +10% on LIBERO-Long and +40% on RoboCasa.
AI.motivation: Humans learn both body movement and world response to actions, but current VLA models excel at semantic understanding yet fail to capture the spatiotemporal dynamics governing physical interaction. The key challenge is incorporating evolving scene geometry into the model's shared representation space.
AI.method: Pri4R augments VLAs with a lightweight point track head that predicts 3D point tracks during training. By injecting VLA features into this head to jointly predict future 3D trajectories, the model learns to incorporate evolving scene geometry. The approach is architecturally simple, compatible with dominant VLA designs with minimal changes, and adds no extra inputs, outputs, or computational overhead during inference.
AI.result: Pri4R significantly improves performance on challenging manipulation tasks across simulation and real-world evaluations: +10% gain on LIBERO-Long and +40% gain on RoboCasa. Ablation studies confirm that 3D point track prediction is an effective supervision target for learning action-world dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that 3D point track prediction serves as an effective supervision signal for learning action-world dynamics. Pri4R's design—simple, compatible, and inference-efficient—proves that adding temporal dynamics understanding through lightweight geometric prediction can substantially enhance robotic manipulation capabilities without architectural complexity or runtime overhead.

title: nchellwig at SemEval-2026 Task 3: Self-Consistent Structured Generation (SCSG) for Dimensional Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis using Large Language Models
summary: We present Self-Consistent Structured Generation (SCSG) for Dimensional Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis in SemEval-2026 Task 3 (Track A). SCSG enhances prediction reliability by executing a LoRA-adapted large language model multiple times per instance, retaining only tuples that achieve a majority consensus across runs. To mitigate the computational overhead of multiple forward passes, we leverage vLLM's PagedAttention mechanism for efficient key--value cache reuse. Evaluation across 6 languages and 8 language--domain combinations demonstrates that self-consistency with 15 executions yields statistically significant improvements over single-inference prompting, with our system (leveraging Gemma 3) ranking in the top seven across all settings, achieving second place on three out of four English subsets and first place on Tatar-Restaurant for DimASTE.

AI.tldr: Self-Consistent Structured Generation (SCSG) for Dimensional Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis using LoRA-adapted LLMs with majority voting across 15 executions and vLLM's PagedAttention for efficient KV cache reuse
AI.motivation: To enhance prediction reliability in Dimensional Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (DimASTE) through self-consistency techniques
AI.method: Executes a LoRA-adapted large language model multiple times per instance, retains only tuples achieving majority consensus across runs, and leverages vLLM's PagedAttention mechanism for efficient key-value cache reuse
AI.result: Self-consistency with 15 executions yields statistically significant improvements over single-inference prompting; system ranked in top seven across all settings, achieved second place on three English subsets and first place on Tatar-Restaurant
AI.conclusion: Self-consistency with majority voting and efficient KV cache management is an effective approach for improving performance in multi-lingual, multi-domain sentiment analysis tasks

title: Cross-Scale Pansharpening via ScaleFormer and the PanScale Benchmark
summary: Pansharpening aims to generate high-resolution multi-spectral images by fusing the spatial detail of panchromatic images with the spectral richness of low-resolution MS data. However, most existing methods are evaluated under limited, low-resolution settings, limiting their generalization to real-world, high-resolution scenarios. To bridge this gap, we systematically investigate the data, algorithmic, and computational challenges of cross-scale pansharpening. We first introduce PanScale, the first large-scale, cross-scale pansharpening dataset, accompanied by PanScale-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating generalization across varying resolutions and scales. To realize scale generalization, we propose ScaleFormer, a novel architecture designed for multi-scale pansharpening. ScaleFormer reframes generalization across image resolutions as generalization across sequence lengths: it tokenizes images into patch sequences of the same resolution but variable length proportional to image scale. A Scale-Aware Patchify module enables training for such variations from fixed-size crops. ScaleFormer then decouples intra-patch spatial feature learning from inter-patch sequential dependency modeling, incorporating Rotary Positional Encoding to enhance extrapolation to unseen scales. Extensive experiments show that our approach outperforms SOTA methods in fusion quality and cross-scale generalization. The datasets and source code are available upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces PanScale, a large-scale cross-scale pansharpening dataset, and ScaleFormer, a novel architecture that generalizes pansharpening across different image resolutions by treating resolution as sequence length variation.
AI.motivation: Existing pansharpening methods are evaluated under limited, low-resolution settings, limiting their generalization to real-world, high-resolution scenarios. The authors aim to systematically investigate the data, algorithmic, and computational challenges of cross-scale pansharpening.
AI.method: The authors introduce PanScale (first large-scale, cross-scale pansharpening dataset) and PanScale-Bench (comprehensive benchmark). They propose ScaleFormer, which tokenizes images into patch sequences of the same resolution but variable length proportional to image scale, with a Scale-Aware Patchify module. ScaleFormer decouples intra-patch spatial feature learning from inter-patch sequential dependency modeling using Rotary Positional Encoding.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that ScaleFormer outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both fusion quality and cross-scale generalization.
AI.conclusion: This work bridges the gap between low-resolution evaluation settings and real-world high-resolution scenarios, providing a new dataset and architecture for better generalization in pansharpening tasks, with datasets and source code available upon acceptance.

title: LiveCultureBench: a Multi-Agent, Multi-Cultural Benchmark for Large Language Models in Dynamic Social Simulations
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents, yet evaluations focus primarily on task success rather than cultural appropriateness or evaluator reliability. We introduce LiveCultureBench, a multi-cultural, dynamic benchmark that embeds LLMs as agents in a simulated town and evaluates them on both task completion and adherence to socio-cultural norms. The simulation models a small city as a location graph with synthetic residents having diverse demographic and cultural profiles. Each episode assigns one resident a daily goal while others provide social context. An LLM-based verifier generates structured judgments on norm violations and task progress, which we aggregate into metrics capturing task-norm trade-offs and verifier uncertainty. Using LiveCultureBench across models and cultural profiles, we study (i) cross-cultural robustness of LLM agents, (ii) how they balance effectiveness against norm sensitivity, and (iii) when LLM-as-a-judge evaluation is reliable for automated benchmarking versus when human oversight is needed.

AI.tldr: LiveCultureBench introduces a multi-cultural, dynamic benchmark that embeds LLM agents in a simulated town, evaluating both task completion and socio-cultural norm adherence through LLM-based verification, enabling analysis of cross-cultural robustness, effectiveness-norm trade-offs, and the reliability of LLM-as-a-judge evaluation.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents, but existing evaluations focus primarily on task success rather than cultural appropriateness or evaluator reliability, creating a gap in understanding how these models perform across different cultural contexts and adhere to socio-cultural norms.
AI.method: LiveCultureBench models a small city as a location graph with synthetic residents having diverse demographic and cultural profiles. Each episode assigns one resident a daily goal while others provide social context. An LLM-based verifier generates structured judgments on norm violations and task progress, aggregating these into metrics capturing task-norm trade-offs and verifier uncertainty.
AI.result: The benchmark enables studying (i) cross-cultural robustness of LLM agents, (ii) how they balance effectiveness against norm sensitivity, and (iii) the reliability of LLM-as-a-judge evaluation for automated benchmarking versus when human oversight is needed.
AI.conclusion: LiveCultureBench provides a comprehensive framework for evaluating cultural appropriateness of LLM agents, revealing important insights into cross-cultural performance and establishing criteria for when automated evaluation is sufficient versus when human oversight is necessary for reliable benchmarking.

title: When Does Margin Clamping Affect Training Variance? Dataset-Dependent Effects in Contrastive Forward-Forward Learning
summary: Contrastive Forward-Forward (CFF) learning trains Vision Transformers layer by layer against supervised contrastive objectives. CFF training can be sensitive to random seed, but the sources of this instability are poorly understood. We focus on one implementation detail: the positive-pair margin in the contrastive loss is applied through saturating similarity clamping, $\min(s + m,\, 1)$. We prove that an alternative formulation, subtracting the margin after the log-probability, is gradient-neutral under the mean-over-positives reduction. On CIFAR-10 ($2 \times 2$ factorial, $n{=}7$ seeds per cell), clamping produces $5.90\times$ higher pooled test-accuracy variance ($p{=}0.003$) with no difference in mean accuracy. Analyses of clamp activation rates, layerwise gradient norms, and a reduced-margin probe point to saturation-driven gradient truncation at early layers. The effect does not transfer cleanly to other datasets: on CIFAR-100, SVHN, and Fashion-MNIST, clamping produces equal or lower variance. Two factors account for the discrepancy. First, positive-pair density per batch controls how often saturation occurs. Second, task difficulty compresses seed-to-seed spread when accuracy is high. An SVHN difficulty sweep confirms the interaction on a single dataset, with the variance ratio moving from $0.25\times$ at high accuracy to $16.73\times$ under aggressive augmentation. In moderate-accuracy regimes with many same-class pairs per batch, switching to the gradient-neutral subtraction reference removes this variance inflation at no cost to mean accuracy. Measuring the layer-0 clamp activation rate serves as a simple check for whether the problem applies.

AI.tldr: Contrastive Forward-Forward (CFF) learning suffers from high variance in test accuracy due to a saturating similarity clamping implementation detail. The authors prove an alternative gradient-neutral margin subtraction formulation and show it eliminates this variance on CIFAR-10 without affecting mean accuracy.
AI.motivation: CFF training is sensitive to random seeds, but the sources of this instability are poorly understood.
AI.method: The authors analyze the positive-pair margin in the contrastive loss, comparing saturating similarity clamping (min(s+m, 1)) with an alternative gradient-neutral formulation (subtracting margin after log-probability). They conduct 2×2 factorial experiments with n=7 seeds per cell on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, SVHN, and Fashion-MNIST, examining clamp activation rates, layerwise gradient norms, and reduced-margin probes.
AI.result: On CIFAR-10, clamping produces 5.90× higher pooled test-accuracy variance (p=0.003) with no difference in mean accuracy. Analyses point to saturation-driven gradient truncation at early layers. The effect does not transfer cleanly to other datasets due to positive-pair density per batch and task difficulty compressing seed-to-seed spread when accuracy is high.
AI.conclusion: In moderate-accuracy regimes with many same-class pairs per batch, switching to the gradient-neutral subtraction reference removes variance inflation at no cost to mean accuracy. Measuring the layer-0 clamp activation rate serves as a simple diagnostic check for whether the problem applies.

title: Semantic Novelty Trajectories in 80,000 Books: A Cross-Corpus Embedding Analysis
summary: I apply Schmidhuber's compression progress theory of interestingness at corpus scale, analyzing semantic novelty trajectories in more than 80,000 books spanning two centuries of English-language publishing. Using sentence-transformer paragraph embeddings and a running-centroid novelty measure, I compare 28,730 pre-1920 Project Gutenberg books (PG19) against 52,796 modern English books (Books3, approximately 1990-2010). The principal findings are fourfold. First, mean paragraph-level novelty is roughly 10% higher in modern books (0.503 vs. 0.459). Second, trajectory circuitousness -- the ratio of cumulative path length to net displacement in embedding space -- nearly doubles in the modern corpus (+67%). Third, convergent narrative curves, in which novelty declines toward a settled semantic register, are 2.3x more common in pre-1920 literature. Fourth, novelty is orthogonal to reader quality ratings (r = -0.002), suggesting that interestingness in Schmidhuber's sense is structurally independent of perceived literary merit. Clustering paragraph-level trajectories via PAA-16 representations reveals eight distinct narrative-shape archetypes whose distribution shifts substantially between eras. All analysis code and an interactive exploration toolkit are publicly available at https://bigfivekiller.online/novelty_hub.

AI.tldr: Applying Schmidhuber's compression progress theory of interestingness to 80,000+ books reveals that modern literature exhibits ~10% higher paragraph-level novelty, nearly twice as circuitous semantic trajectories, and that interestingness is structurally independent of reader quality ratings.
AI.motivation: The authors seek to scale Schmidhuber's compression progress theory of interestingness to analyze semantic novelty trajectories across a large corpus of English literature spanning two centuries, moving beyond individual book analyses.
AI.method: Using sentence-transformer paragraph embeddings and running-centroid novelty measures, the authors compare 28,730 pre-1920 Project Gutenberg books (PG19) against 52,796 modern Books3 books. They analyze mean paragraph-level novelty, trajectory circuitousness (ratio of cumulative path length to net displacement), convergent narrative curves, and cluster trajectories via PAA-16 representations to identify narrative-shape archetypes.
AI.result: Four principal findings: (1) mean paragraph-level novelty is ~10% higher in modern books (0.503 vs. 0.459); (2) trajectory circuitousness nearly doubles in modern corpus (+67%); (3) convergent narrative curves are 2.3x more common in pre-1920 literature; (4) novelty is orthogonal to reader quality ratings (r = -0.002). Clustering reveals eight distinct narrative-shape archetypes with substantial distribution shifts between eras.
AI.conclusion: Schmidhuber's notion of interestingness is structurally independent of perceived literary merit. The analysis identifies eight narrative-shape archetypes whose distributions have shifted significantly between pre-1920 and modern literature, suggesting fundamental changes in how narrative novelty is organized across eras.

title: Multiple Inputs and Mixwd data for Alzheimer's Disease Classification Based on 3D Vision Transformer
summary: The current methods for diagnosing Alzheimer Disease using Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) have significant limitations. Many previous studies used 2D Transformers to analyze individual brain slices independently, potentially losing critical 3D contextual information. Region of interest-based models often focus on only a few brain regions despite Alzheimer's affecting multiple areas. Additionally, most classification models rely on a single test, whereas diagnosing Alzheimer's requires a multifaceted approach integrating diverse data sources for a more accurate assessment. This study introduces a novel methodology called the Multiple Inputs and Mixed Data 3D Vision Transformer (MIMD-3DVT). This method processes consecutive slices together to capture the feature dimensions and spatial information, fuses multiple 3D ROI imaging data inputs, and integrates mixed data from demographic factors, cognitive assessments, and brain imaging. The proposed methodology was experimentally evaluated using a combined dataset that included the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI), the Australian Imaging, Biomarker, and Lifestyle Flagship Study of Ageing (AIBL), and the Open Access Series of Imaging Studies (OASIS). Our MIMD-3DVT, utilizing single or multiple ROIs, achieved an accuracy of 97.14%, outperforming the state-of-the-art methods in distinguishing between Normal Cognition and Alzheimer's Disease.

AI.tldr: A novel 3D Vision Transformer (MIMD-3DVT) that processes consecutive brain slices together, fuses multiple ROI data, and integrates mixed data sources (demographics, cognitive assessments, brain imaging) to diagnose Alzheimer's Disease with 97.14% accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current MRI-based Alzheimer's diagnosis methods have limitations: 2D Transformers analyze slices independently, losing 3D contextual information; ROI-based models focus on only a few brain regions; and most classification models rely on a single test rather than integrating diverse data sources needed for accurate assessment.
AI.method: MIMD-3DVT (Multiple Inputs and Mixed Data 3D Vision Transformer) processes consecutive brain slices together to capture feature dimensions and spatial information, fuses multiple 3D ROI imaging data inputs, and integrates mixed data from demographic factors, cognitive assessments, and brain imaging.
AI.result: Evaluated on combined dataset from ADNI, AIBL, and OASIS, MIMD-3DVT achieved 97.14% accuracy in distinguishing between Normal Cognition and Alzheimer's Disease, outperforming state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MIMD-3DVT methodology effectively addresses previous limitations by capturing 3D contextual information, fusing multiple ROIs, and integrating diverse data sources, demonstrating superior diagnostic performance.

title: According to Me: Long-Term Personalized Referential Memory QA
summary: Personalized AI assistants must recall and reason over long-term user memory, which naturally spans multiple modalities and sources such as images, videos, and emails. However, existing Long-term Memory benchmarks focus primarily on dialogue history, failing to capture realistic personalized references grounded in lived experience. We introduce ATM-Bench, the first benchmark for multimodal, multi-source personalized referential Memory QA. ATM-Bench contains approximately four years of privacy-preserving personal memory data and human-annotated question-answer pairs with ground-truth memory evidence, including queries that require resolving personal references, multi-evidence reasoning from multi-source and handling conflicting evidence. We propose Schema-Guided Memory (SGM) to structurally represent memory items originated from different sources. In experiments, we implement 5 state-of-the-art memory systems along with a standard RAG baseline and evaluate variants with different memory ingestion, retrieval, and answer generation techniques. We find poor performance (under 20\% accuracy) on the ATM-Bench-Hard set, and that SGM improves performance over Descriptive Memory commonly adopted in prior works. Code available at: https://github.com/JingbiaoMei/ATM-Bench

AI.tldr: ATM-Bench introduces the first multimodal, multi-source personalized referential Memory QA benchmark with human-annotated QA pairs requiring resolving personal references and handling conflicting evidence; Schema-Guided Memory (SGM) is proposed to structurally represent memory items, though all tested systems achieve under 20% accuracy on the hard set.
AI.motivation: Personalized AI assistants need to recall and reason over long-term user memory spanning multiple modalities and sources (images, videos, emails, etc.), but existing benchmarks focus primarily on dialogue history and fail to capture realistic personalized references grounded in lived experience.
AI.method: The paper introduces ATM-Bench, containing approximately four years of privacy-preserving personal memory data with human-annotated QA pairs and ground-truth memory evidence. It proposes Schema-Guided Memory (SGM) to structurally represent memory items from different sources. Five state-of-the-art memory systems and a standard RAG baseline are implemented and evaluated with different memory ingestion, retrieval, and answer generation techniques.
AI.result: All tested systems achieve poor performance on the ATM-Bench-Hard set (under 20% accuracy). SGM improves performance over Descriptive Memory, which is commonly adopted in prior works.
AI.conclusion: ATM-Bench provides a more comprehensive benchmark for evaluating personalized memory recall in multimodal settings. The results demonstrate that current memory systems struggle significantly with this complex task, though SGM offers improvements over existing approaches.

title: Stabilizing Policy Optimization via Logits Convexity
summary: While reinforcement learning (RL) has been central to the recent success of large language models (LLMs), RL optimization is notoriously unstable, especially when compared to supervised fine-tuning (SFT). In this work, we investigate the stability gap between SFT and RL from a gradient-based perspective, and show that the convexity of the SFT loss with respect to model logits plays a key role in enabling stable training. Our theoretical analysis demonstrates that this property induces favorable gradient directionality during optimization. In contrast, Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), a widely adopted policy gradient algorithm utilizing a clipped surrogate objective, lacks this stabilizing property. Motivated by this observation, we propose Logits Convex Optimization (LCO), a simple yet effective policy optimization framework that aligns the learned policy with an optimal target derived from the original RL objective, thereby emulating the stabilizing effects of logits-level convexity. Extensive experiments across multiple model families show that our LCO framework consistently improves training stability and outperforms conventional RL methods on a broad range of benchmarks.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the stability gap between supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL) for LLMs, revealing that the convexity of SFT loss with respect to model logits is key to stable training. The authors propose Logits Convex Optimization (LCO), a policy optimization framework that emulates this stabilizing effect, demonstrating improved training stability and performance over conventional RL methods.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning (RL) optimization for large language models (LLMs) is notoriously unstable compared to supervised fine-tuning (SFT), despite RL's central role in recent LLM success. Understanding why SFT is more stable than RL could enable more robust RL training for LLMs.
AI.method: The authors analyze the stability gap from a gradient-based perspective, identifying that the convexity of the SFT loss with respect to model logits induces favorable gradient directionality. They propose Logits Convex Optimization (LCO), a policy optimization framework that aligns the learned policy with an optimal target derived from the original RL objective to emulate the stabilizing effects of logits-level convexity.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple model families demonstrate that LCO consistently improves training stability and outperforms conventional RL methods (specifically PPO) on a broad range of benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The convexity property of SFT loss with respect to model logits plays a crucial role in stable training, and LCO effectively emulates this property to achieve superior RL training stability and performance compared to conventional policy gradient approaches.

title: LEAR: Learning Edge-Aware Representations for Event-to-LiDAR Localization
summary: Event cameras offer high-temporal-resolution sensing that remains reliable under high-speed motion and challenging lighting, making them promising for localization from LiDAR point clouds in GPS-denied and visually degraded environments. However, aligning sparse, asynchronous events with dense LiDAR maps is fundamentally ill-posed, as direct correspondence estimation suffers from modality gaps. We propose LEAR, a dual-task learning framework that jointly estimates edge structures and dense event-depth flow fields to bridge the sensing-modality divide. Instead of treating edges as a post-hoc aid, LEAR couples them with flow estimation through a cross-modal fusion mechanism that injects modality-invariant geometric cues into the motion representation, and an iterative refinement strategy that enforces mutual consistency between the two tasks over multiple update steps. This synergy produces edge-aware, depth-aligned flow fields that enable more robust and accurate pose recovery via Perspective-n-Point (PnP) solvers. On several popular and challenging datasets, LEAR achieves superior performance over the best prior method. The source code, trained models, and demo videos are made publicly available online.

AI.tldr: LEAR is a dual-task learning framework that jointly estimates edge structures and dense event-depth flow fields to align sparse, asynchronous events with dense LiDAR maps, enabling robust pose recovery in GPS-denied and visually degraded environments.
AI.motivation: Event cameras offer high-temporal-resolution sensing reliable under high-speed motion and challenging lighting, making them promising for LiDAR point cloud localization in GPS-denied and visually degraded environments. However, aligning sparse, asynchronous events with dense LiDAR maps is fundamentally ill-posed because direct correspondence estimation suffers from modality gaps.
AI.method: LEAR employs a cross-modal fusion mechanism that injects modality-invariant geometric cues into the motion representation, combined with an iterative refinement strategy enforcing mutual consistency between edge structure estimation and flow estimation tasks over multiple update steps. The framework jointly estimates edge structures and dense event-depth flow fields instead of treating edges as a post-hoc aid.
AI.result: On several popular and challenging datasets, LEAR achieves superior performance compared to the best prior method.
AI.conclusion: The source code, trained models, and demo videos are publicly available online.

title: ALTER: Asymmetric LoRA for Token-Entropy-Guided Unlearning of LLMs
comment: Accepted at The 40th Annual AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence (AAAI 2026)
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have advanced to encompass extensive knowledge across diverse domains. Yet controlling what a LLMs should not know is important for ensuring alignment and thus safe use. However, effective unlearning in LLMs is difficult due to the fuzzy boundary between knowledge retention and forgetting. This challenge is exacerbated by entangled parameter spaces from continuous multi-domain training, often resulting in collateral damage, especially under aggressive unlearning strategies. Furthermore, the computational overhead required to optimize State-of-the-Art (SOTA) models with billions of parameters poses an additional barrier. In this work, we present ALTER, a lightweight unlearning framework for LLMs to address both the challenges of knowledge entanglement and unlearning efficiency. ALTER operates through two phases: (I) high entropy tokens are captured and learned via the shared A matrix in LoRA, followed by (II) an asymmetric LoRA architecture that achieves a specified forgetting objective by parameter isolation and unlearning tokens within the target subdomains. Serving as a new research direction for achieving unlearning via token-level isolation in the asymmetric framework. ALTER achieves SOTA performance on TOFU, WMDP, and MUSE benchmarks with over 95% forget quality and shows minimal side effects through preserving foundational tokens. By decoupling unlearning from LLMs' billion-scale parameters, this framework delivers excellent efficiency while preserving over 90% of model utility, exceeding baseline preservation rates of 47.8-83.6%.

AI.tldr: ALTER is a lightweight framework for LLM unlearning that uses two-phase asymmetric LoRA to achieve high forgetting quality (95%) while preserving 90%+ model utility, outperforming baselines.
AI.motivation: LLMs need controlled unlearning for alignment and safety, but effective unlearning is difficult due to knowledge entanglement from continuous training, collateral damage from aggressive strategies, and high computational costs for billions of parameters.
AI.method: ALTER uses a two-phase asymmetric LoRA approach: (I) high entropy tokens captured via shared A matrix in LoRA, followed by (II) parameter isolation and token-level unlearning within target subdomains via asymmetric architecture.
AI.result: Achieves SOTA on TOFU, WMDP, MUSE benchmarks with 95%+ forget quality and minimal side effects. Preserves over 90% model utility (vs 47.8-83.6% baselines), demonstrating excellent efficiency while decoupling unlearning from billion-scale parameters.
AI.conclusion: ALTER addresses knowledge entanglement and efficiency challenges through token-level isolation in asymmetric LoRA, providing a new research direction that balances effective unlearning with preservation of model utility.

title: Weakly Supervised Video Anomaly Detection with Anomaly-Connected Components and Intention Reasoning
summary: Weakly supervised video anomaly detection (WS-VAD) involves identifying the temporal intervals that contain anomalous events in untrimmed videos, where only video-level annotations are provided as supervisory signals. However, a key limitation persists in WS-VAD, as dense frame-level annotations are absent, which often leaves existing methods struggling to learn anomaly semantics effectively. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework named LAS-VAD, short for Learning Anomaly Semantics for WS-VAD, which integrates anomaly-connected component mechanism and intention awareness mechanism. The former is designed to assign video frames into distinct semantic groups within a video, and frame segments within the same group are deemed to share identical semantic information. The latter leverages an intention-aware strategy to distinguish between similar normal and abnormal behaviors (e.g., taking items and stealing). To further model the semantic information of anomalies, as anomaly occurrence is accompanied by distinct characteristic attributes (i.e., explosions are characterized by flames and thick smoke), we additionally incorporate anomaly attribute information to guide accurate detection. Extensive experiments on two benchmark datasets, XD-Violence and UCF-Crime, demonstrate that our LAS-VAD outperforms current state-of-the-art methods with remarkable gains.

AI.tldr: LAS-VAD is a weakly supervised video anomaly detection framework that leverages anomaly-connected component mechanism, intention awareness mechanism, and anomaly attribute information to learn anomaly semantics from video-level annotations alone, achieving superior performance on XD-Violence and UCF-Crime datasets.
AI.motivation: WS-VAD methods struggle to learn anomaly semantics effectively due to the absence of dense frame-level annotations, which limits their ability to understand the semantic characteristics of anomalous events.
AI.method: LAS-VAD integrates three key components: (1) Anomaly-connected component mechanism that groups video frames into semantic groups sharing identical information, (2) Intention-aware mechanism that distinguishes between similar normal and abnormal behaviors (e.g., taking vs. stealing), and (3) Anomaly attribute information modeling to capture distinct characteristic attributes of anomalies (e.g., flames and smoke for explosions).
AI.result: Extensive experiments on XD-Violence and UCF-Crime benchmark datasets demonstrate that LAS-VAD outperforms current state-of-the-art methods with remarkable gains in video anomaly detection performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed LAS-VAD framework effectively addresses the key limitation of WS-VAD by incorporating semantic learning mechanisms (connected components, intention awareness, and attribute modeling) to learn anomaly semantics from limited video-level annotations, achieving superior detection performance compared to existing methods.

title: Rich Insights from Cheap Signals: Efficient Evaluations via Tensor Factorization
summary: Moving beyond evaluations that collapse performance across heterogeneous prompts toward fine-grained evaluation at the prompt level, or within relatively homogeneous subsets, is necessary to diagnose generative models' strengths and weaknesses. Such fine-grained evaluations, however, suffer from a data bottleneck: human gold-standard labels are too costly at this scale, while automated ratings are often misaligned with human judgment. To resolve this challenge, we propose a novel statistical model based on tensor factorization that merges cheap autorater data with a limited set of human gold-standard labels. Specifically, our approach uses autorater scores to pretrain latent representations of prompts and generative models, and then aligns those pretrained representations to human preferences using a small calibration set. This sample-efficient methodology is robust to autorater quality, more accurately predicts human preferences on a per-prompt basis than standard baselines, and provides tight confidence intervals for key statistical parameters of interest. We also showcase the practical utility of our method by constructing granular leaderboards based on prompt qualities and by estimating model performance solely from autorater scores, eliminating the need for additional human annotations.

AI.tldr: A tensor factorization-based statistical model that combines cheap autorater data with limited human gold-standard labels to enable fine-grained, prompt-level evaluation of generative models with high accuracy and confidence intervals.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations of generative models collapse performance across heterogeneous prompts, making it difficult to diagnose model strengths and weaknesses. Fine-grained evaluations are needed but face a data bottleneck: human gold-standard labels are prohibitively costly at scale, while automated ratings often misalign with human judgment.
AI.method: Proposes a novel statistical model based on tensor factorization that (1) uses autorater scores to pretrain latent representations of prompts and generative models, and (2) aligns those pretrained representations to human preferences using a small calibration set. The approach is sample-efficient and robust to variations in autorater quality.
AI.result: The method more accurately predicts human preferences on a per-prompt basis than standard baselines, provides tight confidence intervals for key statistical parameters, and is robust to autorater quality variations. It enables practical applications including granular leaderboards based on prompt qualities and model performance estimation solely from autorater scores.
AI.conclusion: The proposed tensor factorization approach successfully bridges the data bottleneck in fine-grained generative model evaluation by combining inexpensive autorater data with limited human annotations, eliminating the need for additional human annotations while maintaining high predictive accuracy and providing reliable statistical confidence intervals.

title: Streaming Real-Time Trajectory Prediction Using Endpoint-Aware Modeling
comment: WACV 2026 Oral. Project Page at https://a-pru.github.io/seam/
summary: Future trajectories of neighboring traffic agents have a significant influence on the path planning and decision-making of autonomous vehicles. While trajectory forecasting is a well-studied field, research mainly focuses on snapshot-based prediction, where each scenario is treated independently of its global temporal context. However, real-world autonomous driving systems need to operate in a continuous setting, requiring real-time processing of data streams with low latency and consistent predictions over successive timesteps. We leverage this continuous setting to propose a lightweight yet highly accurate streaming-based trajectory forecasting approach. We integrate valuable information from previous predictions with a novel endpoint-aware modeling scheme. Our temporal context propagation uses the trajectory endpoints of the previous forecasts as anchors to extract targeted scenario context encodings. Our approach efficiently guides its scene encoder to extract highly relevant context information without needing refinement iterations or segment-wise decoding. Our experiments highlight that our approach effectively relays information across consecutive timesteps. Unlike methods using multi-stage refinement processing, our approach significantly reduces inference latency, making it well-suited for real-world deployment. We achieve state-of-the-art streaming trajectory prediction results on the Argoverse~2 multi-agent and single-agent benchmarks, while requiring substantially fewer resources.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, streaming-based trajectory forecasting approach for autonomous vehicles that integrates endpoint-aware context propagation from previous predictions, achieving state-of-the-art results on Argoverse benchmarks with significantly reduced inference latency compared to multi-stage refinement methods.
AI.motivation: Traditional trajectory forecasting methods focus on snapshot-based prediction that treats each scenario independently, lacking temporal context. Real-world autonomous driving requires continuous processing of data streams with low latency and consistent predictions across successive timesteps, which snapshot-based approaches fail to provide.
AI.method: The proposed method integrates valuable information from previous predictions with a novel endpoint-aware modeling scheme. It uses trajectory endpoints from previous forecasts as anchors to extract targeted scenario context encodings, efficiently guiding the scene encoder to extract relevant context information without needing refinement iterations or segment-wise decoding.
AI.result: The approach effectively relays information across consecutive timesteps and achieves state-of-the-art streaming trajectory prediction results on the Argoverse 2 multi-agent and single-agent benchmarks. Compared to methods using multi-stage refinement processing, it significantly reduces inference latency while requiring substantially fewer computational resources.
AI.conclusion: The proposed streaming-based trajectory forecasting approach is well-suited for real-world autonomous driving deployment due to its reduced latency and computational efficiency, maintaining high accuracy while outperforming existing methods on standard benchmarks.

title: OpenAutoNLU: Open Source AutoML Library for NLU
summary: OpenAutoNLU is an open-source automated machine learning library for natural language understanding (NLU) tasks, covering both text classification and named entity recognition (NER). Unlike existing solutions, we introduce data-aware training regime selection that requires no manual configuration from the user. The library also provides integrated data quality diagnostics, configurable out-of-distribution (OOD) detection, and large language model (LLM) features, all within a minimal lowcode API. The demo app is accessible here https://openautonlu.dev.

AI.tldr: OpenAutoNLU is an open-source lowcode API library for NLU tasks (text classification and NER) featuring automatic training regime selection, data quality diagnostics, OOD detection, and LLM integration without manual configuration.
AI.motivation: Existing NLU solutions require manual configuration and are complex to use. The authors aim to simplify the workflow by introducing data-aware training regime selection that eliminates manual setup while providing comprehensive NLU capabilities.
AI.method: OpenAutoNLU implements a data-aware training regime selection mechanism, integrated data quality diagnostics, configurable out-of-distribution (OOD) detection, and large language model (LLM) features within a minimal lowcode API framework.
AI.result: The library provides end-to-end NLU capabilities covering text classification and named entity recognition (NER) with automated training configuration, quality monitoring, and LLM integration.
AI.conclusion: OpenAutoNLU offers a streamlined, user-friendly solution for NLU tasks that reduces configuration complexity through automated training regime selection while maintaining comprehensive functionality through integrated diagnostics, OOD detection, and LLM features.

title: Geometry OR Tracker: Universal Geometric Operating Room Tracking
summary: In operating rooms (OR), world-scale multi-view 3D tracking supports downstream applications such as surgeon behavior recognition, where physically meaningful quantities such as distances and motion statistics must be measured in meters. However, real clinical deployments rarely satisfy the geometric prerequisites for stable multi-view fusion and tracking: camera calibration and RGB-D registration are always unreliable, leading to cross-view geometric inconsistency that produces "ghosting" during fusion and degrades 3D trajectories in a shared OR coordinate frame. To address this, we introduce Geometry OR Tracker, a two-stage pipeline that first rectifies imprecise calibration into a scaleconsistent and geometrically consistent camera setup with a single global scale via a Multi-view Metric Geometry Rectification module, and then performs Occlusion-Robust 3D Point Tracking directly in the unified OR world frame. On the MM-OR benchmark, improved geometric consistency translates into tracking gains: our rectification front-end reduces cross-view depth disagreement by more than 30$\times$ compared to raw calibration. Ablation studies further demonstrate the relationship between calibration quality and tracking accuracy, showing that improved geometric consistency yields stronger world-frame tracking.

AI.tldr: Geometry OR Tracker is a two-stage pipeline for operating room multi-view 3D tracking that first rectifies calibration issues via Multi-view Metric Geometry Rectification and then performs occlusion-robust point tracking in a unified world frame.
AI.motivation: Real clinical deployments have unreliable camera calibration and RGB-D registration, causing cross-view geometric inconsistency that produces "ghosting" during fusion and degrades 3D trajectories in the shared OR coordinate frame.
AI.method: A two-stage pipeline: (1) Multi-view Metric Geometry Rectification module that rectifies imprecise calibration into a scale-consistent and geometrically consistent camera setup with a single global scale, and (2) Occlusion-Robust 3D Point Tracking performed directly in the unified OR world frame.
AI.result: On the MM-OR benchmark, the rectification front-end reduces cross-view depth disagreement by more than 30× compared to raw calibration. Ablation studies demonstrate that improved geometric consistency yields stronger world-frame tracking.
AI.conclusion: Improved geometric consistency translates into tracking gains, validating the importance of robust calibration rectification for reliable multi-view 3D tracking in operating rooms.

title: Forgetting is Competition: Rethinking Unlearning as Representation Interference in Diffusion Models
summary: Unlearning in text-to-image diffusion models often leads to uneven concept removal and unintended forgetting of unrelated capabilities. This complicates tasks such as copyright compliance, protected data mitigation, artist opt-outs, and policy-driven content updates. As models grow larger and adopt more diverse architectures, achieving precise and selective unlearning while preserving generative quality becomes increasingly challenging. We introduce SurgUn (pronounced as Surgeon), a surgical unlearning method that applies targeted weight-space updates to remove specific visual concepts in text-conditioned diffusion models. Our approach is motivated by retroactive interference theory, which holds that newly acquired memories can overwrite, suppress, or impede access to prior ones by competing for shared representational pathways. We adapt this principle to diffusion models by inducing retroactive concept interference, enabling focused destabilization of only the target concept while preserving unrelated capabilities through a novel training paradigm. SurgUn achieves high-precision unlearning across diverse settings. It performs strongly on compact U-Net based models such as Stable Diffusion v1.5, scales effectively to the larger U-Net architecture SDXL, and extends to SANA, representing an underexplored Diffusion Transformer based architecture for unlearning.

AI.tldr: SurgUn is a surgical unlearning method that applies targeted weight-space updates to remove specific visual concepts in text-conditioned diffusion models while preserving unrelated capabilities through retroactive concept interference.
AI.motivation: Unlearning in text-to-image diffusion models leads to uneven concept removal and unintended forgetting of unrelated capabilities, complicating tasks such as copyright compliance, protected data mitigation, artist opt-outs, and policy-driven content updates. Achieving precise and selective unlearning while preserving generative quality becomes increasingly challenging as models grow larger and adopt more diverse architectures.
AI.method: SurgUn adapts retroactive interference theory to diffusion models by inducing retroactive concept interference - a novel training paradigm that destabilizes only target concepts while preserving unrelated capabilities through targeted weight-space updates.
AI.result: SurgUn achieves high-precision unlearning across diverse settings, performs strongly on compact U-Net based models like Stable Diffusion v1.5, scales effectively to larger U-Net architecture SDXL, and extends to SANA (a Diffusion Transformer based architecture).
AI.conclusion: SurgUn provides a generalizable approach for precise and selective unlearning in text-conditioned diffusion models that preserves generative quality across different architectures.

title: Event-Only Drone Trajectory Forecasting with RPM-Modulated Kalman Filtering
summary: Event cameras provide high-temporal-resolution visual sensing that is well suited for observing fast-moving aerial objects; however, their use for drone trajectory prediction remains limited. This work introduces an event-only drone forecasting method that exploits propeller-induced motion cues. Propeller rotational speed are extracted directly from raw event data and fused within an RPM-aware Kalman filtering framework. Evaluations on the FRED dataset show that the proposed method outperforms learning-based approaches and vanilla kalman filter in terms of average distance error and final distance error at 0.4s and 0.8s forecasting horizons. The results demonstrate robust and accurate short- and medium-horizon trajectory forecasting without reliance on RGB imagery or training data.

AI.tldr: Event-only drone trajectory forecasting method that extracts propeller rotational speed from raw event data and fuses it within an RPM-aware Kalman filtering framework.
AI.motivation: Event cameras provide high-temporal-resolution visual sensing for fast-moving aerial objects, but their use for drone trajectory prediction remains limited.
AI.method: Proposes an event-only drone forecasting method that extracts propeller rotational speed directly from raw event data and fuses this information within an RPM-aware Kalman filtering framework, without relying on RGB imagery or training data.
AI.result: Evaluations on the FRED dataset show the method outperforms learning-based approaches and vanilla Kalman filter in terms of average distance error and final distance error at 0.4s and 0.8s forecasting horizons.
AI.conclusion: The results demonstrate robust and accurate short- and medium-horizon trajectory forecasting without reliance on RGB imagery or training data.

title: Let the Agent Search: Autonomous Exploration Beats Rigid Workflows in Temporal Question Answering
summary: Temporal Knowledge Graph Question Answering (TKGQA) demands multi-hop reasoning under temporal constraints. Prior approaches based on large language models (LLMs) typically rely on rigid, hand-crafted retrieval workflows or costly supervised fine-tuning. We show that simply granting an off-the-shelf LLM autonomy, that is, letting it decide what to do next, already yields substantial gains even in a strict zero-shot setting. Building on this insight, we propose AT2QA, an autonomous, training-free agent for temporal question answering that iteratively interacts with the temporal knowledge graph via a general search tool for dynamic retrieval. Experiments on MultiTQ demonstrate large improvements: AT2QA achieves 88.7% Hits@1 (+10.7% over prior SOTA), including a +20.1% gain on challenging multi-target queries, showing that agentic autonomy can decisively outperform fine-tuning for temporal question answering. Code and the full set of sampled trajectories are available on https://github.com/AT2QA-Official-Code/AT2QA-Official-Code

AI.tldr: TKGQA requires multi-hop reasoning under temporal constraints. This paper proposes AT2QA, an autonomous, training-free agent that lets an off-the-shelf LLM decide what to do next via a general search tool, achieving 88.7% Hits@1 on MultiTQ (10.7% over SOTA), demonstrating agentic autonomy can outperform fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Temporal Knowledge Graph Question Answering (TKGQA) demands multi-hop reasoning under temporal constraints. Prior LLM-based approaches rely on rigid, hand-crafted retrieval workflows or costly supervised fine-tuning, limiting flexibility and scalability.
AI.method: AT2QA is an autonomous, training-free agent that grants an off-the-shelf LLM autonomy to decide actions iteratively, interacting with temporal knowledge graphs via a general search tool for dynamic retrieval without any supervised fine-tuning.
AI.result: AT2QA achieves 88.7% Hits@1 on MultiTQ, a 10.7% improvement over previous SOTA. On challenging multi-target queries, it shows a 20.1% gain over prior methods.
AI.conclusion: Agentic autonomy can decisively outperform fine-tuning for temporal question answering, enabling more flexible and effective TKGQA without costly supervised training.

title: MIDAS: Multi-Image Dispersion and Semantic Reconstruction for Jailbreaking MLLMs
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable performance but remain vulnerable to jailbreak attacks that can induce harmful content and undermine their secure deployment. Previous studies have shown that introducing additional inference steps, which disrupt security attention, can make MLLMs more susceptible to being misled into generating malicious content. However, these methods rely on single-image masking or isolated visual cues, which only modestly extend reasoning paths and thus achieve limited effectiveness, particularly against strongly aligned commercial closed-source models. To address this problem, in this paper, we propose Multi-Image Dispersion and Semantic Reconstruction (MIDAS), a multimodal jailbreak framework that decomposes harmful semantics into risk-bearing subunits, disperses them across multiple visual clues, and leverages cross-image reasoning to gradually reconstruct the malicious intent, thereby bypassing existing safety mechanisms. The proposed MIDAS enforces longer and more structured multi-image chained reasoning, substantially increases the model's reliance on visual cues while delaying the exposure of malicious semantics and significantly reducing the model's security attention, thereby improving the performance of jailbreak against advanced MLLMs. Extensive experiments across different datasets and MLLMs demonstrate that the proposed MIDAS outperforms state-of-the-art jailbreak attacks for MLLMs and achieves an average attack success rate of 81.46% across 4 closed-source MLLMs. Our code is available at this [link](https://github.com/Winnie-Lian/MIDAS).

AI.tldr: MIDAS is a multimodal jailbreak framework that decomposes harmful semantics into risk-bearing subunits across multiple images, using cross-image reasoning to reconstruct malicious intent and bypass safety mechanisms in MLLMs.
AI.motivation: MLLMs are vulnerable to jailbreak attacks. Previous methods using single-image masking or isolated visual cues have limited effectiveness, especially against strongly aligned commercial closed-source models, as they only modestly extend reasoning paths.
AI.method: MIDAS decomposes harmful semantics into risk-bearing subunits, disperses them across multiple visual clues, and leverages cross-image reasoning to gradually reconstruct malicious intent. This enforces longer and more structured multi-image chained reasoning, increases visual cue reliance, delays exposure of malicious semantics, and reduces security attention.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate MIDAS outperforms state-of-the-art jailbreak attacks, achieving an average attack success rate of 81.46% across 4 closed-source MLLMs.
AI.conclusion: MIDAS effectively bypasses safety mechanisms in advanced MLLMs through multi-image dispersion and semantic reconstruction, significantly improving jailbreak performance against strongly aligned commercial closed-source models.

title: Compensation-free Machine Unlearning in Text-to-Image Diffusion Models by Eliminating the Mutual Information
summary: The powerful generative capabilities of diffusion models have raised growing privacy and safety concerns regarding generating sensitive or undesired content. In response, machine unlearning (MU) -- commonly referred to as concept erasure (CE) in diffusion models -- has been introduced to remove specific knowledge from model parameters meanwhile preserving innocent knowledge. Despite recent advancements, existing unlearning methods often suffer from excessive and indiscriminate removal, which leads to substantial degradation in the quality of innocent generations. To preserve model utility, prior works rely on compensation, i.e., re-assimilating a subset of the remaining data or explicitly constraining the divergence from the pre-trained model on remaining concepts. However, we reveal that generations beyond the compensation scope still suffer, suggesting such post-remedial compensations are inherently insufficient for preserving the general utility of large-scale generative models. Therefore, in this paper, we advocate for developing compensation-free concept erasure operations, which precisely identify and eliminate the undesired knowledge such that the impact on other generations is minimal. In technique, we propose to MiM-MU, which is to unlearn a concept by minimizing the mutual information with a delicate design for computational effectiveness and for maintaining sampling distribution for other concepts. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our proposed method achieves effective concept removal meanwhile maintaining high-quality generations for other concepts, and remarkably, without relying on any post-remedial compensation for the first time.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces MiM-MU, a compensation-free concept erasure method for diffusion models that minimizes mutual information with undesired concepts while preserving generation quality for innocent concepts.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models have powerful generative capabilities but raise privacy and safety concerns. Existing concept erasure methods suffer from excessive and indiscriminate removal, leading to degraded quality of innocent generations. Prior compensation approaches (re-assimilating data or constraining divergence) are inherently insufficient for preserving general utility.
AI.method: MiM-MU (Mutual Information Minimization for Machine Unlearning) unlearns a concept by minimizing mutual information with a delicate design for computational effectiveness and maintaining the sampling distribution for other concepts, eliminating the need for post-remedial compensation.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations demonstrate that MiM-MU achieves effective concept removal while maintaining high-quality generations for other concepts, without relying on any post-remedial compensation for the first time.
AI.conclusion: The authors advocate for developing compensation-free concept erasure operations that precisely identify and eliminate undesired knowledge while minimizing impact on other generations, establishing MiM-MU as a practical solution for preserving model utility during privacy-preserving generative model deployment.

title: From Leaderboard to Deployment: Code Quality Challenges in AV Perception Repositories
summary: Autonomous vehicle (AV) perception models are typically evaluated solely on benchmark performance metrics, with limited attention to code quality, production readiness and long-term maintainability. This creates a significant gap between research excellence and real-world deployment in safety-critical systems subject to international safety standards. To address this gap, we present the first large-scale empirical study of software quality in AV perception repositories, systematically analyzing 178 unique models from the KITTI and NuScenes 3D Object Detection leaderboards. Using static analysis tools (Pylint, Bandit, and Radon), we evaluated code errors, security vulnerabilities, maintainability, and development practices. Our findings revealed that only 7.3% of the studied repositories meet basic production-readiness criteria, defined as having zero critical errors and no high-severity security vulnerabilities. Security issues are highly concentrated, with the top five issues responsible for almost 80% of occurrences, which prompted us to develop a set of actionable guidelines to prevent them. Additionally, the adoption of Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment pipelines was correlated with better code maintainability. Our findings highlight that leaderboard performance does not reflect production readiness and that targeted interventions could substantially improve the quality and safety of AV perception code.

AI.tldr: This study empirically assesses software quality and production readiness of 178 autonomous vehicle perception models from KITTI and NuScenes leaderboards using static analysis tools, finding only 7.3% meet basic production criteria despite leaderboard performance.
AI.motivation: AV perception models are evaluated solely on benchmark metrics, creating a gap between research excellence and real-world deployment in safety-critical systems subject to international safety standards.
AI.method: The authors conducted a large-scale empirical study analyzing 178 unique models from KITTI and NuScenes 3D Object Detection leaderboards using static analysis tools (Pylint, Bandit, and Radon) to evaluate code errors, security vulnerabilities, maintainability, and development practices.
AI.result: Key findings: only 7.3% of repositories meet basic production-readiness criteria (zero critical errors, no high-severity vulnerabilities); security issues are highly concentrated (top 5 issues account for ~80% of occurrences); adoption of CI/CD pipelines correlates with better code maintainability.
AI.conclusion: Leaderboard performance does not reflect production readiness, and targeted interventions can substantially improve the quality and safety of AV perception code, warranting attention to code quality and maintainability beyond benchmark metrics.

title: CyclicJudge: Mitigating Judge Bias Efficiently in LLM-based Evaluation
summary: LLM-as-judge evaluation has become standard practice for open-ended model assessment; however, judges exhibit systematic biases that cannot be eliminated by increasing the number of scenarios or generations. These biases are often similar in magnitude to the model differences that benchmarks are designed to detect, resulting in unreliable rankings when single-judge evaluations are used. This work introduces a variance decomposition that partitions benchmark score variance into scenario, generation, judge, and residual components. Based on this analysis, CyclicJudge, a round-robin assignment of judges, is demonstrated to be the optimal allocation strategy. It eliminates bias precisely while requiring each judge only once per cycle, maintaining the cost of single-judge evaluation. Empirical validation on MT-Bench supports all theoretical predictions.

AI.tldr: LLM-as-judge evaluation suffers from systematic judge biases that make rankings unreliable. This paper introduces variance decomposition to analyze these biases and proposes CyclicJudge, a round-robin judge assignment strategy that eliminates bias while maintaining single-judge evaluation costs.
AI.motivation: LLM-as-judge evaluation is standard for open-ended model assessment, but judges exhibit systematic biases that cannot be eliminated by increasing scenarios or generations. These biases are similar in magnitude to model differences, resulting in unreliable rankings when single-judge evaluations are used.
AI.method: The paper introduces a variance decomposition framework that partitions benchmark score variance into four components: scenario, generation, judge, and residual. Based on this analysis, CyclicJudge is proposed as a round-robin assignment strategy where each judge evaluates each scenario exactly once per cycle.
AI.result: CyclicJudge is demonstrated to be the optimal allocation strategy that eliminates judge bias precisely while requiring each judge only once per cycle, thereby maintaining the computational cost of single-judge evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Empirical validation on MT-Bench confirms all theoretical predictions, showing that CyclicJudge effectively addresses the bias problem in LLM-as-judge evaluation.

title: Decoupling Stability and Plasticity for Multi-Modal Test-Time Adaptation
summary: Adapting pretrained multi-modal models to evolving test-time distributions, known as multi-modal test-time adaptation, presents a significant challenge. Existing methods frequently encounter negative transfer in the unbiased modality and catastrophic forgetting in the biased modality. To address these challenges, we propose Decoupling Adaptation for Stability and Plasticity (DASP), a novel diagnose-then-mitigate framework. Our analysis reveals a critical discrepancy within the unified latent space: the biased modality exhibits substantially higher interdimensional redundancy (i.e., strong correlations across feature dimensions) compared to the unbiased modality. Leveraging this insight, DASP identifies the biased modality and implements an asymmetric adaptation strategy. This strategy employs a decoupled architecture where each modality-specific adapter is divided into stable and plastic components. The asymmetric mechanism works as follows: for the biased modality, which requires plasticity, the plastic component is activated and updated to capture domain-specific information, while the stable component remains fixed. Conversely, for the unbiased modality, which requires stability, the plastic component is bypassed, and the stable component is updated using KL regularization to prevent negative transfer. This asymmetric design enables the model to adapt flexibly to new domains while preserving generalizable knowledge. Comprehensive evaluations on diverse multi-modal benchmarks demonstrate that DASP significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: DASP is a test-time adaptation framework that decouples adaptation into stable and plastic components for biased and unbiased modalities, addressing negative transfer and catastrophic forgetting challenges in multi-modal test-time adaptation.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-modal test-time adaptation methods suffer from two critical issues: negative transfer in the unbiased modality (where adaptation harms performance) and catastrophic forgetting in the biased modality (where adaptation destroys previously learned knowledge). These challenges stem from the need to simultaneously maintain stability (generalizability) and plasticity (adaptability) across different modalities with varying domain shifts.
AI.method: DASP (Decoupling Adaptation for Stability and Plasticity) employs a diagnose-then-mitigate framework with three key components: (1) redundancy analysis to identify the biased modality by detecting higher interdimensional correlations, (2) a decoupled architecture with stable and plastic components for each modality-specific adapter, (3) an asymmetric adaptation mechanism where the plastic component is activated for the biased modality (to capture domain-specific information) while the stable component remains fixed, and the stable component is updated with KL regularization for the unbiased modality (to prevent negative transfer).
AI.result: Comprehensive evaluations on diverse multi-modal benchmarks demonstrate that DASP significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior performance in adapting to evolving test-time distributions while effectively mitigating both negative transfer and catastrophic forgetting.
AI.conclusion: The asymmetric decoupled adaptation strategy enables flexible domain adaptation while preserving generalizable knowledge, demonstrating that modality-specific balancing of stability and plasticity is key to effective multi-modal test-time adaptation. The redundancy analysis insight provides a principled way to identify which modality requires plasticity versus stability during adaptation.

title: Pencil Puzzle Bench: A Benchmark for Multi-Step Verifiable Reasoning
summary:
We introduce Pencil Puzzle Bench, a framework for evaluating large language model reasoning through pencil puzzles, a family of constraint-satisfaction problems closely related to NP-complete problems, with deterministic, step-level verification. From a database of 62,231 puzzles across 94 varieties with verified unique solutions, we select a benchmark of 300 puzzles spanning 20 varieties and evaluate 51 models from 11 providers in two modes: direct ask (single-shot) and agentic (multi-turn with iterative verification). A key differentiator of our benchmark is that every intermediate board state can be checked against variety-specific constraints, localizing errors to the exact rule violated, providing the infrastructure for dense, per-move reward signals for process supervision and reinforcement learning.
  Our evaluation reveals two distinct axes of capability: (1) reasoning effort scaling, where GPT-5.2 improves 81x from no reasoning to maximum effort; and (2) agentic iteration, where Claude Opus 4.6 rises from 0.3% to 30.0% through iterative checking, while GPT-5.2@xhigh improves from 20.2% to 56.0%. Agentic attempts span a median of 29 turns over 17 minutes, with the longest exceeding 1,221 turns and 14.3 hours - a demanding test of long-context utilization, not just reasoning.

AI.tldr: Pencil Puzzle Bench evaluates LLM reasoning through constraint-satisfaction pencil puzzles with step-level verification, revealing distinct axes of capability: reasoning effort scaling (81x improvement) and agentic iteration (significant gains through multi-turn verification).
AI.motivation: To evaluate large language model reasoning capabilities through pencil puzzles—constraint-satisfaction problems related to NP-complete problems—with deterministic, step-level verification and dense per-move reward signals for process supervision and reinforcement learning.
AI.method: Created a benchmark of 300 pencil puzzles across 20 varieties (from a database of 62,231 puzzles across 94 varieties) and evaluated 51 models from 11 providers in two modes: direct ask (single-shot) and agentic (multi-turn with iterative verification). The framework enables checking every intermediate board state against variety-specific constraints, providing infrastructure for dense, per-move reward signals.
AI.result: Evaluation revealed two distinct axes of capability: (1) reasoning effort scaling, where GPT-5.2 improves 81x from no reasoning to maximum effort; and (2) agentic iteration, where Claude Opus 4.6 rises from 0.3% to 30.0% and GPT-5.2@xhigh improves from 20.2% to 56.0% through iterative checking. Agentic attempts span a median of 29 turns over 17 minutes, with the longest exceeding 1,221 turns and 14.3 hours.
AI.conclusion: Pencil Puzzle Bench provides a rigorous framework for evaluating LLM reasoning that reveals both scaling behavior in reasoning effort and the effectiveness of agentic iteration strategies. The benchmark demonstrates the importance of long-context utilization and provides a foundation for developing better process supervision and reinforcement learning methods through dense, per-move reward signals.

title: DWAFM: Dynamic Weighted Graph Structure Embedding Integrated with Attention and Frequency-Domain MLPs for Traffic Forecasting
summary: Accurate traffic prediction is a key task for intelligent transportation systems. The core difficulty lies in accurately modeling the complex spatial-temporal dependencies in traffic data. In recent years, improvements in network architecture have failed to bring significant performance enhancements, while embedding technology has shown great potential. However, existing embedding methods often ignore graph structure information or rely solely on static graph structures, making it difficult to effectively capture the dynamic associations between nodes that evolve over time. To address this issue, this letter proposes a novel dynamic weighted graph structure (DWGS) embedding method, which relies on a graph structure that can truly reflect the changes in the strength of dynamic associations between nodes over time. By first combining the DWGS embedding with the spatial-temporal adaptive embedding, as well as the temporal embedding and feature embedding, and then integrating attention and frequency-domain multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs), we design a novel traffic prediction model, termed the DWGS embedding integrated with attention and frequency-domain MLPs (DWAFM). Experiments on five real-world traffic datasets show that the DWAFM achieves better prediction performance than some state-of-the-arts.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes DWAFM, a novel traffic prediction model that integrates a dynamic weighted graph structure embedding with attention mechanisms and frequency-domain MLPs to better capture evolving spatial-temporal dependencies in traffic data.
AI.motivation: Accurate traffic prediction is crucial for intelligent transportation systems, but modeling complex spatial-temporal dependencies remains challenging. Existing improvements in network architecture have shown diminishing returns, while embedding methods often ignore graph structure or rely on static structures, failing to capture dynamic node associations that evolve over time.
AI.method: The authors propose a Dynamic Weighted Graph Structure (DWGS) embedding that reflects changing association strengths between nodes over time. They integrate DWGS with spatial-temporal adaptive embedding, temporal embedding, and feature embedding, then augment this with attention mechanisms and frequency-domain multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) to create the DWAFM model.
AI.result: Experiments on five real-world traffic datasets demonstrate that DWAFM achieves superior prediction performance compared to several state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DWGS embedding approach effectively captures dynamic spatial-temporal dependencies in traffic data, and the integrated DWAFM model represents an advancement in traffic prediction capabilities, particularly in handling the evolving nature of traffic associations.

title: Sovereign AI-based Public Services are Viable and Affordable
summary: The rapid expansion of AI-based remote services has intensified debates about the long-term implications of growing structural concentration in infrastructure and expertise. As AI capabilities become increasingly intertwined with geopolitical interests, the availability and reliability of foundational AI services can no longer be taken for granted.
