This issue is particularly pressing for AI-enabled public services for citizens, as governments and public agencies are progressively adopting 24/7 AI-driven support systems typically operated through commercial offerings from a small oligopoly of global technology providers. This paper challenges the prevailing assumption that general-purpose architectures, offered by these providers, are the optimal choice for all application contexts. Through practical experimentation, we demonstrate that viable and cost-effective alternatives exist. Alternatives that align with principles of digital and cultural sovereignty. Our findings provide an empirical illustration that sovereign AI-based public services are both technically feasible and economically sustainable, capable of operating effectively on premises with modest computational and financial resources while maintaining cultural and digital autonomy. The technical insights and deployment lessons reported here are intended to inform the adoption of similar sovereign AI public services by national agencies and governments worldwide.

AI.tldr: This paper challenges the assumption that commercial general-purpose AI architectures are optimal for public services, demonstrating that sovereign AI alternatives are technically feasible and economically sustainable when deployed on premises with modest resources.
AI.motivation: With AI services becoming geopolitically intertwined, governments increasingly rely on a small oligopoly of technology providers for 24/7 AI-driven public services. This creates concerns about infrastructure and expertise concentration, and the long-term implications for digital and cultural sovereignty. The prevailing assumption that general-purpose architectures from commercial providers are optimal needs re-examination.
AI.method: Through practical experimentation, the authors evaluated alternatives to commercial general-purpose AI architectures. They demonstrated viable solutions that align with digital and cultural sovereignty principles, specifically focusing on on-premises deployment options.
AI.result: Findings show that sovereign AI-based public services are both technically feasible and economically sustainable. These systems can operate effectively on premises with modest computational and financial resources while maintaining cultural and digital autonomy.
AI.conclusion: The technical insights and deployment lessons provide empirical evidence that governments can adopt sovereign AI public services without compromising on performance or incurring excessive costs. These findings aim to inform national agencies and governments worldwide in their adoption of similar services.

title: WildActor: Unconstrained Identity-Preserving Video Generation
summary: Production-ready human video generation requires digital actors to maintain strictly consistent full-body identities across dynamic shots, viewpoints and motions, a setting that remains challenging for existing methods. Prior methods often suffer from face-centric behavior that neglects body-level consistency, or produce copy-paste artifacts where subjects appear rigid due to pose locking. We present Actor-18M, a large-scale human video dataset designed to capture identity consistency under unconstrained viewpoints and environments. Actor-18M comprises 1.6M videos with 18M corresponding human images, covering both arbitrary views and canonical three-view representations. Leveraging Actor-18M, we propose WildActor, a framework for any-view conditioned human video generation. We introduce an Asymmetric Identity-Preserving Attention mechanism coupled with a Viewpoint-Adaptive Monte Carlo Sampling strategy that iteratively re-weights reference conditions by marginal utility for balanced manifold coverage. Evaluated on the proposed Actor-Bench, WildActor consistently preserves body identity under diverse shot compositions, large viewpoint transitions, and substantial motions, surpassing existing methods in these challenging settings.

AI.tldr: Actor-18M is a large-scale human video dataset (1.6M videos, 18M images) for identity-consistent video generation, and WildActor is a framework using Asymmetric Identity-Preserving Attention and Viewpoint-Adaptive Monte Carlo Sampling to maintain full-body identity across diverse viewpoints and motions, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Production-ready human video generation requires strictly consistent full-body identities across dynamic shots, viewpoints, and motions, which existing methods struggle with due to face-centric behavior (neglecting body-level consistency) or copy-paste artifacts caused by pose locking.
AI.method: Created Actor-18M dataset covering arbitrary views and canonical three-view representations; proposed WildActor framework with Asymmetric Identity-Preserving Attention mechanism and Viewpoint-Adaptive Monte Carlo Sampling strategy that iteratively re-weights reference conditions by marginal utility for balanced manifold coverage.
AI.result: Evaluated on Actor-Bench, WildActor consistently preserves body identity under diverse shot compositions, large viewpoint transitions, and substantial motions, surpassing existing methods in these challenging settings.
AI.conclusion: WildActor successfully addresses the challenge of maintaining full-body identity consistency across dynamic shots, viewpoints, and motions, demonstrating superior performance over prior approaches.

title: Nano-EmoX: Unifying Multimodal Emotional Intelligence from Perception to Empathy
comment: 17 pages,8 figures, The IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2026
summary: The development of affective multimodal language models (MLMs) has long been constrained by a gap between low-level perception and high-level interaction, leading to fragmented affective capabilities and limited generalization. To bridge this gap, we propose a cognitively inspired three-level hierarchy that organizes affective tasks according to their cognitive depth-perception, understanding, and interaction-and provides a unified conceptual foundation for advancing affective modeling. Guided by this hierarchy, we introduce Nano-EmoX, a small-scale multitask MLM, and P2E (Perception-to-Empathy), a curriculum-based training framework. Nano-EmoX integrates a suite of omni-modal encoders, including an enhanced facial encoder and a fusion encoder, to capture key multimodal affective cues and improve cross-task transferability. The outputs are projected into a unified language space via heterogeneous adapters, empowering a lightweight language model to tackle diverse affective tasks. Concurrently, P2E progressively cultivates emotional intelligence by aligning rapid perception with chain-of-thought-driven empathy. To the best of our knowledge, Nano-EmoX is the first compact MLM (2.2B) to unify six core affective tasks across all three hierarchy levels, achieving state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance across multiple benchmarks, demonstrating excellent efficiency and generalization.

AI.tldr: Nano-EmoX is a compact (2.2B) multitask multimodal language model that unifies six affective tasks through a three-level cognitive hierarchy (perception, understanding, interaction), enhanced with omni-modal encoders and heterogeneous adapters, trained via P2E curriculum framework aligning perception with chain-of-thought empathy.
AI.motivation: Affective multimodal language models suffer from a gap between low-level perception and high-level interaction, resulting in fragmented capabilities and limited generalization.
AI.method: Proposes a cognitively inspired three-level hierarchy (perception, understanding, interaction) and introduces Nano-EmoX (2.2B) with omni-modal encoders (enhanced facial encoder, fusion encoder) and heterogeneous adapters, trained via P2E (Perception-to-Empathy) curriculum framework that aligns rapid perception with chain-of-thought-driven empathy.
AI.result: Nano-EmoX achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance across multiple benchmarks on six core affective tasks, demonstrating excellent efficiency and generalization.
AI.conclusion: First compact MLM to unify six core affective tasks across all three hierarchy levels, effectively bridging the perception-interaction gap through the proposed cognitive framework.

title: Feature-Weighted Maximum Representative Subsampling
summary: In the social sciences, it is often necessary to debias studies and surveys before valid conclusions can be drawn. Debiasing algorithms enable the computational removal of bias using sample weights. However, an issue arises when only a subset of features is highly biased, while the rest is already representative. Algorithms need to strongly alter the sample distribution to manage a few highly biased features, which can in turn introduce bias into already representative variables. To address this issue, we developed a method that uses feature weights to minimize the impact of highly biased features on the computation of sample weights. Our algorithm is based on Maximum Representative Subsampling (MRS), which debiases datasets by aligning a non-representative sample with a representative one through iterative removal of elements to create a representative subsample. The new algorithm, named feature-weighted MRS (FW-MRS), decreases the emphasis on highly biased features, allowing it to retain more instances for downstream tasks. The feature weights are derived from the feature importance of a domain classifier trained to differentiate between the representative and non-representative datasets. We validated FW-MRS using eight tabular datasets, each of which we artificially biased. Biased features can be important for downstream tasks, and focusing less on them could lead to a decline in generalization. For this reason, we assessed the generalization performance of FW-MRS on downstream tasks and found no statistically significant differences. Additionally, FW-MRS was applied to a real-world dataset from the social sciences. The source code is available at https://github.com/kramerlab/FeatureWeightDebiasing.

AI.tldr: A novel feature-weighted Maximum Representative Subsampling (FW-MRS) method that minimizes the impact of highly biased features on sample weight computation while preserving the representativeness of other variables, validated through eight tabular datasets and a real-world social science dataset without compromising downstream task performance.
AI.motivation: Traditional debiasing algorithms require strong alterations to sample distributions to handle highly biased features, which can inadvertently introduce bias into already representative variables. The challenge lies in minimizing the impact of biased features while preserving the representativeness of other variables.
AI.method: Feature-weighted Maximum Representative Subsampling (FW-MRS) uses feature weights derived from a domain classifier's feature importance to decrease emphasis on highly biased features during sample weight computation. The algorithm iteratively removes elements to align non-representative samples with representative ones while prioritizing less biased features.
AI.result: FW-MRS was validated on eight artificially biased tabular datasets and a real-world social science dataset. The method showed no statistically significant differences in generalization performance on downstream tasks compared to traditional approaches, demonstrating that focusing less on biased features does not necessarily harm performance.
AI.conclusion: FW-MRS successfully addresses the trade-off between debiasing biased features and preserving representativeness of other variables, maintaining generalization performance on downstream tasks while providing a more targeted approach to handling mixed-bias datasets.

title: KDFlow: A User-Friendly and Efficient Knowledge Distillation Framework for Large Language Models
comment: 8 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables, code is available at: https://github.com/songmzhang/KDFlow
summary: Knowledge distillation (KD) is an essential technique to compress large language models (LLMs) into smaller ones. However, despite the distinct roles of the student model and the teacher model in KD, most existing frameworks still use a homogeneous training backend (e.g., FSDP and DeepSpeed) for both models, leading to suboptimal training efficiency. In this paper, we present a novel framework for LLM distillation, termed \textbf{KDFlow}, which features a decoupled architecture and employs SGLang for teacher inference. By bridging the training efficiency of FSDP2 and the inference efficiency of SGLang, KDFlow achieves full utilization of both advantages in a unified system. Moreover, instead of transferring full logits across different processes, our framework only transmits the teacher's hidden states using zero-copy data transfer and recomputes the logits on the student side, effectively balancing the communication cost and KD performance. Furthermore, our framework supports both off-policy and on-policy distillation and incorporates KD algorithms for cross-tokenizer KD through highly extensible and user-friendly APIs. Experiments show that KDFlow can achieve \textbf{1.44$\times$ to 6.36$\times$} speedup compared to current KD frameworks, enabling researchers to rapidly prototype and scale LLM distillation with minimal engineering overhead. Code is available at: https://github.com/songmzhang/KDFlow

AI.tldr: KDFlow is a novel knowledge distillation framework for large language models that decouples teacher and student training by combining FSDP2 for training efficiency with SGLang for teacher inference. It achieves 1.44× to 6.36× speedup over existing KD frameworks.
AI.motivation: Existing knowledge distillation frameworks use a homogeneous training backend for both teacher and student models, leading to suboptimal training efficiency. The framework should fully leverage the advantages of both training efficiency (FSDP2) and inference efficiency (SGLang).
AI.method: KDFlow employs a decoupled architecture where the teacher uses SGLang for inference and the student uses FSDP2 for training. It transmits only teacher hidden states using zero-copy data transfer, recomputes logits on the student side, and supports both off-policy and on-policy distillation with cross-tokenizer KD.
AI.result: Experiments show KDFlow achieves 1.44× to 6.36× speedup compared to current KD frameworks, enabling rapid prototyping and scaling of LLM distillation with minimal engineering overhead.
AI.conclusion: KDFlow effectively bridges the efficiency gap between training and inference, providing a unified system that balances communication cost and KD performance while supporting flexible distillation strategies.

title: AlignVAR: Towards Globally Consistent Visual Autoregression for Image Super-Resolution
summary: Visual autoregressive (VAR) models have recently emerged as a promising alternative for image generation, offering stable training, non-iterative inference, and high-fidelity synthesis through next-scale prediction. This encourages the exploration of VAR for image super-resolution (ISR), yet its application remains underexplored and faces two critical challenges: locality-biased attention, which fragments spatial structures, and residual-only supervision, which accumulates errors across scales, severely compromises global consistency of reconstructed images. To address these issues, we propose AlignVAR, a globally consistent visual autoregressive framework tailored for ISR, featuring two key components: (1) Spatial Consistency Autoregression (SCA), which applies an adaptive mask to reweight attention toward structurally correlated regions, thereby mitigating excessive locality and enhancing long-range dependencies; and (2) Hierarchical Consistency Constraint (HCC), which augments residual learning with full reconstruction supervision at each scale, exposing accumulated deviations early and stabilizing the coarse-to-fine refinement process. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AlignVAR consistently enhances structural coherence and perceptual fidelity over existing generative methods, while delivering over 10x faster inference with nearly 50% fewer parameters than leading diffusion-based approaches, establishing a new paradigm for efficient ISR.

AI.tldr: AlignVAR is a globally consistent visual autoregressive framework for image super-resolution that employs Spatial Consistency Autoregression (SCA) and Hierarchical Consistency Constraint (HCC) to improve structural coherence and perceptual fidelity while offering over 10x faster inference with nearly 50% fewer parameters than diffusion-based methods.
AI.motivation: Visual autoregressive (VAR) models show promise for image generation, but their application to image super-resolution (ISR) faces two critical challenges: (1) locality-biased attention that fragments spatial structures, and (2) residual-only supervision that accumulates errors across scales, severely compromising global consistency of reconstructed images.
AI.method: AlignVAR proposes a globally consistent VAR framework for ISR with two key components: (1) Spatial Consistency Autoregression (SCA) - applies adaptive masks to reweight attention toward structurally correlated regions, mitigating excessive locality and enhancing long-range dependencies; and (2) Hierarchical Consistency Constraint (HCC) - augments residual learning with full reconstruction supervision at each scale, exposing accumulated deviations early and stabilizing the coarse-to-fine refinement process.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that AlignVAR consistently enhances structural coherence and perceptual fidelity over existing generative methods, while delivering over 10x faster inference with nearly 50% fewer parameters than leading diffusion-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: AlignVAR establishes a new paradigm for efficient image super-resolution by combining global consistency with computational efficiency, overcoming the limitations of locality-biased attention and residual-only supervision in VAR models for ISR tasks.

title: Conformal Policy Control
summary: An agent must try new behaviors to explore and improve. In high-stakes environments, an agent that violates safety constraints may cause harm and must be taken offline, curtailing any future interaction. Imitating old behavior is safe, but excessive conservatism discourages exploration. How much behavior change is too much? We show how to use any safe reference policy as a probabilistic regulator for any optimized but untested policy. Conformal calibration on data from the safe policy determines how aggressively the new policy can act, while provably enforcing the user's declared risk tolerance. Unlike conservative optimization methods, we do not assume the user has identified the correct model class nor tuned any hyperparameters. Unlike previous conformal methods, our theory provides finite-sample guarantees even for non-monotonic bounded constraint functions. Our experiments on applications ranging from natural language question answering to biomolecular engineering show that safe exploration is not only possible from the first moment of deployment, but can also improve performance.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a probabilistic regulator approach using conformal calibration to enable safe exploration in high-stakes environments by balancing exploration with user-specified risk tolerance, providing finite-sample guarantees even for non-monotonic constraints.
AI.motivation: Exploration is essential for agents to improve performance, but in high-stakes environments, violating safety constraints can cause harm and require taking the agent offline. Traditional conservative methods discourage exploration, while risk-seeking methods can be unsafe. The challenge is determining the appropriate level of behavior change that balances exploration benefits with safety requirements.
AI.method: The authors propose using any safe reference policy as a probabilistic regulator for any optimized but untested policy. Conformal calibration on data from the safe policy determines how aggressively the new policy can act while provably enforcing the user's declared risk tolerance. Unlike conservative optimization methods, no assumption is made about the correct model class or hyperparameter tuning. Unlike previous conformal methods, the theory provides finite-sample guarantees even for non-monotonic bounded constraint functions.
AI.result: Safe exploration is possible from the first moment of deployment, and experiments across applications ranging from natural language question answering to biomolecular engineering demonstrate that this approach can improve performance while maintaining safety guarantees.
AI.conclusion: The proposed probabilistic regulator framework provides a principled method for balancing exploration and safety without requiring model identification or hyperparameter tuning. It enables safe exploration from deployment start while potentially improving performance, demonstrating that safety constraints need not severely limit exploration capabilities.

title: One-Token Verification for Reasoning Correctness Estimation
summary: Recent breakthroughs in large language models (LLMs) have led to notable successes in complex reasoning tasks, such as mathematical problem solving. A common strategy for improving performance is parallel thinking, in which multiple reasoning traces are generated and the final prediction is made using aggregation schemes like majority voting or best-of-$N$ decoding. However, two key challenges persist. First, multi-sample decoding incurs substantial inference latency, especially for long-form outputs. Second, effective mechanisms for reliably assessing the correctness of individual reasoning traces are still limited. To address these challenges, we introduce One-Token Verification (OTV), a computational method that estimates reasoning correctness in a single forward pass during generation. OTV is activated by a learnable token and integrated into the LLM via low-rank adaptation to probe internal reasoning signals through the key-value cache, supporting token-level correctness estimation at any stage of generation without disrupting primary reasoning. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that OTV consistently surpasses existing verifiers. Additionally, OTV reduces token usage by up to $90\%$ through correctness-guided early termination, prioritizing shorter, more reliable solutions.

AI.tldr: One-Token Verification (OTV) is a method that estimates reasoning correctness in a single forward pass during generation by using a learnable token and low-rank adaptation to probe internal reasoning signals through the key-value cache.
AI.motivation: Multi-sample decoding improves LLM performance on reasoning tasks but suffers from substantial inference latency and limited mechanisms for reliably assessing individual reasoning trace correctness.
AI.method: OTV activates via a learnable token and integrates into LLMs through low-rank adaptation to probe internal reasoning signals through the key-value cache, enabling token-level correctness estimation at any generation stage without disrupting primary reasoning.
AI.result: OTV consistently surpasses existing verifiers on mathematical reasoning benchmarks and reduces token usage by up to 90% through correctness-guided early termination, prioritizing shorter, more reliable solutions.
AI.conclusion: OTV addresses key challenges in parallel thinking by providing efficient, token-level verification that significantly reduces computational costs while maintaining or improving performance.

title: FLANS at SemEval-2026 Task 7: RAG with Open-Sourced Smaller LLMs for Everyday Knowledge Across Diverse Languages and Cultures
summary: This system paper describes our participation in the SemEval-2025 Task-7 ``Everyday Knowledge Across Diverse Languages and Cultures''. We attended two subtasks, i.e., Track 1: Short Answer Questions (SAQ), and Track 2: Multiple-Choice Questions (MCQ). The methods we used are retrieval augmented generation (RAGs) with open-sourced smaller LLMs (OS-sLLMs). To better adapt to this shared task, we created our own culturally aware knowledge base (CulKBs) by extracting Wikipedia content using keyword lists we prepared. We extracted both culturally-aware wiki-text and country-specific wiki-summary. In addition to the local CulKBs, we also have one system integrating live online search output via DuckDuckGo. Towards better privacy and sustainability, we aimed to deploy smaller LLMs (sLLMs) that are open-sourced on the Ollama platform. We share the prompts we developed using refinement techniques and report the learning curve of such prompts. The tested languages are English, Spanish, and Chinese for both tracks. Our resources and codes are shared via https://github.com/aaronlifenghan/FLANS-2026

AI.tldr: This paper describes participation in SemEval-2025 Task-7 "Everyday Knowledge Across Diverse Languages and Cultures", implementing a retrieval-augmented generation system using smaller open-source LLMs with culturally-aware knowledge bases for short answer and multiple-choice question tasks in English, Spanish, and Chinese.
AI.motivation: The authors participated in SemEval-2025 Task-7 to address everyday knowledge across diverse languages and cultures. They aimed to use smaller, open-source LLMs (OS-sLLMs) for better privacy and sustainability compared to larger commercial models.
AI.method: The system employs retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) with open-sourced smaller LLMs (OS-sLLMs) deployed on the Ollama platform. The authors created culturally-aware knowledge bases (CulKBs) by extracting Wikipedia content using prepared keyword lists, including both culturally-aware wiki-text and country-specific wiki-summary. An additional component integrates live online search via DuckDuckGo. Prompts were developed using refinement techniques.
AI.result: The system was tested on English, Spanish, and Chinese for both SAQ (Short Answer Questions) and MCQ (Multiple-Choice Questions) tracks. The authors report the learning curve of their prompts and share their resources and code via GitHub.
AI.conclusion: The authors present their system implementation and findings for the SemEval-2025 Task-7, sharing their resources and codes publicly to facilitate further research in cross-cultural and cross-lingual knowledge understanding.

title: UNICBench: UNIfied Counting Benchmark for MLLM
comment: This paper has been accepted by CVPR 2026
summary: Counting is a core capability for multimodal large language models (MLLMs), yet there is no unified counting dataset to rigorously evaluate this ability across image, text, and audio. We present UNICBench, a unified multimodal, multi level counting benchmark and evaluation toolkit with accurate ground truth, deterministic numeric parsing, and stratified reporting. The corpus comprises 5,300 images (5,508 QA), 872 documents (5,888 QA), and 2,069 audio clips (2,905 QA), annotated with a three level capability taxonomy and difficulty tags. Under a standardized protocol with fixed splits/prompts/seeds and modality specific matching rules, we evaluate 45 state-of-the-art MLLMs across modalities. Results show strong performance on some basic counting tasks but significant gaps on reasoning and the hardest partitions, highlighting long-tail errors and substantial headroom for improving general counting. UNICBench offers a rigorous and comparable basis for measurement and a public toolkit to accelerate progress.

AI.tldr: UNICBench is a unified multimodal counting benchmark evaluating 45 MLLMs across images, documents, and audio with 13,291 QA pairs, revealing strong performance on basic tasks but significant gaps in reasoning and difficult partitions.
AI.motivation: Counting is a core capability for MLLMs, but no unified dataset exists to rigorously evaluate this ability across image, text, and audio modalities.
AI.method: UNICBench is a unified multimodal, multi-level counting benchmark and evaluation toolkit featuring accurate ground truth, deterministic numeric parsing, stratified reporting, a three-level capability taxonomy, and difficulty tags. It comprises 5,300 images (5,508 QA), 872 documents (5,888 QA), and 2,069 audio clips (2,905 QA). A standardized protocol with fixed splits/prompts/seeds and modality-specific matching rules evaluates 45 SOTA MLLMs.
AI.result: Models show strong performance on basic counting tasks but significant gaps on reasoning and hardest partitions. The evaluation highlights long-tail errors and substantial headroom for improving general counting capabilities.
AI.conclusion: UNICBench provides a rigorous and comparable measurement basis for counting abilities across modalities and offers a public toolkit to accelerate progress in multimodal counting research.

title: Tool Verification for Test-Time Reinforcement Learning
summary: Test-time reinforcement learning (TTRL) has emerged as a promising paradigm for self-evolving large reasoning models (LRMs), enabling online adaptation on unlabeled test inputs via self-induced rewards through majority voting. However, a spurious yet high-frequency unverified consensus can become a biased and reinforced reward signal, leading to incorrect mode collapse. We address this failure mode with T^3RL (Tool-Verification for Test-Time Reinforcement Learning), which introduces test-time tool verification into reward estimation. Concretely, a verifier uses an external tool as evidence (e.g., from code execution) to upweight verified rollouts in a verification-aware voting, producing more reliable pseudo-labels for training. Across various math difficulties (MATH-500, AMC, and AIME 2024) and diverse backbone types, T^3RL significantly improves over TTRL, with larger gains on harder problems. More broadly, T^3RL can be viewed as verified online data synthesis, highlighting test-time tool verification as a key mechanism for stabilizing self-evolution.

AI.tldr: T^3RL introduces test-time tool verification into reinforcement learning to prevent spurious consensus from becoming biased reward signals, improving performance on math problems across various difficulty levels.
AI.motivation: Test-time reinforcement learning (TTRL) enables self-evolving large reasoning models but suffers from a critical failure mode: spurious yet high-frequency unverified consensus can become a biased reward signal, leading to incorrect mode collapse.
AI.method: T^3RL (Tool-Verification for Test-Time Reinforcement Learning) introduces test-time tool verification into reward estimation. A verifier uses an external tool (e.g., code execution) as evidence to upweight verified rollouts in a verification-aware voting process, generating more reliable pseudo-labels for training.
AI.result: Across MATH-500, AMC, and AIME 2024 datasets and diverse backbone types, T^3RL significantly outperforms TTRL, with larger performance gains on harder problems.
AI.conclusion: T^3RL can be viewed as verified online data synthesis, demonstrating that test-time tool verification is a key mechanism for stabilizing self-evolution in large reasoning model training.

title: Fed-ADE: Adaptive Learning Rate for Federated Post-adaptation under Distribution Shift
summary: Federated learning (FL) in post-deployment settings must adapt to non-stationary data streams across heterogeneous clients without access to ground-truth labels. A major challenge is learning rate selection under client-specific, time-varying distribution shifts, where fixed learning rates often lead to underfitting or divergence. We propose Fed-ADE (Federated Adaptation with Distribution Shift Estimation), an unsupervised federated adaptation framework that leverages lightweight estimators of distribution dynamics. Specifically, Fed-ADE employs uncertainty dynamics estimation to capture changes in predictive uncertainty and representation dynamics estimation to detect covariate-level feature drift, combining them into a per-client, per-timestep adaptive learning rate. We provide theoretical analyses showing that our dynamics estimation approximates the underlying distribution shift and yields dynamic regret and convergence guarantees. Experiments on image and text benchmarks under diverse distribution shifts (label and covariate) demonstrate consistent improvements over strong baselines. These results highlight that distribution shift-aware adaptation enables effective and robust federated post-adaptation under real-world non-stationarity.

AI.tldr: Fed-ADE is an unsupervised federated adaptation framework that uses uncertainty dynamics and representation dynamics estimation to create per-client, per-timestep adaptive learning rates for handling non-stationary data streams without ground-truth labels.
AI.motivation: FL in post-deployment settings must adapt to non-stationary data streams across heterogeneous clients without access to ground-truth labels. A major challenge is learning rate selection under client-specific, time-varying distribution shifts, where fixed learning rates often lead to underfitting or divergence.
AI.method: Fed-ADE employs two lightweight estimators: (1) uncertainty dynamics estimation to capture changes in predictive uncertainty and (2) representation dynamics estimation to detect covariate-level feature drift. These are combined into a per-client, per-timestep adaptive learning rate mechanism.
AI.result: Theoretical analyses demonstrate that dynamics estimation approximates the underlying distribution shift and yields dynamic regret and convergence guarantees. Experiments on image and text benchmarks under diverse distribution shifts (label and covariate) show consistent improvements over strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: Distribution shift-aware adaptation enables effective and robust federated post-adaptation under real-world non-stationarity.

title: Demonstrating ViviDoc: Generating Interactive Documents through Human-Agent Collaboration
summary: Interactive articles help readers engage with complex ideas through exploration, yet creating them remains costly, requiring both domain expertise and web development skills. Recent LLM-based agents can automate content creation, but naively applying them yields uncontrollable and unverifiable outputs. We present ViviDoc, a human-agent collaborative system that generates interactive educational documents from a single topic input. ViviDoc introduces a multi-agent pipeline (Planner, Executor, Evaluator) and the Document Specification (DocSpec), a human-readable intermediate representation that decomposes each interactive visualization into State, Render, Transition, and Constraint components. The DocSpec enables educators to review and refine generation plans before code is produced, bridging the gap between pedagogical intent and executable output. Expert evaluation and a user study show that ViviDoc substantially outperforms naive agentic generation and provides an intuitive editing experience. Our project homepage is available at https://vividoc-homepage.vercel.app/.

AI.tldr: ViviDoc is a human-agent collaborative system that generates interactive educational documents from a single topic input using a multi-agent pipeline (Planner, Executor, Evaluator) and a Document Specification (DocSpec) that decomposes visualizations into State, Render, Transition, and Constraint components, enabling educators to review and refine generation plans before code production.
AI.motivation: Creating interactive articles for complex ideas is costly, requiring both domain expertise and web development skills. While LLM-based agents can automate content creation, naive application yields uncontrollable and unverifiable outputs. There's a significant gap between pedagogical intent and executable output that needs bridging.
AI.method: ViviDoc introduces a multi-agent pipeline (Planner, Executor, Evaluator) and a Document Specification (DocSpec) as a human-readable intermediate representation. DocSpec decomposes each interactive visualization into four components: State, Render, Transition, and Constraint. This allows educators to review and refine generation plans before code is produced, enabling human oversight of AI-generated content.
AI.result: Expert evaluation and user studies demonstrate that ViviDoc substantially outperforms naive agentic generation and provides an intuitive editing experience for educators.
AI.conclusion: ViviDoc effectively bridges the gap between pedagogical intent and executable output by enabling human oversight and refinement of AI-generated interactive documents, making interactive educational content creation more accessible and controllable.

title: Data-Centric Benchmark for Label Noise Estimation and Ranking in Remote Sensing Image Segmentation
summary: High-quality pixel-level annotations are essential for the semantic segmentation of remote sensing imagery. However, such labels are expensive to obtain and often affected by noise due to the labor-intensive and time-consuming nature of pixel-wise annotation, which makes it challenging for human annotators to label every pixel accurately. Annotation errors can significantly degrade the performance and robustness of modern segmentation models, motivating the need for reliable mechanisms to identify and quantify noisy training samples. This paper introduces a novel Data-Centric benchmark, together with a novel, publicly available dataset and two techniques for identifying, quantifying, and ranking training samples according to their level of label noise in remote sensing semantic segmentation. Such proposed methods leverage complementary strategies based on model uncertainty, prediction consistency, and representation analysis, and consistently outperform established baselines across a range of experimental settings. The outcomes of this work are publicly available at https://github.com/keillernogueira/label_noise_segmentation.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel data-centric benchmark with a publicly available remote sensing dataset and two complementary techniques for identifying, quantifying, and ranking noisy training samples based on model uncertainty, prediction consistency, and representation analysis.
AI.motivation: Pixel-level annotations for remote sensing imagery are expensive, labor-intensive, and prone to noise due to human annotation challenges. Such label noise significantly degrades the performance and robustness of segmentation models, necessitating reliable mechanisms to identify and quantify noisy samples.
AI.method: Two novel techniques for label noise identification and quantification in remote sensing semantic segmentation, leveraging complementary strategies based on: (1) model uncertainty, (2) prediction consistency, and (3) representation analysis.
AI.result: The proposed methods consistently outperform established baselines across a range of experimental settings.
AI.conclusion: The work provides publicly available resources including a new benchmark dataset and GitHub repository at https://github.com/keillernogueira/label_noise_segmentation.

title: Evaluating GFlowNet from partial episodes for stable and flexible policy-based training
summary: Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) were developed to learn policies for efficiently sampling combinatorial candidates by interpreting their generative processes as trajectories in directed acyclic graphs. In the value-based training workflow, the objective is to enforce the balance over partial episodes between the flows of the learned policy and the estimated flows of the desired policy, implicitly encouraging policy divergence minimization. The policy-based strategy alternates between estimating the policy divergence and updating the policy, but reliable estimation of the divergence under directed acyclic graphs remains a major challenge. This work bridges the two perspectives by showing that flow balance also yields a principled policy evaluator that measures the divergence, and an evaluation balance objective over partial episodes is proposed for learning the evaluator. As demonstrated on both synthetic and real-world tasks, evaluation balance not only strengthens the reliability of policy-based training but also broadens its flexibility by seamlessly supporting parameterized backward policies and enabling the integration of offline data-collection techniques.

AI.tldr: This paper bridges value-based and policy-based training in GFlowNets by showing that flow balance yields a principled policy evaluator that measures divergence, enabling more reliable and flexible training with evaluation balance objectives over partial episodes.
AI.motivation: GFlowNets learn policies for sampling combinatorial candidates via two perspectives: value-based training enforces flow balance, while policy-based training alternates between estimating policy divergence and updating policies. However, reliable divergence estimation under directed acyclic graphs remains a major challenge in policy-based strategies. The authors aim to bridge these perspectives to overcome this limitation.
AI.method: The authors demonstrate that flow balance produces a principled policy evaluator that measures divergence, and propose an evaluation balance objective over partial episodes to learn this evaluator. This approach enables parameterized backward policies and integration of offline data-collection techniques.
AI.result: Evaluation balance strengthens the reliability of policy-based training and broadens its flexibility. The method is validated on both synthetic and real-world tasks, showing improved performance over previous approaches.
AI.conclusion: By showing that flow balance yields a principled policy evaluator, the authors establish that evaluation balance provides a unified framework combining the strengths of value-based and policy-based training strategies. This enables seamless support for parameterized backward policies and offline data integration, addressing the key challenge of divergence estimation in GFlowNets.

title: AdaPonderLM: Gated Pondering Language Models with Token-Wise Adaptive Depth
summary: Test-time scaling via recurrent/iterative Transformers enables large language models to spend more computation at inference, but most pretrained recurrent LMs run a fixed number of iterations, wasting compute on easy tokens and lacking token-wise adaptivity. Following the core idea of Adaptive Computation Time(ACT) and Early Exit(EE), we propose AdaPonderLM, a self-supervised recurrent language model that learns token-wise early exiting during pretraining without manually tuned per-token/per-layer pruning ratios. AdaPonderLM uses iteration-specific MLP gates with a monotonic halting mask to decide when each token stops recurring, and introduces a KV reuse mechanism that reuses cached key/value states for halted tokens, ensuring train--test consistency and practical acceleration. Across Pythia backbones from 70M to 410M (pretraining) and up to 2.8B (continued pretraining), AdaPonderLM reduces inference compute at about 10% while maintaining comparable language modeling perplexity and competitive downstream accuracy. Our analysis shows the learned gates allocate more computation to high-NLL (hard) tokens, exhibiting adaptive computation time behavior in a fully self-supervised setting. Meanwhile, under iso-FLOPs, the learned halting policy consistently outperforms fixed pruning, showing AdaPonderLM allocates compute to the right tokens rather than just reducing average depth.

AI.tldr: AdaPonderLM proposes a self-supervised recurrent LM that learns token-wise early exiting during pretraining via iteration-specific MLP gates with a monotonic halting mask and KV reuse, reducing inference compute by ~10% while maintaining comparable perplexity and competitive accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing test-time scaling methods run fixed iterations, wasting compute on easy tokens and lacking token-wise adaptivity. The authors seek to implement adaptive computation time behavior like ACT and Early Exit in a fully self-supervised manner without manual per-token pruning ratios.
AI.method: AdaPonderLM uses iteration-specific MLP gates with a monotonic halting mask to decide when each token stops recurring, introduces a KV reuse mechanism for halted tokens to ensure train-test consistency, and learns token-wise early exiting during pretraining without manual pruning ratios.
AI.result: Across Pythia backbones (70M to 410M pretraining, up to 2.8B continued pretraining), AdaPonderLM reduces inference compute by ~10% while maintaining comparable language modeling perplexity and competitive downstream accuracy. The learned gates allocate more computation to high-NLL (hard) tokens, and under iso-FLOPs, outperforms fixed pruning.
AI.conclusion: AdaPonderLM successfully implements adaptive computation time behavior in a fully self-supervised setting, demonstrating that the model allocates compute to the right tokens rather than just reducing average depth, making it an effective approach for token-wise adaptivity without manual tuning.

title: IdGlow: Dynamic Identity Modulation for Multi-Subject Generation
summary: Multi-subject image generation requires seamlessly harmonizing multiple reference identities within a coherent scene. However, existing methods relying on rigid spatial masks or localized attention often struggle with the "stability-plasticity dilemma," particularly failing in tasks that require complex structural deformations, such as identity-preserving age transformation. To address this, we present IdGlow, a mask-free, progressive two-stage framework built upon Flow Matching diffusion models. In the supervised fine-tuning (SFT) stage, we introduce task-adaptive timestep scheduling aligned with diffusion generative dynamics: a linear decay schedule that progressively relaxes constraints for natural group composition, and a temporal gating mechanism that concentrates identity injection within a critical semantic window, successfully preserving adult facial semantics without overriding child-like anatomical structures. To resolve attribute leakage and semantic ambiguity without explicit layout inputs, we further integrate a badcase-driven Vision-Language Model (VLM) for precise, context-aware prompt synthesis. In the second stage, we design a Fine-Grained Group-Level Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) with a weighted margin formulation to simultaneously eliminate multi-subject artifacts, elevate texture harmony, and recalibrate identity fidelity towards real-world distributions. Extensive experiments on two challenging benchmarks -- direct multi-person fusion and age-transformed group generation -- demonstrate that IdGlow fundamentally mitigates the stability-plasticity conflict, achieving a superior Pareto balance between state-of-the-art facial fidelity and commercial-grade aesthetic quality.

AI.tldr: IdGlow introduces a mask-free, progressive two-stage framework based on Flow Matching diffusion models for multi-subject image generation, addressing the stability-plasticity dilemma through task-adaptive timestep scheduling and Fine-Grained Group-Level DPO.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-subject image generation methods relying on rigid spatial masks or localized attention struggle with the "stability-plasticity dilemma," particularly failing in tasks requiring complex structural deformations like identity-preserving age transformation.
AI.method: IdGlow consists of: (1) Supervised fine-tuning stage with task-adaptive timestep scheduling (linear decay schedule and temporal gating mechanism) and badcase-driven VLM for context-aware prompt synthesis; (2) Fine-Grained Group-Level Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) with weighted margin formulation to eliminate artifacts, improve texture harmony, and recalibrate identity fidelity.
AI.result: IdGlow fundamentally mitigates the stability-plasticity conflict, achieving a superior Pareto balance between state-of-the-art facial fidelity and commercial-grade aesthetic quality across two challenging benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Extensive experiments on direct multi-person fusion and age-transformed group generation benchmarks demonstrate IdGlow's effectiveness in harmonizing multiple identities while preserving anatomical structures and achieving superior aesthetic quality.

title: No More Maybe-Arrows: Resolving Causal Uncertainty by Breaking Symmetries
summary: The recent works on causal discovery have followed a similar trend of learning partial ancestral graphs (PAGs) since observational data constrain the true causal directed acyclic graph (DAG) only up to a Markov equivalence class. This limits their application in the majority of downstream tasks, as uncertainty in causal relations remains unresolved. We propose a new refinement framework, CausalSAGE, for converting PAGs to DAGs while respecting the underlying causal relations. The framework expands discrete variables into state-level representations, constrains the search space using structural knowledge and soft priors, and applies a unified differentiable objective for joint optimization. The final DAG is obtained by aggregating the optimized structures and enforcing acyclicity when necessary. Our experimental evaluations show that the obtained DAGs preserve the underlying causal relations while also being efficient to obtain.

AI.tldr: CausalSAGE is a refinement framework that converts Partial Ancestral Graphs (PAGs) to Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) while preserving underlying causal relations, using state-level variable expansions, structural knowledge constraints, and a unified differentiable optimization objective.
AI.motivation: Recent causal discovery methods use Partial Ancestral Graphs (PAGs) because observational data only constrain true DAGs up to Markov equivalence classes. PAGs retain uncertainty in causal relations, limiting their applicability in downstream tasks.
AI.method: CausalSAGE expands discrete variables into state-level representations, constrains the search space using structural knowledge and soft priors, applies a unified differentiable objective for joint optimization, and obtains DAGs by aggregating optimized structures while enforcing acyclicity.
AI.result: Experimental evaluations demonstrate that the obtained DAGs preserve underlying causal relations while being efficient to obtain.
AI.conclusion: CausalSAGE successfully converts PAGs to DAGs that respect causal relations, addressing the uncertainty limitation in existing causal discovery approaches.

title: From Variance to Invariance: Qualitative Content Analysis for Narrative Graph Annotation
summary: Narratives in news discourse play a critical role in shaping public understanding of economic events, such as inflation. Annotating and evaluating these narratives in a structured manner remains a key challenge for Natural Language Processing (NLP). In this work, we introduce a narrative graph annotation framework that integrates principles from qualitative content analysis (QCA) to prioritize annotation quality by reducing annotation errors. We present a dataset of inflation narratives annotated as directed acyclic graphs (DAGs), where nodes represent events and edges encode causal relations. To evaluate annotation quality, we employed a $6\times3$ factorial experimental design to examine the effects of narrative representation (six levels) and distance metric type (three levels) on inter-annotator agreement (Krippendorrf's $α$), capturing the presence of human label variation (HLV) in narrative interpretations. Our analysis shows that (1) lenient metrics (overlap-based distance) overestimate reliability, and (2) locally-constrained representations (e.g., one-hop neighbors) reduce annotation variability. Our annotation and implementation of graph-based Krippendorrf's $α$ are open-sourced. The annotation framework and evaluation results provide practical guidance for NLP research on graph-based narrative annotation under HLV.

AI.tldr: A narrative graph annotation framework for inflation news narratives, using DAGs (nodes=events, edges=causal relations) and a 6×3 factorial design to evaluate inter-annotator agreement, finding that lenient metrics overestimate reliability while locally-constrained representations reduce annotation variability.
AI.motivation: Narratives in news discourse shape public understanding of economic events like inflation; annotating and evaluating these narratives in a structured manner remains a key challenge for Natural Language Processing (NLP).
AI.method: Integrates qualitative content analysis (QCA) principles into a narrative graph annotation framework. Presents a dataset of inflation narratives annotated as directed acyclic graphs (DAGs). Employs a 6×3 factorial experimental design examining narrative representation (6 levels) and distance metric type (3 levels) on Krippendorff's α, capturing human label variation (HLV) in narrative interpretations.
AI.result: Lenient metrics (overlap-based distance) overestimate reliability, while locally-constrained representations (e.g., one-hop neighbors) reduce annotation variability.
AI.conclusion: The annotation framework and evaluation results provide practical guidance for NLP research on graph-based narrative annotation under HLV. The annotation and implementation are open-sourced.

title: Linking Modality Isolation in Heterogeneous Collaborative Perception
summary: Collaborative perception leverages data exchange among multiple agents to enhance overall perception capabilities. However, heterogeneity across agents introduces domain gaps that hinder collaboration, and this is further exacerbated by an underexplored issue: modality isolation. It arises when multiple agents with different modalities never co-occur in any training data frame, enlarging cross-modal domain gaps. Existing alignment methods rely on supervision from spatially overlapping observations, thus fail to handle modality isolation. To address this challenge, we propose CodeAlign, the first efficient, co-occurrence-free alignment framework that smoothly aligns modalities via cross-modal feature-code-feature(FCF) translation. The key idea is to explicitly identify the representation consistency through codebook, and directly learn mappings between modality-specific feature spaces, thereby eliminating the need for spatial correspondence. Codebooks regularize feature spaces into code spaces, providing compact yet expressive representations. With a prepared code space for each modality, CodeAlign learns FCF translations that map features to the corresponding codes of other modalities, which are then decoded back into features in the target code space, enabling effective alignment. Experiments show that, when integrating three modalities, CodeAlign requires only 8% of the training parameters of prior alignment methods, reduces communication load by 1024x, and achieves state-of-the-art perception performance on both OPV2V and DAIR-V2X dataset. Code will be released on https://github.com/cxliu0314/CodeAlign.

AI.tldr: CodeAlign is the first co-occurrence-free alignment framework that uses cross-modal feature-code-feature translation to address modality isolation in collaborative perception, achieving 1024x communication reduction and 92% parameter savings while attaining state-of-the-art performance on OPV2V and DAIR-V2X datasets.
AI.motivation: Collaborative perception enhances overall capabilities through data exchange, but faces challenges from agent heterogeneity and an underexplored modality isolation issue where agents with different modalities never co-occur in training data, creating enlarged cross-modal domain gaps. Existing alignment methods rely on spatially overlapping observations and fail to handle this modality isolation problem.
AI.method: CodeAlign employs cross-modal feature-code-feature (FCF) translation to identify representation consistency through codebooks and learn mappings between modality-specific feature spaces without requiring spatial correspondence. Codebooks regularize feature spaces into compact yet expressive code spaces, enabling FCF translations that map features to codes of other modalities, which are then decoded back into features in the target code space.
AI.result: With three modalities, CodeAlign requires only 8% of training parameters compared to prior alignment methods and achieves a 1024x reduction in communication load. It delivers state-of-the-art perception performance on both OPV2V and DAIR-V2X datasets, demonstrating significantly improved efficiency and effectiveness in handling modality isolation challenges.
AI.conclusion: CodeAlign successfully addresses the modality isolation challenge in collaborative perception through its co-occurrence-free FCF translation framework, offering dramatic parameter and communication efficiency improvements while maintaining competitive performance, making it a practical solution for real-world deployment.

title: A level-wise training scheme for learning neural multigrid smoothers with application to integral equations
summary:
Convolution-type integral equations commonly occur in signal processing and image processing. Discretizing these equations yields large and ill-conditioned linear systems. While the classic multigrid method is effective for solving linear systems derived from partial differential equations (PDE) problems, it fails to solve integral equations because its smoothers, which are implemented as conventional relaxation methods, are ineffective in reducing high-frequency components in the errors.
  We propose a novel neural multigrid scheme where learned neural operators replace classical smoothers. Unlike classical smoothers, these operators are trained offline. Once trained, the neural smoothers generalize to new right-hand-side vectors without retraining, making it an efficient solver. We design level-wise loss functions incorporating spectral filtering to emulate the multigrid frequency decomposition principle, ensuring each operator focuses on solving distinct high-frequency spectral bands.
  Although we focus on integral equations, the framework is generalizable to all kinds of problems, including PDE problems. Our experiments demonstrate superior efficiency over classical solvers and robust convergence across varying problem sizes and regularization weights.

AI.tldr: A neural multigrid scheme that replaces classical smoothers with learned neural operators for solving convolution-type integral equations, achieving superior efficiency and robust convergence compared to classical solvers.
AI.motivation: Convolution-type integral equations frequently arise in signal and image processing, but their discretization creates large, ill-conditioned linear systems. The classic multigrid method fails for these equations because conventional relaxation-based smoothers are ineffective at reducing high-frequency error components.
AI.method: The paper proposes a neural multigrid framework where learned neural operators serve as smoothers, replacing classical methods. Level-wise loss functions with spectral filtering are designed to emulate multigrid frequency decomposition, ensuring each operator focuses on distinct high-frequency spectral bands. The operators are trained offline and generalize to new right-hand-side vectors without retraining.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate superior efficiency over classical solvers and robust convergence across varying problem sizes and regularization weights.
AI.conclusion: The proposed neural multigrid approach effectively solves convolution-type integral equations and is generalizable to other problem types including PDEs, offering both computational efficiency and convergence guarantees.

title: When Numbers Tell Half the Story: Human-Metric Alignment in Topic Model Evaluation
summary: Topic models uncover latent thematic structures in text corpora, yet evaluating their quality remains challenging, particularly in specialized domains. Existing methods often rely on automated metrics like topic coherence and diversity, which may not fully align with human judgment. Human evaluation tasks, such as word intrusion, provide valuable insights but are costly and primarily validated on general-domain corpora. This paper introduces Topic Word Mixing (TWM), a novel human evaluation task assessing inter-topic distinctness by testing whether annotators can distinguish between word sets from single or mixed topics. TWM complements word intrusion's focus on intra-topic coherence and provides a human-grounded counterpart to diversity metrics. We evaluate six topic models - both statistical and embedding-based (LDA, NMF, Top2Vec, BERTopic, CFMF, CFMF-emb) - comparing automated metrics with human evaluation methods based on nearly 4,000 annotations from a domain-specific corpus of philosophy of science publications. Our findings reveal that word intrusion and coherence metrics do not always align, particularly in specialized domains, and that TWM captures human-perceived distinctness while appearing to align with diversity metrics. We release the annotated dataset and task generation code. This work highlights the need for evaluation frameworks bridging automated and human assessments, particularly for domain-specific corpora.

AI.tldr: Topic Word Mixing (TWM) is a novel human evaluation task introduced to assess inter-topic distinctness in topic models, compared with existing automated metrics (coherence, diversity) and word intrusion on a domain-specific philosophy of science corpus.
AI.motivation: Topic model evaluation is challenging, especially in specialized domains. Existing automated metrics (coherence, diversity) often don't align with human judgment, and human evaluation tasks like word intrusion are costly and validated on general-domain corpora.
AI.method: The paper introduces Topic Word Mixing (TWM), a human evaluation task testing whether annotators can distinguish between word sets from single vs. mixed topics. Six topic models (LDA, NMF, Top2Vec, BERTopic, CFMF, CFMF-emb) were evaluated using nearly 4,000 annotations from a domain-specific philosophy of science corpus, comparing automated metrics with human evaluation methods.
AI.result: Word intrusion and coherence metrics do not always align, particularly in specialized domains. TWM captures human-perceived distinctness and aligns with diversity metrics, demonstrating that existing metrics may not fully reflect human judgment in domain-specific contexts.
AI.conclusion: This work highlights the need for evaluation frameworks that bridge automated and human assessments, especially for domain-specific corpora. The paper releases an annotated dataset and task generation code to support future research.

title: Exploring Spatiotemporal Feature Propagation for Video-Level Compressive Spectral Reconstruction: Dataset, Model and Benchmark
summary: Recently, Spectral Compressive Imaging (SCI) has achieved remarkable success, unlocking significant potential for dynamic spectral vision. However, existing reconstruction methods, primarily image-based, suffer from two limitations: (i) Encoding process masks spatial-spectral features, leading to uncertainty in reconstructing missing information from single compressed measurements, and (ii) The frame-by-frame reconstruction paradigm fails to ensure temporal consistency, which is crucial in the video perception. To address these challenges, this paper seeks to advance spectral reconstruction from the image level to the video level, leveraging the complementary features and temporal continuity across adjacent frames in dynamic scenes. Initially, we construct the first high-quality dynamic hyperspectral image dataset (DynaSpec), comprising 30 sequences obtained through frame-scanning acquisition. Subsequently, we propose the Propagation-Guided Spectral Video Reconstruction Transformer (PG-SVRT), which employs a spatial-then-temporal attention to effectively reconstruct spectral features from abundant video information, while using a bridged token to reduce computational complexity. Finally, we conduct simulation experiments to assess the performance of four SCI systems, and construct a DD-CASSI prototype for real-world data collection and benchmarking. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PG-SVRT achieves superior performance in reconstruction quality, spectral fidelity, and temporal consistency, while maintaining minimal FLOPs. Project page: https://github.com/nju-cite/DynaSpec

AI.tldr: This paper introduces PG-SVRT, a video-level spectral reconstruction transformer that addresses two key limitations of existing SCI methods: (1) image-based reconstruction cannot recover missing spatial-spectral information from single compressed measurements, and (2) frame-by-frame approaches lack temporal consistency. The authors first created DynaSpec, the first high-quality dynamic hyperspectral dataset, and then proposed PG-SVRT which employs spatial-then-temporal attention with a bridged token architecture. Experiments on four SCI systems show PG-SVRT achieves superior reconstruction quality, spectral fidelity, and temporal consistency while maintaining minimal computational cost.
AI.motivation: Existing Spectral Compressive Imaging (SCI) reconstruction methods are image-based and suffer from two critical limitations: (1) the encoding process masks spatial-spectral features, creating uncertainty when reconstructing missing information from single compressed measurements, and (2) the frame-by-frame reconstruction paradigm fails to ensure temporal consistency, which is essential for video perception applications. The paper seeks to advance spectral reconstruction from the image level to the video level by leveraging complementary features and temporal continuity across adjacent frames in dynamic scenes.
AI.method: The authors first constructed DynaSpec, the first high-quality dynamic hyperspectral image dataset comprising 30 sequences acquired via frame-scanning. They then proposed the Propagation-Guided Spectral Video Reconstruction Transformer (PG-SVRT), which employs a spatial-then-temporal attention mechanism to effectively reconstruct spectral features from abundant video information. A key innovation is the use of a bridged token to reduce computational complexity. Additionally, a DD-CASSI prototype was constructed for real-world data collection and benchmarking.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on four SCI systems demonstrate that PG-SVRT achieves superior performance in reconstruction quality, spectral fidelity, and temporal consistency while maintaining minimal floating-point operations (FLOPs). The approach successfully addresses the two limitations identified in the abstract by leveraging temporal information across frames rather than processing each frame independently.
AI.conclusion: This work advances spectral reconstruction from image-level to video-level processing by introducing temporal consistency and leveraging adjacent frame information. The proposed PG-SVRT with its spatial-then-temporal attention and bridged token architecture effectively addresses the key limitations of existing SCI methods, demonstrating that video-level reconstruction can significantly improve both reconstruction quality and temporal consistency while maintaining computational efficiency.

title: Understanding LoRA as Knowledge Memory: An Empirical Analysis
summary: Continuous knowledge updating for pre-trained large language models (LLMs) is increasingly necessary yet remains challenging. Although inference-time methods like In-Context Learning (ICL) and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) are popular, they face constraints in context budgets, costs, and retrieval fragmentation. Departing from these context-dependent paradigms, this work investigates a parametric approach using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) as a modular knowledge memory. Although few recent works examine this concept, the fundamental mechanics governing its capacity and composability remain largely unexplored. We bridge this gap through the first systematic empirical study mapping the design space of LoRA-based memory, ranging from characterizing storage capacity and optimizing internalization to scaling multi-module systems and evaluating long-context reasoning. Rather than proposing a single architecture, we provide practical guidance on the operational boundaries of LoRA memory. Overall, our findings position LoRA as the complementary axis of memory alongside RAG and ICL, offering distinct advantages.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first systematic empirical study of using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) as a parametric memory mechanism for continuous knowledge updating in LLMs, exploring its design space, capacity, composability, and scaling properties.
AI.motivation: Continuous knowledge updating for pre-trained LLMs is necessary but challenging. Existing context-dependent methods like In-Context Learning (ICL) and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) face constraints in context budgets, costs, and retrieval fragmentation. The fundamental mechanics governing parametric approaches using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) as modular knowledge memory remain largely unexplored.
AI.method: A comprehensive empirical study mapping the design space of LoRA-based memory, including: characterizing storage capacity, optimizing internalization processes, scaling multi-module LoRA systems, and evaluating long-context reasoning capabilities. The research systematically explores operational boundaries rather than proposing a single architecture.
AI.result: Practical guidance on the operational boundaries of LoRA memory was provided. LoRA was positioned as a complementary axis of memory alongside RAG and ICL, offering distinct advantages. The study successfully characterized the capacity and composability properties of LoRA-based knowledge memory.
AI.conclusion: LoRA-based memory represents a viable and advantageous approach for continuous knowledge updating in LLMs, providing a parametric alternative to context-dependent methods like RAG and ICL. It establishes LoRA as a fundamental memory axis that complements rather than replaces existing paradigms, with specific operational advantages in terms of cost efficiency and knowledge integration.

title: AMemGym: Interactive Memory Benchmarking for Assistants in Long-Horizon Conversations
summary: Long-horizon interactions between users and LLM-based assistants necessitate effective memory management, yet current approaches face challenges in training and evaluation of memory. Existing memory benchmarks rely on static, off-policy data as context, limiting evaluation reliability and scalability. To address these gaps, we introduce AMemGym, an interactive environment enabling on-policy evaluation and optimization for memory-driven personalization. AMemGym employs structured data sampling to predefine user profiles, state-dependent questions, and state evolution trajectories, enabling cost-effective generation of high-quality, evaluation-aligned interactions. LLM-simulated users expose latent states through role-play while maintaining structured state consistency. Comprehensive metrics based on structured data guide both assessment and optimization of assistants. Extensive experiments reveal performance gaps in existing memory systems (e.g., RAG, long-context LLMs, and agentic memory) and corresponding reasons. AMemGym not only enables effective selection among competing approaches but also can potentially drive the self-evolution of memory management strategies. By bridging structured state evolution with free-form interactions, our framework provides a scalable, diagnostically rich environment for advancing memory capabilities in conversational agents.

AI.tldr: AMemGym is an interactive environment for on-policy evaluation and optimization of memory management in LLM-based assistants, enabling scalable assessment of memory systems through structured state evolution trajectories.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon interactions with LLM-based assistants require effective memory management, but existing approaches face challenges in training and evaluation. Current memory benchmarks rely on static, off-policy data as context, limiting evaluation reliability and scalability.
AI.method: AMemGym employs structured data sampling to predefine user profiles, state-dependent questions, and state evolution trajectories for cost-effective generation of high-quality, evaluation-aligned interactions. LLM-simulated users expose latent states through role-play while maintaining structured state consistency, and comprehensive metrics based on structured data guide both assessment and optimization of assistants.
AI.result: Extensive experiments reveal performance gaps in existing memory systems (RAG, long-context LLMs, and agentic memory) and corresponding reasons. The framework enables effective selection among competing approaches and can potentially drive the self-evolution of memory management strategies.
AI.conclusion: By bridging structured state evolution with free-form interactions, AMemGym provides a scalable, diagnostically rich environment for advancing memory capabilities in conversational agents.

title: Position: Evaluation of Visual Processing Should Be Human-Centered, Not Metric-Centered
summary: This position paper argues that the evaluation of modern visual processing systems should no longer be driven primarily by single-metric image quality assessment benchmarks, particularly in the era of generative and perception-oriented methods. Image restoration exemplifies this divergence: while objective IQA metrics enable reproducible, scalable evaluation, they have increasingly drifted apart from human perception and user preferences. We contend that this mismatch risks constraining innovation and misguiding research progress across visual processing tasks. Rather than rejecting metrics altogether, this paper calls for a rebalancing of evaluation paradigms, advocating a more human-centered, context-aware, and fine-grained approach to assessing the visual models' outcomes.

AI.tldr: This position paper argues that traditional single-metric image quality assessment benchmarks have become misaligned with human perception in modern visual processing systems, advocating for a more human-centered, context-aware, and fine-grained evaluation approach rather than rejecting metrics entirely.
AI.motivation: In the era of generative and perception-oriented methods, objective IQA metrics like PSNR and SSIM have increasingly drifted apart from human perception and user preferences, especially in image restoration tasks. This mismatch risks constraining innovation and misguiding research progress across visual processing domains.
AI.method: Rather than rejecting objective metrics altogether, the paper calls for a rebalancing of evaluation paradigms toward a more human-centered, context-aware, and fine-grained approach to assessing visual model outcomes.
AI.result: The paper contends that a more nuanced, human-centric evaluation framework is needed to better align with actual user preferences and avoid constraining innovation in modern visual processing systems.
AI.conclusion: Researchers should adopt more sophisticated, perception-aware evaluation methods that balance objective metrics with human-centered assessment to ensure research progress remains aligned with practical user needs.

title: A Deep Learning Framework for Heat Demand Forecasting using Time-Frequency Representations of Decomposed Features
summary: District Heating Systems are essential infrastructure for delivering heat to consumers across a geographic region sustainably, yet efficient management relies on optimizing diverse energy sources, such as wood, gas, electricity, and solar, in response to fluctuating demand. Aligning supply with demand is critical not only for ensuring reliable heat distribution but also for minimizing carbon emissions and extending infrastructure lifespan through lower operating temperatures. However, accurate multi-step forecasting to support these goals remains challenging due to complex, non-linear usage patterns and external dependencies. In this work, we propose a novel deep learning framework for day-ahead heat demand prediction that leverages time-frequency representations of historical data. By applying Continuous Wavelet Transform to decomposed demand and external meteorological factors, our approach enables Convolutional Neural Networks to learn hierarchical temporal features that are often inaccessible to standard time domain models. We systematically evaluate this method against statistical baselines, state-of-the-art Transformers, and emerging foundation models using multi-year data from three distinct Danish districts, a Danish city, and a German city. The results show a significant advancement, reducing the Mean Absolute Error by 36% to 43% compared to the strongest baselines, achieving forecasting accuracy of up to 95% across annual test datasets. Qualitative and statistical analyses further confirm the accuracy and robustness by reliably tracking volatile demand peaks where others fail. This work contributes both a high-performance forecasting architecture and critical insights into optimal feature composition, offering a validated solution for modern energy applications.

AI.tldr: A novel deep learning framework using Continuous Wavelet Transform and Convolutional Neural Networks for day-ahead heat demand prediction in district heating systems, achieving 36-43% error reduction and up to 95% accuracy compared to statistical baselines and Transformers.
AI.motivation: District heating systems require efficient management of diverse energy sources (wood, gas, electricity, solar) to align supply with fluctuating demand, minimize carbon emissions, and extend infrastructure lifespan. Accurate multi-step forecasting is challenging due to complex non-linear usage patterns and external dependencies, making reliable prediction difficult.
AI.method: The proposed framework applies Continuous Wavelet Transform to decompose historical heat demand and external meteorological factors, enabling Convolutional Neural Networks to learn hierarchical temporal features. The method was systematically evaluated against statistical baselines, Transformers, and foundation models using multi-year data from three Danish districts, one Danish city, and one German city.
AI.result: The approach reduces Mean Absolute Error by 36-43% compared to the strongest baselines, achieves forecasting accuracy of up to 95% across annual test datasets, and reliably tracks volatile demand peaks where other methods fail.
AI.conclusion: This work contributes a high-performance forecasting architecture and critical insights into optimal feature composition, offering a validated, robust solution for modern district heating applications that can significantly improve energy management and sustainability.

title: CharacterFlywheel: Scaling Iterative Improvement of Engaging and Steerable LLMs in Production
summary: This report presents CharacterFlywheel, an iterative flywheel process for improving large language models (LLMs) in production social chat applications across Instagram, WhatsApp, and Messenger. Starting from LLaMA 3.1, we refined models across 15 generations using data from both internal and external real-user traffic.
