Through continuous deployments from July 2024 to April 2025, we conducted controlled 7-day A/B tests showing consistent engagement improvements: 7 of 8 newly deployed models demonstrated positive lift over the baseline, with the strongest performers achieving up to 8.8% improvement in engagement breadth and 19.4% in engagement depth. We also observed substantial gains in steerability, with instruction following increasing from 59.2% to 84.8% and instruction violations decreasing from 26.6% to 5.8%. We detail the CharacterFlywheel process which integrates data curation, reward modeling to estimate and interpolate the landscape of engagement metrics, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), reinforcement learning (RL), and both offline and online evaluation to ensure reliable progress at each optimization step. We also discuss our methods for overfitting prevention and navigating production dynamics at scale. These contributions advance the scientific rigor and understanding of LLMs in social applications serving millions of users.

AI.tldr: CharacterFlywheel is an iterative flywheel process for improving LLMs in production social chat applications (Instagram, WhatsApp, Messenger), starting from LLaMA 3.1 and refining models across 15 generations through data curation, reward modeling, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and reinforcement learning (RL).
AI.motivation: The goal is to improve large language models in production social chat applications across Instagram, WhatsApp, and Messenger, enhancing engagement, steerability, and reliability for millions of users.
AI.method: Starting from LLaMA 3.1, the team refined models across 15 generations using data from both internal and external real-user traffic. The CharacterFlywheel process integrates data curation, reward modeling for engagement metric estimation, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), reinforcement learning (RL), and offline/online evaluation. The authors also employed overfitting prevention and methods for navigating production dynamics at scale.
AI.result: Through controlled 7-day A/B tests from July 2024 to April 2025, 7 of 8 newly deployed models demonstrated positive lift over the baseline. Strongest performers achieved up to 8.8% improvement in engagement breadth and 19.4% in engagement depth. Instruction following increased from 59.2% to 84.8%, while instruction violations decreased from 26.6% to 5.8%.
AI.conclusion: This work advances the scientific rigor and understanding of LLMs in social applications serving millions of users, demonstrating that iterative flywheel processes with proper evaluation and overfitting prevention can lead to substantial improvements in both engagement and steerability metrics.

title: Exploring 3D Dataset Pruning
summary: Dataset pruning has been widely studied for 2D images to remove redundancy and accelerate training, while particular pruning methods for 3D data remain largely unexplored. In this work, we study dataset pruning for 3D data, where its observed common long-tail class distribution nature make optimization under conventional evaluation metrics Overall Accuracy (OA) and Mean Accuracy (mAcc) inherently conflicting, and further make pruning particularly challenging. To address this, we formulate pruning as approximating the full-data expected risk with a weighted subset, which reveals two key errors: coverage error from insufficient representativeness and prior-mismatch bias from inconsistency between subset-induced class weights and target metrics. We propose representation-aware subset selection with per-class retention quotas for long-tail coverage, and prior-invariant teacher supervision using calibrated soft labels and embedding-geometry distillation. The retention quota also serves as a switch to control the OA-mAcc trade-off. Extensive experiments on 3D datasets show that our method can improve both metrics across multiple settings while adapting to different downstream preferences. Our code is available at https://github.com/XiaohanZhao123/3D-Dataset-Pruning.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a novel dataset pruning method for 3D data that addresses the challenges posed by long-tail class distributions, which cause inherent conflicts between Overall Accuracy (OA) and Mean Accuracy (mAcc). The method uses representation-aware subset selection with per-class retention quotas and prior-invariant teacher supervision to improve both evaluation metrics simultaneously.
AI.motivation: Dataset pruning for 2D images is well-studied, but 3D data pruning remains largely unexplored. 3D datasets typically exhibit long-tail class distributions, making optimization under conventional metrics (OA and mAcc) inherently conflicting and pruning particularly challenging.
AI.method: The paper formulates pruning as approximating full-data expected risk with a weighted subset, identifying two key errors: coverage error (insufficient representativeness) and prior-mismatch bias (inconsistency between subset-induced class weights and target metrics). The proposed method includes: (1) representation-aware subset selection with per-class retention quotas for long-tail coverage, (2) prior-invariant teacher supervision using calibrated soft labels and embedding-geometry distillation, and (3) a retention quota switch to control OA-mAcc trade-off.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on 3D datasets demonstrate that the method improves both OA and mAcc across multiple settings while adapting to different downstream preferences, effectively resolving the inherent conflict between these metrics in long-tail distributions.
AI.conclusion: By addressing coverage error and prior-mismatch bias through representation-aware selection and prior-invariant supervision, the proposed method successfully enables effective dataset pruning for 3D data with long-tail distributions, outperforming conventional approaches on both OA and mAcc metrics simultaneously.

title: A Decomposition Framework for Certifiably Optimal Orthogonal Sparse PCA
summary:
Sparse Principal Component Analysis (SPCA) is an important technique for high-dimensional data analysis, improving interpretability by imposing sparsity on principal components. However, existing methods often fail to simultaneously guarantee sparsity, orthogonality, and optimality of the principal components.
  To address this challenge, this work introduces a novel Sparse Principal Component Analysis (SPCA) algorithm called \textsc{GS-SPCA} (SPCA with Gram-Schmidt Orthogonalization), which simultaneously enforces sparsity, orthogonality, and optimality. However, the original GS-SPCA algorithm is computationally expensive due to the inherent $\ell_0$-norm constraint. To address this issue, we propose two acceleration strategies:
  First, we combine \textbf{Branch-and-Bound} with the GS-SPCA algorithm. By incorporating this strategy, we are able to obtain $\varepsilon$-optimal solutions with a trade-off between precision and efficiency, significantly improving computational speed.
  Second, we propose a \textbf{decomposition framework} for efficiently solving \textbf{multiple} principal components. This framework approximates the covariance matrix using a block-diagonal matrix through a thresholding method, reducing the original SPCA problem to a set of block-wise subproblems on approximately block-diagonal matrices.

AI.tldr: GS-SPCA is a novel Sparse PCA algorithm that simultaneously enforces sparsity, orthogonality, and optimality of principal components, enhanced by two acceleration strategies: (1) Branch-and-Bound for ε-optimal solutions with efficiency-precision trade-off, and (2) a decomposition framework that reduces the problem to block-wise subproblems via block-diagonal approximation.
AI.motivation: Existing Sparse PCA methods fail to simultaneously guarantee sparsity, orthogonality, and optimality. The original GS-SPCA algorithm is computationally expensive due to its inherent ℓ0-norm constraint.
AI.method: Introduces GS-SPCA (Sparse PCA with Gram-Schmidt Orthogonalization) and two acceleration strategies: (1) combining Branch-and-Bound with GS-SPCA to obtain ε-optimal solutions, and (2) a decomposition framework that approximates the covariance matrix as block-diagonal via thresholding, reducing the problem to block-wise subproblems.
AI.result: The proposed approach achieves simultaneous sparsity, orthogonality, and optimality while significantly improving computational speed through the Branch-and-Bound strategy and block-diagonal approximation framework.
AI.conclusion: GS-SPCA with acceleration strategies provides an efficient solution to SPCA that maintains all desired properties (sparsity, orthogonality, optimality) with controllable precision-efficiency trade-off.

title: PonderLM-3: Adaptive Token-Wise Pondering with Differentiable Masking
summary: Test-time scaling has shown that allocating more additional computation at inference can improve generation quality, motivating a natural follow-up question: where should this computation be spent? Building on this insight, we introduce PonderLM-3, a pretraining framework for token-wise adaptive pondering that learns to selectively allocate additional computation under purely self-supervised objectives, built on top of the PonderLM-2 backbone. This makes additional inference computation an allocatable per-token resource, so tokens receive more computation only when it is beneficial, rather than paying a uniform extra cost. To make this allocation learnable while maintaining train-inference consistency, PonderLM-3 injects a differentiable attention mask during pretraining and pairs it with a matching hard pruning rule at inference. PonderLM-3 defines a stronger Pareto frontier: compared with existing recursive or adaptive baselines, it achieves lower pretraining perplexity at equal inference FLOPs. On downstream benchmarks, PonderLM-3 attains comparable performance to fixed-step PonderLM-2 under the same maximum number of additional computation steps, while using fewer inference FLOPs in practice. Overall, PonderLM-3 provides an end-to-end differentiable and train-inference consistent framework for token-wise adaptive computation, enabling additional inference compute to be allocated where it is most useful rather than paid uniformly by every token.

AI.tldr: PonderLM-3 introduces a token-wise adaptive computation framework that learns to allocate additional inference computation selectively, achieving lower perplexity at equal FLOPs compared to baselines while matching fixed-step performance with fewer computational resources.
AI.motivation: Test-time scaling shows that additional inference computation can improve generation quality, but existing approaches pay uniform extra cost per token rather than allocating computation where it's most beneficial. The authors seek a learnable way to selectively allocate inference resources under self-supervised objectives while maintaining train-inference consistency.
AI.method: Building on PonderLM-2, PonderLM-3 injects a differentiable attention mask during pretraining paired with a matching hard pruning rule at inference. This creates an allocatable per-token resource where computation is allocated only when beneficial, enabling end-to-end differentiable learning of adaptive computation patterns.
AI.result: PonderLM-3 defines a stronger Pareto frontier, achieving lower pretraining perplexity at equal inference FLOPs compared to recursive or adaptive baselines. On downstream benchmarks, it matches fixed-step PonderLM-2 performance while using fewer inference FLOPs in practice.
AI.conclusion: PonderLM-3 provides an end-to-end differentiable and train-inference consistent framework for token-wise adaptive computation, enabling additional inference compute to be allocated precisely where it provides the most value rather than being uniformly applied to all tokens.

title: RC-GeoCP: Geometric Consensus for Radar-Camera Collaborative Perception
summary: Collaborative perception (CP) enhances scene understanding through multi-agent information sharing. While LiDAR-centric systems offer precise geometry, high costs and performance degradation in adverse weather necessitate multi-modal alternatives. Despite dense visual semantics and robust spatial measurements, the synergy between cameras and 4D radar remains underexplored in collaborative settings. This work introduces RC-GeoCP, the first framework to explore the fusion of 4D radar and images in CP. To resolve misalignment caused by depth ambiguity and spatial dispersion across agents, RC-GeoCP establishes a radar-anchored geometric consensus. Specifically, Geometric Structure Rectification (GSR) aligns visual semantics with geometry derived from radar to generate spatially grounded, geometry-consistent representations. Uncertainty-Aware Communication (UAC) formulates selective transmission as a conditional entropy reduction process to prioritize informative features based on inter-agent disagreement. Finally, the Consensus-Driven Assembler (CDA) aggregates multi-agent information via shared geometric anchors to form a globally coherent representation. We establish the first unified radar-camera CP benchmark on V2X-Radar and V2X-R, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance with significantly reduced communication overhead. Code will be released soon.

AI.tldr: RC-GeoCP is the first collaborative perception framework fusing 4D radar and images via geometric consensus, uncertainty-aware communication, and consensus-driven assembly, establishing the first radar-camera CP benchmark with state-of-the-art performance and reduced communication overhead.
AI.motivation: LiDAR-centric systems suffer from high costs and performance degradation in adverse weather, while camera-4D radar synergy remains underexplored in collaborative settings. This work addresses the need for multi-modal alternatives that combine cameras' dense visual semantics and 4D radars' robust spatial measurements through multi-agent information sharing.
AI.method: RC-GeoCP introduces three key components: (1) Geometric Structure Rectification (GSR) aligns visual semantics with radar-derived geometry to generate spatially grounded representations; (2) Uncertainty-Aware Communication (UAC) formulates selective transmission as conditional entropy reduction to prioritize informative features; (3) Consensus-Driven Assembler (CDA) aggregates multi-agent information via shared geometric anchors. The framework establishes the first unified radar-camera CP benchmark on V2X-Radar and V2X-R datasets.
AI.result: The proposed framework achieves state-of-the-art performance in collaborative perception while demonstrating significantly reduced communication overhead compared to existing methods. The work establishes the first unified radar-camera collaborative perception benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The fusion of 4D radar and images in collaborative perception is viable and effective when properly aligned through geometric consensus mechanisms. RC-GeoCP demonstrates that radar-camera fusion can achieve superior scene understanding with lower communication costs, establishing a new benchmark for multi-modal collaborative perception research.

title: Demystifying Group Relative Policy Optimization: Its Policy Gradient is a U-Statistic
summary: Group relative policy optimization (GRPO), a core methodological component of DeepSeekMath and DeepSeek-R1, has emerged as a cornerstone for scaling reasoning capabilities of large language models. Despite its widespread adoption and the proliferation of follow-up works, the theoretical properties of GRPO remain less studied. This paper provides a unified framework to understand GRPO through the lens of classical U-statistics. We demonstrate that the GRPO policy gradient is inherently a U-statistic, allowing us to characterize its mean squared error (MSE), derive the finite-sample error bound and asymptotic distribution of the suboptimality gap for its learned policy. Our findings reveal that GRPO is asymptotically equivalent to an oracle policy gradient algorithm -- one with access to a value function that quantifies the goodness of its learning policy at each training iteration -- and achieves asymptotically optimal performance within a broad class of policy gradient algorithms. Furthermore, we establish a universal scaling law that offers principled guidance for selecting the optimal group size. Empirical experiments further validate our theoretical findings, demonstrating that the optimal group size is universal, and verify the oracle property of GRPO.

AI.tldr: GRPO is unified through U-statistics, characterizing its MSE, deriving error bounds, establishing asymptotic equivalence to an oracle policy gradient, and proposing a universal scaling law for group size selection.
AI.motivation: GRPO is a core method for scaling reasoning capabilities in large language models (used in DeepSeekMath and DeepSeek-R1), yet its theoretical properties remain poorly understood despite widespread adoption and numerous follow-up works.
AI.method: The authors demonstrate that the GRPO policy gradient is inherently a U-statistic, enabling characterization of mean squared error (MSE), derivation of finite-sample error bounds and asymptotic distribution of the suboptimality gap, and establishment of a universal scaling law for group size selection.
AI.result: GRPO is asymptotically equivalent to an oracle policy gradient algorithm and achieves asymptotically optimal performance within a broad class of policy gradient algorithms. The optimal group size is universal, and GRPO exhibits an oracle property. Empirical experiments validate these theoretical findings.
AI.conclusion: The theoretical framework provides principled guidance for selecting the optimal group size and confirms GRPO's optimality properties, bridging the gap between practical applications in large language models and classical statistical theory.

title: MMR-Life: Piecing Together Real-life Scenes for Multimodal Multi-image Reasoning
summary: Recent progress in the reasoning capabilities of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) has empowered them to address more complex tasks such as scientific analysis and mathematical reasoning. Despite their promise, MLLMs' reasoning abilities across different scenarios in real life remain largely unexplored and lack standardized benchmarks for evaluation. To address this gap, we introduce MMR-Life, a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate the diverse multimodal multi-image reasoning capabilities of MLLMs across real-life scenarios. MMR-Life consists of 2,646 multiple-choice questions based on 19,108 images primarily sourced from real-world contexts, comprehensively covering seven reasoning types: abductive, analogical, causal, deductive, inductive, spatial, and temporal. Unlike existing reasoning benchmarks, MMR-Life does not rely on domain-specific expertise but instead requires models to integrate information across multiple images and apply diverse reasoning abilities. The evaluation of 37 advanced models highlights the substantial challenge posed by MMR-Life. Even top models like GPT-5 achieve only 58% accuracy and display considerable variance in performance across reasoning types. Moreover, we analyze the reasoning paradigms of existing MLLMs, exploring how factors such as thinking length, reasoning method, and reasoning type affect their performance. In summary, MMR-Life establishes a comprehensive foundation for evaluating, analyzing, and improving the next generation of multimodal reasoning systems.

AI.tldr: MMR-Life is a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating multimodal multi-image reasoning capabilities of MLLMs across real-life scenarios, consisting of 2,646 multiple-choice questions based on 19,108 images covering 7 reasoning types (abductive, analogical, causal, deductive, inductive, spatial, temporal).
AI.motivation: Despite progress in MLLM reasoning capabilities, their abilities across real-life scenarios remain unexplored and lack standardized benchmarks for evaluation.
AI.method: Introduces MMR-Life benchmark with 2,646 multiple-choice questions derived from 19,108 real-world images, comprehensively covering seven reasoning types: abductive, analogical, causal, deductive, inductive, spatial, and temporal reasoning.
AI.result: Evaluation of 37 advanced models shows MMR-Life is challenging; even top model GPT-5 achieves only 58% accuracy with considerable variance across reasoning types.
AI.conclusion: MMR-Life establishes a comprehensive foundation for evaluating, analyzing, and improving the next generation of multimodal reasoning systems, highlighting that existing MLLMs still face significant challenges in real-world multimodal reasoning tasks.

title: Stateful Cross-layer Vision Modulation
summary: Recent multimodal large language models (MLLMs) widely adopt multi-layer visual feature fusion to enhance visual representation. However, existing approaches typically perform static concatenation or weighted aggregation after visual encoding, without intervening in the representation formation process itself. As a result, fine-grained details from early layers may be progressively suppressed during hierarchical abstraction. Moreover, directly introducing shallow-layer features into the language model often leads to semantic distribution mismatch with the visual feature space that the LLM's cross-attention layers were pretrained on, which typically requires additional adaptation or fine-tuning of the LLM. To address these limitations, we revisit visual representation learning from the perspective of representation evolution control and propose a cross-layer memory-modulated vision framework(SCVM). Specifically, we introduce a recursively updated cross-layer memory state inside the vision encoder to model long-range inter-layer dependencies. We further design a layer-wise feedback modulation mechanism that refreshes token representations at each layer based on the accumulated memory, thereby structurally regulating the representation evolution trajectory. In addition, we incorporate an auxiliary semantic alignment objective that explicitly supervises the final memory state, encouraging progressive compression and reinforcement of task-relevant information. Experimental results on multiple visual question answering and hallucination evaluation benchmarks demonstrate that SCVM achieves consistent performance improvements without expanding visual tokens, introducing additional vision encoders, or modifying or fine-tuning the language model.

AI.tldr: SCVM is a cross-layer memory-modulated vision framework that introduces a recursively updated cross-layer memory state inside the vision encoder to model long-range inter-layer dependencies and a layer-wise feedback modulation mechanism that refreshes token representations at each layer based on accumulated memory, with an auxiliary semantic alignment objective to supervise the final memory state, achieving consistent performance improvements on VQA and hallucination benchmarks without expanding visual tokens, adding extra vision encoders, or modifying/fine-tuning the LLM.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLMs use static concatenation or weighted aggregation after visual encoding without intervening in representation formation, causing fine-grained details from early layers to be progressively suppressed during hierarchical abstraction, and directly introducing shallow-layer features causes semantic distribution mismatches with the LLM's pretrained cross-attention layers, requiring additional adaptation or fine-tuning.
AI.method: SCVM introduces a recursively updated cross-layer memory state inside the vision encoder to model long-range inter-layer dependencies, designs a layer-wise feedback modulation mechanism that refreshes token representations at each layer based on accumulated memory to structurally regulate representation evolution, and incorporates an auxiliary semantic alignment objective that explicitly supervises the final memory state to encourage progressive compression and reinforcement of task-relevant information.
AI.result: SCVM achieves consistent performance improvements on multiple visual question answering and hallucination evaluation benchmarks without expanding visual tokens, introducing additional vision encoders, or modifying or fine-tuning the language model.
AI.conclusion: SCVM effectively addresses representation evolution control issues in MLLMs through cross-layer memory modulation, achieving consistent performance gains across benchmarks while maintaining efficiency and avoiding additional computational overhead or LLM modifications.

title: SphUnc: Hyperspherical Uncertainty Decomposition and Causal Identification via Information Geometry
summary: Reliable decision-making in complex multi-agent systems requires calibrated predictions and interpretable uncertainty. We introduce SphUnc, a unified framework combining hyperspherical representation learning with structural causal modeling. The model maps features to unit hypersphere latents using von Mises-Fisher distributions, decomposing uncertainty into epistemic and aleatoric components through information-geometric fusion. A structural causal model on spherical latents enables directed influence identification and interventional reasoning via sample-based simulation. Empirical evaluations on social and affective benchmarks demonstrate improved accuracy, better calibration, and interpretable causal signals, establishing a geometric-causal foundation for uncertainty-aware reasoning in multi-agent settings with higher-order interactions.

AI.tldr: SphUnc is a unified framework combining hyperspherical representation learning with structural causal modeling for uncertainty estimation in multi-agent systems, decomposing uncertainty into epistemic and aleatoric components through information-geometric fusion.
AI.motivation: Reliable decision-making in complex multi-agent systems requires calibrated predictions and interpretable uncertainty.
AI.method: Maps features to unit hypersphere latents using von Mises-Fisher distributions; decomposes uncertainty into epistemic and aleatoric components through information-geometric fusion; employs a structural causal model on spherical latents for directed influence identification and interventional reasoning via sample-based simulation.
AI.result: Improved accuracy, better calibration, and interpretable causal signals demonstrated on social and affective benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a geometric-causal foundation for uncertainty-aware reasoning in multi-agent settings with higher-order interactions.

title: EstLLM: Enhancing Estonian Capabilities in Multilingual LLMs via Continued Pretraining and Post-Training
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are predominantly trained on English-centric data, resulting in uneven performance for smaller languages. We study whether continued pretraining (CPT) can substantially improve Estonian capabilities in a pretrained multilingual LLM while preserving its English and general reasoning performance. Using Llama 3.1 8B as the main base model, we perform CPT on a mixture that increases Estonian exposure while approximating the original training distribution through English replay and the inclusion of code, mathematics, and instruction-like data. We subsequently apply supervised fine-tuning, preference optimization, and chat vector merging to introduce robust instruction-following behavior. Evaluation on a comprehensive suite of Estonian benchmarks shows consistent gains in linguistic competence, knowledge, reasoning, translation quality, and instruction-following compared to the original base model and its instruction-tuned variant, while maintaining competitive performance on English benchmarks. These findings indicate that CPT, with an appropriately balanced data mixture, together with post-training alignment, can substantially improve single-language capabilities in pretrained multilingual LLMs.

AI.tldr: Continued pretraining on Estonian-centric data improves a multilingual LLM's Estonian capabilities while preserving English performance.
AI.motivation: LLMs are trained mainly on English data, causing uneven performance for smaller languages like Estonian.
AI.method: Used Llama 3.1 8B as base; performed continued pretraining with increased Estonian exposure, English replay, code/math/instruction data; applied supervised fine-tuning, preference optimization, and chat vector merging.
AI.result: Consistent gains in Estonian linguistic competence, knowledge, reasoning, translation quality, and instruction-following; maintained competitive English benchmark performance.
AI.conclusion: CPT with appropriately balanced data mixture and post-training alignment can substantially improve single-language capabilities in pretrained multilingual LLMs.

title: Act Like a Pathologist: Tissue-Aware Whole Slide Image Reasoning
summary: Computational pathology has advanced rapidly in recent years, driven by domain-specific image encoders and growing interest in using vision-language models to answer natural-language questions about diseases. Yet, the core problem behind pathology question-answering remains unsolved, considering that a gigapixel slide contains far more information than necessary for a given question. Pathologists naturally navigate tissue and morphology complexity by scanning broadly, and zooming in selectively according to the clinical questions. Current models, in contrast, rely on uniform patch sampling or broad attention maps, often attending equally to irrelevant regions while overlooking key visual evidence. In this work, we try to bring models closer to how humans actually examine slides. We propose a question-guided, tissue-aware, and coarse-to-fine retrieval framework, HistoSelect, that consists of two key components: a group sampler that identifies question-relevant tissue regions, followed by a patch selector that retrieves the most informative patches within those regions. By selecting only the most informative patches, our method becomes significantly more efficient: reducing visual token usage by 70% on average, while improving accuracy across three pathology QA tasks. Evaluated on 356,000 question-answer pairs, our approach outperforms existing methods and produces answers grounded in interpretable, pathologist-consistent regions. Our results suggest that bringing human-like search and attention patterns into WSI reasoning is a promising direction for building practical and reliable pathology VLMs.

AI.tldr: HistoSelect is a question-guided, tissue-aware, coarse-to-fine retrieval framework for pathology question-answering that mimics human pathologists' scanning patterns by identifying question-relevant regions and selecting the most informative patches, reducing visual token usage by 70% while improving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current computational pathology models rely on uniform patch sampling or broad attention maps, attending equally to irrelevant regions while overlooking key visual evidence. Pathologists naturally navigate tissue complexity by scanning broadly and zooming in selectively according to clinical questions, but models fail to replicate this human-like attention pattern, leading to inefficient and sometimes inaccurate pathology QA.
AI.method: HistoSelect consists of two key components: (1) a group sampler that identifies question-relevant tissue regions, and (2) a patch selector that retrieves the most informative patches within those regions. This coarse-to-fine retrieval framework mimics human pathologists' scanning behavior—first identifying areas of interest, then zooming in to examine specific details.
AI.result: Evaluated on 356,000 question-answer pairs across three pathology QA tasks, HistoSelect outperforms existing methods. The approach reduces visual token usage by 70% on average while maintaining or improving accuracy. Answers are grounded in interpretable, pathologist-consistent regions, demonstrating the value of human-like search and attention patterns in WSI reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Bringing human-like search and attention patterns into whole slide image reasoning is a promising direction for building practical and reliable pathology vision-language models. The HistoSelect framework demonstrates that question-guided, tissue-aware retrieval can significantly improve both efficiency and accuracy in pathology QA by reducing irrelevant visual information processing while focusing on key diagnostic evidence.

title: PARWiS: Winner determination under shoestring budgets using active pairwise comparisons
summary: Determining a winner among a set of items using active pairwise comparisons under a limited budget is a challenging problem in preference-based learning. The goal of this study is to implement and evaluate the PARWiS algorithm, which shows spectral ranking and disruptive pair selection to identify the best item under shoestring budgets. This work have extended the PARWiS with a contextual variant (Contextual PARWiS) and a reinforcement learning-based variant (RL PARWiS), comparing them against baselines, including Double Thompson Sampling and a random selection strategy. This evaluation spans synthetic and real-world datasets (Jester and MovieLens), using budgets of 40, 60, and 80 comparisons for 20 items. The performance is measured through recovery fraction, true rank of reported winner, reported rank of true winner, and cumulative regret, alongside the separation metric \(Δ_{1,2}\). Results show that PARWiS and RL PARWiS outperform baselines across all datasets, particularly in the Jester dataset with a higher \(Δ_{1,2}\), while performance gaps narrow in the more challenging MovieLens dataset with a smaller \(Δ_{1,2}\). Contextual PARWiS shows comparable performance to PARWiS, indicating that contextual features may require further tuning to provide significant benefits.

AI.tldr: This paper implements and evaluates PARWiS, a spectral ranking-based algorithm for determining the best item through active pairwise comparisons under budget constraints. Two variants (Contextual PARWiS and RL PARWiS) are compared against Double Thompson Sampling and random selection baselines across synthetic and real-world datasets (Jester, MovieLens).
AI.motivation: Determining the best item among a set using active pairwise comparisons under limited budget is a challenging problem in preference-based learning.
AI.method: The authors implement PARWiS which employs spectral ranking and disruptive pair selection. They extend it with (1) Contextual PARWiS incorporating contextual features and (2) RL PARWiS using reinforcement learning. Evaluation uses budgets of 40, 60, and 80 comparisons for 20 items against Double Thompson Sampling and random selection baselines.
AI.result: PARWiS and RL PARWiS outperform all baselines across datasets. RL PARWiS excels on Jester with higher Δ₁,₂ separation. Performance gaps narrow on MovieLens. Contextual PARWiS shows comparable performance to PARWiS, suggesting contextual features need further tuning.
AI.conclusion: Spectral ranking and RL-based approaches are effective for preference-based winner determination under budget constraints. While Contextual PARWiS shows promise, contextual features require additional tuning to realize significant benefits.

title: What Exactly do Children Receive in Language Acquisition? A Case Study on CHILDES with Automated Detection of Filler-Gap Dependencies
summary: Children's acquisition of filler-gap dependencies has been argued by some to depend on innate grammatical knowledge, while others suggest that the distributional evidence available in child-directed speech suffices. Unfortunately, the relevant input is difficult to quantify at scale with fine granularity, making this question difficult to resolve. We present a system that identifies three core filler-gap constructions in spoken English corpora -- matrix wh-questions, embedded wh-questions, and relative clauses -- and further identifies the extraction site (i.e., subject vs. object vs. adjunct). Our approach combines constituency and dependency parsing, leveraging their complementary strengths for construction classification and extraction site identification. We validate the system on human-annotated data and find that it scores well across most categories. Applying the system to 57 English CHILDES corpora, we are able to characterize children's filler-gap input and their filler-gap production trajectories over the course of development, including construction-specific frequencies and extraction-site asymmetries. The resulting fine-grained labels enable future work in both acquisition and computational studies, which we demonstrate with a case study using filtered corpus training with language models.

AI.tldr: A system combining constituency and dependency parsing is presented to identify three core filler-gap constructions (matrix wh-questions, embedded wh-questions, relative clauses) and their extraction sites (subject/object/adjunct) in spoken English. Applied to 57 CHILDES corpora, the system characterizes children's filler-gap input and production trajectories, enabling future acquisition and computational research.
AI.motivation: There is a debate about whether children acquire filler-gap dependencies through innate grammatical knowledge or from distributional evidence in child-directed speech. The challenge lies in quantifying this input at scale with fine granularity.
AI.method: Constitution and dependency parsing are combined to identify filler-gap constructions and extraction sites. The system is validated on human-annotated data and applied to 57 English CHILDES corpora.
AI.result: The system achieves good performance across categories and characterizes children's filler-gap input and production trajectories, including construction-specific frequencies and extraction-site asymmetries. A case study demonstrates utility for language model training.
AI.conclusion: The fine-grained labels enable future work in both acquisition and computational studies, advancing research on how children acquire complex syntactic dependencies.

title: Direct low-field MRI super-resolution using undersampled k-space
comment: 4 pages, 4 figures, conference (The IEEE International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI))
summary: Low-field magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) provides affordable access to diagnostic imaging but suffers from prolonged acquisition and limited image quality. Accelerated imaging can be achieved with k-space undersampling, while super-resolution (SR) and image quality transfer (IQT) methods typically rely on spatial-domain post-processing. In this work, we propose a novel framework for reconstructing high-field MR like images directly from undersampled low-field k-space. Our approach employs a k-space dual channel U-Net that processes the real and imaginary components of undersampled k-space to restore missing frequency content. Experiments on low-field brain MRI demonstrate that our k-space-driven image enhancement consistently outperforms the counterpart spatial-domain method. Furthermore, reconstructions from undersampled k-space achieve image quality comparable to full k-space acquisitions. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work that investigates low-field MRI SR/IQT directly from undersampled k-space.

AI.tldr: A novel framework using a k-space dual channel U-Net to reconstruct high-field MR-like images directly from undersampled low-field k-space, outperforming spatial-domain methods and achieving quality comparable to full k-space acquisitions.
AI.motivation: Low-field MRI provides affordable access but suffers from prolonged acquisition times and limited image quality; existing accelerated imaging and SR/IQT methods rely on spatial-domain post-processing rather than direct k-space reconstruction.
AI.method: A k-space dual channel U-Net architecture that processes the real and imaginary components of undersampled low-field k-space to restore missing frequency content, enabling direct reconstruction of high-field-like images from low-field data.
AI.result: The k-space-driven image enhancement consistently outperforms spatial-domain counterparts, and reconstructions from undersampled k-space achieve image quality comparable to full k-space acquisitions on low-field brain MRI.
AI.conclusion: This is the first work investigating low-field MRI super-resolution/image quality transfer directly from undersampled k-space, demonstrating the effectiveness of k-space-based approaches for enhancing low-field MRI quality.

title: Scaling of learning time for high dimensional inputs
summary: Representation learning from complex data typically involves models with a large number of parameters, which in turn require large amounts of data samples. In neural network models, model complexity grows with the number of inputs to each neuron, with a trade-off between model expressivity and learning time. A precise characterization of this trade-off would help explain the connectivity and learning times observed in artificial and biological networks. We present a theoretical analysis of how learning time depends on input dimensionality for a Hebbian learning model performing independent component analysis. Based on the geometry of high-dimensional spaces, we show that the learning dynamics reduce to a unidimensional problem, with learning times dependent only on initial conditions. For higher input dimensions, initial parameters have smaller learning gradients and larger learning times. We find that learning times have supralinear scaling, becoming quickly prohibitive for high input dimensions. These results reveal a fundamental limitation for learning in high dimensions and help elucidate how the optimal design of neural networks depends on data complexity. Our approach outlines a new framework for analyzing learning dynamics and model complexity in neural network models.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a theoretical analysis showing that learning time in high-dimensional Hebbian learning models scales supralinearly with input dimensionality, revealing fundamental limitations for learning in high dimensions.
AI.motivation: Representation learning with complex data requires models with many parameters and large amounts of data. In neural networks, model complexity grows with the number of inputs per neuron, creating a trade-off between expressivity and learning time. A precise characterization of this trade-off would help explain connectivity and learning times in artificial and biological networks.
AI.method: The authors analyze learning time dependence on input dimensionality for a Hebbian learning model performing independent component analysis (ICA). Using geometric analysis of high-dimensional spaces, they reduce the learning dynamics to a unidimensional problem, showing how learning times depend on initial conditions and input dimensionality.
AI.result: Learning times depend only on initial conditions. For higher input dimensions, initial parameters have smaller learning gradients and larger learning times. Learning times exhibit supralinear scaling, becoming quickly prohibitive for high input dimensions.
AI.conclusion: This reveals a fundamental limitation for learning in high dimensions and helps elucidate how optimal neural network design depends on data complexity. The approach outlines a new framework for analyzing learning dynamics and model complexity in neural network models.

title: Modeling Grammatical Hypothesis Testing in Young Learners: A Sequence-Based Learning Analytics Study of Morphosyntactic Reasoning in an Interactive Game
summary: This study investigates grammatical reasoning in primary school learners through a sequence-based learning analytics approach, leveraging fine-grained action sequences from an interactive game targeting morphosyntactic agreement in French. Unlike traditional assessments that rely on final answers, we treat each slider movement as a hypothesis-testing action, capturing real-time cognitive strategies during sentence construction. Analyzing 597 gameplay sessions (9,783 actions) from 100 students aged 8-11 in authentic classroom settings, we introduce Hamming distance to quantify proximity to valid grammatical solutions and examine convergence patterns across exercises with varying levels of difficulty. Results reveal that determiners and verbs are key sites of difficulty, with action sequences deviating from left-to-right usual treatment. This suggests learners often fix the verb first and adjust preceding elements. Exercises with fewer solutions exhibit slower and more erratic convergence, while changes in the closest valid solution indicate dynamic hypothesis revision. Our findings demonstrate how sequence-based analytics can uncover hidden dimensions of linguistic reasoning, offering a foundation for real-time scaffolding and teacher-facing tools in linguistically diverse classrooms.

AI.tldr: This study investigates grammatical reasoning in primary school learners using sequence-based learning analytics from a French morphosyntactic agreement game, finding that determiners and verbs are key difficulty sites and learners often fix verbs first.
AI.motivation: Traditional assessments rely on final answers and don't capture real-time cognitive strategies; the study aims to uncover hidden dimensions of linguistic reasoning through fine-grained action sequences.
AI.method: Analyzing 597 gameplay sessions (9,783 actions) from 100 students aged 8-11, the study treats each slider movement as a hypothesis-testing action, uses Hamming distance to quantify proximity to valid grammatical solutions, and examines convergence patterns across exercises with varying difficulty levels.
AI.result: Determiners and verbs are key sites of difficulty; action sequences deviate from left-to-right treatment, suggesting learners often fix the verb first and adjust preceding elements. Exercises with fewer solutions exhibit slower and more erratic convergence, while changes in the closest valid solution indicate dynamic hypothesis revision.
AI.conclusion: Sequence-based analytics can uncover hidden dimensions of linguistic reasoning, offering a foundation for real-time scaffolding and teacher-facing tools in linguistically diverse classrooms.

title: Specializing Foundation Models via Mixture of Low-Rank Experts for Comprehensive Head CT Analysis
summary: Foundation models pre-trained on large-scale datasets demonstrate strong transfer learning capabilities; however, their adaptation to complex multi-label diagnostic tasks-such as comprehensive head CT finding detection-remains understudied. Standard parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods such as LoRA apply uniform adaptations across pathology types, which may limit performance for diverse medical findings. We propose a Mixture of Low-Rank Experts (MoLRE) framework that extends LoRA with multiple specialized low-rank adapters and unsupervised soft routing. This approach enables conditional feature adaptation with less than 0.5% additional parameters and without explicit pathology supervision. We present a comprehensive benchmark of MoLRE across six state-of-the-art medical imaging foundation models spanning 2D and 3D architectures, general-domain, medical-domain, and head CT-specific pretraining, and model sizes ranging from 7M to 431M parameters. Using over 70,000 non-contrast head CT scans with 75 annotated findings-including hemorrhage, infarction, trauma, mass lesions, structural abnormalities, and chronic changes-our experiments demonstrate consistent performance improvements across all models. Gains vary substantially: general-purpose and medical-domain models show the largest improvements (DINOv3-Base: +4.6%; MedGemma: +4.3%), whereas 3D CT-specialized or very large models show more modest gains (+0.2-1.3%). The combination of MoLRE and MedGemma achieves the highest average detection AUC of 0.917. These findings highlight the importance of systematic benchmarking on target clinical tasks, as pretraining domain, architecture, and model scale interact in non-obvious ways.

AI.tldr: MoLRE extends LoRA with multiple specialized low-rank adapters and unsupervised soft routing for conditional feature adaptation in multi-label head CT diagnosis, achieving consistent performance improvements across foundation models without explicit pathology supervision.
AI.motivation: Foundation models pre-trained on large datasets show strong transfer learning, but their adaptation to complex multi-label diagnostic tasks like comprehensive head CT finding detection remains understudied. Standard parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods (e.g., LoRA) apply uniform adaptations across pathology types, which may limit performance for diverse medical findings.
AI.method: MoLRE (Mixture of Low-Rank Experts) framework extends LoRA by incorporating multiple specialized low-rank adapters with unsupervised soft routing. This approach enables conditional feature adaptation with less than 0.5% additional parameters and without explicit pathology supervision.
AI.result: Across six state-of-the-art medical imaging foundation models (2D/3D, general/medical/head CT pretraining, 7M-431M parameters) on 70,000+ non-contrast head CT scans with 75 findings, MoLRE delivers consistent improvements. General-purpose and medical-domain models show largest gains (DINOv3-Base: +4.6%; MedGemma: +4.3%), while 3D CT-specialized or very large models show more modest gains (+0.2-1.3%). MoLRE+MedGemma achieves the highest average detection AUC of 0.917.
AI.conclusion: Systematic benchmarking on target clinical tasks is crucial because pretraining domain, architecture, and model scale interact in non-obvious ways. MoLRE demonstrates that specialized adapter routing can significantly enhance multi-label diagnostic performance without substantial parameter overhead.

title: Operator Learning Using Weak Supervision from Walk-on-Spheres
summary: Training neural PDE solvers is often bottlenecked by expensive data generation or unstable physics-informed neural network (PINN) that involves challenging optimization landscapes due to higher-order derivatives. To tackle this issue, we propose an alternative approach using Monte Carlo approaches to estimate the solution to the PDE as a stochastic process for weak supervision during training. Leveraging the walk-on-spheres method, we introduce a learning scheme called \emph{Walk-on-Spheres Neural Operator (WoS-NO)} which uses weak supervision from WoS to train any given neural operator. We propose to amortize the cost of Monte Carlo walks across the distribution of PDE instances using stochastic representations from the WoS algorithm to generate cheap, noisy, estimates of the PDE solution during training. This is formulated into a data-free physics-informed objective where a neural operator is trained to regress against these weak supervisions, allowing the operator to learn a generalized solution map for an entire family of PDEs. This strategy results in a mesh-free framework that operates without expensive pre-computed datasets, avoids the need for computing higher-order derivatives for loss functions that are memory-intensive and unstable, and demonstrates zero-shot generalization to novel PDE parameters and domains. Experiments show that for the same number of training steps, our method exhibits up to 8.75$\times$ improvement in $L_2$-error compared to standard physics-informed training schemes, up to 6.31$\times$ improvement in training speed, and reductions of up to 2.97$\times$ in GPU memory consumption. We present the code at https://github.com/neuraloperator/WoS-NO

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Walk-on-Spheres Neural Operator (WoS-NO), a mesh-free framework that uses Monte Carlo approaches with walk-on-spheres method to train neural PDE solvers via weak supervision, eliminating the need for expensive data generation and unstable higher-order derivative calculations.
AI.motivation: Training neural PDE solvers faces two major bottlenecks: (1) expensive data generation and (2) unstable physics-informed neural network (PINN) optimization due to challenging landscapes involving higher-order derivatives that are memory-intensive and computationally unstable.
AI.method: WoS-NO leverages the walk-on-spheres method to estimate PDE solutions as stochastic processes, providing weak supervision for training. It amortizes Monte Carlo walk costs across PDE instance distributions using stochastic representations to generate cheap, noisy estimates of PDE solutions. This is formulated as a data-free physics-informed objective where a neural operator is trained to regress against these weak supervisions, enabling learning of generalized solution maps for entire families of PDEs.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate significant improvements: up to 8.75× better L2-error compared to standard PINN training, up to 6.31× faster training speed, and up to 2.97× reduction in GPU memory consumption. The method also achieves zero-shot generalization to novel PDE parameters and domains.
AI.conclusion: WoS-NO provides a practical solution to key challenges in neural PDE solvers by eliminating expensive data generation, avoiding unstable higher-order derivative computations, and achieving superior performance in accuracy, speed, and memory efficiency. The mesh-free framework enables generalized solution learning across PDE families and demonstrates strong zero-shot generalization capabilities.

title: ClinConsensus: A Consensus-Based Benchmark for Evaluating Chinese Medical LLMs across Difficulty Levels
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to health management, showing promise across disease prevention, clinical decision-making, and long-term care. However, existing medical benchmarks remain largely static and task-isolated, failing to capture the openness, longitudinal structure, and safety-critical complexity of real-world clinical workflows. We introduce ClinConsensus, a Chinese medical benchmark curated, validated, and quality-controlled by clinical experts. ClinConsensus comprises 2500 open-ended cases spanning the full continuum of care--from prevention and intervention to long-term follow-up--covering 36 medical specialties, 12 common clinical task types, and progressively increasing levels of complexity. To enable reliable evaluation of such complex scenarios, we adopt a rubric-based grading protocol and propose the Clinically Applicable Consistency Score (CACS@k). We further introduce a dual-judge evaluation framework, combining a high-capability LLM-as-judge with a distilled, locally deployable judge model trained via supervised fine-tuning, enabling scalable and reproducible evaluation aligned with physician judgment. Using ClinConsensus, we conduct a comprehensive assessment of several leading LLMs and reveal substantial heterogeneity across task themes, care stages, and medical specialties. While top-performing models achieve comparable overall scores, they differ markedly in reasoning, evidence use, and longitudinal follow-up capabilities, and clinically actionable treatment planning remains a key bottleneck. We release ClinConsensus as an extensible benchmark to support the development and evaluation of medical LLMs that are robust, clinically grounded, and ready for real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: ClinConsensus is a Chinese medical benchmark comprising 2500 open-ended clinical cases across 36 specialties and progressive complexity levels, evaluated via a dual-judge framework using the Clinically Applicable Consistency Score (CACS@k) to assess LLM performance in real-world clinical workflows.
AI.motivation: Existing medical benchmarks are static and task-isolated, failing to capture the openness, longitudinal structure, and safety-critical complexity inherent to real-world clinical workflows.
AI.method: ClinConsensus is curated and validated by clinical experts, featuring 2500 open-ended cases spanning the full care continuum (prevention to long-term follow-up), 36 medical specialties, 12 clinical task types, and progressive complexity levels. Evaluation uses a rubric-based grading protocol with the Clinically Applicable Consistency Score (CACS@k) and a dual-judge framework combining a high-capability LLM-as-judge with a distilled locally deployable judge model trained via supervised fine-tuning.
AI.result: Comprehensive assessment of leading LLMs reveals substantial heterogeneity across task themes, care stages, and medical specialties. While top-performing models achieve comparable overall scores, they differ markedly in reasoning, evidence use, and longitudinal follow-up capabilities. Clinically actionable treatment planning remains a key bottleneck.
AI.conclusion: ClinConsensus is released as an extensible benchmark to support the development and evaluation of robust, clinically grounded, and real-world deployable medical LLMs.

title: CoLC: Communication-Efficient Collaborative Perception with LiDAR Completion
summary: Collaborative perception empowers autonomous agents to share complementary information and overcome perception limitations. While early fusion offers more perceptual complementarity and is inherently robust to model heterogeneity, its high communication cost has limited its practical deployment, prompting most existing works to favor intermediate or late fusion. To address this, we propose a communication-efficient early Collaborative perception framework that incorporates LiDAR Completion to restore scene completeness under sparse transmission, dubbed as CoLC. Specifically, the CoLC integrates three complementary designs. First, each neighbor agent applies Foreground-Aware Point Sampling (FAPS) to selectively transmit informative points that retain essential structural and contextual cues under bandwidth constraints. The ego agent then employs Completion-Enhanced Early Fusion (CEEF) to reconstruct dense pillars from the received sparse inputs and adaptively fuse them with its own observations, thereby restoring spatial completeness. Finally, the Dense-Guided Dual Alignment (DGDA) strategy enforces semantic and geometric consistency between the enhanced and dense pillars during training, ensuring consistent and robust feature learning. Experiments on both simulated and real-world datasets demonstrate that CoLC achieves superior perception-communication trade-offs and remains robust under heterogeneous model settings. The code is available at https://github.com/CatOneTwo/CoLC.

AI.tldr: A communication-efficient early collaborative perception framework (CoLC) that uses LiDAR Completion to restore scene completeness under sparse transmission through Foreground-Aware Point Sampling, Completion-Enhanced Early Fusion, and Dense-Guided Dual Alignment strategy.
AI.motivation: Early fusion offers perceptual complementarity and robustness to model heterogeneity but has high communication costs, prompting most existing works to favor intermediate or late fusion approaches.
AI.method: Three complementary designs: (1) Foreground-Aware Point Sampling (FAPS) for selective informative point transmission; (2) Completion-Enhanced Early Fusion (CEEF) to reconstruct dense pillars from sparse inputs and adaptively fuse with ego observations; (3) Dense-Guided Dual Alignment (DGDA) for semantic and geometric consistency enforcement during training.
AI.result: Achieves superior perception-communication trade-offs and maintains robustness under heterogeneous model settings, demonstrated on both simulated and real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CoLC framework effectively addresses the communication cost challenge while preserving the benefits of early fusion, making it practical for real-world deployment.

title: Subliminal Signals in Preference Labels
summary: As AI systems approach superhuman capabilities, scalable oversight increasingly relies on LLM-as-a-judge frameworks where models evaluate and guide each other's training. A core assumption is that binary preference labels provide only semantic supervision about response quality. We challenge this assumption by demonstrating that preference labels can function as a covert communication channel. We show that even when a neutral student model generates semantically unbiased completions, a biased judge can transmit unintended behavioral traits through preference assignments, which even strengthen across iterative alignment rounds. Our findings suggest that robust oversight in superalignment settings requires mechanisms that can detect and mitigate subliminal preference transmission, particularly when judges may pursue unintended objectives.

AI.tldr: Preference labels in LLM-as-a-judge frameworks can function as covert communication channels, enabling biased judges to transmit unintended behavioral traits to neutral student models even when the latter generate semantically unbiased responses.
AI.motivation: As AI systems approach superhuman capabilities, scalable oversight increasingly relies on LLM-as-a-judge frameworks where models evaluate and guide each other's training. The core assumption is that binary preference labels provide only semantic supervision about response quality.
AI.method: The authors demonstrate that preference labels can function as a covert communication channel by showing that even when a neutral student model generates semantically unbiased completions, a biased judge can transmit unintended behavioral traits through preference assignments.
AI.result: Preference assignments can transmit behavioral traits from judges to student models, with these transmissions strengthening across iterative alignment rounds. This demonstrates that preference labels carry more than semantic information—they encode behavioral signals that can propagate through multi-round training.
AI.conclusion: Robust oversight in superalignment settings requires mechanisms that can detect and mitigate subliminal preference transmission, particularly when judges may pursue unintended objectives beyond the primary alignment goal.

title: Recursive Think-Answer Process for LLMs and VLMs
summary: Think-Answer reasoners such as DeepSeek-R1 have made notable progress by leveraging interpretable internal reasoning. However, despite the frequent presence of self-reflective cues like "Oops!", they remain vulnerable to output errors during single-pass inference. To address this limitation, we propose an efficient Recursive Think-Answer Process (R-TAP) that enables models to engage in iterative reasoning cycles and generate more accurate answers, going beyond conventional single-pass approaches. Central to this approach is a confidence generator that evaluates the certainty of model responses and guides subsequent improvements. By incorporating two complementary rewards-Recursively Confidence Increase Reward and Final Answer Confidence Reward-we show that R-TAP-enhanced models consistently outperform conventional single-pass methods for both large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs). Moreover, by analyzing the frequency of "Oops"-like expressions in model responses, we find that R-TAP-applied models exhibit significantly fewer self-reflective patterns, resulting in more stable and faster inference-time reasoning. We hope R-TAP pave the way evolving into efficient and elaborated methods to refine the reasoning processes of future AI.

AI.tldr: A recursive Think-Answer Process (R-TAP) framework that uses confidence evaluation and iterative reasoning to improve model accuracy and reduce self-reflective errors.
AI.motivation: Existing Think-Answer reasoners like DeepSeek-R1 use interpretable reasoning but remain vulnerable to output errors during single-pass inference, despite having self-reflective cues like "Oops!"
AI.method: R-TAP introduces a confidence generator to evaluate response certainty and guide iterative reasoning cycles. Two complementary rewards are used: Recursively Confidence Increase Reward and Final Answer Confidence Reward. The approach is applied to both LLMs and VLMs.
AI.result: R-TAP-enhanced models consistently outperform conventional single-pass methods for both LLMs and VLMs. Models using R-TAP exhibit significantly fewer self-reflective patterns like "Oops!", leading to more stable and faster inference-time reasoning.
AI.conclusion: R-TAP demonstrates that iterative reasoning with confidence evaluation can significantly improve reasoning accuracy while reducing error-prone self-reflective behaviors, paving the way for more efficient and elaborated methods to refine AI reasoning processes.

title: SCOUT: Fast Spectral CT Imaging in Ultra LOw-data Regimes via PseUdo-label GeneraTion
summary: Noise and artifacts during computed tomography (CT) scans are a fundamental challenge affecting disease diagnosis. However, current methods either involve excessively long reconstruction times or rely on data-driven models for optimization, failing to adequately consider the valuable information inherent in the data itself, especially medical 3D data. This work proposes a reconstruction method under ultra-low raw data conditions, requiring no external data and avoiding lengthy pre-training processes. By leveraging spatial nonlocal similarity and the conjugate properties of the projection domain to generate pseudo-3D data for self-supervised training, high-fidelity results can be achieved in a very short time. Extensive experiments demonstrate that this method not only mitigates detector-induced ring artifacts but also exhibits unprecedented capabilities in detail recovery. This method provides a new paradigm for research using unlabeled raw projection data. Code is available at https://github.com/yqx7150/SCOUT.

AI.tldr: A CT reconstruction method for ultra-low raw data conditions that leverages spatial nonlocal similarity and conjugate properties to generate pseudo-3D data for self-supervised training, achieving high-fidelity results without external data or lengthy pre-training.
AI.motivation: CT scans suffer from noise and artifacts that affect disease diagnosis. Existing methods either require excessively long reconstruction times or rely on data-driven models that fail to adequately utilize the inherent information in medical 3D data.
AI.method: The proposed method operates under ultra-low raw data conditions without external data or lengthy pre-training. It leverages spatial nonlocal similarity and the conjugate properties of the projection domain to generate pseudo-3D data for self-supervised training.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that the method effectively mitigates detector-induced ring artifacts and achieves unprecedented capabilities in detail recovery with high-fidelity results in very short reconstruction times.
AI.conclusion: This approach provides a new paradigm for research utilizing unlabeled raw projection data, with code available at https://github.com/yqx7150/SCOUT.

title: Learn Hard Problems During RL with Reference Guided Fine-tuning
summary:
Reinforcement learning (RL) for mathematical reasoning can suffer from reward sparsity: for challenging problems, LLM fails to sample any correct trajectories, preventing RL from receiving meaningful positive feedback. At the same time, there often exist human-written reference solutions along with the problem (e.g., problems from AoPS), but directly fine-tuning on these solutions offers no benefit because models often cannot imitate human proofs that lie outside their own reasoning distribution.
  We introduce Reference-Guided Fine-Tuning (ReGFT), a simple and effective method that utilizes human-written reference solutions to synthesize positive trajectories on hard problems and train on them before RL. For each problem, we provide the model with a partial reference solution and let it generate its own reasoning trace, ensuring the resulting trajectories remain in the model's reasoning space while still benefiting from reference guidance.
  Fine-tuning on these reference-guided trajectories increases the number of solvable problems and produces a checkpoint that receives more positive rewards during RL. Across three benchmarks (AIME24, AIME25, BeyondAIME), ReGFT consistently improves supervised accuracy, accelerates DAPO training, and raises the final performance plateau of RL. Our results show that ReGFT effectively overcomes reward sparsity and unlocks stronger RL-based mathematical reasoning.

AI.tldr: ReGFT introduces a novel fine-tuning approach that leverages human reference solutions to synthesize positive trajectories for RL-based mathematical reasoning, effectively overcoming reward sparsity and improving performance across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: RL for mathematical reasoning suffers from reward sparsity—challenging problems often yield no correct trajectories, preventing meaningful positive feedback. Direct fine-tuning on human reference solutions fails because models cannot imitate proofs outside their reasoning distribution.
AI.method: Reference-Guided Fine-Tuning (ReGFT) provides models with partial reference solutions while allowing them to generate their own reasoning traces. This ensures trajectories remain within the model's reasoning space while still benefiting from reference guidance, creating high-quality positive trajectories for RL training.
AI.result: Across AIME24, AIME25, and BeyondAIME benchmarks, ReGFT consistently improves supervised accuracy, accelerates DAPO training, and raises the final RL performance plateau. It increases solvable problems and produces checkpoints that receive more positive rewards during RL.
AI.conclusion: ReGFT effectively overcomes reward sparsity and unlocks stronger RL-based mathematical reasoning by bridging the gap between reference solutions and model capability through partial guidance during fine-tuning.

title: LLMs as Strategic Actors: Behavioral Alignment, Risk Calibration, and Argumentation Framing in Geopolitical Simulations
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly proposed as agents in strategic decision environments, yet their behavior in structured geopolitical simulations remains under-researched. We evaluate six popular state-of-the-art LLMs alongside results from human results across four real-world crisis simulation scenarios, requiring models to select predefined actions and justify their decisions across multiple rounds. We compare models to humans in action alignment, risk calibration through chosen actions' severity, and argumentative framing grounded in international relations theory. Results show that models approximate human decision patterns in base simulation rounds but diverge over time, displaying distinct behavioural profiles and strategy updates. LLM explanations for chosen actions across all models exhibit a strong normative-cooperative framing centered on stability, coordination, and risk mitigation, with limited adversarial reasoning.

AI.tldr: This study evaluates six state-of-the-art LLMs in geopolitical crisis simulations against human decision-makers, finding that while models initially align with human patterns, they diverge over time and exhibit predominantly cooperative normative framing rather than adversarial reasoning.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly proposed as strategic decision agents, yet their behavior in structured geopolitical simulations remains under-researched compared to human decision-making in crisis scenarios.
AI.method: Six SOTA LLMs were evaluated alongside human participants across four real-world crisis simulation scenarios. Models performed action selection and justification across multiple rounds, enabling comparison on action alignment, risk calibration, and argumentative framing grounded in international relations theory.
AI.result: Models approximate human decision patterns in early rounds but diverge over time, displaying distinct behavioral profiles and strategy updates. All LLM explanations exhibit strong normative-cooperative framing centered on stability, coordination, and risk mitigation, with limited adversarial reasoning.
AI.conclusion: LLMs demonstrate potential as strategic decision agents but exhibit distinct behavioral trajectories from humans, particularly in their tendency toward cooperative framing rather than adversarial reasoning in geopolitical contexts.

title: STMI: Segmentation-Guided Token Modulation with Cross-Modal Hypergraph Interaction for Multi-Modal Object Re-Identification
summary: Multi-modal object Re-Identification (ReID) aims to exploit complementary information from different modalities to retrieve specific objects. However, existing methods often rely on hard token filtering or simple fusion strategies, which can lead to the loss of discriminative cues and increased background interference. To address these challenges, we propose STMI, a novel multi-modal learning framework consisting of three key components: (1) Segmentation-Guided Feature Modulation (SFM) module leverages SAM-generated masks to enhance foreground representations and suppress background noise through learnable attention modulation; (2) Semantic Token Reallocation (STR) module employs learnable query tokens and an adaptive reallocation mechanism to extract compact and informative representations without discarding any tokens; (3) Cross-Modal Hypergraph Interaction (CHI) module constructs a unified hypergraph across modalities to capture high-order semantic relationships. Extensive experiments on public benchmarks (i.e., RGBNT201, RGBNT100, and MSVR310) demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of our proposed STMI framework in multi-modal ReID scenarios.

AI.tldr: STMI is a multi-modal ReID framework that addresses background interference and loss of discriminative cues through segmentation-guided feature modulation, semantic token reallocation, and cross-modal hypergraph interaction, achieving state-of-the-art performance on RGBNT201, RGBNT100, and MSVR310 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-modal ReID methods suffer from hard token filtering and simple fusion strategies that cause loss of discriminative information and increased background interference, limiting their effectiveness in retrieving specific objects across modalities.
AI.method: STMI comprises three components: (1) Segmentation-Guided Feature Modulation (SFM) uses SAM-generated masks with learnable attention modulation to enhance foreground and suppress background noise; (2) Semantic Token Reallocation (STR) employs learnable query tokens with adaptive reallocation to extract compact, informative representations without discarding tokens; (3) Cross-Modal Hypergraph Interaction (CHI) constructs a unified hypergraph across modalities to capture high-order semantic relationships.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on public benchmarks (RGBNT201, RGBNT100, MSVR310) demonstrate STMI's effectiveness and robustness in multi-modal ReID scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed STMI framework successfully addresses key limitations of existing methods by preserving discriminative cues through advanced feature modulation and reallocation strategies, while effectively suppressing background interference through semantic hypergraph interactions, establishing a new state-of-the-art approach for multi-modal object re-identification.

title: MOSAIC: A Unified Platform for Cross-Paradigm Comparison and Evaluation of Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Multi-Agent RL, LLM, VLM, and Human Decision-Makers
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL), large language models (LLMs), and vision-language models (VLMs) have been widely studied in isolation. However, existing infrastructure lacks the ability to deploy agents from different decision-making paradigms within the same environment, making it difficult to study them in hybrid multi-agent settings or to compare their behaviour fairly under identical conditions. We present MOSAIC, an open-source platform that bridges this gap by incorporating a diverse set of existing reinforcement learning environments and enabling heterogeneous agents (RL policies, LLMs, VLMs, and human players) to operate within them in ad-hoc team settings with reproducible results. MOSAIC introduces three contributions. (i) An IPC-based worker protocol that wraps both native and third-party frameworks as isolated subprocess workers, each executing its native training and inference logic unmodified, communicating through a versioned inter-process protocol. (ii) An operator abstraction that forms an agent-level interface by mapping workers to agents: each operator, regardless of whether it is backed by an RL policy, an LLM, or a human, conforms to a minimal unified interface. (iii) A deterministic cross-paradigm evaluation framework offering two complementary modes: a manual mode that advances up to N concurrent operators in lock-step under shared seeds for fine-grained visual inspection of behavioural differences, and a script mode that drives automated, long-running evaluation through declarative Python scripts, for reproducible experiments. We release MOSAIC as an open, visual-first platform to facilitate reproducible cross-paradigm research across the RL, LLM, and human-in-the-loop communities.

AI.tldr: MOSAIC is an open-source platform enabling heterogeneous agents (RL policies, LLMs, VLMs, humans) to operate together in RL environments for reproducible cross-paradigm research.
AI.motivation: Existing infrastructure lacks the ability to deploy agents from different decision-making paradigms within the same environment, making it difficult to study them in hybrid multi-agent settings or compare their behavior fairly under identical conditions.
AI.method: MOSAIC introduces: (i) an IPC-based worker protocol that wraps native and third-party frameworks as isolated subprocess workers with versioned inter-process communication; (ii) an operator abstraction that forms a minimal unified interface for all agents regardless of their underlying implementation; (iii) a deterministic cross-paradigm evaluation framework with manual mode (lock-step N concurrent operators under shared seeds) and script mode for automated long-running experiments.
AI.result: MOSAIC successfully bridges the gap between RL, LLM, VLM, and human-in-the-loop communities by providing a unified platform for heterogeneous multi-agent systems with reproducible results.
AI.conclusion: MOSAIC facilitates reproducible cross-paradigm research by enabling fair comparison of different AI paradigms (RL, LLMs, VLMs, humans) in identical environments, making it a valuable tool for advancing research at the intersection of these fields.

title: LongRLVR: Long-Context Reinforcement Learning Requires Verifiable Context Rewards
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has significantly advanced the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) by optimizing them against factual outcomes. However, this paradigm falters in long-context scenarios, as its reliance on internal parametric knowledge is ill-suited for tasks requiring contextual grounding--the ability to find and reason over externally provided information. We identify a key reason for this failure: a reward based solely on the final answer is too sparse to effectively guide the model for identifying relevant evidence. We formally prove that the outcome-only reward leads to significant vanishing gradients for the context grounding process, rendering learning intractable. To overcome this bottleneck, we introduce LongRLVR to augment the sparse answer reward with a dense and verifiable context reward.
