On CT-RATE, our model achieves state-of-the-art text-to-image retrieval (R@10 31.5 vs. 22.2) and competitive disease classification (AUC 83.8 vs. 83.8), with consistent results on Rad-ChestCT (AUC 77.0 vs. 77.3). We further observe that radiologists routinely reference specific images within their reports (e.g., ``series X, image Y''), linking textual descriptions to precise axial locations. We automatically mine 262k such snippet-slice pairs and introduce the task of intra-scan snippet localization -- predicting the axial depth referred to by a text snippet -- reducing mean absolute error to 36.3 mm at 12 mm feature resolution, compared with 67.0 mm for the best baseline. Adding this localization objective leaves retrieval and classification broadly unchanged within confidence bounds, yielding a single unified model for retrieval, classification, and intra-scan grounding.

AI.tldr: A 3D CT vision-language model trained on 98k report-volume pairs using SigLIP-style contrastive pretraining and prompt-based disease supervision, achieving state-of-the-art retrieval performance and introducing intra-scan snippet localization for precise image-text grounding.
AI.motivation: Recent 3D CT vision-language models are limited by reliance on small public datasets and coarse global supervision. Additionally, radiologists routinely reference specific axial image locations in their reports, but existing models cannot capture these precise snippet-slice correspondences.
AI.method: The authors trained a model on 98k report-volume pairs from 50k patients at a single hospital combined with public datasets using SigLIP-style contrastive pretraining and prompt-based disease supervision. They also introduced intra-scan snippet localization by mining 262k snippet-slice pairs to predict axial depth referred to by text snippets.
AI.result: The model achieved state-of-the-art text-to-image retrieval (R@10 31.5 vs. 22.2 baseline) and competitive disease classification (AUC 83.8) on CT-RATE. On Rad-ChestCT, it achieved AUC 77.0. Intra-scan snippet localization reduced mean absolute error to 36.3 mm at 12 mm feature resolution (vs. 67.0 mm baseline). Adding the localization objective left retrieval and classification performance largely unchanged.
AI.conclusion: A single unified model can effectively perform retrieval, classification, and intra-scan grounding tasks, demonstrating that large-scale training data and precise supervision signals (like snippet-slice pairs) are valuable for 3D CT vision-language models.

title: Mobile-VTON: High-Fidelity On-Device Virtual Try-On
comment: The project page is available at: https://zhenchenwan.github.io/Mobile-VTON/
summary: Virtual try-on (VTON) has recently achieved impressive visual fidelity, but most existing systems require uploading personal photos to cloud-based GPUs, raising privacy concerns and limiting on-device deployment. To address this, we present Mobile-VTON, a high-quality, privacy-preserving framework that enables fully offline virtual try-on on commodity mobile devices using only a single user image and a garment image. Mobile-VTON introduces a modular TeacherNet-GarmentNet-TryonNet (TGT) architecture that integrates knowledge distillation, garment-conditioned generation, and garment alignment into a unified pipeline optimized for on-device efficiency. Within this framework, we propose a Feature-Guided Adversarial (FGA) Distillation strategy that combines teacher supervision with adversarial learning to better match real-world image distributions. GarmentNet is trained with a trajectory-consistency loss to preserve garment semantics across diffusion steps, while TryonNet uses latent concatenation and lightweight cross-modal conditioning to enable robust garment-to-person alignment without large-scale pretraining. By combining these components, Mobile-VTON achieves high-fidelity generation with low computational overhead. Experiments on VITON-HD and DressCode at 1024 x 768 show that it matches or outperforms strong server-based baselines while running entirely offline. These results demonstrate that high-quality VTON is not only feasible but also practical on-device, offering a secure solution for real-world applications.

AI.tldr: Mobile-VTON is a privacy-preserving virtual try-on framework that enables fully offline, on-device generation using a modular TGT architecture with feature-guided adversarial distillation, garment-conditioned generation, and garment alignment, achieving server-level quality with low computational overhead.
AI.motivation: Existing VTON systems require cloud-based GPUs and uploading personal photos, raising privacy concerns and limiting deployment on commodity mobile devices.
AI.method: A modular TeacherNet-GarmentNet-TryonNet (TGT) architecture that integrates knowledge distillation, garment-conditioned generation, and garment alignment. Key innovations include Feature-Guided Adversarial (FGA) Distillation for matching real-world distributions, trajectory-consistency loss for garmentNet, and latent concatenation with lightweight cross-modal conditioning for TryonNet.
AI.result: Matches or outperforms strong server-based baselines on VITON-HD and DressCode datasets at 1024×768 resolution while running entirely offline with low computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: High-quality VTON is feasible and practical on-device, offering a secure solution for real-world applications without privacy concerns.

title: LFPO: Likelihood-Free Policy Optimization for Masked Diffusion Models
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has achieved remarkable success in improving autoregressive models, especially in domains requiring correctness like mathematical reasoning and code generation. However, directly applying such paradigms to Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) is fundamentally hindered by the intractability of exact likelihood computation, which forces existing methods to rely on high-variance approximations. To bridge this gap, we propose Likelihood-Free Policy Optimization (LFPO), a native framework that maps the concept of vector field flow matching to the discrete token space. Specifically, LFPO formulates alignment as geometric velocity rectification, which directly optimizes denoising logits via contrastive updates. This design effectively bypasses the errors inherent in likelihood approximation, yielding the precise gradient estimation. Furthermore, LFPO enforce consistency by predicting final solutions from intermediate steps, effectively straightening the probability flow to enable high-quality generation with significantly fewer iterations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LFPO not only outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on code and reasoning benchmarks but also accelerates inference by approximately 20% through reduced diffusion steps.

AI.tldr: LFPO is a likelihood-free framework that optimizes diffusion LLMs using geometric velocity rectification and contrastive updates, avoiding high-variance likelihood approximations while improving performance and accelerating inference.
AI.motivation: RLVR has succeeded for autoregressive models in correctness-critical domains like math and code, but direct application to diffusion LLMs is hindered by intractable exact likelihood computation, forcing reliance on high-variance approximations.
AI.method: LFPO maps vector field flow matching to discrete token space, formulates alignment as geometric velocity rectification, directly optimizes denoising logits via contrastive updates, and enforces consistency by predicting final solutions from intermediate steps to straighten probability flow.
AI.result: LFPO outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on code and reasoning benchmarks and accelerates inference by approximately 20% through reduced diffusion steps.
AI.conclusion: LFPO successfully bridges the gap between reinforcement learning optimization and diffusion LLMs by avoiding likelihood approximation errors, demonstrating that geometric velocity rectification and consistency enforcement enable both higher quality generation and more efficient inference.

title: StegoNGP: 3D Cryptographic Steganography using Instant-NGP
summary: Recently, Instant Neural Graphics Primitives (Instant-NGP) has achieved significant success in rapid 3D scene reconstruction, but securely embedding high-capacity hidden data, such as an entire 3D scene, remains a challenge. Existing methods rely on external decoders, require architectural modifications, and suffer from limited capacity, which makes them easily detectable. We propose a novel parameter-free 3D Cryptographic Steganography using Instant-NGP (StegoNGP), which leverages the Instant-NGP hash encoding function as a key-controlled scene switcher. By associating a default key with a cover scene and a secret key with a hidden scene, our method trains a single model to interweave both representations within the same network weights. The resulting model is indistinguishable from a standard Instant-NGP in architecture and parameter count. We also introduce an enhanced Multi-Key scheme, which assigns multiple independent keys across hash levels, dramatically expanding the key space and providing high robustness against partial key disclosure attacks. Experimental results demonstrated that StegoNGP can hide a complete high-quality 3D scene with strong imperceptibility and security, providing a new paradigm for high-capacity, undetectable information hiding in neural fields. The code can be found at https://github.com/jiang-wenxiang/StegoNGP.

AI.tldr: StegoNGP introduces a parameter-free 3D cryptographic steganography method that hides entire 3D scenes within a single Instant-NGP network by using hash encoding functions as key-controlled scene switches, achieving high-capacity, undetectable information hiding with an enhanced Multi-Key scheme for robustness.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D scene reconstruction methods face challenges in securely embedding high-capacity hidden data due to reliance on external decoders, architectural modifications, and limited capacity that makes them easily detectable.
AI.method: StegoNGP leverages the Instant-NGP hash encoding function as a key-controlled scene switcher. It associates a default key with a cover scene and a secret key with a hidden scene, training a single model to interweave both representations within the same network weights. The method also introduces an enhanced Multi-Key scheme assigning multiple independent keys across hash levels to expand key space.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate that StegoNGP can hide complete high-quality 3D scenes with strong imperceptibility and security. The method produces a model indistinguishable from standard Instant-NGP in architecture and parameter count.
AI.conclusion: StegoNGP provides a new paradigm for high-capacity, undetectable information hiding in neural fields, achieving secure 3D scene embedding without architectural changes or external decoders.

title: Rate-Distortion Signatures of Generalization and Information Trade-offs
summary: Generalization to novel visual conditions remains a central challenge for both human and machine vision, yet standard robustness metrics offer limited insight into how systems trade accuracy for robustness. We introduce a rate-distortion-theoretic framework that treats stimulus-response behavior as an effective communication channel, derives rate-distortion (RD) frontiers from confusion matrices, and summarizes each system with two interpretable geometric signatures - slope ($β$) and curvature ($κ$) - which capture the marginal cost and abruptness of accuracy-robustness trade-offs. Applying this framework to human psychophysics and 18 deep vision models under controlled image perturbations, we compare generalization geometry across model architectures and training regimes. We find that both biological and artificial systems follow a common lossy-compression principle but occupy systematically different regions of RD space. In particular, humans exhibit smoother, more flexible trade-offs, whereas modern deep networks operate in steeper and more brittle regimes even at matched accuracy. Across training regimes, robustness training induces systematic but dissociable shifts in beta/kappa, revealing cases where improved robustness or accuracy does not translate into more human-like generalization geometry. These results demonstrate that RD geometry provides a compact, model-agnostic lens for comparing generalization behavior across systems beyond standard accuracy-based metrics.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a rate-distortion-theoretic framework that treats stimulus-response behavior as an effective communication channel, deriving rate-distortion frontiers from confusion matrices. It uses two geometric signatures—slope (β) and curvature (κ)—to capture the marginal cost and abruptness of accuracy-robustness trade-offs. Applied to human psychophysics and 18 deep vision models, the study finds both systems follow a lossy-compression principle but occupy different regions of RD space, with humans exhibiting smoother, more flexible trade-offs compared to steeper, more brittle deep networks.
AI.motivation: Generalization to novel visual conditions is a central challenge for both human and machine vision. Standard robustness metrics offer limited insight into how systems trade accuracy for robustness, making it difficult to compare generalization behavior across different systems in a meaningful way.
AI.method: The authors develop a rate-distortion-theoretic framework that models stimulus-response behavior as an effective communication channel. They derive rate-distortion (RD) frontiers from confusion matrices and summarize each system with two interpretable geometric signatures: slope (β) capturing the marginal cost of trade-offs, and curvature (κ) capturing the abruptness of these trade-offs. The framework is applied to human psychophysics data and 18 deep vision models under controlled image perturbations.
AI.result: Both biological and artificial systems follow a lossy-compression principle but occupy systematically different regions of RD space. Humans exhibit smoother, more flexible accuracy-robustness trade-offs, while modern deep networks operate in steeper and more brittle regimes even at matched accuracy. Robustness training induces systematic but dissociable shifts in β and κ, revealing cases where improved robustness or accuracy does not translate into more human-like generalization geometry.
AI.conclusion: Rate-distortion geometry provides a compact, model-agnostic lens for comparing generalization behavior across systems beyond standard accuracy-based metrics, revealing fundamental differences in how biological and artificial vision systems approach accuracy-robustness trade-offs.

title: Learning from Synthetic Data Improves Multi-hop Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has been shown to significantly boost reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) in math, coding, and multi-hop reasoning tasks. However, RL fine-tuning requires abundant high-quality verifiable data, often sourced from human annotations, generated from frontier LLMs, or scored by LLM-based verifiers. All three have considerable limitations: human-annotated datasets are small and expensive to curate, LLM-generated data is hallucination-prone and costly, and LLM-based verifiers are inaccurate and slow. In this work, we investigate a cheaper alternative: RL fine-tuning on rule-generated synthetic data for multi-hop reasoning tasks. We discover that LLMs fine-tuned on synthetic data perform significantly better on popular real-world question-answering benchmarks, despite the synthetic data containing only fictional knowledge. On stratifying performance by question difficulty, we find that synthetic data teaches LLMs to compose knowledge -- a fundamental and generalizable reasoning skill. Our work highlights rule-generated synthetic reasoning data as a free and scalable resource to improve LLM reasoning capabilities.

AI.tldr: RL fine-tuning on rule-generated synthetic data (even with fictional knowledge) significantly improves LLMs' multi-hop reasoning capabilities, teaching them knowledge composition skills at no cost.
AI.motivation: RL fine-tuning typically requires expensive high-quality data (human annotations, LLM-generated data, or LLM-based verifiers), each with limitations: human data is small and costly, LLM-generated data is hallucination-prone and costly, and LLM verifiers are inaccurate and slow.
AI.method: Investigating RL fine-tuning on rule-generated synthetic data for multi-hop reasoning tasks, where synthetic data contains only fictional knowledge but is generated through explicit rules.
AI.result: LLMs fine-tuned on synthetic data perform significantly better on real-world QA benchmarks. Performance stratification shows synthetic data specifically teaches LLMs to "compose knowledge" — a fundamental and generalizable reasoning skill.
AI.conclusion: Rule-generated synthetic reasoning data is a free and scalable resource for improving LLM reasoning capabilities, overcoming the data bottlenecks of traditional RL fine-tuning approaches.

title: Decoupling Motion and Geometry in 4D Gaussian Splatting
summary: High-fidelity reconstruction of dynamic scenes is an important yet challenging problem. While recent 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) has demonstrated the ability to model temporal dynamics, it couples Gaussian motion and geometric attributes within a single covariance formulation, which limits its expressiveness for complex motions and often leads to visual artifacts. To address this, we propose VeGaS, a novel velocity-based 4D Gaussian Splatting framework that decouples Gaussian motion and geometry. Specifically, we introduce a Galilean shearing matrix that explicitly incorporates time-varying velocity to flexibly model complex non-linear motions, while strictly isolating the effects of Gaussian motion from the geometry-related conditional Gaussian covariance. Furthermore, a Geometric Deformation Network is introduced to refine Gaussian shapes and orientations using spatio-temporal context and velocity cues, enhancing temporal geometric modeling. Extensive experiments on public datasets demonstrate that VeGaS achieves state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: VeGaS is a velocity-based 4D Gaussian Splatting framework that decouples Gaussian motion and geometry using a Galilean shearing matrix and a Geometric Deformation Network, achieving state-of-the-art performance in dynamic scene reconstruction.
AI.motivation: Existing 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) methods couple Gaussian motion and geometric attributes within a single covariance formulation, which limits expressiveness for modeling complex motions and often results in visual artifacts.
AI.method: VeGaS introduces (1) a Galilean shearing matrix to explicitly incorporate time-varying velocity for flexible modeling of complex non-linear motions while strictly isolating motion effects from geometry-related covariance, and (2) a Geometric Deformation Network that refines Gaussian shapes and orientations using spatio-temporal context and velocity cues.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on public datasets demonstrate that VeGaS achieves state-of-the-art performance in dynamic scene reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully decouples Gaussian motion and geometry, enabling more flexible modeling of complex non-linear motions and significantly enhancing temporal geometric modeling capabilities.

title: Jump Like A Squirrel: Optimized Execution Step Order for Anytime Random Forest Inference
summary:
Due to their efficiency and small size, decision trees and random forests are popular machine learning models used for classification on resource-constrained systems. In such systems, the available execution time for inference in a random forest might not be sufficient for a complete model execution. Ideally, the already gained prediction confidence should be retained. An anytime algorithm is designed to be able to be aborted anytime, while giving a result with an increasing quality over time. Previous approaches have realized random forests as anytime algorithms on the granularity of trees, stopping after some but not all trees of a forest have been executed. However, due to the way decision trees subdivide the sample space in every step, an increase in prediction quality is achieved with every additional step in one tree. In this paper, we realize decision trees and random forest as anytime algorithms on the granularity of single steps in trees. This approach opens a design space to define the step order in a forest, which has the potential to optimize the mean accuracy. We propose the Optimal Order, which finds a step order with a maximal mean accuracy in exponential runtime and the polynomial runtime heuristics Forward Squirrel Order and Backward Squirrel Order, which greedily maximize the accuracy for each additional step taken down and up the trees, respectively.
  Our evaluation shows, that the Backward Squirrel Order performs $\sim94\%$ as well as the Optimal Order and $\sim99\%$ as well as all other step orders.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an anytime algorithm for decision trees and random forests that executes at the granularity of individual steps (nodes) rather than whole trees, with "Optimal Order" and two heuristics ("Forward Squirrel Order" and "Backward Squirrel Order") for determining step execution order to maximize prediction accuracy.
AI.motivation: Decision trees and random forests are popular on resource-constrained systems due to efficiency and small size, but inference time may be insufficient for complete execution. Existing approaches stop after some trees but miss fine-grained quality improvements that occur with each additional step in a tree. The goal is to retain prediction confidence while allowing early termination.
AI.method: Realizes decision trees and random forests as anytime algorithms at the granularity of single steps (nodes) in trees, creating a design space for step order. Proposes Optimal Order (exponential runtime, maximal mean accuracy), Forward Squirrel Order (greedy, steps taken down trees), and Backward Squirrel Order (greedy, steps taken up trees).
AI.result: Backward Squirrel Order performs ~94% as well as the Optimal Order and ~99% as well as all other step orders, demonstrating near-optimal accuracy with polynomial runtime.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively maximizes prediction accuracy in resource-constrained scenarios requiring early termination, with Backward Squirrel Order being the most efficient and accurate heuristic.

title: Recursive Models for Long-Horizon Reasoning
summary: Modern language models reason within bounded context, an inherent constraint that poses a fundamental barrier to long-horizon reasoning. We identify recursion as a core principle for overcoming this barrier, and propose recursive models as a minimal realization, where the model can recursively invoke itself to solve subtasks in isolated contexts. We prove that any computable problem admits a recursive decomposition in which each subtask requires only exponentially smaller active context than standard autoregressive models; this strictly surpasses any context management approach confined to a single sequence, such as summarization. We further generalize our framework to modern agentic systems with arbitrary context processing and control flows, and prove that recursive models can achieve optimal power within this broader class. Experimentally, we train a 3B model to reason recursively and evaluate on Boolean satisfiability, a task requiring long-horizon combinatorial search, where it significantly outperforms frontier LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces recursive models where language models can invoke themselves to solve subtasks in isolated contexts, overcoming the bounded context limitation of standard LLMs. These models achieve exponential reduction in active context compared to autoregressive models and significantly outperform frontier LLMs on Boolean satisfiability problems.
AI.motivation: Modern language models face a fundamental barrier to long-horizon reasoning due to bounded context constraints. Current context management approaches (e.g., summarization) confined to single sequences are insufficient for solving complex, long-horizon problems.
AI.method: The authors propose recursive models where the model recursively invokes itself to solve subtasks in isolated contexts. They prove that any computable problem admits a recursive decomposition where each subtask requires exponentially smaller active context than standard autoregressive models. They further generalize this framework to modern agentic systems with arbitrary context processing and control flows, proving recursive models can achieve optimal power within this broader class.
AI.result: A 3B model trained to reason recursively was evaluated on Boolean satisfiability (a task requiring long-horizon combinatorial search). The recursive model significantly outperformed frontier LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Recursive models represent a minimal realization that overcomes context limitations and can achieve optimal power in broader agentic systems, surpassing all context management approaches confined to single sequences.

title: PreciseCache: Precise Feature Caching for Efficient and High-fidelity Video Generation
summary: High computational costs and slow inference hinder the practical application of video generation models. While prior works accelerate the generation process through feature caching, they often suffer from notable quality degradation. In this work, we reveal that this issue arises from their inability to distinguish truly redundant features, which leads to the unintended skipping of computations on important features. To address this, we propose \textbf{PreciseCache}, a plug-and-play framework that precisely detects and skips truly redundant computations, thereby accelerating inference without sacrificing quality. Specifically, PreciseCache contains two components: LFCache for step-wise caching and BlockCache for block-wise caching. For LFCache, we compute the Low-Frequency Difference (LFD) between the prediction features of the current step and those from the previous cached step. Empirically, we observe that LFD serves as an effective measure of step-wise redundancy, accurately detecting highly redundant steps whose computation can be skipped through reusing cached features. To further accelerate generation within each non-skipped step, we propose BlockCache, which precisely detects and skips redundant computations at the block level within the network. Extensive experiments on various backbones demonstrate the effectiveness of our PreciseCache, such as achieving an average of $2.6\times$ speedup on Wan2.1-14B without noticeable quality loss.

AI.tldr: PreciseCache is a plug-and-play framework for video generation models that precisely detects and skips redundant computations through LFCache (step-wise) and BlockCache (block-wise) components, achieving 2.6× speedup without quality degradation.
AI.motivation: Prior works accelerate video generation via feature caching but suffer from quality degradation because they cannot distinguish truly redundant features, leading to unintended skipping of important computations.
AI.method: PreciseCache contains two components: (1) LFCache - computes Low-Frequency Difference (LFD) between current step prediction features and previous cached step features to detect step-wise redundancy; (2) BlockCache - detects and skips redundant computations at block level within each non-skipped step.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on various backbones demonstrate effectiveness, achieving an average of 2.6× speedup on Wan2.1-14B without noticeable quality loss.
AI.conclusion: PreciseCache effectively accelerates inference without sacrificing quality by precisely identifying and skipping only truly redundant computations.

title: SafeSci: Safety Evaluation of Large Language Models in Science Domains and Beyond
summary: The success of large language models (LLMs) in scientific domains has heightened safety concerns, prompting numerous benchmarks to evaluate their scientific safety. Existing benchmarks often suffer from limited risk coverage and a reliance on subjective evaluation. To address these problems, we introduce SafeSci, a comprehensive framework for safety evaluation and enhancement in scientific contexts. SafeSci comprises SafeSciBench, a multi-disciplinary benchmark with 0.25M samples, and SafeSciTrain, a large-scale dataset containing 1.5M samples for safety enhancement. SafeSciBench distinguishes between safety knowledge and risk to cover extensive scopes and employs objective metrics such as deterministically answerable questions to mitigate evaluation bias. We evaluate 24 advanced LLMs, revealing critical vulnerabilities in current models. We also observe that LLMs exhibit varying degrees of excessive refusal behaviors on safety-related issues. For safety enhancement, we demonstrate that fine-tuning on SafeSciTrain significantly enhances the safety alignment of models. Finally, we argue that knowledge is a double-edged sword, and determining the safety of a scientific question should depend on specific context, rather than universally categorizing it as safe or unsafe. Our work provides both a diagnostic tool and a practical resource for building safer scientific AI systems.

AI.tldr: SafeSci is a comprehensive framework for safety evaluation and enhancement in scientific contexts, comprising SafeSciBench (a multi-disciplinary benchmark with 0.25M samples) and SafeSciTrain (a 1.5M sample dataset for safety enhancement). SafeSciBench distinguishes between safety knowledge and risk while using objective metrics to mitigate evaluation bias.
AI.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved success in scientific domains, but this has raised significant safety concerns. Existing benchmarks suffer from limited risk coverage and rely on subjective evaluation methods, making them inadequate for comprehensive safety assessment.
AI.method: SafeSci introduces two key components: (1) SafeSciBench, a multi-disciplinary benchmark with 250,000 samples that distinguishes between safety knowledge and risk to cover extensive scopes, using objective metrics like deterministically answerable questions to reduce evaluation bias; (2) SafeSciTrain, a large-scale dataset of 1.5 million samples designed for safety enhancement through fine-tuning.
AI.result: Evaluation of 24 advanced LLMs revealed critical safety vulnerabilities and observed varying degrees of excessive refusal behaviors on safety-related issues. Fine-tuning models on SafeSciTrain significantly improved safety alignment, demonstrating the framework's effectiveness for both diagnosis and enhancement of scientific AI safety.
AI.conclusion: The authors argue that knowledge is a double-edged sword, and safety determination should depend on specific context rather than universally categorizing scientific questions as safe or unsafe. SafeSci provides both a diagnostic tool and practical resource for building safer scientific AI systems.

title: EraseAnything++: Enabling Concept Erasure in Rectified Flow Transformers Leveraging Multi-Object Optimization
summary: Removing undesired concepts from large-scale text-to-image (T2I) and text-to-video (T2V) diffusion models while preserving overall generative quality remains a major challenge, particularly as modern models such as Stable Diffusion v3, Flux, and OpenSora employ flow-matching and transformer-based architectures and extend to long-horizon video generation. Existing concept erasure methods, designed for earlier T2I/T2V models, often fail to generalize to these paradigms. To address this issue, we propose EraseAnything++, a unified framework for concept erasure in both image and video diffusion models with flow-matching objectives. Central to our approach is formulating concept erasure as a constrained multi-objective optimization problem that explicitly balances concept removal with preservation of generative utility. To solve the resulting conflicting objectives, we introduce an efficient utility-preserving unlearning strategy based on implicit gradient surgery. Furthermore, by integrating LoRA-based parameter tuning with attention-level regularization, our method anchors erasure on key visual representations and propagates it consistently across spatial and temporal dimensions. In the video setting, we further enhance consistency through an anchor-and-propagate mechanism that initializes erasure on reference frames and enforces it throughout subsequent transformer layers, thereby mitigating temporal drift. Extensive experiments on both image and video benchmarks demonstrate that EraseAnything++ substantially outperforms prior methods in erasure effectiveness, generative fidelity, and temporal consistency, establishing a new state of the art for concept erasure in next-generation diffusion models.

AI.tldr: EraseAnything++ is a unified framework for concept erasure in image and video diffusion models using flow-matching objectives, formulated as a constrained multi-objective optimization problem solved via implicit gradient surgery, LoRA-based tuning, and attention-level regularization with an anchor-and-propagate mechanism for temporal consistency.
AI.motivation: Removing undesired concepts from modern large-scale T2I/T2V diffusion models (Stable Diffusion v3, Flux, OpenSora) while preserving generative quality is challenging due to their flow-matching and transformer-based architectures supporting long-horizon video generation; existing erasure methods designed for earlier models fail to generalize.
AI.method: Formulates concept erasure as a constrained multi-objective optimization balancing removal and utility preservation. Introduces an efficient utility-preserving unlearning strategy based on implicit gradient surgery. Integrates LoRA-based parameter tuning with attention-level regularization to anchor erasure on key visual representations and propagate it across spatial and temporal dimensions. Uses an anchor-and-propagate mechanism in video to initialize erasure on reference frames and enforce it throughout transformer layers to mitigate temporal drift.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on image and video benchmarks demonstrate that EraseAnything++ substantially outperforms prior methods in erasure effectiveness, generative fidelity, and temporal consistency.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a new state-of-the-art for concept erasure in next-generation diffusion models, successfully addressing the challenge of concept removal while maintaining overall generative quality in both image and video diffusion models.

title: FAST-DIPS: Adjoint-Free Analytic Steps and Hard-Constrained Likelihood Correction for Diffusion-Prior Inverse Problems
summary: Training-free diffusion priors enable inverse-problem solvers without retraining, but for nonlinear forward operators data consistency often relies on repeated derivatives or inner optimization/MCMC loops with conservative step sizes, incurring many iterations and denoiser/score evaluations. We propose a training-free solver that replaces these inner loops with a hard measurement-space feasibility constraint (closed-form projection) and an analytic, model-optimal step size, enabling a small, fixed compute budget per noise level. Anchored at the denoiser prediction, the correction is approximated via an adjoint-free, ADMM-style splitting with projection and a few steepest-descent updates, using one VJP and either one JVP or a forward-difference probe, followed by backtracking and decoupled re-annealing. We prove local model optimality and descent under backtracking for the step-size rule, and derive an explicit KL bound for mode-substitution re-annealing under a local Gaussian conditional surrogate. We also develop a latent variant and a one-parameter pixel$\rightarrow$latent hybrid schedule. Experiments achieve competitive PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS with up to 19.5$\times$ speedup, without hand-coded adjoints or inner MCMC.

AI.tldr: A training-free diffusion prior solver for inverse problems that replaces inner optimization loops with a closed-form measurement-space projection and analytic step size, achieving up to 19.5× speedup without hand-coded adjoints or inner MCMC
AI.motivation: Training-free diffusion priors enable inverse-problem solvers without retraining, but nonlinear forward operators require repeated derivatives or inner optimization/MCMC loops with conservative step sizes, leading to many iterations and expensive denoiser/score evaluations
AI.method: Anchored at denoiser prediction, the correction uses an adjoint-free ADMM-style splitting with projection and few steepest-descent updates, using one VJP and either one JVP or forward-difference probe, followed by backtracking and decoupled re-annealing; includes latent variant and one-parameter pixel→latent hybrid schedule
AI.result: Achieves competitive PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS metrics with up to 19.5× speedup compared to methods requiring inner MCMC or repeated derivatives
AI.conclusion: Proves local model optimality and descent under backtracking for the step-size rule, derives explicit KL bound for mode-substitution re-annealing under local Gaussian conditional surrogate, demonstrating the method's theoretical guarantees and practical effectiveness

title: Boosting Entropy with Bell Box Quantization
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026
summary: Quantization-Aware Pre-Training (QAPT) is an effective technique to reduce the compute and memory overhead of Deep Neural Networks while improving their energy efficiency on edge devices. Existing QAPT methods produce models stored in compute-efficient data types (e.g. integers) that are not information theoretically optimal (ITO). On the other hand, existing ITO data types (e.g. Quantile/NormalFloat Quantization) are not compute-efficient. We propose BBQ, the first ITO quantization method that is also compute-efficient. BBQ builds on our key insight that since learning is domain-agnostic, the output of a quantizer does not need to reside in the same domain as its input. BBQ performs ITO quantization in its input domain, and returns its output in a compute-efficient domain where ITO data types are mapped to compute-efficient data types. Without sacrificing compute efficiency, BBQ outperforms prior SOTA QAPT methods by a perplexity reduction of up to 2 points for 4-bit models, up to 4 points for 3-bit models, up to 5 points for 2-bit models, and up to 18 points for 1-bit models. Code is available at https://github.com/1733116199/bbq.

AI.tldr: BBQ is the first information-theoretically optimal (ITO) quantization method that is also compute-efficient, achieving significant perplexity improvements across various bit-widths.
AI.motivation: Existing QAPT methods produce models in compute-efficient data types that are not ITO, while existing ITO methods are not compute-efficient. There's a gap between information-theoretic optimality and compute efficiency that needs to be bridged.
AI.method: BBQ leverages the insight that learning is domain-agnostic, so quantizer output doesn't need to be in the same domain as input. It performs ITO quantization in the input domain and returns output in a compute-efficient domain where ITO data types are mapped to compute-efficient data types.
AI.result: BBQ outperforms prior SOTA QAPT methods without sacrificing compute efficiency, achieving perplexity reductions of up to 2 points (4-bit), 4 points (3-bit), 5 points (2-bit), and 18 points (1-bit).
AI.conclusion: BBQ successfully achieves the combination of ITO quantization and compute efficiency, demonstrating significant perplexity improvements across different bit-widths.

title: Event-Anchored Frame Selection for Effective Long-Video Understanding
summary: Massive frame redundancy and limited context window make efficient frame selection crucial for long-video understanding with large vision-language models (LVLMs). Prevailing approaches, however, adopt a flat sampling paradigm which treats the video as an unstructured collection of frames. In this paper, we introduce Event-Anchored Frame Selection (EFS), a hierarchical, event-aware pipeline. Leveraging self-supervised DINO embeddings, EFS first partitions the video stream into visually homogeneous temporal segments, which serve as proxies for semantic events. Within each event, it then selects the most query-relevant frame as an anchor. These anchors act as structural priors that guide a global refinement stage using an adaptive Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) scheme. This pipeline ensures the final keyframe set jointly optimizes for event coverage, query relevance, and visual diversity. As a training-free, plug-and-play module, EFS can be seamlessly integrated into off-the-shelf LVLMs, yielding substantial gains on challenging video understanding benchmarks. Specifically, when applied to LLaVA-Video-7B, EFS improves accuracy by 4.7%, 4.9%, and 8.8% on VideoMME, LongVideoBench, and MLVU, respectively.

AI.tldr: Event-Anchored Frame Selection (EFS) introduces a hierarchical, event-aware pipeline for efficient frame selection in long videos, using DINO embeddings for event partitioning, query-relevant anchor selection, and adaptive MMR refinement to improve LVLM performance.
AI.motivation: Massive frame redundancy and limited context windows in long videos create challenges for LVLMs. Prevailing flat sampling paradigms treat videos as unstructured collections, lacking event awareness and failing to optimize for event coverage, query relevance, and visual diversity simultaneously.
AI.method: EFS employs a hierarchical pipeline: (1) partitions video streams into visually homogeneous temporal segments using self-supervised DINO embeddings (proxy for semantic events); (2) selects the most query-relevant frame as an anchor within each segment; (3) applies adaptive Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) for global refinement. EFS is training-free and plug-and-play.
AI.result: Integrated into LLaVA-Video-7B, EFS improves accuracy by 4.7% on VideoMME, 4.9% on LongVideoBench, and 8.8% on MLVU, demonstrating substantial gains on challenging long-video understanding benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: EFS effectively addresses frame redundancy in long videos by ensuring the final keyframe set jointly optimizes for event coverage, query relevance, and visual diversity. As a training-free, plug-and-play module, EFS can be seamlessly integrated into off-the-shelf LVLMs, making it a practical solution for long-video understanding tasks.

title: Towards OOD Generalization in Dynamic Graphs via Causal Invariant Learning
summary: Although dynamic graph neural networks (DyGNNs) have demonstrated promising capabilities, most existing methods ignore out-of-distribution (OOD) shifts that commonly exist in dynamic graphs. Dynamic graph OOD generalization is non-trivial due to the following challenges: 1) Identifying invariant and variant patterns amid complex graph evolution, 2) Capturing the intrinsic evolution rationale from these patterns, and 3) Ensuring model generalization across diverse OOD shifts despite limited data distribution observations. Although several attempts have been made to tackle these challenges, none has successfully addressed all three simultaneously, and they face various limitations in complex OOD scenarios. To solve these issues, we propose a Dynamic graph Causal Invariant Learning (DyCIL) model for OOD generalization via exploiting invariant spatio-temporal patterns from a causal view. Specifically, we first develop a dynamic causal subgraph generator to identify causal dynamic subgraphs explicitly. Next, we design a causal-aware spatio-temporal attention module to extract the intrinsic evolution rationale behind invariant patterns. Finally, we further introduce an adaptive environment generator to capture the underlying dynamics of distributional shifts. Extensive experiments on both real-world and synthetic dynamic graph datasets demonstrate the superiority of our model over state-of-the-art baselines in handling OOD shifts.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes DyCIL, a Dynamic graph Causal Invariant Learning framework that addresses OOD generalization in dynamic graphs by exploiting invariant spatio-temporal patterns through a causal perspective.
AI.motivation: Most existing dynamic graph neural networks (DyGNNs) ignore out-of-distribution (OOD) shifts, which are common in dynamic graphs. The authors identify three key challenges: 1) identifying invariant and variant patterns amid complex graph evolution, 2) capturing the intrinsic evolution rationale from these patterns, and 3) ensuring model generalization across diverse OOD shifts despite limited distribution observations.
AI.method: The proposed DyCIL model consists of three main components: 1) A dynamic causal subgraph generator to identify causal dynamic subgraphs explicitly, 2) A causal-aware spatio-temporal attention module to extract intrinsic evolution rationale behind invariant patterns, and 3) An adaptive environment generator to capture underlying dynamics of distributional shifts.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on both real-world and synthetic dynamic graph datasets demonstrate that DyCIL outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in handling OOD shifts.
AI.conclusion: The DyCIL framework successfully addresses all three challenges simultaneously by exploiting invariant spatio-temporal patterns from a causal view, offering a comprehensive solution for OOD generalization in dynamic graph learning scenarios.

title: DeLo: Dual Decomposed Low-Rank Experts Collaboration for Continual Missing Modality Learning
summary: Adapting Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) to real-world scenarios poses the dual challenges of learning from sequential data streams while handling frequent modality incompleteness, a task known as Continual Missing Modality Learning (CMML). However, existing works on CMML have predominantly relied on prompt tuning, a technique that struggles with this task due to cross-task interference between its learnable prompts in their shared embedding space. A naive application of Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) with modality-shared module will also suffer modality interference from competing gradients. To this end, we propose DeLo, the first framework to leverage a novel dual-decomposed low-rank expert architecture for CMML. Specifically, this architecture resolves modality interference through decomposed LoRA expert, dynamically composing LoRA update matrix with rank-one factors from disentangled modality-specific factor pools. Embedded within a task-partitioned framework that structurally prevents catastrophic forgetting, this expert system is supported by two key mechanisms: a Cross-Modal Guided Routing strategy to handle incomplete data and a Task-Key Memory for efficient, task-agnostic inference. Extensive experiments on established CMML benchmarks demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches. This highlights the value of a principled, architecturally-aware LoRA design for real-world multimodal challenges.

AI.tldr: DeLo is a novel dual-decomposed low-rank expert framework for Continual Missing Modality Learning (CMML), using decomposed LoRA experts, Cross-Modal Guided Routing, and Task-Key Memory to overcome modality interference and achieve state-of-the-art performance on CMML benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Continual Missing Modality Learning (CMML) faces two key challenges: learning from sequential data streams and handling frequent modality incompleteness. Existing CMML methods relying on prompt tuning struggle with cross-task interference between learnable prompts in shared embedding spaces, while naive Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) with modality-shared modules suffers from modality interference due to competing gradients.
AI.method: DeLo proposes a dual-decomposed low-rank expert architecture that resolves modality interference through decomposed LoRA experts, dynamically composing LoRA update matrices with rank-one factors from disentangled modality-specific factor pools. The framework includes: 1) Cross-Modal Guided Routing strategy to handle incomplete data, and 2) Task-Key Memory for efficient, task-agnostic inference. It operates within a task-partitioned framework that structurally prevents catastrophic forgetting.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on established CMML benchmarks demonstrate that DeLo significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, validating the effectiveness of the principled, architecturally-aware LoRA design for real-world multimodal challenges.
AI.conclusion: The research highlights the importance of architecturally-aware LoRA design in multimodal learning. By using dual-decomposed low-rank expert architecture, DeLo effectively addresses modality interference and enables effective continual learning from sequential data streams with missing modalities, establishing a new standard for CMML approaches.

title: Foundation Models in Remote Sensing: Evolving from Unimodality to Multimodality
summary: Remote sensing (RS) techniques are increasingly crucial for deepening our understanding of the planet. As the volume and diversity of RS data continue to grow exponentially, there is an urgent need for advanced data modeling and understanding capabilities to manage and interpret these vast datasets effectively. Foundation models present significant new growth opportunities and immense potential to revolutionize the RS field. In this paper, we conduct a comprehensive technical survey on foundation models in RS, offering a brand-new perspective by exploring their evolution from unimodality to multimodality. We hope this work serves as a valuable entry point for researchers interested in both foundation models and RS and helps them launch new projects or explore new research topics in this rapidly evolving area. This survey addresses the following three key questions: What are foundation models in RS? Why are foundation models needed in RS? How can we effectively guide junior researchers in gaining a comprehensive and practical understanding of foundation models in RS applications? More specifically, we begin by outlining the background and motivation, emphasizing the importance of foundation models in RS. We then review existing foundation models in RS, systematically categorizing them into unimodal and multimodal approaches. Additionally, we provide a tutorial-like section to guide researchers, especially beginners, on how to train foundation models in RS and apply them to real-world tasks. The survey aims to equip researchers in RS with a deeper and more efficient understanding of foundation models, enabling them to get started easily and effectively apply these models across various RS applications.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey on foundation models in remote sensing (RS), exploring their evolution from unimodality to multimodality and providing guidance for researchers to train and apply these models in RS applications.
AI.motivation: As RS data volume and diversity grow exponentially, there's an urgent need for advanced data modeling capabilities. Foundation models present significant opportunities to revolutionize the RS field by enabling effective management and interpretation of vast RS datasets.
AI.method: The paper reviews existing foundation models in RS, systematically categorizing them into unimodal and multimodal approaches. It also provides a tutorial-like section guiding researchers (especially beginners) on training foundation models in RS and applying them to real-world tasks.
AI.result: Foundation models in RS are systematically categorized into unimodal and multimodal approaches. The survey provides practical guidance for researchers to effectively apply these models across various RS applications, enabling deeper and more efficient understanding.
AI.conclusion: This survey serves as a valuable entry point for researchers interested in both foundation models and RS, helping them launch new projects and explore new research topics in this rapidly evolving area. It equips researchers with the knowledge needed to get started easily and effectively apply foundation models across various RS applications.

title: Transform-Invariant Generative Ray Path Sampling for Efficient Radio Propagation Modeling
comment: submitted to npj Wireless Technology, 26 pages, 14 figures
summary: Ray tracing has become a standard for accurate radio propagation modeling, but suffers from exponential computational complexity, as the number of candidate paths scales with the number of objects raised to the power of the interaction order. This bottleneck limits its use in large-scale or real-time applications, forcing traditional tools to rely on heuristics to reduce the number of path candidates at the cost of potentially reduced accuracy. To overcome this limitation, we propose a comprehensive machine-learning-assisted framework that replaces exhaustive path searching with intelligent sampling via Generative Flow Networks. Applying such generative models to this domain presents significant challenges, particularly sparse rewards due to the rarity of valid paths, which can lead to convergence failures and trivial solutions when evaluating high-order interactions in complex environments. To ensure robust learning and efficient exploration, our framework incorporates three key architectural components. First, we implement an \emph{experience replay buffer} to capture and retain rare valid paths. Second, we adopt a uniform exploratory policy to improve generalization and prevent the model from overfitting to simple geometries. Third, we apply a physics-based action masking strategy that filters out physically impossible paths before the model even considers them. As demonstrated in our experimental validation, the proposed model achieves substantial speedups over exhaustive search -- up to $10\times$ faster on GPU and $1000\times$ faster on CPU -- while maintaining high coverage accuracy and successfully uncovering complex propagation paths. The complete source code, tests, and tutorial are available at https://github.com/jeertmans/sampling-paths.

AI.tldr: A machine-learning framework using Generative Flow Networks replaces exhaustive ray-tracing search with intelligent sampling for radio propagation modeling, achieving 10× GPU and 1000× CPU speedups while maintaining high accuracy.
AI.motivation: Ray tracing for radio propagation suffers from exponential computational complexity (path candidates scale with objects^interaction order), limiting its use in large-scale or real-time applications. Traditional tools rely on heuristics that reduce accuracy to address this bottleneck.
AI.method: The proposed framework employs Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) for intelligent sampling instead of exhaustive path searching. Three key architectural components ensure robust learning: (1) experience replay buffer to capture rare valid paths, (2) uniform exploratory policy for generalization and prevention of overfitting to simple geometries, and (3) physics-based action masking to filter physically impossible paths before model evaluation.
AI.result: Experimental validation demonstrates substantial speedups: up to 10× faster on GPU and 1000× faster on CPU compared to exhaustive search. The model maintains high coverage accuracy and successfully uncovers complex propagation paths.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully overcomes the computational bottleneck of ray tracing by replacing exhaustive search with efficient, accurate intelligent sampling. This enables real-time and large-scale radio propagation modeling applications that were previously impractical with traditional ray tracing methods.

title: MLRecon: Robust Markerless Freehand 3D Ultrasound Reconstruction via Coarse-to-Fine Pose Estimation
summary: Freehand 3D ultrasound (US) reconstruction promises volumetric imaging with the flexibility of standard 2D probes, yet existing tracking paradigms face a restrictive trilemma: marker-based systems demand prohibitive costs, inside-out methods require intrusive sensor attachment, and sensorless approaches suffer from severe cumulative drift. To overcome these limitations, we present MLRecon, a robust markerless 3D US reconstruction framework delivering drift-resilient 6D probe pose tracking using a single commodity RGB-D camera. Leveraging the generalization power of vision foundation models, our pipeline enables continuous markerless tracking of the probe, augmented by a vision-guided divergence detector that autonomously monitors tracking integrity and triggers failure recovery to ensure uninterrupted scanning. Crucially, we further propose a dual-stage pose refinement network that explicitly disentangles high-frequency jitter from low-frequency bias, effectively denoising the trajectory while maintaining the kinematic fidelity of operator maneuvers. Experiments demonstrate that MLRecon significantly outperforms competing sensorless and sensor-aided methods, achieving average position errors as low as 0.88 mm on complex trajectories and yielding high-quality 3D reconstructions with sub-millimeter mean surface accuracy. This establishes a new benchmark for low-cost, accessible volumetric US imaging in resource-limited clinical settings.

AI.tldr: MLRecon is a markerless, drift-resilient 3D ultrasound reconstruction framework that uses a single commodity RGB-D camera with a vision foundation model for probe tracking, a divergence detector for failure recovery, and a dual-stage network to separate high-frequency jitter from low-frequency bias, achieving sub-millimeter accuracy and outperforming existing sensorless and sensor-aided methods.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D ultrasound reconstruction methods face a restrictive trilemma: marker-based systems are costly, inside-out methods require intrusive sensors, and sensorless approaches suffer from severe cumulative drift, limiting their applicability especially in resource-limited settings.
AI.method: MLRecon uses a single commodity RGB-D camera and vision foundation models for continuous markerless probe tracking, augmented by a vision-guided divergence detector for failure recovery and a dual-stage pose refinement network that disentangles high-frequency jitter from low-frequency bias to denoise trajectories while preserving kinematic fidelity.
AI.result: MLRecon achieves average position errors as low as 0.88 mm on complex trajectories and sub-millimeter mean surface accuracy, significantly outperforming competing sensorless and sensor-aided methods.
AI.conclusion: MLRecon establishes a new benchmark for low-cost, accessible volumetric ultrasound imaging in resource-limited clinical settings by delivering drift-resilient, high-quality 3D reconstructions without the need for expensive markers or invasive sensors.

title: FreeGNN: Continual Source-Free Graph Neural Network Adaptation for Renewable Energy Forecasting
summary: Accurate forecasting of renewable energy generation is essential for efficient grid management and sustainable power planning. However, traditional supervised models often require access to labeled data from the target site, which may be unavailable due to privacy, cost, or logistical constraints. In this work, we propose FreeGNN, a Continual Source-Free Graph Domain Adaptation framework that enables adaptive forecasting on unseen renewable energy sites without requiring source data or target labels. Our approach integrates a spatio-temporal Graph Neural Network (GNN) backbone with a teacher--student strategy, a memory replay mechanism to mitigate catastrophic forgetting, graph-based regularization to preserve spatial correlations, and a drift-aware weighting scheme to dynamically adjust adaptation strength during streaming updates. This combination allows the model to continuously adapt to non-stationary environmental conditions while maintaining robustness and stability. We conduct extensive experiments on three real-world datasets: GEFCom2012, Solar PV, and Wind SCADA, encompassing multiple sites, temporal resolutions, and meteorological features. The ablation study confirms that each component memory, graph regularization, drift-aware adaptation, and teacher--student strategy contributes significantly to overall performance. The experiments show that FreeGNN achieves an MAE of 5.237 and an RMSE of 7.123 on the GEFCom dataset, an MAE of 1.107 and an RMSE of 1.512 on the Solar PV dataset, and an MAE of 0.382 and an RMSE of 0.523 on the Wind SCADA dataset. These results demonstrate its ability to achieve accurate and robust forecasts in a source-free, continual learning setting, highlighting its potential for real-world deployment in adaptive renewable energy systems. For reproducibility, implementation details are available at: https://github.com/AraoufBh/FreeGNN.

AI.tldr: FreeGNN is a Continual Source-Free Graph Domain Adaptation framework for renewable energy forecasting that adapts to unseen sites without requiring source data or target labels.
AI.motivation: Accurate renewable energy generation forecasting is essential for grid management, but traditional supervised models require labeled data from target sites, which is often unavailable due to privacy, cost, or logistical constraints.
AI.method: FreeGNN integrates a spatio-temporal Graph Neural Network (GNN) backbone with a teacher-student strategy, memory replay mechanism, graph-based regularization, and a drift-aware weighting scheme to enable continual adaptation to non-stationary conditions without source data or target labels.
AI.result: Experiments on GEFCom2012, Solar PV, and Wind SCADA datasets demonstrate strong performance: MAE of 5.237 and RMSE of 7.123 on GEFCom; MAE of 1.107 and RMSE of 1.512 on Solar PV; MAE of 0.382 and RMSE of 0.523 on Wind SCADA. Ablation studies confirm each component contributes significantly to performance.
AI.conclusion: FreeGNN achieves accurate and robust forecasts in a source-free, continual learning setting, demonstrating its potential for real-world deployment in adaptive renewable energy systems.

title: Let Your Image Move with Your Motion! -- Implicit Multi-Object Multi-Motion Transfer
summary: Motion transfer has emerged as a promising direction for controllable video generation, yet existing methods largely focus on single-object scenarios and struggle when multiple objects require distinct motion patterns. In this work, we present FlexiMMT, the first implicit image-to-video (I2V) motion transfer framework that explicitly enables multi-object, multi-motion transfer. Given a static multi-object image and multiple reference videos, FlexiMMT independently extracts motion representations and accurately assigns them to different objects, supporting flexible recombination and arbitrary motion-to-object mappings. To address the core challenge of cross-object motion entanglement, we introduce a Motion Decoupled Mask Attention Mechanism that uses object-specific masks to constrain attention, ensuring that motion and text tokens only influence their designated regions. We further propose a Differentiated Mask Propagation Mechanism that derives object-specific masks directly from diffusion attention and progressively propagates them across frames efficiently. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FlexiMMT achieves precise, compositional, and state-of-the-art performance in I2V-based multi-object multi-motion transfer.

AI.tldr: FlexiMMT is the first implicit image-to-video motion transfer framework that enables multi-object, multi-motion transfer with state-of-the-art performance through motion decoupling and differentiated mask propagation mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Existing motion transfer methods focus on single-object scenarios and struggle when multiple objects require distinct motion patterns in controllable video generation.
AI.method: FlexiMMT introduces: (1) independent extraction of motion representations from multiple reference videos; (2) accurate assignment of motion representations to different objects; (3) flexible recombination and arbitrary motion-to-object mappings; (4) Motion Decoupled Mask Attention Mechanism to constrain attention using object-specific masks; (5) Differentiated Mask Propagation Mechanism that derives object-specific masks from diffusion attention and progressively propagates them across frames.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that FlexiMMT achieves precise, compositional, and state-of-the-art performance in I2V-based multi-object multi-motion transfer.
AI.conclusion: FlexiMMT successfully addresses the core challenge of cross-object motion entanglement in multi-object video generation, enabling controllable and flexible motion transfer for complex scenes.

title: Dr.Occ: Depth- and Region-Guided 3D Occupancy from Surround-View Cameras for Autonomous Driving
summary: 3D semantic occupancy prediction is crucial for autonomous driving perception, offering comprehensive geometric scene understanding and semantic recognition. However, existing methods struggle with geometric misalignment in view transformation due to the lack of pixel-level accurate depth estimation, and severe spatial class imbalance where semantic categories exhibit strong spatial anisotropy. To address these challenges, we propose Dr.Occ, a depth- and region-guided occupancy prediction framework. Specifically, we introduce a depth-guided 2D-to-3D View Transformer (D$^2$-VFormer) that effectively leverages high-quality dense depth cues from MoGe-2 to construct reliable geometric priors, thereby enabling precise geometric alignment of voxel features. Moreover, inspired by the Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) framework, we propose a region-guided Expert Transformer (R/R$^2$-EFormer) that adaptively allocates region-specific experts to focus on different spatial regions, effectively addressing spatial semantic variations. Thus, the two components make complementary contributions: depth guidance ensures geometric alignment, while region experts enhance semantic learning. Experiments on the Occ3D-nuScenes benchmark demonstrate that \textbf{Dr.Occ} improves the strong baseline BEVDet4D by 7.43\% mIoU and 3.09\% IoU under the full vision-only setting.

AI.tldr: Dr.Occ is a depth- and region-guided occupancy prediction framework for 3D semantic scene understanding in autonomous driving, using a depth-guided View Transformer for geometric alignment and region-guided Expert Transformers for addressing spatial class imbalance.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D semantic occupancy prediction methods face two key challenges: (1) geometric misalignment in view transformation due to lack of pixel-level accurate depth estimation, and (2) severe spatial class imbalance where semantic categories exhibit strong spatial anisotropy.
AI.method: Dr.Occ consists of two complementary components: (1) D²-VFormer (Depth-guided 2D-to-3D View Transformer) that leverages high-quality dense depth cues from MoGe-2 to construct reliable geometric priors for precise voxel feature alignment, and (2) R²-EFormer (Region-guided Expert Transformer) inspired by the Mixture-of-Experts framework that adaptively allocates region-specific experts to focus on different spatial regions for enhanced semantic learning.
AI.result: On the Occ3D-nuScenes benchmark, Dr.Occ improves the strong baseline BEVDet4D by 7.43% mIoU and 3.09% IoU under the full vision-only setting.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses geometric misalignment and spatial class imbalance through its dual-component design—depth guidance ensures geometric precision while region experts enhance semantic representation—demonstrating significant performance gains over existing methods.

title: Reasoning as Gradient: Scaling MLE Agents Beyond Tree Search
summary: LLM-based agents for machine learning engineering (MLE) predominantly rely on tree search, a form of gradient-free optimization that uses scalar validation scores to rank candidates. As LLM reasoning capabilities improve, exhaustive enumeration becomes increasingly inefficient compared to directed updates, analogous to how accurate gradients enable efficient descent over random search. We introduce \textsc{Gome}, an MLE agent that operationalizes gradient-based optimization. \textsc{Gome} maps structured diagnostic reasoning to gradient computation, success memory to momentum, and multi-trace execution to distributed optimization. Under a closed-world protocol that isolates architectural effects from external knowledge, \textsc{Gome} achieves a state-of-the-art 35.1\% any-medal rate on MLE-Bench with a restricted 12-hour budget on a single V100 GPU. Scaling experiments across 10 models reveal a critical crossover: with weaker models, tree search retains advantages by compensating for unreliable reasoning through exhaustive exploration; as reasoning capability strengthens, gradient-based optimization progressively outperforms, with the gap widening at frontier-tier models. Given the rapid advancement of reasoning-oriented LLMs, this positions gradient-based optimization as an increasingly favorable paradigm. We release our codebase and GPT-5 traces.

AI.tldr: Gome introduces gradient-based optimization for LLM-based MLE agents, achieving state-of-the-art 35.1% any-medal rate on MLE-Bench and demonstrating superior performance over tree search as LLM reasoning capabilities improve.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based MLE agents rely on tree search, which is inefficient for improved LLM reasoning capabilities. Gradient-based optimization, analogous to how accurate gradients enable efficient descent compared to random search, is increasingly favorable as reasoning-oriented LLMs advance rapidly.
AI.method: Gome operationalizes gradient-based optimization by mapping structured diagnostic reasoning to gradient computation, success memory to momentum, and multi-trace execution to distributed optimization. A closed-world protocol isolates architectural effects from external knowledge to ensure fair evaluation.
AI.result: Gome achieves a state-of-the-art 35.1% any-medal rate on MLE-Bench with a restricted 12-hour budget on a single V100 GPU. Scaling experiments across 10 models reveal that with weaker models, tree search retains advantages, but as reasoning capability strengthens, gradient-based optimization progressively outperforms, with the gap widening at frontier-tier models.
AI.conclusion: Given the rapid advancement of reasoning-oriented LLMs, gradient-based optimization positions as an increasingly favorable paradigm for LLM-based MLE agents, outperforming tree search for capable reasoning models.

title: GeodesicNVS: Probability Density Geodesic Flow Matching for Novel View Synthesis
summary: Recent advances in generative modeling have substantially enhanced novel view synthesis, yet maintaining consistency across viewpoints remains challenging. Diffusion-based models rely on stochastic noise-to-data transitions, which obscure deterministic structures and yield inconsistent view predictions. We propose a Data-to-Data Flow Matching framework that learns deterministic transformations directly between paired views, enhancing view-consistent synthesis through explicit data coupling. To further enhance geometric coherence, we introduce Probability Density Geodesic Flow Matching (PDG-FM), which constrains flow trajectories using geodesic interpolants derived from probability density metrics of pretrained diffusion models. Such alignment with high-density regions of the data manifold promotes more realistic interpolants between samples. Empirically, our method surpasses diffusion-based NVS baselines, demonstrating improved structural coherence and smoother transitions across views. These results highlight the advantages of incorporating data-dependent geometric regularization into deterministic flow matching for consistent novel view generation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Data-to-Data Flow Matching framework and Probability Density Geodesic Flow Matching (PDG-FM) for novel view synthesis that maintains view consistency by learning deterministic transformations between paired views, constrained by geodesic interpolants from probability density metrics of pretrained diffusion models.
AI.motivation: Recent advances in generative modeling have improved novel view synthesis, but maintaining consistency across viewpoints remains challenging. Diffusion-based models rely on stochastic noise-to-data transitions that obscure deterministic structures and yield inconsistent view predictions.
AI.method: The authors propose two main contributions: (1) A Data-to-Data Flow Matching framework that learns deterministic transformations directly between paired views to enhance view-consistent synthesis through explicit data coupling, and (2) Probability Density Geodesic Flow Matching (PDG-FM), which constrains flow trajectories using geodesic interpolants derived from probability density metrics of pretrained diffusion models to align with high-density regions of the data manifold.
AI.result: Empirical results demonstrate that the proposed method surpasses diffusion-based novel view synthesis (NVS) baselines, showing improved structural coherence and smoother transitions across views.
AI.conclusion: The results highlight the advantages of incorporating data-dependent geometric regularization into deterministic flow matching for consistent novel view generation, suggesting that aligning flow trajectories with the data manifold's high-density regions promotes more realistic interpolants between samples.

title: Implementation of Licensed Plate Detection and Noise Removal in Image Processing
comment: 13 pages. This is the author's version, accepted manuscript
summary: Car license plate recognition system is an image processing technology used to identify vehicles by capturing their Car License Plates. The car license plate recognition technology is also known as automatic number-plate recognition, automatic vehicle identification, car license plate recognition or optical character recognition for cars. In Malaysia, as the number of vehicle is increasing rapidly nowadays, a pretty great number of vehicle on the road has brought about the considerable demands of car license plate recognition system. Car license plate recognition system can be implemented in electronic parking payment system, highway toll-fee system, traffic surveillance system and as police enforcement tools. Additionally, car license plate recognition system technology also has potential to be combined with various techniques in other different fields like biology, aerospace and so on to achieve the goal of solving some specialized problems.

AI.tldr: This abstract introduces car license plate recognition (LPR) systems, which are image processing technologies used to identify vehicles by capturing license plates. These systems are increasingly important in Malaysia due to rapid vehicle growth and have applications in parking payments, toll systems, traffic surveillance, and law enforcement.
AI.motivation: The increasing number of vehicles in Malaysia has created significant demand for car license plate recognition systems to manage traffic and enforcement challenges.
AI.method: The abstract presents a general overview of LPR technology without specifying particular technical methods or algorithms used.
AI.result: The abstract states that LPR systems have demonstrated potential in multiple applications including electronic parking payments, highway toll collection, traffic surveillance, and police enforcement.
AI.conclusion: LPR technology shows promise for integration with other fields such as biology and aerospace to solve specialized problems, though specific implementation details are not provided.

title: DynaMoE: Dynamic Token-Level Expert Activation with Layer-Wise Adaptive Capacity for Mixture-of-Experts Neural Networks
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures have emerged as a powerful paradigm for scaling neural networks while maintaining computational efficiency. However, standard MoE implementations rely on two rigid design assumptions: (1) fixed Top-K routing where exactly K experts are activated per token, and (2) uniform expert allocation across all layers. This paper introduces DynaMoE, a novel MoE framework that relaxes both constraints through dynamic token-level expert activation and layer-wise adaptive capacity allocation. DynaMoE introduces a principled routing mechanism where the number of active experts per token varies based on input complexity. Concurrently, the framework implements six distinct scheduling strategies for distributing expert capacity across network depth, including descending, ascending, pyramid, and wave patterns. We theoretically analyze the expressivity gains of dynamic routing and derive bounds on computational efficiency. Through extensive experiments on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10 (image classification), and Recycling-the-Web (language modeling) across multiple model scales, we demonstrate that DynaMoE achieves superior parameter efficiency compared to static baselines. Our key finding is that optimal expert schedules are task- and scale-dependent: descending schedules (concentrating capacity in early layers) outperform uniform baselines on image classification. For language modeling, optimal schedules vary by model size, descending for Tiny, ascending for Small, and uniform for Medium. Furthermore, dynamic routing reduces gradient variance during training, leading to improved convergence stability. DynaMoE establishes a new framework for adaptive computation in neural networks, providing principled guidance for MoE architecture design.

AI.tldr: DynaMoE is a novel Mixture-of-Experts framework that relaxes rigid design assumptions through dynamic token-level expert activation and layer-wise adaptive capacity allocation with six scheduling strategies (descending, ascending, pyramid, and wave patterns).
AI.motivation: Standard MoE implementations rely on two rigid design assumptions: (1) fixed Top-K routing where exactly K experts are activated per token, and (2) uniform expert allocation across all layers.
AI.method: DynaMoE introduces dynamic token-level expert activation where the number of active experts per token varies based on input complexity, and six distinct scheduling strategies for distributing expert capacity across network depth. The framework includes a principled routing mechanism and theoretical analysis of expressivity gains and computational efficiency bounds.
AI.result: DynaMoE achieves superior parameter efficiency compared to static baselines. Key findings: (1) descending schedules outperform uniform baselines on image classification, (2) for language modeling, optimal schedules vary by model size (descending for Tiny, ascending for Small, uniform for Medium), and (3) dynamic routing reduces gradient variance during training, leading to improved convergence stability. Experiments conducted on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, and Recycling-the-Web.
AI.conclusion: DynaMoE establishes a new framework for adaptive computation in neural networks, providing principled guidance for MoE architecture design that accommodates task- and scale-dependent optimal configurations.

title: RaUF: Learning the Spatial Uncertainty Field of Radar
