summary: Millimeter-wave radar offers unique advantages in adverse weather but suffers from low spatial fidelity, severe azimuth ambiguity, and clutter-induced spurious returns. Existing methods mainly focus on improving spatial perception effectiveness via coarse-to-fine cross-modal supervision, yet often overlook the ambiguous feature-to-label mapping, which may lead to ill-posed geometric inference and pose fundamental challenges to downstream perception tasks. In this work, we propose RaUF, a spatial uncertainty field learning framework that models radar measurements through their physically grounded anisotropic properties. To resolve conflicting feature-to-label mapping, we design an anisotropic probabilistic model that learns fine-grained uncertainty. To further enhance reliability, we propose a Bidirectional Domain Attention mechanism that exploits the mutual complementarity between spatial structure and Doppler consistency, effectively suppressing spurious or multipath-induced reflections. Extensive experiments on public benchmarks and real-world datasets demonstrate that RaUF delivers highly reliable spatial detections with well-calibrated uncertainty. Moreover, downstream case studies further validate the enhanced reliability and scalability of RaUF under challenging real-world driving scenarios.

AI.tldr: RaUF is a spatial uncertainty field learning framework for millimeter-wave radar that models anisotropic properties to resolve ambiguous feature-to-label mapping and suppress spurious returns, delivering reliable detections with well-calibrated uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Millimeter-wave radar excels in adverse weather but suffers from low spatial fidelity, severe azimuth ambiguity, and clutter-induced spurious returns. Existing methods focus on coarse-to-fine cross-modal supervision but overlook ambiguous feature-to-label mapping, leading to ill-posed geometric inference and challenges for downstream perception tasks.
AI.method: RaUF models radar measurements through physically grounded anisotropic properties and employs an anisotropic probabilistic model to learn fine-grained uncertainty. A Bidirectional Domain Attention mechanism exploits mutual complementarity between spatial structure and Doppler consistency to suppress spurious or multipath-induced reflections.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on public benchmarks and real-world datasets demonstrate RaUF delivers highly reliable spatial detections with well-calibrated uncertainty. Downstream case studies validate enhanced reliability and scalability under challenging real-world driving scenarios.
AI.conclusion: RaUF effectively addresses the fundamental challenges of millimeter-wave radar by resolving conflicting feature-to-label mapping and providing reliable spatial detections with calibrated uncertainty, significantly improving downstream perception task performance.

title: Content-Aware Frequency Encoding for Implicit Neural Representations with Fourier-Chebyshev Features
summary: Implicit Neural Representations (INRs) have emerged as a powerful paradigm for various signal processing tasks, but their inherent spectral bias limits the ability to capture high-frequency details. Existing methods partially mitigate this issue by using Fourier-based features, which usually rely on fixed frequency bases. This forces multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) to inefficiently compose the required frequencies, thereby constraining their representational capacity. To address this limitation, we propose Content-Aware Frequency Encoding (CAFE), which builds upon Fourier features through multiple parallel linear layers combined via a Hadamard product. CAFE can explicitly and efficiently synthesize a broader range of frequency bases, while the learned weights enable the selection of task-relevant frequencies. Furthermore, we extend this framework to CAFE+, which incorporates Chebyshev features as a complementary component to Fourier bases. This combination provides a stronger and more stable frequency representation. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks validate the effectiveness and efficiency of our approach, consistently achieving superior performance over existing methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/JunboKe0619/CAFE.

AI.tldr: CAFE is a new approach for Implicit Neural Representations that uses Content-Aware Frequency Encoding to overcome spectral bias and improve representation of high-frequency details.
AI.motivation: Existing INR methods suffer from spectral bias and use Fourier features with fixed frequency bases, forcing MLPs to inefficiently compose required frequencies, limiting representational capacity.
AI.method: CAFE builds upon Fourier features using multiple parallel linear layers combined via Hadamard product, enabling explicit and efficient synthesis of a broader range of frequency bases. CAFE+ extends this with Chebyshev features as a complementary component.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks show CAFE and CAFE+ consistently achieve superior performance and efficiency over existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CAFE framework effectively addresses spectral bias in INRs and provides stronger frequency representations, validated through extensive experiments.

title: Decentralized Federated Learning by Partial Message Exchange
summary: Decentralized federated learning (DFL) has emerged as a transformative server-free paradigm that enables collaborative learning over large-scale heterogeneous networks. However, it continues to face fundamental challenges, including data heterogeneity, restrictive assumptions for theoretical analysis, and degraded convergence when standard communication- or privacyenhancing techniques are applied. To overcome these drawbacks, this paper develops a novel algorithm, PaME (DFL by Partial Message Exchange). The central principle is to allow only randomly selected sparse coordinates to be exchanged between two neighbor nodes. Consequently, PaME achieves substantial reductions in communication costs while still preserving a high level of privacy, without sacrificing accuracy. Moreover, grounded in rigorous analysis, the algorithm is shown to converge at a linear rate under the gradient to be locally Lipschitz continuous and the communication matrix to be doubly stochastic. These two mild assumptions not only dispense with many restrictive conditions commonly imposed by existing DFL methods but also enables PaME to effectively address data heterogeneity. Furthermore, comprehensive numerical experiments demonstrate its superior performance compared with several representative decentralized learning algorithms.

AI.tldr: PaME (DFL by Partial Message Exchange) is a decentralized federated learning algorithm that enables collaborative learning over large-scale heterogeneous networks by exchanging only randomly selected sparse coordinates between neighbor nodes, achieving reduced communication costs while preserving privacy and accuracy.
AI.motivation: Decentralized federated learning (DFL) faces fundamental challenges: data heterogeneity, restrictive assumptions for theoretical analysis, and degraded convergence when applying standard communication- or privacy-enhancing techniques.
AI.method: PaME allows only randomly selected sparse coordinates to be exchanged between two neighbor nodes, grounded in rigorous analysis showing linear convergence under mild assumptions: locally Lipschitz continuous gradient and doubly stochastic communication matrix.
AI.result: PaME achieves substantial reductions in communication costs while preserving high privacy and accuracy, converges at a linear rate, effectively addresses data heterogeneity, and outperforms several representative decentralized learning algorithms in comprehensive numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: The algorithm dispenses with restrictive conditions commonly imposed by existing DFL methods and demonstrates superior performance compared to representative decentralized learning algorithms, offering a practical solution to the challenges of large-scale heterogeneous networks in decentralized federated learning.

title: CA-AFP: Cluster-Aware Adaptive Federated Pruning
summary:
Federated Learning (FL) faces major challenges in real-world deployments due to statistical heterogeneity across clients and system heterogeneity arising from resource-constrained devices. While clustering-based approaches mitigate statistical heterogeneity and pruning techniques improve memory and communication efficiency, these strategies are typically studied in isolation.
  We propose CA-AFP, a unified framework that jointly addresses both challenges by performing cluster-specific model pruning. In CA-AFP, clients are first grouped into clusters, and a separate model for each cluster is adaptively pruned during training. The framework introduces two key innovations: (1) a cluster-aware importance scoring mechanism that combines weight magnitude, intra-cluster coherence, and gradient consistency to identify parameters for pruning, and (2) an iterative pruning schedule that progressively removes parameters while enabling model self-healing through weight regrowth.
  We evaluate CA-AFP on two widely used human activity recognition benchmarks, UCI HAR and WISDM, under natural user-based federated partitions. Experimental results demonstrate that CA-AFP achieves a favorable balance between predictive accuracy, inter-client fairness, and communication efficiency. Compared to pruning-based baselines, CA-AFP consistently improves accuracy and lower performance disparity across clients with limited fine-tuning, while requiring substantially less communication than dense clustering-based methods. It also shows robustness to different Non-IID levels of data. Finally, ablation studies analyze the impact of clustering, pruning schedules and scoring mechanism offering practical insights into the design of efficient and adaptive FL systems.

AI.tldr: CA-AFP is a unified Federated Learning framework that jointly addresses statistical heterogeneity (data distribution differences) and system heterogeneity (resource constraints) through cluster-specific model pruning with a cluster-aware importance scoring mechanism and iterative pruning schedule, achieving better accuracy, fairness, and communication efficiency compared to standalone approaches.
AI.motivation: Federated Learning faces major challenges in real-world deployments due to (1) statistical heterogeneity across clients with different data distributions and (2) system heterogeneity from resource-constrained devices. Existing solutions address these challenges in isolation: clustering handles statistical heterogeneity while pruning improves memory and communication efficiency.
AI.method: CA-AFP introduces two key innovations: (1) A cluster-aware importance scoring mechanism combining weight magnitude, intra-cluster coherence, and gradient consistency to identify parameters for pruning; (2) An iterative pruning schedule that progressively removes parameters while enabling model self-healing through weight regrowth. The framework first groups clients into clusters and adaptively prunes separate models for each cluster during training.
AI.result: On UCI HAR and WISDM benchmarks, CA-AFP achieves a favorable balance between predictive accuracy, inter-client fairness, and communication efficiency. Compared to pruning-based baselines, it consistently improves accuracy and reduces performance disparity across clients with limited fine-tuning while requiring substantially less communication than dense clustering-based methods. It demonstrates robustness to different Non-IID levels of data.
AI.conclusion: Ablation studies reveal the impact of clustering, pruning schedules, and the scoring mechanism, offering practical insights for designing efficient and adaptive FL systems that can handle both data distribution and system heterogeneity simultaneously.

title: Reparameterized Tensor Ring Functional Decomposition for Multi-Dimensional Data Recovery
comment: 22 pages, 18 figures, 12 tables. Accepted by CVPR 2026
summary: Tensor Ring (TR) decomposition is a powerful tool for high-order data modeling, but is inherently restricted to discrete forms defined on fixed meshgrids. In this work, we propose a TR functional decomposition for both meshgrid and non-meshgrid data, where factors are parameterized by Implicit Neural Representations (INRs). However, optimizing this continuous framework to capture fine-scale details is intrinsically difficult. Through a frequency-domain analysis, we demonstrate that the spectral structure of TR factors determines the frequency composition of the reconstructed tensor and limits the high-frequency modeling capacity. To mitigate this, we propose a reparameterized TR functional decomposition, in which each TR factor is a structured combination of a learnable latent tensor and a fixed basis. This reparameterization is theoretically shown to improve the training dynamics of TR factor learning. We further derive a principled initialization scheme for the fixed basis and prove the Lipschitz continuity of our proposed model. Extensive experiments on image inpainting, denoising, super-resolution, and point cloud recovery demonstrate that our method achieves consistently superior performance over existing approaches. Code is available at https://github.com/YangyangXu2002/RepTRFD.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a reparameterized Tensor Ring (TR) functional decomposition using Implicit Neural Representations (INRs) to enable continuous modeling of both meshgrid and non-meshgrid data, with theoretical and practical improvements in high-frequency modeling capacity.
AI.motivation: Standard Tensor Ring (TR) decomposition is inherently restricted to discrete forms defined on fixed meshgrids. When extending TR to continuous frameworks via Implicit Neural Representations (INRs), optimizing the continuous framework to capture fine-scale details becomes intrinsically difficult due to the spectral structure of TR factors limiting high-frequency modeling capacity.
AI.method: The authors propose a reparameterized TR functional decomposition where each TR factor is a structured combination of a learnable latent tensor and a fixed basis. Key contributions include: (1) a frequency-domain analysis demonstrating how TR factor spectral structure determines frequency composition, (2) theoretical proof that reparameterization improves training dynamics, (3) a principled initialization scheme for the fixed basis, and (4) proof of Lipschitz continuity of the proposed model.
AI.result: Experiments on image inpainting, denoising, super-resolution, and point cloud recovery demonstrate that the proposed method achieves consistently superior performance over existing approaches. The reparameterization enables better high-frequency modeling and more stable training dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The reparameterized TR functional decomposition with INR parameterization effectively extends TR decomposition to continuous domains while overcoming the high-frequency modeling limitations. The method achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple computer vision and graphics tasks, with code available at https://github.com/YangyangXu2002/RepTRFD.

title: Discrete World Models via Regularization
summary: World models aim to capture the states and dynamics of an environment in a compact latent space. Moreover, using Boolean state representations is particularly useful for search heuristics and symbolic reasoning and planning. Existing approaches keep latents informative via decoder-based reconstruction, or instead via contrastive or reward signals. In this work, we introduce Discrete World Models via Regularization (DWMR): a reconstruction-free and contrastive-free method for unsupervised Boolean world-model learning. In particular, we introduce a novel world-modeling loss that couples latent prediction with specialized regularizers. Such regularizers maximize the entropy and independence of the representation bits through variance, correlation, and coskewness penalties, while simultaneously enforcing a locality prior for sparse action changes. To enable effective optimization, we also introduce a novel training scheme improving robustness to discrete roll-outs. Experiments on two benchmarks with underlying combinatorial structure show that DWMR learns more accurate representations and transitions than reconstruction-based alternatives. Finally, DWMR can also be paired with an auxiliary reconstruction decoder, and this combination yields additional gains.

AI.tldr: A reconstruction-free and contrastive-free method for unsupervised Boolean world-model learning that uses specialized regularizers (variance, correlation, coskewness penalties, and locality prior) to maximize entropy and independence of representation bits while enforcing sparse action changes.
AI.motivation: World models aim to capture environment states and dynamics in a compact latent space. Boolean state representations are valuable for search heuristics and symbolic reasoning/planning. Existing approaches rely on decoder-based reconstruction, contrastive signals, or reward signals to keep latents informative.
AI.method: Introduces Discrete World Models via Regularization (DWMR): a novel world-modeling loss coupling latent prediction with specialized regularizers. The method maximizes entropy and independence through variance, correlation, and coskewness penalties while enforcing locality prior for sparse action changes. Also introduces a novel training scheme improving robustness to discrete roll-outs and can be paired with auxiliary reconstruction decoder.
AI.result: Experiments on two benchmarks with combinatorial structure demonstrate that DWMR learns more accurate representations and transitions than reconstruction-based alternatives. When paired with auxiliary reconstruction decoder, additional gains are achieved.
AI.conclusion: DWMR effectively learns Boolean world models without reconstruction or contrastive signals, outperforming reconstruction-based methods in accuracy and achieving benefits when combined with auxiliary reconstruction decoder.

title: From Intuition to Investigation: A Tool-Augmented Reasoning MLLM Framework for Generalizable Face Anti-Spoofing
summary: Face recognition remains vulnerable to presentation attacks, calling for robust Face Anti-Spoofing (FAS) solutions. Recent MLLM-based FAS methods reformulate the binary classification task as the generation of brief textual descriptions to improve cross-domain generalization. However, their generalizability is still limited, as such descriptions mainly capture intuitive semantic cues (e.g., mask contours) while struggling to perceive fine-grained visual patterns. To address this limitation, we incorporate external visual tools into MLLMs to encourage deeper investigation of subtle spoof clues. Specifically, we propose the Tool-Augmented Reasoning FAS (TAR-FAS) framework, which reformulates the FAS task as a Chain-of-Thought with Visual Tools (CoT-VT) paradigm, allowing MLLMs to begin with intuitive observations and adaptively invoke external visual tools for fine-grained investigation. To this end, we design a tool-augmented data annotation pipeline and construct the ToolFAS-16K dataset, which contains multi-turn tool-use reasoning trajectories. Furthermore, we introduce a tool-aware FAS training pipeline, where Diverse-Tool Group Relative Policy Optimization (DT-GRPO) enables the model to autonomously learn efficient tool use. Extensive experiments under a challenging one-to-eleven cross-domain protocol demonstrate that TAR-FAS achieves SOTA performance while providing fine-grained visual investigation for trustworthy spoof detection.

AI.tldr: TAR-FAS is a framework that enhances multimodal LLMs with external visual tools and chain-of-thought reasoning for more robust face anti-spoofing, achieving SOTA performance through adaptive tool invocation and fine-grained visual analysis.
AI.motivation: Recent MLLM-based FAS methods have limited generalizability because their textual descriptions mainly capture intuitive semantic cues (e.g., mask contours) while struggling to perceive fine-grained visual patterns, leading to vulnerabilities in presentation attack detection.
AI.method: The authors propose the Tool-Augmented Reasoning FAS (TAR-FAS) framework reformulating FAS as a Chain-of-Thought with Visual Tools (CoT-VT) paradigm, where MLLMs start with intuitive observations and adaptively invoke external visual tools for fine-grained investigation. They also design a tool-augmented data annotation pipeline, construct the ToolFAS-16K dataset with multi-turn tool-use reasoning trajectories, and introduce a tool-aware training pipeline using Diverse-Tool Group Relative Policy Optimization (DT-GRPO).
AI.result: Under a challenging one-to-eleven cross-domain protocol, TAR-FAS achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance while providing fine-grained visual investigation for trustworthy spoof detection.
AI.conclusion: By incorporating external visual tools and chain-of-thought reasoning into MLLMs, TAR-FAS effectively addresses the limitation of previous methods in capturing fine-grained visual patterns, achieving superior performance in face anti-spoofing tasks.

title: Practical Deep Heteroskedastic Regression
summary: Uncertainty quantification (UQ) in deep learning regression is of wide interest, as it supports critical applications including sequential decision making and risk-sensitive tasks. In heteroskedastic regression, where the uncertainty of the target depends on the input, a common approach is to train a neural network that parameterizes the mean and the variance of the predictive distribution. Still, training deep heteroskedastic regression models poses practical challenges in the trade-off between uncertainty quantification and mean prediction, such as optimization difficulties, representation collapse, and variance overfitting. In this work we identify previously undiscussed fallacies and propose a simple and efficient procedure that addresses these challenges jointly by post-hoc fitting a variance model across the intermediate layers of a pretrained network on a hold-out dataset. We demonstrate that our method achieves on-par or state-of-the-art uncertainty quantification on several molecular graph datasets, without compromising mean prediction accuracy and remaining cheap to use at prediction time.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses practical challenges in deep heteroskedastic regression, proposing a post-hoc variance model fitting procedure across intermediate layers of pretrained networks that achieves state-of-the-art uncertainty quantification without compromising mean prediction accuracy.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty quantification in deep learning regression is critical for sequential decision making and risk-sensitive tasks. However, training deep heteroskedastic regression models faces practical challenges including optimization difficulties, representation collapse, and variance overfitting, which create a trade-off between uncertainty quantification and mean prediction performance.
AI.method: The authors identify previously undiscussed fallacies and propose a simple, efficient procedure that jointly addresses these challenges by post-hoc fitting a variance model across the intermediate layers of a pretrained network using a hold-out dataset.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves on-par or state-of-the-art uncertainty quantification on several molecular graph datasets, without compromising mean prediction accuracy and remaining computationally cheap at prediction time.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that post-hoc variance modeling across network layers effectively resolves the trade-off between uncertainty quantification and mean prediction in deep heteroskedastic regression, offering a practical solution that maintains both prediction accuracy and uncertainty reliability.

title: MM-DeepResearch: A Simple and Effective Multimodal Agentic Search Baseline
summary: We aim to develop a multimodal research agent capable of explicit reasoning and planning, multi-tool invocation, and cross-modal information synthesis, enabling it to conduct deep research tasks. However, we observe three main challenges in developing such agents: (1) scarcity of search-intensive multimodal QA data, (2) lack of effective search trajectories, and (3) prohibitive cost of training with online search APIs. To tackle them, we first propose Hyper-Search, a hypergraph-based QA generation method that models and connects visual and textual nodes within and across modalities, enabling to generate search-intensive multimodal QA pairs that require invoking various search tools to solve. Second, we introduce DR-TTS, which first decomposes search-involved tasks into several categories according to search tool types, and respectively optimize specialized search tool experts for each tool. It then recomposes tool experts to jointly explore search trajectories via tree search, producing trajectories that successfully solve complex tasks using various search tools. Third, we build an offline search engine supporting multiple search tools, enabling agentic reinforcement learning without using costly online search APIs. With the three designs, we develop MM-DeepResearch, a powerful multimodal deep research agent, and extensive results shows its superiority across benchmarks. Code is available at https://github.com/HJYao00/MM-DeepResearch

AI.tldr: MM-DeepResearch is a multimodal deep research agent that can conduct deep research tasks with explicit reasoning, planning, multi-tool invocation, and cross-modal information synthesis.
AI.motivation: Developing a multimodal research agent faces three main challenges: (1) scarcity of search-intensive multimodal QA data, (2) lack of effective search trajectories, and (3) prohibitive cost of training with online search APIs.
AI.method: The paper proposes three key contributions: (1) Hyper-Search - a hypergraph-based QA generation method that models and connects visual and textual nodes to generate search-intensive multimodal QA pairs requiring various search tools; (2) DR-TTS (Decompose and Re-compose Tool-Trajectory Search) - decomposes search-involved tasks into categories, optimizes specialized search tool experts for each tool, and recomposes them via tree search to produce successful search trajectories; (3) An offline search engine supporting multiple search tools to enable agentic reinforcement learning without costly online APIs.
AI.result: The authors develop MM-DeepResearch and demonstrate its superiority across benchmarks, showing that the proposed approaches effectively address the challenges of multimodal deep research.
AI.conclusion: By combining Hyper-Search for data generation, DR-TTS for trajectory optimization, and an offline search engine for cost-effective training, MM-DeepResearch successfully enables multimodal deep research agents with explicit reasoning, planning, multi-tool invocation, and cross-modal information synthesis capabilities.

title: Causal Circuit Tracing Reveals Distinct Computational Architectures in Single-Cell Foundation Models: Inhibitory Dominance, Biological Coherence, and Cross-Model Convergence
summary:
Motivation: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) decompose foundation model activations into interpretable features, but causal feature-to-feature interactions across network depth remain unknown for biological foundation models.
  Results: We introduce causal circuit tracing by ablating SAE features and measuring downstream responses, and apply it to Geneformer V2-316M and scGPT whole-human across four conditions (96,892 edges, 80,191 forward passes). Both models show approximately 53 percent biological coherence and 65 to 89 percent inhibitory dominance, invariant to architecture and cell type. scGPT produces stronger effects (mean absolute d = 1.40 vs. 1.05) with more balanced dynamics. Cross-model consensus yields 1,142 conserved domain pairs (10.6x enrichment, p < 0.001). Disease-associated domains are 3.59x more likely to be consensus. Gene-level CRISPRi validation shows 56.4 percent directional accuracy, confirming co-expression rather than causal encoding.

title: Unleashing VLA Potentials in Autonomous Driving via Explicit Learning from Failures
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for autonomous driving often hit a performance plateau during Reinforcement Learning (RL) optimization. This stagnation arises from exploration capabilities constrained by previous Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), leading to persistent failures in long-tail scenarios. In these critical situations, all explored actions yield a zero-value driving score. This information-sparse reward signals a failure, yet fails to identify its root cause -- whether it is due to incorrect planning, flawed reasoning, or poor trajectory execution. To address this limitation, we propose VLA with Explicit Learning from Failures (ELF-VLA), a framework that augments RL with structured diagnostic feedback. Instead of relying on a vague scalar reward, our method produces detailed, interpretable reports that identify the specific failure mode. The VLA policy then leverages this explicit feedback to generate a Feedback-Guided Refinement. By injecting these corrected, high-reward samples back into the RL training batch, our approach provides a targeted gradient, which enables the policy to solve critical scenarios that unguided exploration cannot. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method unlocks the latent capabilities of VLA models, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on the public NAVSIM benchmark for overall PDMS, EPDMS score and high-level planning accuracy.

AI.tldr: ELF-VLA is a framework that augments VLA models for autonomous driving with explicit learning from failures, using diagnostic feedback to refine policies and solve critical long-tail scenarios.
AI.motivation: VLA models for autonomous driving hit performance plateaus during RL optimization because exploration is constrained by previous Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT). In long-tail scenarios, all explored actions yield zero-value driving scores, but the vague scalar reward fails to identify the root cause of failure (incorrect planning, flawed reasoning, or poor trajectory execution).
AI.method: ELF-VLA augments RL with structured diagnostic feedback that produces detailed, interpretable reports identifying specific failure modes. The VLA policy generates Feedback-Guided Refinement and injects corrected, high-reward samples back into the RL training batch, providing targeted gradients to the policy.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on the NAVSIM benchmark for overall PDMS, EPDMS score, and high-level planning accuracy, showing the method unlocks latent capabilities of VLA models.
AI.conclusion: By replacing vague scalar rewards with explicit diagnostic feedback and targeted gradient injection, ELF-VLA enables VLA policies to solve critical scenarios that unguided exploration cannot, effectively addressing the exploration plateau problem in autonomous driving.

title: Meta-Learning Hyperparameters for Parameter Efficient Fine-Tuning
comment: Accepted by CVPR 2025 (Highlight). Code is available at: https://github.com/doem97/metalora
summary: Training large foundation models from scratch for domain-specific applications is almost impossible due to data limits and long-tailed distributions -- taking remote sensing (RS) as an example. Fine-tuning natural image pre-trained models on RS images is a straightforward solution. To reduce computational costs and improve performance on tail classes, existing methods apply parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) techniques, such as LoRA and AdaptFormer. However, we observe that fixed hyperparameters -- such as intra-layer positions, layer depth, and scaling factors, can considerably hinder PEFT performance, as fine-tuning on RS images proves highly sensitive to these settings. To address this, we propose MetaPEFT, a method incorporating adaptive scalers that dynamically adjust module influence during fine-tuning. MetaPEFT dynamically adjusts three key factors of PEFT on RS images: module insertion, layer selection, and module-wise learning rates, which collectively control the influence of PEFT modules across the network. We conduct extensive experiments on three transfer-learning scenarios and five datasets in both RS and natural image domains. The results show that MetaPEFT achieves state-of-the-art performance in cross-spectral adaptation, requiring only a small amount of trainable parameters and improving tail-class accuracy significantly.

AI.tldr: MetaPEFT is a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method for remote sensing images that dynamically adjusts module insertion, layer selection, and learning rates using adaptive scalers to improve performance on tail classes while minimizing trainable parameters.
AI.motivation: Training large foundation models from scratch for domain-specific applications like remote sensing is impractical due to data limits and long-tailed distributions. Fine-tuning natural image pre-trained models using PEFT techniques like LoRA and AdaptFormer faces challenges because fixed hyperparameters (intra-layer positions, layer depth, scaling factors) hinder performance due to the high sensitivity of RS images to these settings.
AI.method: MetaPEFT incorporates adaptive scalers that dynamically adjust module influence during fine-tuning by automatically controlling three key factors: module insertion, layer selection, and module-wise learning rates. These adaptive scalars allow the method to adjust the influence of PEFT modules across the network in response to the characteristics of RS images.
AI.result: MetaPEFT achieves state-of-the-art performance in cross-spectral adaptation across three transfer-learning scenarios and five datasets in both RS and natural image domains, requiring only a small amount of trainable parameters while significantly improving tail-class accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic hyperparameter adjustment through adaptive scalars effectively addresses the sensitivity of PEFT to fixed settings in remote sensing applications, enabling superior performance with minimal computational overhead and better handling of long-tailed distributions in domain-specific fine-tuning scenarios.

title: LLaDA-o: An Effective and Length-Adaptive Omni Diffusion Model
summary: We present \textbf{LLaDA-o}, an effective and length-adaptive omni diffusion model for multimodal understanding and generation. LLaDA-o is built on a Mixture of Diffusion (MoD) framework that decouples discrete masked diffusion for text understanding and continuous diffusion for visual generation, while coupling them through a shared, simple, and efficient attention backbone that reduces redundant computation for fixed conditions. Building on MoD, we further introduce a data-centric length adaptation strategy that enables flexible-length decoding in multimodal settings without architectural changes. Extensive experiments show that LLaDA-o achieves state-of-the-art performance among omni-diffusion models on multimodal understanding and generation benchmarks, and reaches 87.04 on DPG-Bench for text-to-image generation, supporting the effectiveness of unified omni diffusion modeling. Code is available at https://github.com/ML-GSAI/LLaDA-o.

AI.tldr: LLaDA-o is a length-adaptive omni diffusion model using a Mixture of Diffusion framework that decouples text understanding (discrete masked diffusion) from visual generation (continuous diffusion) while sharing an efficient attention backbone, achieving state-of-the-art performance on multimodal benchmarks with 87.04 on DPG-Bench.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to develop an effective and length-adaptive omni diffusion model for unified multimodal understanding and generation that reduces redundant computation and enables flexible-length decoding without architectural changes.
AI.method: LLaDA-o builds on a Mixture of Diffusion (MoD) framework that decouples discrete masked diffusion for text understanding from continuous diffusion for visual generation, couples them through a shared simple attention backbone to reduce redundant computation for fixed conditions, and introduces a data-centric length adaptation strategy enabling flexible-length decoding in multimodal settings.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that LLaDA-o achieves state-of-the-art performance among omni-diffusion models on multimodal understanding and generation benchmarks, reaching 87.04 on DPG-Bench for text-to-image generation.
AI.conclusion: The results validate the effectiveness of unified omni diffusion modeling, with LLaDA-o demonstrating superior performance in both text understanding and visual generation tasks.

title: Modular Memory is the Key to Continual Learning Agents
comment: This work stems from discussions held at the Dagstuhl seminar on Continual Learning in the Era of Foundation Models (October 2025)
summary: Foundation models have transformed machine learning through large-scale pretraining and increased test-time compute. Despite surpassing human performance in several domains, these models remain fundamentally limited in continuous operation, experience accumulation, and personalization, capabilities that are central to adaptive intelligence. While continual learning research has long targeted these goals, its historical focus on in-weight learning (IWL), i.e., updating a single model's parameters to absorb new knowledge, has rendered catastrophic forgetting a persistent challenge. Our position is that combining the strengths of In-Weight Learning (IWL) and the newly emerged capabilities of In-Context Learning (ICL) through the design of modular memory is the missing piece for continual adaptation at scale. We outline a conceptual framework for modular memory-centric architectures that leverage ICL for rapid adaptation and knowledge accumulation, and IWL for stable updates to model capabilities, charting a practical roadmap toward continually learning agents.

AI.tldr: Foundation models lack continuous adaptation capabilities; this paper proposes combining in-weight learning (IWL) and in-context learning (ICL) through modular memory architectures to enable continual learning at scale.
AI.motivation: Foundation models excel at static tasks but lack continuous operation, experience accumulation, and personalization - key components of adaptive intelligence. Traditional continual learning focused on in-weight learning (IWL) suffers from catastrophic forgetting.
AI.method: A conceptual framework for modular memory-centric architectures that integrates IWL for stable parameter updates with ICL for rapid adaptation and knowledge accumulation.
AI.result: Outlines a practical roadmap for continually learning agents using the proposed modular memory approach that leverages both learning paradigms.
AI.conclusion: Combining IWL and ICL through modular memory design represents the missing piece for achieving continual adaptation at scale in foundation models.

title: Flow Matching-enabled Test-Time Refinement for Unsupervised Cardiac MR Registration
summary: Diffusion-based unsupervised image registration has been explored for cardiac cine MR, but expensive multi-step inference limits practical use. We propose FlowReg, a flow-matching framework in displacement field space that achieves strong registration in as few as two steps and supports further refinement with more steps. FlowReg uses warmup-reflow training: a single-step network first acts as a teacher, then a student learns to refine from arbitrary intermediate states, removing the need for a pre-trained model as in existing methods. An Initial Guess strategy feeds back the model prediction as the next starting point, improving refinement from step two onward. On ACDC and MM2 across six tasks (including cross-dataset generalization), FlowReg outperforms the state of the art on five tasks (+0.6% mean Dice score on average), with the largest gain in the left ventricle (+1.09%), and reduces LVEF estimation error on all six tasks (-2.58 percentage points), using only 0.7% extra parameters and no segmentation labels. Code is available at https://github.com/mathpluscode/FlowReg.

AI.tldr: FlowReg is a flow-matching framework for unsupervised cardiac cine MR image registration that achieves strong performance with only 2-3 inference steps, eliminating the need for pre-trained models and segmentation labels.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based unsupervised image registration for cardiac cine MR suffers from expensive multi-step inference and requires pre-trained models, limiting its practical utility.
AI.method: FlowReg employs a flow-matching framework in displacement field space with warmup-reflow training: a single-step network acts as teacher while a student learns to refine from arbitrary intermediate states. An Initial Guess strategy feeds predictions back for iterative refinement, supporting 2-step inference with optional further steps.
AI.result: On ACDC and MM2 datasets across six tasks, FlowReg outperforms state-of-the-art on five tasks (+0.6% mean Dice score average, +1.09% in left ventricle) and reduces LVEF estimation error by -2.58 percentage points. It achieves this with only 0.7% extra parameters and without requiring segmentation labels.
AI.conclusion: FlowReg demonstrates that flow-matching in displacement field space can achieve superior registration performance with minimal computational cost, eliminating the need for pre-trained models and segmentation labels while enabling practical 2-step inference.

title: DGNet: Discrete Green Networks for Data-Efficient Learning of Spatiotemporal PDEs
comment: Accepted as a conference paper at ICLR 2026
summary: Spatiotemporal partial differential equations (PDEs) underpin a wide range of scientific and engineering applications. Neural PDE solvers offer a promising alternative to classical numerical methods. However, existing approaches typically require large numbers of training trajectories, while high-fidelity PDE data are expensive to generate. Under limited data, their performance degrades substantially, highlighting their low data efficiency. A key reason is that PDE dynamics embody strong structural inductive biases that are not explicitly encoded in neural architectures, forcing models to learn fundamental physical structure from data. A particularly salient manifestation of this inefficiency is poor generalization to unseen source terms. In this work, we revisit Green's function theory-a cornerstone of PDE theory-as a principled source of structural inductive bias for PDE learning. Based on this insight, we propose DGNet, a discrete Green network for data-efficient learning of spatiotemporal PDEs. The key idea is to transform the Green's function into a graph-based discrete formulation, and embed the superposition principle into the hybrid physics-neural architecture, which reduces the burden of learning physical priors from data, thereby improving sample efficiency. Across diverse spatiotemporal PDE scenarios, DGNet consistently achieves state-of-the-art accuracy using only tens of training trajectories. Moreover, it exhibits robust zero-shot generalization to unseen source terms, serving as a stress test that highlights its data-efficient structural design.

AI.tldr: Neural PDE solvers typically require large numbers of training trajectories, but high-fidelity PDE data are expensive. The authors propose DGNet, a discrete Green network that transforms Green's function theory into a graph-based formulation and embeds the superposition principle into a hybrid physics-neural architecture, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with tens of training trajectories and robust zero-shot generalization to unseen source terms.
AI.motivation: Existing neural PDE solvers suffer from low data efficiency because PDE dynamics embody strong structural inductive biases that are not explicitly encoded in neural architectures. This forces models to learn physical structure from data, particularly leading to poor generalization to unseen source terms.
AI.method: DGNet (Discrete Green Network) transforms Green's function theory into a graph-based discrete formulation and embeds the superposition principle into a hybrid physics-neural architecture, reducing the burden of learning physical priors from data.
AI.result: DGNet consistently achieves state-of-the-art accuracy across diverse spatiotemporal PDE scenarios using only tens of training trajectories, and exhibits robust zero-shot generalization to unseen source terms.
AI.conclusion: By encoding structural inductive biases through Green's function theory, DGNet significantly improves sample efficiency and generalization capabilities for neural PDE solvers.

title: CHLU: The Causal Hamiltonian Learning Unit as a Symplectic Primitive for Deep Learning
comment: Accepted as a short paper at ICLR 2026 (AI & PDE)
summary: Current deep learning primitives dealing with temporal dynamics suffer from a fundamental dichotomy: they are either discrete and unstable (LSTMs) \citep{pascanu_difficulty_2013}, leading to exploding or vanishing gradients; or they are continuous and dissipative (Neural ODEs) \citep{dupont_augmented_2019}, which destroy information over time to ensure stability. We propose the \textbf{Causal Hamiltonian Learning Unit} (pronounced: \textit{clue}), a novel Physics-grounded computational learning primitive. By enforcing a Relativistic Hamiltonian structure and utilizing symplectic integration, a CHLU strictly conserves phase-space volume, as an attempt to solve the memory-stability trade-off. We show that the CHLU is designed for infinite-horizon stability, as well as controllable noise filtering. We then demonstrate a CHLU's generative ability using the MNIST dataset as a proof-of-principle.

AI.tldr: The Causal Hamiltonian Learning Unit (CHLU) is a novel physics-grounded learning primitive that enforces a relativistic Hamiltonian structure with symplectic integration to strictly conserve phase-space volume, potentially resolving the memory-stability trade-off in temporal deep learning models.
AI.motivation: Temporal deep learning primitives face a fundamental dichotomy: discrete models like LSTMs suffer from instability due to exploding/vanishing gradients, while continuous models like Neural ODEs are dissipative and destroy information over time. This creates an inherent trade-off between memory preservation and stability.
AI.method: CHLU enforces a relativistic Hamiltonian structure and utilizes symplectic integration to strictly conserve phase-space volume, creating a physics-grounded computational primitive designed for infinite-horizon stability and controllable noise filtering.
AI.result: The CHLU demonstrates generative capabilities using the MNIST dataset as a proof-of-principle, showing its potential to achieve both memory retention and stability simultaneously.
AI.conclusion: CHLU represents a novel approach to temporal modeling that attempts to overcome the fundamental limitations of existing discrete and continuous models by leveraging Hamiltonian mechanics and symplectic integration to preserve information while maintaining stability.

title: Beyond Global Similarity: Towards Fine-Grained, Multi-Condition Multimodal Retrieval
summary: Recent advances in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have substantially expanded the capabilities of multimodal retrieval, enabling systems to align and retrieve information across visual and textual modalities. Yet, existing benchmarks largely focus on coarse-grained or single-condition alignment, overlooking real-world scenarios where user queries specify multiple interdependent constraints across modalities. To bridge this gap, we introduce MCMR (Multi-Conditional Multimodal Retrieval): a large-scale benchmark designed to evaluate fine-grained, multi-condition cross-modal retrieval under natural-language queries. MCMR spans five product domains: upper and bottom clothing, jewelry, shoes, and furniture. It also preserves rich long-form metadata essential for compositional matching. Each query integrates complementary visual and textual attributes, requiring models to jointly satisfy all specified conditions for relevance. We benchmark a diverse suite of MLLM-based multimodal retrievers and vision-language rerankers to assess their condition-aware reasoning abilities. Experimental results reveal: (i) distinct modality asymmetries across models; (ii) visual cues dominate early-rank precision, while textual metadata stabilizes long-tail ordering; and (iii) MLLM-based pointwise rerankers markedly improve fine-grained matching by explicitly verifying query-candidate consistency. Overall, MCMR establishes a challenging and diagnostic benchmark for advancing multimodal retrieval toward compositional, constraint-aware, and interpretable understanding. Our code and dataset is available at https://github.com/EIT-NLP/MCMR

AI.tldr: MCMR is a large-scale benchmark for multi-conditional multimodal retrieval, evaluating fine-grained matching across five product domains with natural-language queries requiring joint satisfaction of multiple visual and textual constraints.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks focus on coarse-grained or single-condition alignment, overlooking real-world scenarios where user queries specify multiple interdependent constraints across modalities.
AI.method: MCMR spans five product domains (upper and bottom clothing, jewelry, shoes, furniture) with rich long-form metadata for compositional matching; each query integrates complementary visual and textual attributes requiring joint condition satisfaction.
AI.result: Experiments reveal distinct modality asymmetries across models; visual cues dominate early-rank precision while textual metadata stabilizes long-tail ordering; MLLM-based pointwise rerankers markedly improve fine-grained matching by verifying query-candidate consistency.
AI.conclusion: MCMR establishes a challenging and diagnostic benchmark for advancing multimodal retrieval toward compositional, constraint-aware, and interpretable understanding, with code and dataset publicly available.

title: Hyperparameter Trajectory Inference with Conditional Lagrangian Optimal Transport
summary: Neural networks (NNs) often have critical behavioural trade-offs that are set at design time with hyperparameters-such as reward weights in reinforcement learning or quantile targets in regression. Post-deployment, however, user preferences can evolve, making initial settings undesirable, necessitating potentially expensive retraining. To circumvent this, we introduce the task of Hyperparameter Trajectory Inference (HTI): to learn, from observed data, how a NN's conditional output distribution changes with its hyperparameters, and construct a surrogate model that approximates the NN at unobserved hyperparameter settings. HTI requires extending existing trajectory inference approaches to incorporate conditions, exacerbating the challenge of ensuring inferred paths are feasible. We propose an approach based on conditional Lagrangian optimal transport, jointly learning the Lagrangian function governing hyperparameter-induced dynamics along with the associated optimal transport maps and geodesics between observed marginals, which form the surrogate model. We incorporate inductive biases based on the manifold hypothesis and least-action principles into the learned Lagrangian, improving surrogate model feasibility. We empirically demonstrate that our approach reconstructs NN outputs across various hyperparameter spectra better than other alternatives.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Hyperparameter Trajectory Inference (HTI), a task to learn how a neural network's conditional output distribution changes with hyperparameters and construct a surrogate model that approximates the NN at unobserved hyperparameter settings, using conditional Lagrangian optimal transport with manifold-based inductive biases.
AI.motivation: Neural networks have critical behavioral trade-offs set at design time with hyperparameters (e.g., reward weights in reinforcement learning or quantile targets in regression). Post-deployment, user preferences can evolve, making initial settings undesirable and necessitating potentially expensive retraining. HTI aims to circumvent this by learning a surrogate model from observed data without retraining.
AI.method: The authors propose an approach based on conditional Lagrangian optimal transport that jointly learns the Lagrangian function governing hyperparameter-induced dynamics, along with associated optimal transport maps and geodesics between observed marginals. The method incorporates inductive biases based on the manifold hypothesis and least-action principles to improve surrogate model feasibility.
AI.result: Empirically demonstrated that the proposed approach reconstructs neural network outputs across various hyperparameter spectra better than other alternatives.
AI.conclusion: The proposed HTI method with conditional Lagrangian optimal transport effectively reconstructs neural network outputs across different hyperparameter settings, providing a more efficient alternative to retraining.

title: Can Vision Language Models Assess Graphic Design Aesthetics? A Benchmark, Evaluation, and Dataset Perspective
summary: Assessing the aesthetic quality of graphic design is central to visual communication, yet remains underexplored in vision language models (VLMs). We investigate whether VLMs can evaluate design aesthetics in ways comparable to humans. Prior work faces three key limitations: benchmarks restricted to narrow principles and coarse evaluation protocols, a lack of systematic VLM comparisons, and limited training data for model improvement. In this work, we introduce AesEval-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark spanning four dimensions, twelve indicators, and three fully quantifiable tasks: aesthetic judgment, region selection, and precise localization. Then, we systematically evaluate proprietary, open-source, and reasoning-augmented VLMs, revealing clear performance gaps against the nuanced demands of aesthetic assessment. Moreover, we construct a training dataset to fine-tune VLMs for this domain, leveraging human-guided VLM labeling to produce task labels at scale and indicator-grounded reasoning to tie abstract indicators to concrete design regions.Together, our work establishes the first systematic framework for aesthetic quality assessment in graphic design. Our code and dataset will be released at: \href{https://github.com/arctanxarc/AesEval-Bench}{https://github.com/arctanxarc/AesEval-Bench}

AI.tldr: AesEval-Bench introduces a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating aesthetic quality of graphic design using vision language models (VLMs). It spans four dimensions, twelve indicators, and three tasks (aesthetic judgment, region selection, precise localization), revealing performance gaps between VLMs and human-level assessment, and provides a training dataset with human-guided labeling for fine-tuning VLMs.
AI.motivation: Aesthetic quality assessment in graphic design remains underexplored in vision language models (VLMs). Prior work suffers from three limitations: benchmarks restricted to narrow principles and coarse evaluation protocols, lack of systematic VLM comparisons, and limited training data for model improvement.
AI.method: 1. Introduce AesEval-Bench: a benchmark spanning four dimensions, twelve indicators, and three quantifiable tasks (aesthetic judgment, region selection, precise localization). 2. Systematically evaluate proprietary, open-source, and reasoning-augmented VLMs. 3. Construct a training dataset using human-guided VLM labeling at scale and indicator-grounded reasoning to connect abstract indicators with concrete design regions.
AI.result: Clear performance gaps exist between VLMs and the nuanced demands of aesthetic assessment. The systematic evaluation reveals that current VLMs struggle with the complexity of graphic design aesthetics.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes the first systematic framework for aesthetic quality assessment in graphic design. The benchmark, evaluation methodology, and training dataset will be released publicly to advance research in this domain.

title: D3LM: A Discrete DNA Diffusion Language Model for Bidirectional DNA Understanding and Generation
comment: Accepted as a workshop paper at MLGenX 2026
summary: Early DNA foundation models adopted BERT-style training, achieving good performance on DNA understanding tasks but lacking generative capabilities. Recent autoregressive models enable DNA generation, but employ left-to-right causal modeling that is suboptimal for DNA where regulatory relationships are inherently bidirectional. We present D3LM (\textbf{D}iscrete \textbf{D}NA \textbf{D}iffusion \textbf{L}anguage \textbf{M}odel), which unifies bidirectional representation learning and DNA generation through masked diffusion. D3LM directly adopts the Nucleotide Transformer (NT) v2 architecture but reformulates the training objective as masked diffusion in discrete DNA space, enabling both bidirectional understanding and generation capabilities within a single model. Compared to NT v2 of the same size, D3LM achieves improved performance on understanding tasks. Notably, on regulatory element generation, D3LM achieves an SFID of 10.92, closely approaching real DNA sequences (7.85) and substantially outperforming the previous best result of 29.16 from autoregressive models. Our work suggests diffusion language models as a promising paradigm for unified DNA foundation models. We further present the first systematic study of masked diffusion models in the DNA domain, investigating practical design choices such as tokenization schemes and sampling strategies, thereby providing empirical insights and a solid foundation for future research. D3LM has been released at https://huggingface.co/collections/Hengchang-Liu/d3lm.

AI.tldr: D3LM unifies bidirectional DNA representation learning and generation through masked diffusion, outperforming both BERT-style and autoregressive models in understanding and regulatory element generation tasks.
AI.motivation: Early DNA foundation models (BERT-style) lacked generative capabilities, while recent autoregressive models use left-to-right causal modeling that is suboptimal for DNA's inherently bidirectional regulatory relationships.
AI.method: D3LM adopts the Nucleotide Transformer v2 architecture but reformulates training as masked diffusion in discrete DNA space, enabling unified bidirectional understanding and generation within a single model.
AI.result: D3LM achieves improved performance on understanding tasks over NT v2 and a regulatory element generation SFID of 10.92 (vs. 7.85 for real DNA and 29.16 for previous autoregressive best), demonstrating the effectiveness of diffusion models for DNA foundation models.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion language models represent a promising paradigm for unified DNA foundation models, with the work providing empirical insights and a foundation for future research on masked diffusion in the DNA domain.

title: Learning Shortest Paths with Generative Flow Networks
summary: In this paper, we present a novel learning framework for finding shortest paths in graphs utilizing Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets). First, we examine theoretical properties of GFlowNets in non-acyclic environments in relation to shortest paths. We prove that, if the total flow is minimized, forward and backward policies traverse the environment graph exclusively along shortest paths between the initial and terminal states. Building on this result, we show that the pathfinding problem in an arbitrary graph can be solved by training a non-acyclic GFlowNet with flow regularization. We experimentally demonstrate the performance of our method in pathfinding in permutation environments and in solving Rubik's Cubes. For the latter problem, our approach shows competitive results with state-of-the-art machine learning approaches designed specifically for this task in terms of the solution length, while requiring smaller search budget at test-time.

AI.tldr: A novel learning framework that uses GFlowNets to find shortest paths in graphs, demonstrating competitive performance on Rubik's Cube solving with smaller test-time search budgets than state-of-the-art approaches.
AI.motivation: Finding shortest paths in graphs is a fundamental problem with applications across many domains. While traditional algorithms exist, the authors aim to explore whether GFlowNets—a class of generative models that learn to sample trajectories from a source to a target—can be effectively applied to pathfinding, potentially offering advantages in terms of sample efficiency and generalization.
AI.method: The authors develop a theoretical framework showing that in non-acyclic environments, minimizing total flow in a GFlowNet causes forward and backward policies to traverse exclusively along shortest paths. Building on this, they propose training a non-acyclic GFlowNet with flow regularization to solve arbitrary graph pathfinding problems. The approach is evaluated on permutation environments and Rubik's Cube.
AI.result: The method demonstrates competitive performance on Rubik's Cube in terms of solution length compared to state-of-the-art ML approaches, while requiring significantly smaller search budgets at test-time. The theoretical results confirm that flow minimization in non-acyclic GFlowNets ensures optimal shortest-path behavior.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes that GFlowNets can effectively solve shortest path problems in graphs, particularly in non-acyclic environments. The key insight—that flow minimization induces shortest-path traversal—provides a principled connection between GFlowNet training and combinatorial optimization, suggesting broader applicability for using generative flow networks in structured search and planning tasks.

title: Differential privacy representation geometry for medical image analysis
summary: Differential privacy (DP)'s effect in medical imaging is typically evaluated only through end-to-end performance, leaving the mechanism of privacy-induced utility loss unclear. We introduce Differential Privacy Representation Geometry for Medical Imaging (DP-RGMI), a framework that interprets DP as a structured transformation of representation space and decomposes performance degradation into encoder geometry and task-head utilization. Geometry is quantified by representation displacement from initialization and spectral effective dimension, while utilization is measured as the gap between linear-probe and end-to-end utility. Across over 594,000 images from four chest X-ray datasets and multiple pretrained initializations, we show that DP is consistently associated with a utilization gap even when linear separability is largely preserved. At the same time, displacement and spectral dimension exhibit non-monotonic, initialization- and dataset-dependent reshaping, indicating that DP alters representation anisotropy rather than uniformly collapsing features. Correlation analysis reveals that the association between end-to-end performance and utilization is robust across datasets but can vary by initialization, while geometric quantities capture additional prior- and dataset-conditioned variation. These findings position DP-RGMI as a reproducible framework for diagnosing privacy-induced failure modes and informing privacy model selection.

AI.tldr: DP-RGMI analyzes how differential privacy affects medical imaging by decomposing utility loss into encoder geometry (displacement and spectral dimension) and task-head utilization (gap between linear-probe and end-to-end performance), revealing that DP consistently creates a utilization gap and alters representation anisotropy rather than uniformly collapsing features.
AI.motivation: Differential privacy (DP) in medical imaging is typically evaluated only through end-to-end performance, leaving the mechanism of privacy-induced utility loss unclear and the relationship between geometric properties of representation spaces and performance degradation poorly understood.
AI.method: DP-RGMI (Differential Privacy Representation Geometry for Medical Imaging) interprets DP as a structured transformation of representation space, decomposing performance degradation into encoder geometry (quantified by representation displacement from initialization and spectral effective dimension) and task-head utilization (measured as the gap between linear-probe and end-to-end utility).
AI.result: Across 594,000+ chest X-ray images from four datasets with multiple pretrained initializations, DP consistently creates a utilization gap even when linear separability is preserved. Displacement and spectral dimension exhibit non-monotonic, initialization- and dataset-dependent reshaping, indicating DP alters representation anisotropy rather than uniformly collapsing features. Correlation analysis shows the end-to-end performance and utilization association is robust across datasets but varies by initialization, while geometric quantities capture additional prior- and dataset-conditioned variation.
AI.conclusion: DP-RGMI establishes a reproducible framework for diagnosing privacy-induced failure modes and informing privacy model selection, demonstrating that utility loss stems from task-head utilization issues rather than pure feature collapse, and that geometric properties provide important insights into how DP transforms representation spaces.

title: Phase-Type Variational Autoencoders for Heavy-Tailed Data
summary:
Heavy-tailed distributions are ubiquitous in real-world data, where rare but extreme events dominate risk and variability. However, standard Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) employ simple decoder distributions (e.g., Gaussian) that fail to capture heavy-tailed behavior, while existing heavy-tail-aware extensions remain restricted to predefined parametric families whose tail behavior is fixed a priori.
  We propose the Phase-Type Variational Autoencoder (PH-VAE), whose decoder distribution is a latent-conditioned Phase-Type (PH) distribution defined as the absorption time of a continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC). This formulation composes multiple exponential time scales, yielding a flexible and analytically tractable decoder that adapts its tail behavior directly from the observed data. Experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks demonstrate that PH-VAE accurately recovers diverse heavy-tailed distributions, significantly outperforming Gaussian, Student-t, and extreme-value-based VAE decoders in modeling tail behavior and extreme quantiles. In multivariate settings, PH-VAE captures realistic cross-dimensional tail dependence through its shared latent representation. To our knowledge, this is the first work to integrate Phase-Type distributions into deep generative modeling, bridging applied probability and representation learning.

AI.tldr: PH-VAE is a novel VAE variant that uses Phase-Type distributions (absorption times of continuous-time Markov chains) as a decoder, enabling flexible, data-driven heavy-tail modeling without predefined parametric families.
AI.motivation: Heavy-tailed distributions are common in real-world data where rare extreme events dominate risk. Standard VAE decoders (e.g., Gaussian) fail to capture heavy-tail behavior, while existing heavy-tail extensions are limited to predefined parametric families with fixed tail behavior.
AI.method: PH-VAE employs a Phase-Type distribution as a latent-conditioned decoder defined as the absorption time of a continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC). This formulation composes multiple exponential time scales, yielding a flexible and analytically tractable decoder that adapts its tail behavior directly from observed data.
AI.result: PH-VAE accurately recovers diverse heavy-tailed distributions, significantly outperforming Gaussian, Student-t, and extreme-value-based VAE decoders in modeling tail behavior and extreme quantiles. In multivariate settings, it captures realistic cross-dimensional tail dependence through its shared latent representation.
AI.conclusion: This is the first work to integrate Phase-Type distributions into deep generative modeling, bridging applied probability and representation learning. The approach provides a flexible, analytically tractable framework for heavy-tail modeling that adapts to data rather than relying on fixed parametric assumptions.

title: HeroGS: Hierarchical Guidance for Robust 3D Gaussian Splatting under Sparse Views
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has recently emerged as a promising approach in novel view synthesis, combining photorealistic rendering with real-time efficiency. However, its success heavily relies on dense camera coverage; under sparse-view conditions, insufficient supervision leads to irregular Gaussian distributions, characterized by globally sparse coverage, blurred background, and distorted high-frequency areas. To address this, we propose HeroGS, Hierarchical Guidance for Robust 3D Gaussian Splatting, a unified framework that establishes hierarchical guidance across the image, feature, and parameter levels. At the image level, sparse supervision is converted into pseudo-dense guidance, globally regularizing the Gaussian distributions and forming a consistent foundation for subsequent optimization. Building upon this, Feature-Adaptive Densification and Pruning (FADP) at the feature level leverages low-level features to refine high-frequency details and adaptively densifies Gaussians in background regions. The optimized distributions then support Co-Pruned Geometry Consistency (CPG) at parameter level, which guides geometric consistency through parameter freezing and co-pruning, effectively removing inconsistent splats. The hierarchical guidance strategy effectively constrains and optimizes the overall Gaussian distributions, thereby enhancing both structural fidelity and rendering quality. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HeroGS achieves high-fidelity reconstructions and consistently surpasses state-of-the-art baselines under sparse-view conditions.

AI.tldr: HeroGS is a hierarchical guidance framework for robust 3D Gaussian Splatting under sparse-view conditions, addressing irregular Gaussian distributions through image-level pseudo-dense guidance, feature-adaptive densification and pruning, and co-pruned geometry consistency.
AI.motivation: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) struggles under sparse-view conditions due to insufficient supervision, leading to irregular Gaussian distributions characterized by globally sparse coverage, blurred backgrounds, and distorted high-frequency areas.
AI.method: HeroGS establishes hierarchical guidance across three levels: (1) Image level converts sparse supervision to pseudo-dense guidance for global regularization; (2) Feature level employs Feature-Adaptive Densification and Pruning (FADP) using low-level features to refine high-frequency details and adaptively densify background Gaussians; (3) Parameter level implements Co-Pruned Geometry Consistency (CPG) through parameter freezing and co-pruning to remove inconsistent splats.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that HeroGS achieves high-fidelity reconstructions and consistently surpasses state-of-the-art baselines under sparse-view conditions.
AI.conclusion: The hierarchical guidance strategy effectively constrains and optimizes overall Gaussian distributions, thereby enhancing both structural fidelity and rendering quality.

title: Uncertainty Quantification of Click and Conversion Estimates for the Autobidding
comment: 17 pages (10 main text + 7 appendix), 5 figures, 2 tables
summary: Modern e-commerce platforms employ various auction mechanisms to allocate paid slots for a given item. To scale this approach to the millions of auctions, the platforms suggest promotion tools based on the autobidding algorithms. These algorithms typically depend on the Click-Through-Rate (CTR) and Conversion-Rate (CVR) estimates provided by a pre-trained machine learning model. However, the predictions of such models are uncertain and can significantly affect the performance of the autobidding algorithm. To address this issue, we propose the DenoiseBid method, which corrects the generated CTRs and CVRs to make the resulting bids more efficient in auctions. The underlying idea of our method is to employ a Bayesian approach and replace noisy CTR or CVR estimates with those from recovered distributions. To demonstrate the performance of the proposed approach, we perform extensive experiments on the synthetic, iPinYou, and BAT datasets. To evaluate the robustness of our approach to the noise scale, we use synthetic noise and noise estimated from the predictions of the pre-trained machine learning model.

AI.tldr: DenoiseBid corrects noisy CTR/CVR estimates in e-commerce auction autobidding systems using a Bayesian approach to improve bid efficiency.
AI.motivation: Modern e-commerce platforms use auction mechanisms for slot allocation, with autobidding algorithms dependent on CTR/CVR predictions from pre-trained ML models. However, these predictions are uncertain and significantly affect auction performance.
AI.method: The authors propose DenoiseBid, a Bayesian approach that replaces noisy CTR or CVR point estimates with values from recovered distributions, correcting the estimates to make bids more efficient.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on synthetic, iPinYou, and BAT datasets demonstrate the method's effectiveness, with robust performance across different noise scales.
AI.conclusion: DenoiseBid effectively improves auction bid efficiency by correcting noisy ML predictions through a Bayesian distribution-based approach.

title: Data-Efficient Brushstroke Generation with Diffusion Models for Oil Painting
summary: Many creative multimedia systems are built upon visual primitives such as strokes or textures, which are difficult to collect at scale and fundamentally different from natural image data. This data scarcity makes it challenging for modern generative models to learn expressive and controllable primitives, limiting their use in process-aware content creation. In this work, we study the problem of learning human-like brushstroke generation from a small set of hand-drawn samples (n=470) and propose StrokeDiff, a diffusion-based framework with Smooth Regularization (SmR). SmR injects stochastic visual priors during training, providing a simple mechanism to stabilize diffusion models under sparse supervision without altering the inference process. We further show how the learned primitives can be made controllable through a Bézier-based conditioning module and integrated into a complete stroke-based painting pipeline, including prediction, generation, ordering, and compositing. This demonstrates how data-efficient primitive modeling can support expressive and structured multimedia content creation. Experiments indicate that the proposed approach produces diverse and structurally coherent brushstrokes and enables paintings with richer texture and layering, validated by both automatic metrics and human evaluation.

AI.tldr: StrokeDiff is a diffusion-based framework with Smooth Regularization (SmR) that learns human-like brushstroke generation from only 470 hand-drawn samples, enabling controllable and diverse stroke primitives for process-aware multimedia content creation.
AI.motivation: Creative multimedia systems rely on visual primitives like strokes and textures, but these are difficult to collect at scale and fundamentally different from natural image data. This data scarcity makes it challenging for modern generative models to learn expressive and controllable primitives, limiting their use in process-aware content creation workflows.
AI.method: StrokeDiff employs Smooth Regularization (SmR) that injects stochastic visual priors during training to stabilize diffusion models under sparse supervision without altering the inference process. The framework includes a Bézier-based conditioning module for controllability and integrates into a complete stroke-based painting pipeline covering prediction, generation, ordering, and compositing.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that the approach produces diverse and structurally coherent brushstrokes, enabling paintings with richer texture and layering. Both automatic metrics and human evaluation validate the quality of the generated strokes and the effectiveness of the control mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: Data-efficient primitive modeling can successfully support expressive and structured multimedia content creation. The results show that with proper regularization techniques, diffusion models can learn from very limited training data (n=470) while maintaining high quality, diversity, and controllability in the generated primitives.

title: Constrained Particle Seeking: Solving Diffusion Inverse Problems with Just Forward Passes
summary: Diffusion models have gained prominence as powerful generative tools for solving inverse problems due to their ability to model complex data distributions. However, existing methods typically rely on complete knowledge of the forward observation process to compute gradients for guided sampling, limiting their applicability in scenarios where such information is unavailable. In this work, we introduce \textbf{\emph{Constrained Particle Seeking (CPS)}}, a novel gradient-free approach that leverages all candidate particle information to actively search for the optimal particle while incorporating constraints aligned with high-density regions of the unconditional prior. Unlike previous methods that passively select promising candidates, CPS reformulates the inverse problem as a constrained optimization task, enabling more flexible and efficient particle seeking. We demonstrate that CPS can effectively solve both image and scientific inverse problems, achieving results comparable to gradient-based methods while significantly outperforming gradient-free alternatives. Code is available at https://github.com/deng-ai-lab/CPS.

AI.tldr: Constrained Particle Seeking (CPS) is a novel gradient-free approach for solving inverse problems with diffusion models that actively searches for optimal particles while incorporating constraints from the unconditional prior distribution.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion model methods for inverse problems require complete knowledge of the forward observation process to compute gradients for guided sampling, limiting applicability when such information is unavailable.
AI.method: CPS reformulates the inverse problem as a constrained optimization task, leveraging all candidate particle information to actively search for the optimal particle while incorporating constraints aligned with high-density regions of the unconditional prior distribution.
AI.result: CPS effectively solves both image and scientific inverse problems, achieving results comparable to gradient-based methods while significantly outperforming gradient-free alternatives.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method provides a flexible and efficient solution for inverse problems with diffusion models when forward process information is unavailable, bridging the gap between gradient-based and gradient-free approaches.

title: GroundedSurg: A Multi-Procedure Benchmark for Language-Conditioned Surgical Tool Segmentation
