To address this, we introduce a decoupled active-to-stable state evolution paradigm. Our framework decouples the memory state into a dedicated active state and a persistent stable state, and then cohesively fuses the information from the former into the latter to achieve both fidelity and stability. Moreover, we jointly model visual appearance and language fields and incorporate an implicit Gaussian fusion module to enhance reconstruction quality. Experiments on mainstream datasets demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms prior work in novel view synthesis and semantic understanding, showcasing robust performance across input sequences of varying lengths with real-time inference speed.

AI.tldr: OnlineX introduces a feed-forward framework for online 3D scene reconstruction that decouples memory states into active and stable components, jointly modeling visual appearance and language fields to achieve both fidelity and stability in real-time reconstruction scenarios.
AI.motivation: Existing generalizable 3D Gaussian Splatting methods are offline paradigms lacking continuous reconstruction capabilities, limiting applicability to online scenarios such as robotics and VR/AR applications.
AI.method: OnlineX employs a decoupled active-to-stable state evolution paradigm that separates memory into dedicated active and persistent stable states, fuses information cohesively between them, and incorporates an implicit Gaussian fusion module to jointly model visual appearance and language fields using only streaming images.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate consistent superiority over prior methods in novel view synthesis and semantic understanding, with robust performance across varying input sequence lengths and real-time inference speed.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully addresses the cumulative drift challenge in online formulation by achieving both high-fidelity reconstruction and stable global structure preservation, making it suitable for demanding online applications.

title: 3D Field of Junctions: A Noise-Robust, Training-Free Structural Prior for Volumetric Inverse Problems
summary: Volume denoising is a foundational problem in computational imaging, as many 3D imaging inverse problems face high levels of measurement noise. Inspired by the strong 2D image denoising properties of Field of Junctions (ICCV 2021), we propose a novel, fully volumetric 3D Field of Junctions (3D FoJ) representation that optimizes a junction of 3D wedges that best explain each 3D patch of a full volume, while encouraging consistency between overlapping patches. In addition to direct volume denoising, we leverage our 3D FoJ representation as a structural prior that: (i) requires no training data, and thus precludes the risk of hallucination, (ii) preserves and enhances sharp edge and corner structures in 3D, even under low signal to noise ratio (SNR), and (iii) can be used as a drop-in denoising representation via projected or proximal gradient descent for any volumetric inverse problem with low SNR. We demonstrate successful volume reconstruction and denoising with 3D FoJ across three diverse 3D imaging tasks with low-SNR measurements: low-dose X-ray computed tomography (CT), cryogenic electron tomography (cryo-ET), and denoising point clouds such as those from lidar in adverse weather. Across these challenging low-SNR volumetric imaging problems, 3D FoJ outperforms a mixture of classical and neural methods.

AI.tldr: A novel 3D Field of Junctions (3D FoJ) representation is proposed for volume denoising, optimizing a junction of 3D wedges to explain volumetric patches while preserving structural details. This training-free approach outperforms classical and neural methods across low-dose CT, cryo-ET, and lidar point cloud denoising tasks.
AI.motivation: Volume denoising is a fundamental challenge in computational imaging, with many 3D imaging inverse problems suffering from high measurement noise. The authors were motivated by the strong 2D image denoising properties demonstrated by Field of Junctions (ICCV 2021), seeking to extend these capabilities to the 3D domain.
AI.method: The authors propose a fully volumetric 3D Field of Junctions (3D FoJ) representation that optimizes a junction of 3D wedges to best explain each 3D patch within a full volume, while encouraging consistency between overlapping patches. This serves as a structural prior with three key advantages: (1) requires no training data (eliminating hallucination risk), (2) preserves and enhances sharp edge and corner structures in 3D even under low SNR, and (3) functions as a drop-in denoising representation via projected or proximal gradient descent for any volumetric inverse problem.
AI.result: 3D FoJ successfully reconstructs and denoises volumes across three diverse low-SNR imaging tasks: low-dose X-ray computed tomography (CT), cryogenic electron tomography (cryo-ET), and denoising point clouds from lidar in adverse weather. The method outperforms a mixture of classical and neural denoising methods on these challenging problems.
AI.conclusion: The 3D FoJ representation is an effective training-free approach for volume denoising that preserves structural details (edges and corners) and performs competitively across various low-SNR 3D imaging applications, offering a robust alternative to data-driven methods.

title: Bridging the gap between Performance and Interpretability: An Explainable Disentangled Multimodal Framework for Cancer Survival Prediction
summary: While multimodal survival prediction models are increasingly more accurate, their complexity often reduces interpretability, limiting insight into how different data sources influence predictions. To address this, we introduce DIMAFx, an explainable multimodal framework for cancer survival prediction that produces disentangled, interpretable modality-specific and modality-shared representations from histopathology whole-slide images and transcriptomics data. Across multiple cancer cohorts, DIMAFx achieves state-of-the-art performance and improved representation disentanglement. Leveraging its interpretable design and SHapley Additive exPlanations, DIMAFx systematically reveals key multimodal interactions and the biological information encoded in the disentangled representations. In breast cancer survival prediction, the most predictive features contain modality-shared information, including one capturing solid tumor morphology contextualized primarily by late estrogen response, where higher-grade morphology aligned with pathway upregulation and increased risk, consistent with known breast cancer biology. Key modality-specific features capture microenvironmental signals from interacting adipose and stromal morphologies. These results show that multimodal models can overcome the traditional trade-off between performance and explainability, supporting their application in precision medicine.

AI.tldr: DIMAFx is an explainable multimodal framework for cancer survival prediction that disentangles histopathology and transcriptomics data into interpretable representations, achieving state-of-the-art performance while providing biological insights via SHAP explanations.
AI.motivation: Multimodal survival prediction models increasingly improve accuracy but often sacrifice interpretability, making it difficult to understand how different data sources influence predictions—a critical limitation for precision medicine applications.
AI.method: DIMAFx learns disentangled, interpretable representations from histopathology whole-slide images and transcriptomics data, producing both modality-specific and modality-shared representations. It employs SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) to systematically explain predictions and reveal key multimodal interactions.
AI.result: Across multiple cancer cohorts, DIMAFx achieves state-of-the-art performance and improved representation disentanglement. In breast cancer, the most predictive features contain modality-shared information (solid tumor morphology contextualized by late estrogen response), while modality-specific features capture microenvironmental signals from adipose and stromal morphologies.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal models can overcome the traditional performance-interpretability trade-off, supporting their application in precision medicine by providing both high predictive accuracy and interpretable biological insights into cancer survival.

title: GeoDiT: Point-Conditioned Diffusion Transformer for Satellite Image Synthesis
summary: We introduce GeoDiT, a diffusion transformer designed for text-to-satellite image generation with point-based control. Existing controlled satellite image generative models often require pixel-level maps that are time-consuming to acquire, yet semantically limited. To address this limitation, we introduce a novel point-based conditioning framework that controls the generation process through the spatial location of the points and the textual description associated with each point, providing semantically rich control signals. This approach enables flexible, annotation-friendly, and computationally simple inference for satellite image generation. To this end, we introduce an adaptive local attention mechanism that effectively regularizes the attention scores based on the input point queries. We systematically evaluate various domain-specific design choices for training GeoDiT, including the selection of satellite image representation for alignment and geolocation representation for conditioning. Our experiments demonstrate that GeoDiT achieves impressive generation performance, surpassing the state-of-the-art remote sensing generative models.

AI.tldr: GeoDiT is a diffusion transformer for text-to-satellite image generation using point-based control, enabling flexible and annotation-friendly generation that surpasses state-of-the-art models.
AI.motivation: Existing controlled satellite image generative models require pixel-level maps that are time-consuming to acquire and semantically limited.
AI.method: Introduces a point-based conditioning framework controlling generation through spatial location and textual description of points, combined with an adaptive local attention mechanism to regularize attention scores based on point queries. Also systematically evaluates domain-specific design choices for satellite image representation and geolocation representation.
AI.result: GeoDiT achieves impressive generation performance, surpassing state-of-the-art remote sensing generative models.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables flexible, annotation-friendly, and computationally simple inference for satellite image generation.

title: Kiwi-Edit: Versatile Video Editing via Instruction and Reference Guidance
summary: Instruction-based video editing has witnessed rapid progress, yet current methods often struggle with precise visual control, as natural language is inherently limited in describing complex visual nuances. Although reference-guided editing offers a robust solution, its potential is currently bottlenecked by the scarcity of high-quality paired training data. To bridge this gap, we introduce a scalable data generation pipeline that transforms existing video editing pairs into high-fidelity training quadruplets, leveraging image generative models to create synthesized reference scaffolds. Using this pipeline, we construct RefVIE, a large-scale dataset tailored for instruction-reference-following tasks, and establish RefVIE-Bench for comprehensive evaluation. Furthermore, we propose a unified editing architecture, Kiwi-Edit, that synergizes learnable queries and latent visual features for reference semantic guidance. Our model achieves significant gains in instruction following and reference fidelity via a progressive multi-stage training curriculum. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our data and architecture establish a new state-of-the-art in controllable video editing. All datasets, models, and code is released at https://github.com/showlab/Kiwi-Edit.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a scalable data generation pipeline that creates high-fidelity training quadruplets from existing video editing pairs using image generative models, constructs RefVIE dataset and RefVIE-Bench, proposes Kiwi-Edit architecture synergizing learnable queries and latent visual features for reference semantic guidance, achieving significant gains in instruction following and reference fidelity, establishing new SOTA in controllable video editing.
AI.motivation: Current instruction-based video editing struggles with precise visual control due to natural language limitations. Reference-guided editing is robust but bottlenecked by scarcity of high-quality paired training data.
AI.method: A scalable data generation pipeline transforming existing video editing pairs into high-fidelity training quadruplets using image generative models to create synthesized reference scaffolds; constructs RefVIE dataset and RefVIE-Bench for instruction-reference-following tasks; proposes Kiwi-Edit architecture synergizing learnable queries and latent visual features for reference semantic guidance; employs progressive multi-stage training curriculum.
AI.result: Achieves significant gains in instruction following and reference fidelity, establishing new state-of-the-art in controllable video editing.
AI.conclusion: The data and architecture establish new SOTA in controllable video editing, with all datasets, models, and code released at https://github.com/showlab/Kiwi-Edit.

title: HiFi-Inpaint: Towards High-Fidelity Reference-Based Inpainting for Generating Detail-Preserving Human-Product Images
summary: Human-product images, which showcase the integration of humans and products, play a vital role in advertising, e-commerce, and digital marketing. The essential challenge of generating such images lies in ensuring the high-fidelity preservation of product details. Among existing paradigms, reference-based inpainting offers a targeted solution by leveraging product reference images to guide the inpainting process. However, limitations remain in three key aspects: the lack of diverse large-scale training data, the struggle of current models to focus on product detail preservation, and the inability of coarse supervision for achieving precise guidance. To address these issues, we propose HiFi-Inpaint, a novel high-fidelity reference-based inpainting framework tailored for generating human-product images. HiFi-Inpaint introduces Shared Enhancement Attention (SEA) to refine fine-grained product features and Detail-Aware Loss (DAL) to enforce precise pixel-level supervision using high-frequency maps. Additionally, we construct a new dataset, HP-Image-40K, with samples curated from self-synthesis data and processed with automatic filtering. Experimental results show that HiFi-Inpaint achieves state-of-the-art performance, delivering detail-preserving human-product images.

AI.tldr: HiFi-Inpaint proposes a high-fidelity reference-based inpainting framework for generating human-product images with enhanced product detail preservation. It introduces Shared Enhancement Attention (SEA) and Detail-Aware Loss (DAL), and constructs a new dataset HP-Image-40K, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Existing reference-based inpainting approaches face three key limitations: (1) lack of diverse large-scale training data, (2) struggle to focus on preserving product details, and (3) inability of coarse supervision to provide precise guidance. These limitations hinder the generation of high-fidelity human-product images critical for advertising, e-commerce, and digital marketing.
AI.method: HiFi-Inpaint introduces two novel components: (1) Shared Enhancement Attention (SEA) to refine fine-grained product features, and (2) Detail-Aware Loss (DAL) for enforcing precise pixel-level supervision using high-frequency maps. Additionally, the authors construct a new dataset HP-Image-40K from self-synthesis data processed with automatic filtering.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate that HiFi-Inpaint achieves state-of-the-art performance in generating human-product images, successfully delivering images that preserve product details with high fidelity.
AI.conclusion: HiFi-Inpaint effectively addresses the limitations of existing approaches through its innovative attention mechanism and loss function design, providing a robust solution for high-fidelity human-product image generation that surpasses current methods in detail preservation.

title: MemeIntel: Explainable Detection of Propagandistic and Hateful Memes
comment: disinformation, misinformation, factuality, harmfulness, fake news, propaganda, hateful meme, multimodality, text, images
summary: The proliferation of multimodal content on social media presents significant challenges in understanding and moderating complex, context-dependent issues such as misinformation, hate speech, and propaganda. While efforts have been made to develop resources and propose new methods for automatic detection, limited attention has been given to jointly modeling label detection and the generation of explanation-based rationales, which often leads to degraded classification performance when trained simultaneously. To address this challenge, we introduce MemeXplain, an explanation-enhanced dataset for propagandistic memes in Arabic and hateful memes in English, making it the first large-scale resource for these tasks. To solve these tasks, we propose a multi-stage optimization approach and train Vision-Language Models (VLMs). Our results show that this strategy significantly improves both label detection and explanation generation quality over the base model, outperforming the current state-of-the-art with an absolute improvement of ~1.4% (Acc) on ArMeme and ~2.2% (Acc) on Hateful Memes. For reproducibility and future research, we aim to make the MemeXplain dataset and scripts publicly available (https://github.com/MohamedBayan/MemeIntel).

AI.tldr: MemeXplain is an explanation-enhanced dataset for propagandistic memes in Arabic and hateful memes in English, using a multi-stage optimization approach with Vision-Language Models (VLMs) that improves both label detection and explanation generation quality.
AI.motivation: Multimodal content on social media presents challenges in understanding and moderating misinformation, hate speech, and propaganda. Limited attention has been given to jointly modeling label detection and explanation-based rationales, which leads to degraded classification performance when trained simultaneously.
AI.method: The researchers introduce MemeXplain, the first large-scale resource for propagandistic memes in Arabic and hateful memes in English. They propose a multi-stage optimization approach and train Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to jointly perform label detection and explanation generation.
AI.result: The multi-stage VLM approach significantly improves both label detection and explanation generation quality over base models, outperforming current state-of-the-art with ~1.4% accuracy improvement on ArMeme and ~2.2% accuracy improvement on Hateful Memes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses the challenge of jointly modeling label detection and explanation generation in multimodal content moderation. The MemeXplain dataset and training scripts will be publicly available to ensure reproducibility and support future research.
